title: Roma Civilization_The History of Rome (Volumes 1-5) by Theodor Mommsen
paragraphs: The struggle of parties during Pompeius VI's absence was a necessary step in explaining alphabetic development. A single symbol could represent multiple sounds across various languages or even within the same language at different times. Thirty-three years later, such an introduction is no longer required, yet I still cherish the debt the book owes to Dr. Schmitz's helpful guidance at its inception.

I did not take on this task until I had investigated the possibility of someone else being better suited to handle it. When Dr Mommsen's work somehow came into my hands years after its initial appearance, and it revived my interest in studies I had long set aside for more professional pursuits. I had little doubt that its merits would have already attracted sufficient attention among Oxford's learned scholars, who might have been inspired to translate it into English. It seemed that despite a strong desire to translate it, and having considered the purpose of translation in multiple quarters, the projects had failed due to various reasons. Mr George Robertson published an excellent translation of the introductory chapters on the early inhabitants of Italy. I wish to acknowledge my obligations to this work as far as it goes.

Many of our translations from German are so literal that they reproduce the very order of the German sentence. This results in translations that are either unintelligible or far from readable for the English reader. In contrast, others deviate so entirely from the original form that they can no longer be considered true translations in the proper sense of the term. This, however, is a characteristic feature of the original. It owes much of its charm to this aspect. Dr Mommsen often employs expressions not found in dictionaries, taking full advantage of the German language's limitless possibilities to coin new terms or combine existing ones. I have not infrequently, in deference to his wishes, used such combinations as "Carthaginian" and "Hellenistic," although less congenial to our English idiom, for the sake of avoiding longer phrases. "In Dr As with Mommsen's book, many German works that address abstract topics often employ unusual terminology, making them difficult to translate.

The Index, which had not been issued for the German book until nine years after the English translation was published, has now been greatly enlarged from its earlier German form, and has been, at the expense of considerable labor, adapted to the altered pagination of the English. The cycle that includes cities such as Thebes, Carthage, Athens, and Rome can be considered a single entity. The four nations, having each developed their own unique and noble civilizations, interacted with one another in diverse ways, exchanging ideas and refining the complexities of human nature.

The distinction between ancient and modern history is not merely an accident, nor is it solely a matter of chronological convenience. The Apennines take a south-eastern course initially, between the broader gulf of the Mediterranean to the west and the narrower one to the east. In the vicinity of the latter, they reach their greatest elevation, which, however, rarely reaches the line of perpetual snow, in the Abruzzi Mountains. From the Abruzzi, the chain continues in a southern direction, at first undivided and of considerable height. After a depression that forms a hill country, it splits into a somewhat flattened succession of heights towards the south-east and a more rugged chain towards the south. In both directions, it terminates in the formation of narrow peninsulas.

The Greek peninsula faces east, while the Italian peninsula faces west. As the coasts of Epirus and Acarnania had but a subordinate importance in Hellas, so did the Apulian and Messapian coasts in Italy. The regions that were mainly dependent on historical development—Attica and Macedonia—looked eastward, while Etruria, Latium, and Campania looked westward. Although the naked eye can discern from Otranto the Acroceraunian mountains, the Italians and Hellenes came into closer contact with each other on every other pathway rather than across the Adriatic Sea. In their instance, as has often happened, the historical vocation of the nations was prefigured in the relations they occupied; the two great stocks, which nurtured the civilization of the ancient world, cast a shadow and sowed a seed, one towards the east and the other towards the west.

It was the civic community of Rome that initially gained sovereignty over Italy and eventually the world in the formal sense of political law. However, this perspective does not accurately convey the deeper, more profound meaning of history. U C  C Apulia, which in the time of Timaeus (400) was still described as a barbarous land, became a Greek province by the sixth century of its founding. Despite no direct colonization from Greece having occurred, the region showed signs of a similar cultural inclination among the Messapii, a less refined subgroup.

With the recognition of such a general family relationship or peculiar affinity between the Iapygians and Hellenes, an investigation must rest content for the time being, pending the attainment of more precise and assured results. The lack of information is not greatly felt; for this race, which was already in decline during the time when our history starts, appears to us only when it is giving way and disappearing. The excessive disorder of the terminations in the Umbrian language did not have its roots in the original spirit of the language. Instead, it was a corruption of later date that appeared in a similar, albeit weaker, tendency also at Rome. In Italian languages, short vowels are regularly dropped in the final sound, whereas long ones are frequently retained. In contrast, the concluding consonants have been tenaciously retained in Latin and even more so in Samnite dialects. The Umbrian language, however, drops both short and long vowels, as well as the concluding consonants.

In connection with this, we find that the middle voice has left only slight traces in Italian languages. A peculiar passive is formed by adding -r. Furthermore, most tenses are created by combining roots -es and -fu. The Greeks' richer terminational system, along with the augment, enable them to use auxiliary verbs in great part. The Italian languages, like the Aeolic dialect, abandoned the dual form but retained the ablative case, which the Greeks had previously lost, and in large measure also preserved the locative case. The Italian approach to logic seems to have taken issue with the separation of the concept of plurality from that of duality and multitude. They have made precise distinctions in expressing word relationships through inflections.

Some, such as the Martian and Volscian, have reached us in fragments too scanty to enable us to form any conception of their individual peculiarities or to classify the varieties of dialect themselves with certainty and precision. Others, like the Sabine, have completely disappeared, except for a few traces preserved as dialectic peculiarities in provincial Latin. A conjoint view of the facts of language and history leaves no doubt that all these dialects belonged to the Umbro-Samnite branch of the Italian stock. This branch, although more closely related to Latin than to Greek, was distinctly separate from the Latin. The genitive forms of words ending in -a are found in this group, as seen among the Greeks (-as), among the Romans in the mature language (-ae); those of words ending in -us are found in the Samnite (-eis) and Umbrian (-es), among the Romans (-ei). The locative form has largely disappeared from the latter dialects, whereas it remains in full use in other Italian dialects. The dative plural in -bus is preserved only in Latin. The Umbro-Samnite infinitive in -um is foreign to the Romans. In contrast, the Osco-Umbrian future formed from the root -es, following Greek patterns (-her-est-like), has largely disappeared in Latin. Instead, it is supplied by the optative of the simple verb or analogous formations derived from -fuo- (-amabo-).

In many instances, however, the differences only emerge when the cases are fully formed, whereas at the outset they coincide. It appears that, while Italian stands apart from Greek, the Latin dialect within it shares a relation with the Umbro-Samnite language similar to that of Ionic with Doric; and the differences between Oscan, Umbrian, and related dialects can be compared to the differences between Dorism in Sicily and Dorism in Sparta. With perfect certainty, they guide us to the conclusion that from the common cradle of peoples and languages, a stock emerged which encompassed the ancestors of both Greeks and Italians. This stock subsequently branched off into two distinct groups: one in Italy and another that split further into western and eastern stocks. The eastern group eventually divided into the Umbrians and Oscans at a later date.

During the period when the Indo-Germanic nations, which are now separated, still shared a common heritage, they developed a distinct culture. At this stage, their shared language enabled them to attain a certain level of cultural sophistication. In it we find not merely the simplest terms denoting existence, actions, and perceptions, such as -sum-, -do-, and -pater-. These words are the original echo of the impression made by the external world on the mind of man. They also include a number of words that reflect culture, both in their origins and through custom, which are shared among the Indo-Germanic languages. It is unclear how these words developed uniformly across languages or if they were borrowed from one another at a later time. Although we have reached no conclusive results through this approach, further insight into the subject is gained from observing that many key terms relevant to this cultural domain are found in Sanskrit, albeit with a broader meaning. Agras among the Indians denote a level surface in general. Kurnu refers to anything pounded. Aritram denotes an oar and a ship. Venas signifies what is pleasant in general, particularly a pleasant drink.

We may accordingly assume it is probable that the primeval Indo-Germanic people were not yet acquainted with agriculture. It is certain that if they were so, agriculture played but a very subordinate part in their economy. If it had held its place among the Greeks and Romans as it did afterwards, it would have left a deeper impression on their language. This cannot, however, be equally affirmed of the higher art of weaving. The knowledge of using fire to prepare food and salt for seasoning it is a primal inheritance of the Indo-Germanic nations. Similarly, the knowledge of the earliest metals used as tools or adornments by humans can also be confirmed. The conception of heaven as the father and earth as the mother of being, is a fundamental idea shared by Indian, Greek, and Roman mythologies. The gods' festal expeditions, where they travel from place to place in their own chariots along carefully levelled paths, are also an integral part of these mythologies. Additionally, the concept of the soul's continuation after death is a recurring theme in all three cultures.

The Graeco-Italian cultural influence is a topic of interest. However, the task of determining the extent of culture that the Indo-Germanic people had achieved prior to their separation from the Greek and Italian civilizations should be considered within the broader context of ancient world history. The Indo-Germans likely followed a pastoral lifestyle and may have been familiar with grains in their natural state. However, evidence suggests that the Graeco-Italians were primarily grain cultivators, possibly also wine producers. It would thus appear that the transition from a pastoral life to agriculture occurred when the Indians left the common cradle of the nations. This transition likely took place before the Hellenes and Italians dissolved their ancient communion with one another.

For the present, therefore, it must suffice to indicate the differences between the culture of the Indo-Germanic family in its oldest, undivided form, and the culture of that epoch when the Graeco-Italians still lived together. The task of distinguishing results common to European family members from their Asiatic counterparts requires a deeper understanding of cultural achievements unique to each European group, such as the Graeco-Italian and Germano-Slavonic groups. Among the Italians, as among the Hellenes, legend and faith, laws and customs, are closely associated with agriculture. Cultivation of the soil cannot be conceived without some measure of it, however rough.

The Greeks and Italians have a particularly close relationship beyond agriculture, which is evident in other areas of human activity. The boat with oars was an old common possession of the Indo-Germans. However, the transition to using sailing vessels cannot be attributed to the Graeco-Italian period, as no nautical terms were originally shared by the Greeks and Italians that are also part of the Indo-Germanic family.

On the other hand, the primitive Italian custom of husbandmen having common midday meals, which the myth connects with the introduction of agriculture, is compared by Aristotle to the Cretan Syssitia. The earliest Romans further agreed with the Cretans and Laconians in taking their meals while sitting, rather than reclining, as was later customary among both peoples. While under Greece's weaker political development, the clan-bond remained a corporate power distinct from that of the state, even into historical times. In contrast, the state in Italy emerged complete, with its authority neutralizing the clans and forming an association of citizens rather than clans. In ancient Greece, clan names often included an adjective form of the individual's name. In contrast, Roman scholars recognized that their ancestors initially had only one name, known as praenomen. While in Greece, the clan-name as an adjective had largely fallen out of use, it became a primary name among the Italians, not just the Romans. The distinctive personal name, known as praenomen, then took its place as a secondary identifier.

It could not be otherwise; for just as in the peoples themselves, great contrasts that had lain side by side during the Graeco-Italian period were distinctly evolved after their division, so too did a separation occur between the idea and the image in their religion. Hitherto, these two concepts had been one whole within the soul. To everything that exists, from the man to the tree, from the state to the store-room, a spirit was assigned, which came into being with it and perished along with it. This counterpart mirrored the natural phenomenon in the spiritual realm; for humans, it was the male Genius, while women were associated with the female Juno. Similarly, boundaries were represented by Terminus, forests by Silvanus, and the changing seasons by Vertumnus, mirroring the characteristics of each object. While the Greeks assumed everything had a concrete and corporeal shape, the Romans relied on abstract, transparent formulas. In contrast, the Greeks largely discarded their ancient, legendary treasures from primitive times, as they were too straightforward in expression. The Romans, however, found it even more difficult to retain these relics, as the sacred conceptions seemed diminished by even the slightest hint of allegory.

The training of youth was strictly confined to domestic education in Latium. In contrast, ancient Greece fostered a desire for a well-rounded training that combined mental and physical development, giving rise to the disciplines of Gymnastics and Paideia, which were highly valued by both the nation and individuals as essential components of a fulfilling life. Both nations underwent complete development on their own terms; it is only a pitiful narrow-mindedness that would object to the Athenian's inability to shape his state like the Fabii and the Valerii, or to the Roman's failure to master the art of sculpture like Pheidias and writing like Aristophanes.

The connection between agriculture and marriage was a crucial aspect of early cultures. The establishment of cities also played a vital role in this period. The lance was one of the oldest names of weapons on both sides. Few can be linked with certainty. Lancea is likely related to logchei, but it's a Roman word, making it relatively recent. It may have been adopted from Germanic or Spanish sources. 9 It is challenging to pinpoint the exact location of the Indo-Germanic people's origins. Nonetheless, it is clear that their ancestral homeland was likely situated inland, far removed from the coast. This conclusion is supported by the fact that there is no shared name for the sea in both the Asiatic and European branches of the Indo-European language family.

This circumstance suggests that these races may have had an original connection, although any such connection would necessarily predate all observable cultural and linguistic developments. The name for the sea is shared by many European countries, including Latins, Celts, Germans, and Slavs. It's likely that these groups converged on the coast of either the Black Sea or the Caspian Sea before their separation. By what route did the Italians reach the chain of the Alps, and where were they specifically settled while still united with the Hellenes alone? These questions can only be answered once the problem is solved by determining whether the Hellenes arrived in Greece via Asia Minor or the regions of the Danube.

The stream only overflows when the lower grounds are already occupied. It is only through the supposition that there were existing Latin settlements on the coast that we can explain why the Sabellians chose to settle in the rougher mountain districts. From these districts, they later issued and intruded into the territories of the Latin tribes wherever possible. The primitive inhabitants of the areas occupied by the Lucani and Bruttii, who are known as the Itali proper (the people of the land of oxen), are associated with the Italian stock by the most knowledgeable observers. They are not linked to the Iapygian people but rather to the Italian branch of the Latin people. This connection is supported because the Hellenization of these regions, which occurred even before Italy's political development began, and their subsequent invasion by Samnite tribes, have erased all evidence of their original nationality. The affinity of certain dialectical features in Sicilian Greek with Latin is likely due to the old commercial ties between Rome and the Sicilian Greeks, rather than their shared linguistic heritage.

It was otherwise with Latium, where no Greek colonies were founded. The inhabitants, having endured hard struggles, successfully maintained their territory against both the Sabines and their northern neighbors. The plain of Latium must have been the scene of the grandest conflicts of nature in primeval times. The slow formation of water deposited layers, and the eruptions of mighty volcanoes upheaved the soil, creating successive strata that would decide which people should claim sovereignty over the world. The malaria can be largely eradicated through thorough farming practices. While this phenomenon is not yet fully understood, it's possible that the increased drainage caused by surface cultivation contributes to the reduction of stagnant water.

In other respects, the district must have appeared attractive to an immigrant agricultural people. The soil is easily laboured with a mattock and hoe and is productive even without being manured. Although tried by an Italian standard, it does not yield any extraordinary return. Wheat yields on average about five times its value. Good water is scarce; the higher and more sacred was the esteem in which every fresh spring was held by the inhabitants. Some knowledge can be gained in this way, or at least some conjectures about probability. As each household had its own portion of land, so too did the clan-household or village have a clan-land belonging to it. This arrangement, as will be explained later, was managed in a manner similar to household-land, following the principle of joint-possession.

Cantons. However, these clanships were regarded from the beginning not as independent societies, but as integral parts of a political community (-civitas-, -populus-). In Italy, a place was called "height" (capitolium), similar to --akra--, the mountain-top, or "stronghold" (arx), derived from -arcere-. Initially, it was not a town, but rather the nucleus of one, as houses naturally congregated around the stronghold and were later enclosed within the "ring" (urbs), connected to -urvus-, -rurvus-, possibly also linked to -orbis-. We shall certainly be nearer the truth in recognizing these structures not as walled towns, but as places of refuge for the inhabitants of the district, such as those found in more ancient times throughout Italy, although they were constructed in a less artistic style.

When peace was securely established throughout the land, fortresses that once served as refuges were abandoned. Over time, they became a mystery for future generations. During the Latin festival, a truce of God was observed throughout all Latium, as it had been during the festivals of the Hellenic leagues. Tribes at feud likely granted safe-conducts to each other on this occasion. Like -latus- (side) and -platus- (flat), it denotes a flat country in contrast to the Sabine mountain land. Similarly, Campania, the "plain", forms a contrast with Samnium.

Sat i  iv l c ) 6 All history begins not with the union, but with the disunion of a nation. It is highly unlikely that the problem of uniting Latium was resolved before Rome's eventual solution after several centuries of conflict. After the union, each of these three communities – once separate but now forming subdivisions of a single community – still held one-third of the common domain and enjoyed proportional representation in both the burgess force and the council of elders.

Leaving aside self-contradictory and unfounded hypotheses, we can summarize in a few words what is known about the nationality of the component elements of the early Roman commonwealth. The Romans were indeed a Latin people, and it is clear that they played a significant role in shaping the identity of the new Roman Commonwealth. Their influence on the community was substantial, as evidenced by the fact that their name became synonymous with the new entity. It may be, therefore, that at a period very remote, when the Latin and Sabellian stocks were beyond question far less sharply contrasted in language, manners, and customs than were the Romans and Samnites of a later age, a Sabellian community entered into a Latin confederation; and, as in the older and more credible traditions without exception, the Tities took precedence over the Ramnians, it is probable that the intruding Tities compelled the older Ramnians to accept synoikismos.

A mixture of different nationalities certainly took place. However, this did not have a significant impact compared to the migration that occurred later, such as when the Sabine Attus Clauzus or Appius Claudius and his followers arrived in Rome. Rome, the Etruscan civilization of Latium, existed long before an urban settlement arose on the Tiber. The region was inhabited by various tribes, including the Ramnes, Tities, and Luceres, who initially lived separately but later united. They established their stronghold on the Roman hills and cultivated their fields from surrounding villages. Neither the vine nor the fig succeed well in the immediate environs. There is a lack of springs yielding a good supply of water. The Camenae fountain before the Porta Capena and the Capitoline well, later enclosed within the Tullianum, do not provide an abundance of water.

Its very slight fall renders it unable to carry off the water, which during the rainy season descends in large quantities from the mountains to the sea. As a result, the low-lying lands and valleys that lie between the hills are flooded, transforming them into swamps. It is the first duty of history to dismiss tales that claim to be historical but are merely improvised explanations of little significance. However, it may be permissible to take a step further and, after considering the special connections between the location, propose a positive conjecture. This should focus on the circumstances that led to the area's rapid growth and its unique position in Latium, rather than the origin of the place itself. In contrast, while the Roman canton was limited to a landward direction, it stretched freely along both banks of the Tiber towards the sea.

The galley arrived in the city; this also brought the ancient Roman port duties on exports and imports of Ostia, which were first levied only on goods intended for sale, not on those reserved for the shipper's use. As a result, these duties constituted a tax on commerce. Giorgio in Velabro, and the Porta Mugionis at the Arch of Titus, are still known to us. The Palatine ring-wall is described by Tacitus from his own observation, particularly on its sides facing the Aventine and Caelian hills. The public assemblies of the community were initially relocated to another location, making their original site unknown. It can be inferred that the open space surrounding the -mundus-, later known as the -area Apollinis-, served as the primary gathering place for the burgesses and the senate. The stage constructed over the -mundus- itself became the earliest seat of justice for the Roman community.

The suburbs developed one after another, each surrounded by its own distinct but weaker boundary wall and connected to the original ring wall of the Palatine, like fen districts where the outer dikes are linked to the main dike. The additions, manifestly the result of a gradual growth, clearly reveal to a certain extent the earliest history of Palatine Rome, particularly when compared with the Servian arrangement of districts, which was later formed on the basis of this earliest division. The urban settlement, however, began at Rome as well as elsewhere, but not within its boundaries. Rather, it was situated under the protection of the stronghold. The oldest settlements with which we are acquainted, and those that later formed the first and second regions in the Servian division of the city, lay in a circular arrangement around the Palatine Hill. Pietro in Vincoli, located below the valley, seems to be more significant and possibly older than the settlements established within the Palatine district under the Servian arrangement.

Each of the three tribes - Ramnes, Tities, and Luceres - must have been distributed throughout both the oldest city's two regions: the Subura and Palatine, as well as its suburban region. This distribution may be connected to the fact that, in addition to the Subura and Palatine, each of the subsequent regions added to the city also featured three pairs of Argean chapels. As the seasons change and the forest prepares for new life, its fallen leaves remain hidden from human sight. Similarly, the ancient city of the Seven Mountains has cleared a path for a new era, much like the passing of time has allowed the legacy of Rome to flourish. The Hill-Roman city on the Quirinal was not the only one that existed in ancient times within the circle of the Servian walls. Opposite to it, in its immediate vicinity, lay another city also on the Quirinal. Thus, too, we discover an explanation for the reason why the Subura was built beyond the city wall in the valley between the Esquiline and Quirinal. It was at this point that the two territories came into contact, and the Palatine Romans, after taking possession of the low ground, were compelled to construct a stronghold for protection against those on the Quirinal. As the Palatine city took the name of "the Seven Mounts," its citizens called themselves the "mount-men" (-montani-). The term "mount," applied to the other heights belonging to the city, was particularly associated with the Palatine. In contrast, the Quirinal height, although not lower but somewhat higher than the former, and the adjacent Viminal were never given any other name in strict use of the language than "hill" (collis).

We have already discussed how the Subura and Palatine annually contested for the horse's head; several Mounts also, and even several curies (there was no common hearth for the city, but the various hearths of the curies coexisted side by side) likely felt themselves more separated than united. Rome as a whole was probably an aggregate of urban settlements rather than a single city. It appears from various indications that the houses of the old and powerful families were built in a style similar to fortresses and were designed to provide defense—a prudent measure, one might assume, was not warranted. The synoikismos did not necessarily involve an actual settlement together at one spot. Instead, each person continued to reside on their own land. However, from that point forward, there was only one council hall and courthouse for the entire community. ii Herodotus i 170) 3

Although the older opinion that Latin is to be viewed as a mixed language composed of Greek and non-Greek elements has been largely abandoned, judicious inquirers still recognize its validity. g Research The form commonly occurring in authors, such as Lupercus Quinctilius and Quinctilianus, is actually a misnomer. The college belonged to the older Quinctii, rather than the relatively more recent Quinctii. Although the name "Hill of Quirinus" was afterwards ordinarily used to designate the height where the Hill-Romans had their abode, we need not consider the name "Quirites" as originally reserved solely for the burgesses on the Quirinal. For the earliest indications, all point to the name -Collini-, whereas it is undisputedly certain that the name Quirites denoted simply the full burgess from the start, as well as subsequently, without any connection to the distinction between montani and collini. chap v infra)

The later designation of the Quirinal rests on the circumstance that, while the Mars quirinus, the spear-bearing god of Death, was originally worshipped as well on the Palatine as on the Quirinal. This is evident from the oldest inscriptions found at what was afterwards called the Temple of Quirinus, which simply designate this divinity as Mars. Over time, for the sake of distinction, the god of the Mount-Romans was referred to as Mars, while the god of the Hill Romans was specifically known as Quirinus. The gods Mars Quirinus, Sun, Salus, Flora, and Semo Sancus, or Deus Fidius, were undoubtedly Sabine, but also Latin deities, formed clearly during the time when both Latins and Sabines coexisted. If a name such as Semo Sancus, which also appears in connection with the Tiber Island, is particularly associated with the sacred sites on the Quirinal Hill, which later lost its significance. The Porta Sanqualis derives its name from there. Every unbiased inquirer will recognize in such a circumstance only a proof of the high antiquity of that worship, not a proof of its derivation from a neighboring land.

Children born to daughters were excluded. If born in wedlock, they belonged to their father's family. If born out of wedlock, they had no place in a family. The death of an individual was not evil, for it was a matter of necessity. However, the extinction of a household or clan was injurious to the community itself. In the earliest times, this led the community to open up to childless individuals the means of avoiding such a fatality by adopting the children of others as their own. Woman was not a servant within the house; she was its mistress.

The family was governed by the single, all-powerful will of the "father of the household," or pater familias. The only instance of support accorded by the community in Rome is the enactment that aid should be given to the father who had three children presented to him at birth. Their ideas regarding exposure are indicated by the prohibition of it, except for deformed births and at least the first daughter. The prohibition of exposure soon took on a new form, shifting from a legal penalty to a religious curse. The father's authority over his household was absolute, making him the ultimate decision-maker.

As long as the father was alive, his children could not own property on their own and thus could not transfer it without his permission. A father might transfer ownership of both his son and his slave to a third party. If the buyer is foreign, the son becomes their slave. If the buyer is Roman, the son cannot become a Roman's slave but stands as a substitute for him in terms of slavery (-in mancipii causa-). The power of the master of the household over his wife and child closely approximated to his proprietary power over slaves and cattle. However, this did not create a uniform separation between the family members and their property. Instead, there existed a broad line of distinction, both in fact and law, that separated them from the family assets. Their rights were dormant in respect of exercise because the unity of the household demanded it be governed by a single representative. However, when the master of the household died, his sons immediately took control as its masters and now obtained the same rights they had previously enjoyed over women and children and property on their own account.

Both denoted the male line; however, the family only accepted individuals who, tracing their lineage back through generations, could demonstrate the successive steps of their descent from a common ancestor. In contrast, the clan (gens) also included those who claimed descent from a common ancestor but were unable to fully establish the intermediate links, thereby determining the degree of their relationship with one another. This term denoted not the guests, that is, the members of other similar circles who were temporarily sojourning in another household than their own. It also did not apply to slaves, who were viewed as property under the law and not as members of the household. Instead, it applied to individuals who lived within a protected freedom, even if they were not free citizens of any commonwealth. The position of protected dependents was naturally altered so far that freedmen and clients of every patron received toleration in the community at large; indeed, they were immediately dependent on the protection of the family to which they belonged. However, the very nature of the case implied that clients of members of the community could not be wholly excluded from its worship and festivals, although they were not capable of enjoying the proper rights or fulfilling the proper duties of burgesses.

The king as the head of the family was the central figure, and the clans that rested on a familial basis were the fundamental building blocks of the state. The structure of the state was thus modeled after the family, both in its overall form and in its detailed organization. The agreements he concluded on behalf of the community with foreigners were binding on the entire population. However, in other cases, only members of the community were bound by agreements with non-members. A formal cooperation in the election of a king pertained to the burgesses only after his nomination. The de jure basis of the kingly office was based on the permanent college of the Fathers (-patres-), which, by means of the interim holder of power, installed the new king for life.

The unity of the Roman people was represented by two figures: the Roman Diovis in the field of religion, and the prince in the field of law. As a result, their costumes were identical, reflecting the supreme god's attire. Even in the city, where everyone else walked on foot, the Roman king rode a chariot, accompanied by an ivory sceptre with an eagle, a vermilion-painted face, and a chaplet made from oaken leaves in gold. It would be a great mistake, however, to regard the Roman constitution on that account as a theocracy. The Romans had distinct concepts of god and king, which remained separate among the Italians, unlike in Egypt and the East where these ideas blended together. The Roman constitutional tradition, deserving of credit in such matters, historically accounts for the other divisions of the burgesses. This makes the division into curies alone originate with the city's origin. In harmony with this view, the curial constitution presents itself not only in Rome but also as an essential part of the Latin municipal system.

We may not therefore conceive the number of the clans and households as legally fixed; if the curiahad to furnish a hundred men on foot and ten horsemen, it is not affirmed by tradition or credible that one horseman was taken from each clan and one foot soldier from each household. This organization could not have been introduced primarily with a view to voting, as it would have resulted in an uneven number of subdivisions. In connection with this, it's worth recalling that in Italy, there is no recorded evidence of an indigenous population less capable of culture than those who arrived as Latin immigrants. They had no conquered race to deal with, so there was no need for a social hierarchy like the one that led to the Indian system of caste. This was in contrast to the nobility of Thessaly and Sparta, as well as possibly the rest of Hellas, and it may have also influenced the Germanic distinction of ranks.

The burgesses were both the "body of warriors" (-populus-, related to -populari-, to lay waste) and the spear-armed body of warriors (-pilumnus poplus-). In ancient litanies, it was upon this body that the blessing of Mars is invoked. The king's designation for them, Quirites,(9) is also interpreted as signifying "warriors". "We have already stated that the army of aggression, known as the 'gathering' (legio), was formed." In cases of need, a contribution (-tributum-) was imposed, which was viewed as a forced loan and repaid when times improved. It is unclear whether this burden fell on the burgesses generally or only on the metoeci; the latter assumption appears more likely. The property of the state was not identical to the private property of the king. Judging from the statements about the extensive landed possessions of the last Roman royal house, the Tarquins, this property must have been considerable.

The rights of the burgesses extended beyond mere provision of services and participation in governance. They played a significant role in the public government. The burgesses, however, were always summoned not to speak, but to listen. As the legal relation between ruler and ruled was itself sanctioned after the manner of a contract, every sovereign act of the community was accomplished through a question (-rogatio-) addressed by the king to the burgesses, to which the majority of the curies responded with an affirmative answer.

Among the Romans, law was not primarily conceived as a command addressed by the sovereign to the entire community. Rather, it was a contract concluded between the state's constituent powers through mutual addresses and responses. Such a legislative contract was de jure prerequisite in all cases involving a deviation from the ordinary consistency of the legal system. The freeman could not lose or surrender his inalienable right to freedom. Therefore, one who was subject to no housemaster could not voluntarily submit to another authority, unless the community granted him permission to do so. It was necessary to consult them when an aggressive war was contemplated, but not during defensive wars, where the other state had broken the treaty or at the conclusion of peace. However, it seems that the question was directed not to the usual assembly of burgesses, but rather to the army.

The assembly of the community, though initially restricted and limited in its early stages, had been an integral part of the Roman commonwealth since ancient times. It held a position of authority that surpassed mere coordination with the monarch. To express the matter briefly, the position of the senate as something other and more than a mere state-council - than an assemblage of trusted men whose advice the king found fitting to obtain - was rooted in its origins as an assembly, like that described by Homer, of princes and rulers sitting for deliberation around the king. The selection of senators certainly lay with the king after there were no longer heads of clans. However, during the earlier epoch, as long as the people retained a vivid sense of individual clan identity, it was likely that when a senator died, the king would call another experienced and aged man from the same clanship to fill his place.

The Interregnum

The prerogatives of this council of elders were based on the view that the rule over the community composed of clans rightfully belonged to the collective clan-elders. However, in accordance with the monarchical principle of the Romans, which already found a stern expression in the household, the rule could only be exercised for the time being by one of these elders, namely the king. Every member of the senate was similarly endowed with prerogative, like the king of the community. As such, their insignia, though inferior to those of the monarch, shared a similar character. The senator's insignia was comparable to that of the king, with one notable exception: the senator's red shoe was lower and less ornate than that of the king. The interrex was entitled and bound not merely to perform all official acts pertaining to the king, but even to nominate a king for life—with the single exception that this latter right was not vested in the first who held the office. This was presumably because the first was regarded as defectively appointed, as he was not nominated by his predecessor.

If therefore this senate seemed to the Greeks to be an assembly of kings, this was only what could be expected; for it had been so from its inception. The senate, in its collective capacity, was never consulted in the leadership of the army or in the administration of justice. As a result, there was no rightful authority for military command and no jurisdiction vested in the Roman Senate. The Senate as a state council was likely connected to the ancient practice of submitting proposals to the senate by the king, who would then present them to the burgesses, allowing each member to offer their opinion on the matter. It was from this usage of giving counsel that the subsequent extensive powers of the senate were developed. This development was more significant than the prerogatives previously described. In its origin, however, the senate's powers were insignificant and essentially limited to answering questions posed by others.

The advice, accordingly, was not a command; the king could choose to comply with it, while the senate had no other means for giving practical effect to its views except the right of cassation, which was not universally applicable. The king is considered the holder and embodiment of the state's full authority. As a result, acts of grace, such as pardons or exemptions, are granted exclusively by him. In contrast, the management of the state falls under the responsibility of its elected representatives and the executive officials accountable to them. In the Roman constitution, the community of the people exercised many functions similar to those of the king in England. The right of pardon, which in England is a royal prerogative, was in Rome a communal privilege. All government authority was vested in the president of the state.

These were the principles on which the community of Rome governed itself: a free people who understood the duty of obedience and clearly rejected all mystical priestly delusions. They were absolutely equal in the eyes of the law and considered themselves one with each other, bearing the distinct mark of their own nationality. At the same time, they wisely and magnanimously opened their gates to intercourse with other lands. This was not merely the case under the old religious marriage; the civil marriage also, although not in itself giving proprietary power over his wife to the husband, opened up the way for him to acquire this power. The legal concepts of "formal delivery" and "prescription" were applied without ceremony to such a marriage. He had not yet acquired it, and this was particularly true during the time that passed before the completion of the prescription. The wife was not considered his wife (-not uxor-) but rather his wife's representative (-pro uxore-).

The following epitaph, although belonging to a later period, is not unworthy of being included here. It is self-evident that Roman citizenship was a condition for both royal office and consulate. This fact makes it unnecessary to explicitly reject the fictions surrounding the burgess of Cures. The term "part" (tribus) refers to something that has once been or may become a whole, thereby lacking inherent existence in the present.

In referring to the locality, "Quirites" was never used; instead, "Rome" and "Romans" (-urbs Roma-, -populus-, -civis-, -ager Romanus-) were employed. This is because the term "Quirites" had as little local meaning as "civis" or "miles." For the same reason these designations could not be combined; they did not say "civis quiris," because both denoted, though from different points of view, the same legal concept. Among the eight ritual institutions of Numa, Dionysius II. After naming the Curiones and Flamines, it is specified that the third were the leaders of the horsemen.

The statements regarding the tribuni celerum are limited. The one mentioned is not supported by late and unreliable sources, but rather contradicts the term's meaning, which can only signify "divisional leaders of horsemen." Moreover, the master of the horse in the republican period, who was appointed only on extraordinary occasions and later no longer nominated, cannot have been identical with the magistracy required for the annual festival of March 19th. Laying aside the account of Pomponius, which has arisen solely out of the anecdote of Brutus dressed in increasing ignorance as history, we reach a simple result. The tribuni celeriter correspond in number and character to the tribuni militum, and they were leaders of division among the horsemen, making them distinct from the magister equitum. 11  However, it also carries the implication of a contract where the proposer sets the terms, and the other party either accepts or declines them. This connotation is distinct from the more general concept of a contract, highlighting the specific nature of such agreements. g

The Roman community had two guilds: the Salii and the Luperci. It had two forms of Mars, which also resulted in two priests for that divinity. The Palatine priest would later be known as the priest of Mars, while the Colline priest was referred to as the priest of Quirinus. It is likely, although it can no longer be proved, that all the old Latin priesthoods of Rome—the Augurs, Pontifices, Vestals, and Fetials—originated in the same way from a combination of the priestly colleges of the Palatine and Quirinal communities. In the case of synoikismos, the annexed community was recognized after union as at least a tribe within the new burgess-body. This recognition gave it some political existence. However, this course was not followed in the case of the Hill-Romans or in any later processes of annexation.

The Roman community was to be divided as formerly into three tribes, each containing ten curiae. The Hill Romans, whether they had been previously distributed into tribes of their own, must have been inserted into the existing tribes and curiae. This insertion was probably so arranged that each tribe and wardship received its assigned proportion of the new burgesses. However, the new burgesses in these divisions were not amalgamated completely with the old. Instead, the tribes henceforth presented two ranks: the Tities, Ramnes, and Luceres, which were subdivided into first and second ranks.

This arrangement is especially apparent in the army: after the union, each half-tribe of the tripartite community furnished a hundred horsemen. The Roman burgess cavalry was thus raised to six "hundreds." If we have rightly assumed that the contrast between the Palatine old and the Quirinal new burgesses was identical with the contrast between the first and second Tities, Ramnes, and Luceres, it was thus the gentes of the Quirinal city that formed the "second" or lesser class. "The distinction, however, was more of an honorary precedent than a legal one." It thus appears that synoikismos, as seen in the Palatine community's incorporation of the Quirinal, represented an intermediate stage between the earliest synoikismos, where the Tities, Ramnes, and Luceres merged, and subsequent instances.

The annexed community was no longer allowed to form a separate tribe in the new whole, but it was permitted to furnish at least a distinct portion of each tribe. Its ritual institutions were allowed to subsist and were even elevated into institutions of the united community, a course that was not pursued in any subsequent instance. In the first place, the community might itself possess half-free clients as well as slaves. After the conquest of a town and the breaking up of its commonwealth, it often appears advisable not to sell the mass of burgesses formally as slaves. Instead, allowing them de facto possession of freedom, they can enter into relations with the clans or the king in their capacity as freedmen of the community. The clients and their posterity did not possess either the rights of burgesses or those of guests by virtue of their position. To constitute a burgess, a formal bestowal of the privilege was requisite on the part of the community. In contrast, the relation of guest presumed the holding of burgess-rights in a community that had a treaty with Rome.

They obtained a legally protected possession of freedom. The body of metoeci outgrew these fetters; they began to acquire and alienate in their own name, claiming and obtaining legal redress from the Roman burgess-tribunals without the need for their patron's formal intervention. In matters of marriage and inheritance, equality of rights with the burgesses was far sooner conceded to foreigners than to those who were strictly non-free and belonged to no community. However, the latter could not well be prohibited from contracting marriages in their own circle and forming legal relations arising out of marriage – including marital power, agnatio, gentilitas of heritage, and tutelage – after the model of corresponding relations among the burgesses.

It allowed on one hand that every man capable of making a disposition had the entirely unlimited disposal of his property during his lifetime. On the other hand, so far as we know, it granted to everyone who was entitled to have dealings with Roman burgesses, including foreigners and clients, the unlimited right to acquire movable property, as well as within certain limits, immovable estate in Rome. Class of Metoeci: Subsisting by the Side of the Community. At first, the burgesses were in reality the protectors, while the non-burgesses were the protected. However, as in Rome and other communities that freely admit settlement but do not grant full citizenship rights, this relation soon became a matter of increasing difficulty to harmonize between de jure and actual state of affairs. The burdens of war fell exclusively on the old burgesses, who were constantly thinning the ranks of their patrician descendants. The metoeci, however, shared in the results of victory without having to pay for it with their blood.

The fact that it has continued for a prolonged period is scarcely comprehensible by the bestowal of Roman burgess-rights on several distinguished foreign clans. These grants, which appeared to be rare from the start, became increasingly scarce as the franchise's value grew. A likely cause of greater influence was the introduction of civil marriage. In this arrangement, children born to noble families who lived together as married couples would acquire full citizenship rights, regardless of whether they were born through a traditional confarreatio marriage or not. In earlier times, clients relied solely on their patrons for legal protection. As the state solidified and the significance of clanships and households decreased, individuals had to seek justice and compensation for injuries more frequently, even without their patron's involvement, directly from the king.

Many non-burgesses, especially those from the dissolved Latin communities, likely never had a place as clients of the royal or powerful clans. Instead, they followed the king's rules almost identical to how the burgesses did. The Servian Constitution

The first step towards the amalgamation of the two portions of the people was not taken until the revolutionary period, when their antagonism seemed to foreshadow a union. As the land was allocated at that time, nearly half of the farms were full hides. The classes possessing three-fourths, half, and a quarter of a hide collectively accounted for only about one-eighth of the freeholders. Those who held an eighth of a hide comprised roughly another eighth.

The number of divisions was tripled. The only difference in this case was that the six existing divisions with old names (-Tities-, -Ramnes-, -Luceres- and -primiand- and -secundi-) were left to the patricians, while the twelve new divisions were formed chiefly from non-burgesses. The male population capable of bearing arms was divided into two levies: the first, comprising those who had reached their 18th year and were still serving, and the second, consisting of older men who had completed their military service. The latter group, known as "seniors," were responsible for defending the homeland's fortifications. This phalanx was the army destined for the field; a like force of troops was reckoned for those who remained behind to defend the city.

To those who were to be added, the cavalry consisted of 1,800 horses. Often, when the army took the field, however, only one-third of the total number was attached to it. As the population grew, the number of centuries remained unchanged, but the various divisions were reinforced with additional personnel, while still maintaining a focus on their core principles. Although the organization of centuries was introduced merely to enlarge the military resources of the burgesses by the inclusion of the metoeci, there is no greater error than to exhibit the Servian organization as the introduction of a timocracy in Rome. However, the new obligation imposed upon the inhabitants to bear arms had a material influence on their political position. Although the institution of the centuries was not intended to curtail the political privileges exclusively possessed by the burgesses, it was inevitable that those rights, which the burgesses had hitherto exercised as a levy, should pass over to the new centuries of burgesses. By the side of these new landowning full-burgesses stood the domiciled foreigners from the allied Latium, participating in public burdens, tribute, and task-works. The burgesses not domiciled, beyond the pale of the tribes, and without the right to serve in war or vote, were only seen as owing tribute.

There was no text provided. For the same reason, when the levy was enlarged after the admission of the Hill-Romans, the equites were doubled, while in the infantry force, instead of a single legion, two legions were called out. Athens, like Rome, opened its gates to the metoeci at an early period, and subsequently invited them to share in the burdens of the state.

No Italian Homer has preserved for us a picture of these earliest frays and plundering excursions, in which the character of nations is moulded and expressed like the mind of the man in the sports and enterprises of the boy. Historical tradition, however, does not enable us to form an accurate judgment about the outward development of power and the comparative resources of the several Latin cantons. The Romans' earliest military triumphs were preserved, albeit in a legendary context. The city of Alba, an ancient sacred metropolis in Latium, was conquered and subsequently destroyed by Roman forces. We know nothing at all beyond the naked fact that Alba was subjugated and destroyed by Rome. It is not improbable that, at the same period when Rome was establishing herself on the Anio and Alban hills, Praeneste, which later became mistress of eight neighboring townships, including Tibur and others of the Latin communities, were similarly occupied in expanding their territory and laying the foundations for their subsequent considerable power.

Even the temples often remained at the same location as the old site is demonstrated by the examples of Alba and Caenina, two towns that likely continued to hold some significance related to religion even after their destruction. The conquered, however, were not unfrequently compelled either as a whole or in part to settle in their new capital. This is proved more satisfactorily than all the stories from the legendary period of Latium could prove it by the maxim of Roman state-law: only he who had extended the boundaries of the territory was entitled to advance the wall of the city, known as the pomerium. In the time of the Roman Empire, recognized Alban clans persisted among the burgesses. The Julii, Servilii, Quinctilii, Cloelii, Geganii, Curiatii, and Metili families were among them. Their Alban heritage was preserved through family shrines, including the sanctuary of the Julii at Bovillae, which regained prominence under the Empire.

Rome adhered to the principle of unity with greater consistency, earnestness, and success than any other Italian canton. Just as Athens' prominent position in Hellas was the result of her early centralization, Rome's greatness was solely due to the same system, which was applied more energetically in her case. The Hegemony of Rome over Latium, particularly Alba, was a significant factor. While Rome's conquests in Latium were mainly extensions of her territory and people, presenting similar features, the conquest of Alba held a special significance. The Roman dominance over Latium was widely acknowledged, but some communities like Labici and Gabii initially resisted. Although intermarriage between different communities was not uncommon in the league, it is worth noting that this practice often occurred despite the lack of a formal rule against it.

Each Latin could primarily exercise political rights only where he was enrolled as a burgess. However, it was implied that any Latin had an equality of private rights, allowing him to take up his abode in any place within the Latin bounds. In essence, there existed a general right of settlement co-extensive with the confederacy, alongside the special burgess-rights of individual communities. Once a plebeian was acknowledged as a burgess in Rome, this right became equivalent to full freedom of settlement. It is easy to understand how this should have turned to the advantage of the capital, which alone in Latium offered the means for urban intercourse, acquisition, and enjoyments. The number of metoeci in Rome should have increased remarkably after the Latin land came to live in perpetual peace with Rome. In fact, Alba exercised her right to preside over the federal council, while Rome allowed its Latin deputies to hold their consultations independently under the guidance of a president chosen from among themselves. Alba contented herself with an honorary presidency at the federal festival, where sacrifices were offered for both Rome and Latium, and also erected a second federal sanctuary in Rome—the temple of Diana on the Aventine—so that from then on, sacrifices were offered on Roman soil for Rome and Latium, as well as on Latin soil for Latium and Rome.

The supreme command lay with the Roman generals. Year by year, the Latin contingent appeared before the gates of Rome, and there they saluted the elected commander by acclamation as their general. The Romans, commissioned by the Latin federal council to take the auspices, thereby assured themselves that the gods were content with the choice made. Extension of the Roman Territory after the Fall of Alba: The Hernici, Rutulli, and Volscii

After the fall of Alba, Rome expanded its territory considerably. As the mistress of this new territory, Rome became the leading power in the Latin confederacy. Its direct and indirect dominion continued to grow, but the exact steps by which it did so cannot be traced. As regards the Sabines and Aequi, Rome appears in a more advantageous position. The connection that later became so intimate with the more distant Hernici must have had at least its beginning under the monarchy. The united Latins and Hernici then enclosed their eastern neighbors on two sides and held them in check.

The extent of the territory reduced under Roman rule at the end of the monarchy cannot be precisely determined. The regal period laid not only the political foundations of Rome but also the foundations of her external power. It cannot be doubted that this was a crucial aspect of Rome's rise to prominence. The city's position as a distinct entity, separate from the league of Latin states, is already evident at the beginning of the republic. This distinction enables us to infer that an energetic development of external power must have occurred in Rome during the time of the kings. Enlargement of the City of Rome--Servian Wall

While the Latin stock was tending towards union under the leadership of Rome, it was also extending its territory on both the east and south. Meanwhile, Rome itself, thanks to fortune's favour and the energy of its citizens, had been transformed from a bustling commercial and rural town into the powerful capital of a thriving country.

The amalgamation of the adjoining communities on the Quirinal and Palatine must have been already accomplished when the Servian reform took place. Following this reform, the community united and consolidated its military strength, allowing the burgesses to no longer rest content with entrenching individual hills. Instead, they built structures upon each hill, effectively filling them in. Additionally, they maintained control over the island in the Tiber and the height on the opposite bank, ensuring a commanding view of the river's course. The Palatine had been the stronghold, but now this hill was left open to be built upon by the growing city. On the other hand, the Tarpeian Hill, standing free on every side and easily defensible due to its moderate extent, was constructed as a new stronghold (-arx-). This stronghold featured the spring of the stronghold, a carefully enclosed "well-house" (-tullianum-), a treasury (-aerarium-), a prison, and the most ancient place of assembly for the burgesses (-area Capitolina-). In later times, regular announcements of the changes in the moon's phases were still made at this location. The Capitol was in reality as well as in name the Acropolis of Rome, an independent fortress that could be defended even after the city had fallen. Its gate likely lay near what would later become the Forum. The Aventine appears to have been fortified in a similar style, although less strongly, and was preserved free from permanent occupation.

The work, however, was not complete as long as the ground, protected by laborious exertions from outward foes, remained reclaimed from the dominion of water. This permanently occupied the valley between the Palatine and the Capitol, making it possible to have a ferry there. As a result, the valleys between the Capitol and the Velia, and those between the Palatine and the Aventine, were converted into marshes. The subterranean drains still existing today are composed of magnificent square blocks, which excited the astonishment of posterity as a remarkable work of Roman engineering. It is more likely that they belong to a later period than the design of the Servian wall and the Capitoline stronghold, although the use of travertine suggests a connection to the republican era. At one side of the comitium, which adjoined the stronghold, and upon the stronghold wall that rose above the comitium in the fashion of a balcony, the members of the senate and the guests of the city were assigned the place of honour on occasion of festivals and assemblies of the people. The senate-house was erected at the place of assembly itself, later bearing the name of the Curia Hostilia.

But upon the whole, we must be content to learn from this tradition: what is indeed evident of itself—that this second creation of Rome stood in intimate connection with the commencement of her hegemony over Latium and with the remodelling of her burgess-army. And that, while it originated in one and the same great conception, its execution was not the work either of a single man or of a single generation. The Servian military constitution is essentially of a Hellenic type. It will be subsequently demonstrated that the games of the Circus were organized on a Hellenic model. It cannot be proved, and it is highly unlikely that, with respect to these towns, a real historical curse was placed on the land where they were founded, as happened at Veii, Carthage, and Fregellae.

The circumstance that Rome claims to be in a religious and political point of view the heir-at-law of Alba may be regarded as decisive of the matter. For such a claim could not be based solely on the migration of individual clans to Rome, but rather it was based on the conquest of the town. 4 The Latin allies cannot be referred to here, as their legal position was defined by federal treaties, and the law of the Twelve Tables pertained only to the law of Rome. Both names, although later applied as local names (-capitolium was applied to the summit of the stronghold-hill next to the river, -arxto that next to the Quirinal), were originally appellatives, corresponding exactly to the Greek --akra-- and --koruphei--, just like every Latin town had its own -capitolium-, as did Rome.

The gate had vanished beneath the massive structures erected in later ages on the Clivus. Rom As "Seven-hill-city," probably because the festival of the Septimontium, which was celebrated with great zeal even under the Empire, began to be regarded as a festival for the city generally; but there was hardly any definite agreement reached as to which of the heights embraced by the Servian ring-wall belonged to the seven hills. The seven mountains that are well-known to us, namely Cholongshan, Taibai Mountain, Wutai Mountain, Hua Shan, Song Shan, and Hebei Mountains. The city was built on the site of an ancient settlement, its origins dating back to the 6th century BC. The area was strategically located at the intersection of two major trade routes, making it a hub for commerce and cultural exchange. Over time, the city grew in size and importance, becoming a center of politics, religion, and learning. As the city expanded, it became a melting pot of different cultures and civilizations, with various empires rising and falling over the centuries. The city's architecture, art, and literature reflect this rich cultural heritage, showcasing a unique blend of styles and influences. Despite its tumultuous history, the city has continued to thrive, adapting to changing times and circumstances while retaining its distinct character and identity. 206 seq The Janiculum is passed over.

The Etruscans likely conquered the southern regions from the Umbrians at a time later than their occupation of the northern areas near the Ciminian Forest. An Umbrian population persisted in these districts even after the Tuscan takeover. The southern portion of Etruria became Latinized more rapidly than the north due to the Roman conquest. The Umbrians were driven back from obstinate struggles into the narrow mountainous country between the two arms of the Apennines, a region they subsequently held. This is clearly indicated by their geographical position, much like the current situation of the Grisons and Basques inhabitants, who share a similar fate. In consequence of the pressure exerted upon them from the north, the Umbrians advanced towards the south, generally staying on high ground. They found the plains already occupied by Latin settlements, but frequently made incursions into the territory of their kindred race, intermingling with them more easily. This would have blurred any distinction in language and habits that might have existed between the two groups.

The Samnites, a chief branch of the Umbrian stock, threw themselves eastward from Sabina into the mountains of the Abruzzi, and the adjacent hill country to the south of them. As on the west coast, they occupied the mountainous districts, whose thinly scattered population gave way to the immigrants or submitted to their rule. In the plain along the Apulian coast, the ancient native population, the Iapygians, maintained their ground, despite being involved in constant feuds, particularly on the northern frontier around Luceria and Arpi. While the Umbrians were defeated in the unequal struggle, the western offshoots of the same stock merged with the Latin or Hellenic population. In contrast, the Sabellian tribes thrived in their isolated mountain land, remaining separate from the Etruscans, Latins, and Greeks.

There was little or no development of urban life amongst them. Their geographical position almost wholly precluded them from engaging in commercial intercourse. The mountain tops and strongholds were sufficient for their defence needs, while husbandmen dwelled in open hamlets or wherever they found a well-spring and the forest or pasture that suited their needs. The conquests, however, that the Samnites made along the coasts of the Tyrrhenian and Ionic seas, belong to a later age. During the Roman royal period, they seemed to be merely gaining control over the settlements where we later found them. The aspirate consonants were treated similarly. The Italians rejected them except for the aspirated -b and -f, while the Greeks reversed this rejection and kept all other sounds. The Etruscans allowed the softest and most pleasing sound, -phi, to drop entirely, except in words borrowed from other languages. However, they used the other three sounds extensively, even when they didn't belong. For example, Thetis became -Thethis-, Telephus -Thelaphe-, and Odysseus -Utuzeor- or -Uthuze-.

Proper names are formed substantially in accordance with the general Italian system. As these analogies may have originated only in the subsequent political and religious relations between the Etruscans and Latins, and in the accommodations and borrowings that arose from these relations, they do not invalidate the conclusion we reach through other observed phenomena. The Tuscan language differed at least as widely from all Graeco-Italian dialects as did the languages of the Celts or Slavonians. Despite their significant differences from the Graeco-Italian language family, no one has yet established a connection between the Etruscan language and any other known racial group. The Etruscans, as Dionysius stated long ago, are unlike any other nation in terms of their language and customs.

The emperor Tiberius had inquired about Hecuba's mother. We must therefore seek out the earliest known home of the Etruscans, located either to the west or north of Italy. It is a very ancient concept: it appears even in Herodotus; and it reappears in later writers with numerous changes and additions. Despite several intelligent inquirers, such as Dionysius, who emphatically declared their disbelief in it, there is no apparent similarity between the Lydians and Etruscans in religion, laws, manners, or language.

The term Tyrrhenians refers to the Lydian Torrhebi, as seen in the earliest sources, such as the Homeric hymns. It also denotes the Italian Etruscans, although they never came into lasting contact with the Pelasgians or Torrhebians, or were connected by common descent. To the south of the Po River, Etruscans and Umbrians were mingled. The former dominated, while the latter was the older race that had founded the old commercial towns of Atria and Spina. The Tuscans are believed to have been the founders of Felsina (Bologna) and Ravenna. All the regions, however, to the north of the Apennines passed too rapidly from one nation to another, preventing the formation of any continuous national development.

Although Ligurians or Umbrians may have once settled there, their traces have been largely erased by the Etruscan occupation and civilization. We have already noticed the fact that the territory between the Ciminian range and the Tiber, which includes the towns of Sutrium, Nepete, Falerii, Veii, and Caere, was not taken possession of by the Etruscans until a period considerably later than the more northern districts. This may have occurred as late as the second century of Rome, and it is likely that the original Italian population maintained its ground in this region, particularly in Falerii, albeit in a state of dependence. As for the migration of Etruscan bodies to Rome, we come across an isolated statement in Tuscan annals. It reports on a Tuscan band led by Caelius Vivenna of Volsinii. After his death, Mastarna, his loyal companion, took charge and guided the group to Rome.

This may be trustworthy, although the derivation of the name "Caelian Mount" from Caelius is evidently a philological invention. The addition that Mastarna became king in Rome under the name of Servius Tullius is likely an improbable conjecture of archaeologists who drew parallels with legendary figures. Tanaquil, a female name intertwined with legend, is found in Etruria. The history of the Tarquins was centered in Latium, rather than Etruria. Etruria, as far as we can observe, had no significant impact on either the language or customs of Rome during the entire regal period. The influence of Etruria did not interrupt the regular development of the Roman state or the Latin league at all. The cause of Etruria's comparatively passive attitude towards its neighbouring land of Latium is likely due to two factors. Firstly, the struggles of the Etruscans with the Celts on the Po River, which probably occurred after the expulsion of the kings from Rome. Secondly, the Etruscan people's tendency towards seafaring and their desire for supremacy on the sea and its coastline, as evidenced by their settlements in Campania.

Kings or Lucumones ruled, possessing similar insignia and likely a comparable amount of power as the Roman kings. Each of these leagues consisted of twelve communities, which recognized a metropolis, especially for purposes of worship, and a federal head or rather a high priest. They appeared to be substantially equal in respect of rights; some at least were so powerful that neither could establish a hegemony nor could the central authority attain consolidation. It was, however, quite unusual for the Etruscans to act in concert, just as it was for the Latin confederacy.

The Italian peninsula remained shrouded in the dim twilight of morning, but the eastern basin of the Mediterranean had already burst forth into the full light of a diverse and thriving civilization. The circumstances of its geographical position, however, prevented this influence from being brought to bear upon the peninsula. The Phoenicians in Italy were not a civilization. The Phoenicians were a seafaring people who established colonies along the Mediterranean coast, including those in Italy. They had a significant impact on the region's culture, trade, and architecture. All the oldest relations, indeed, of the Italians with the civilization of the east point decidedly towards Greece. The rise of the Phoenician factory at Caere can be explained without resorting to the pre-Hellenic period by the subsequent well-known relations between the commercial state of Caere and Carthage.

There is no historical evidence to support the claim that the Phoenicians had a direct influence on the Italians. Greeks in Italy—The Home of Greek Immigrants. To all appearances, the Hellenic mariners were the first among the inhabitants of the eastern basin of the Mediterranean to navigate the coastlines of Italy. The origin of the Greek seafarers is a matter of debate, but their arrival in the region can be pinpointed with relative accuracy.

After the discovery of America, European nations competed to send expeditions and establish settlements. The new settlers, once among barbarians, realized that they shared a common character and interests with other civilized Europeans more so than they had in their homeland. The original Ionian group, encompassed under the name of the Chalcidian towns, included in Italy Cumae with other Greek settlements at Vesuvius and Rhegium, and in Sicily Zankle (later Messana), Naxos, Catana, Leontini, and Himera. The Dorian group encompassed Syracuse, Gela, Agrigentum, and most of the Sicilian colonies. In Italy, only Taras (Tarentum) and its satellite city, Heraclea, belonged to it.

The Greeks, not lonians, were a trading and sea-faring people. It was only at a comparatively late period that the Dorians settled in the coastal regions, descending from their inland mountains. They maintained a distance from maritime commerce throughout their history. We can still distinctly recognize a certain order of sequence. It is assumed that the Greeks first became neighbors of the Italians in Cumae. This assumption makes it easy to explain why the name "Opicans," which referred to a group settled around Cumae, was used by the Greeks to describe the Italians collectively for centuries.

Naxos in Sicily was said to be the oldest of all the Greek towns founded through strict colonization. The Achaean and Dorian colonizations followed, but only after a later period. It appears that fixing the dates of this series of events is highly unlikely to achieve even approximate accuracy. The Achaean Greeks, however, were not merely confined to a narrow belt along the coast, but ruled from sea to sea in the "land of wine" and "of oxen" (Oinotria, Italia) or the "great Hellas." The native agricultural population was compelled to farm their lands and pay tribute to them as clients or serfs.

For the Italian Achaeans, skilled in great independence, struck large, thin silver coins from two similar dies, partly cut in relief and partly sunk. These coins featured inscriptions and displayed the advanced organization of a civilized state through the mode of impression, which carefully protected them from counterfeiting common in that age—the practice of plating inferior metal with thin silver foil. None of the brilliant names in Greek art or literature shed glory on the Italian Achaean people, but Sicily could claim many of them. Even in Italy, the city of Rhegium produced an Ibycus, while Tarentum gave birth to Archytas. The Achaeans' settlement in Italy had little impact on its civilization compared to other Greek colonies.

Within their own domains, they enslaved the native population and stifled the seeds of their national development.
