As Italians, they refused to adopt Greek culture through complete assimilation, instead pursuing a separate path. This event, however, belongs to a later time. They by no means despised agriculture and the acquisition of territory. It was not the wont of the Hellenes, at least when they had reached their full vigor, to rest content after the manner of the Phoenicians with a fortified factory in the midst of a barbarian land.

At this time, when Tarentum was still competing with Sybaris for first place among the Greek cities of Lower Italy, its extensive commercial connections must already have been forming. However, the Tarentines seem never to have steadily and successfully directed their efforts towards a substantial extension of their territory in the same manner as the Achaean cities. Greek cities near Vesuvius. The easternmost Greek settlements in Italy flourished rapidly, while those farther north, in the vicinity of Vesuvius, achieved a more modest prosperity. The Cumaeans crossed from the fertile island of Aenaria (Ischia) to the mainland and built a second home on a hill near the sea. From this location, they founded the seaport of Dicaearchia (later known as Puteoli), as well as the "new city" Neapolis.

Relations of the Adriatic Regions to the Greeks

On one side of the straits of Rhegium, the southern coast of the mainland and its western coast as far as Vesuvius were Greek territory. On the other side, the larger eastern half of the island of Sicily was also Greek territory. In contrast, the west coast of Italy northward of Vesuvius and the entire east coast were in a fundamentally different position. Two considerable mercantile towns, Epidamnus or Dyrrachium (now Durazzo), and Apollonia (near Avlona), were founded on the coast nearest to Greece during Rome's regal period; no old Greek colony can be identified further north, with the exception of a small settlement at Black Corcyra (Curzola). ) Nature herself appeared to direct the Hellenes towards that region. From the earliest times, there existed a regular trade route from Corinth and even more so from the settlement at Corcyra (Corfu), which was founded not long after Rome's establishment around 44 BC. This trade had its emporia on the Italian coast in the towns of Spina and Atria, situated at the mouth of the Po River.

The Hellenism of Apulia was not established until a later period. Other traces of these very ancient voyages can be found in the Greek name of the island Aethalia (Ilva, Elba), which appears to have been one of the places earliest occupied by Greeks. This is also suggested by the seaport Telamon in Etruria. Additionally, two townships on the Caerite coast, Pyrgi, near Sybaris, and another nearby settlement, are also worth mentioning. Caere's location was particularly noteworthy. The Caerites, according to Strabo, were highly regarded by the Hellenes for their courage and moral integrity. Despite their considerable power, they refrained from engaging in robbery. It is not piracy that is thus referred to. For in this case, the merchant of Caere must have indulged in like manner as every other.

The Etruscans, however, did not limit themselves to mere robbery and pillage. The peaceful intercourse between them and Greek towns is attested by the gold and silver coins that, from at least the year 200, were struck by the Etruscan cities, particularly by Populonia, in a Greek model and to a Greek standard. Inhabiting the entire country, they controlled the great Italian free ports on both western and eastern seas. The Po's mouth and Venice were under their command on the eastern sea, while a land route from ancient times connected Pisa on the Tyrrhenian Sea to Spina on the Adriatic. In southern Italy, they dominated the fertile plains of Capua and Nola.

Rivalry between the Phoenicians and Hellenes existed in Italy, where the Etruscans and Latins opposed the Hellenes to a lesser extent. This antagonism had an impact on the commercial and navigational activities of the Mediterranean, which were dominated by the rivalry between the Phoenicians and Hellenes. During the Roman Empire's reign, two powerful nations - Greece and Asia Minor - clashed over dominance along the Mediterranean's shores, including Greece itself and the islands of Crete and Cyprus. Their struggle extended to the African, Spanish, and Celtic coastlines. The earlier Phoenician settlements, such as those in Sicily, were mercantile centers. Carthage dominated extensive territories with numerous subjects and powerful fortresses.

When the Phocaeans settled around 217 BC in Alalia (Aleria), Corsica, opposite Caere, a combined fleet of Etruscans and Carthaginians appeared to expel them. The fleet numbered about 120 ships. In the ensuing naval battle, one of the earliest recorded in history, the Phocaean fleet, which was half as strong, claimed victory. However, the Carthaginians and Etruscans achieved their objective in the attack. The Phocaeans abandoned Corsica and preferred to settle at Hyde (Velia) on Lucania's less exposed coast. The Romans did not join the Hellenes in these hostilities. Instead, they maintained friendly relations with the Phocaeans in Velia and Massilia from ancient times. The Ardeates even collaborated with the Zacynthians to establish a colony in Spain, which later became Saguntum. The Latins did not align themselves with the Hellenes; rather, they maintained a neutral stance. This is evident in the close ties between Caere and Rome, as well as the remnants of ancient interactions between the Latins and the Carthaginians.

The Hellenes no longer gained ground. After establishing Agrigentum, they failed to acquire significant territorial gains along the Adriatic coast or on the western sea. They were also barred from accessing the Spanish waters and the Atlantic Ocean. In the Eoiai of Hesiod, it appears as the older collective name for the nation. However, it is evident that this term has been deliberately set aside and subordinated to that of Hellenes. The Italians had been familiar with the Greeks long before this period. As a result, the name "Hellas" remained with them as a collective term for the Greek nation, even after they began using alternative forms of self-identification.

The three old Oriental forms of the "id:i" (-"id:S"), "id:l" (-"id:/"), and "id:r" (-"id:P") remained in exclusive use among the Achaean colonies, while the other Greeks in Italy and Sicily used either exclusively or chiefly the more recent forms. 3 Among Greek writers, this Tyrrhene legend of Odysseus makes its earliest appearance in Hesiod's Theogony, one of its more recent sections. It is then found in authors of the period shortly before Alexander, including Ephorus, from whom the so-called Scymnus drew his materials, and the writer known as Scylax. The first of these sources dates back to an age when Italy was still regarded by the Greeks as a group of islands. It is certainly very old, and therefore its origin can be confidently placed in the regal period of Rome. 5

The thoughts and imaginings of the individual, however strongly they may reflect the characteristics of the national mind, are not considered part of history. The power over sons and grandsons who were under the paternal authority subsisted concurrently with the royal jurisdiction. However, this power was not a jurisdiction in the proper sense of the term, but rather a consequence of the father's inherent right to property in his children. It was applicable in cases of public treason or communion with a public enemy (proditio) and violent rebellion against the magistracy (perduellio).

But the public peace was also shattered by the foul murderer, the sodomite, the violator of a maiden's or matron's chastity, the incendiary, the false witness. Those who, with evil spells, sought to destroy the harvest, or who cut down grain in the fields under the protection of gods and people, were dealt with as if they had committed high treason. The king opened and conducted the proceedings, then pronounced his verdict after consulting with the senators whom he had summoned to advise him. He was at liberty, however, once the process had been initiated, to entrust the further handling and adjudication of the matter to deputies, typically drawn from the senate.

The later extraordinary deputies, including the two men for adjudicating on rebellion (duoviri perduellionis) and the later standing deputies known as "trackers of murder" (quaestores parricidii), whose primary duty was to search out and arrest murderers, did not belong to the regal period. Instead, they may have originated from or been influenced by certain institutions within that period. Prisoners were held during the investigation, but the accused could be released on bail. When both parties had appeared, the plaintiff verbally stated his claim, and the defendant similarly refused to comply. The king could then choose to investigate the matter personally or appoint a deputy to handle it on his behalf.

Theft: What Circumstances Make it Justifiable? During this epoch, theft was considered justifiable under certain circumstances. However, it is unclear what specific conditions would render theft expiable and what compensation the victim could reasonably expect from the perpetrator. The injured party sought greater compensation from the thief who was caught in the act than from one discovered later. This was because the sense of frustration that needed to be alleviated was stronger in the first case than in the second. It was not the right of the stronger that was regarded as the foundation of a title to it. On the contrary, all property was considered as conferred by the community upon the individual burgess for his exclusive possession and use. It was therefore only the burgess, and those equal to him in this respect, who were capable of holding property.

It was not within the father's power to arbitrarily deny his children their right of inheritance, as he could neither dissolve the paternal power nor execute a will without the consent of the entire community. This consent was often refused, especially in situations like this one. The regulation that placed an individual who had alienated their hereditary property and deprived their children of it under guardianship akin to a lunatic was likely as old as the time when arable land was first divided, leading to private property gaining greater significance for the commonwealth. The only protection of the creditor was the debtor's word of honour, which was held in high esteem after the custom of merchants. In addition, if an oath had been sworn, the fear of the gods also played a role, as they were believed to avenge perjury.

Copper became the standard unit of value after it surpassed sheep and cattle. The process involved weighing out a specified amount of copper in a balanced scale, with an impartial person adjusting the weight to ensure accuracy. These conditions having been complied with, the seller was required to prove ownership. In addition, both the seller and the purchaser were obligated to fulfill any stipulations that had been specially agreed upon. If either party failed to do so, they were liable for reparation, just as if the other had deprived them of the article in question. The party who lost the wager and failed to provide due satisfaction to their opponent within thirty days was subject to liability for proceedings in execution. The party whose obligation to pay was established from the start would typically become liable if they had borrowed money without witnesses to attest its repayment. In such cases, the plaintiff could seize them wherever they were found and bring them before the judge to settle the acknowledged debt. Guardianship protection was provided for the estate of persons incapable of bearing arms, including minors and lunatics, as well as women. The nearest heirs were summoned to assume guardianship in these cases.

After a man's death, his property falls to the nearest heirs. In the division, those who are equal in degree of relationship, including women, share equally. The widow, along with her children, is also entitled to their proportional share. If no dispensation was obtained, the lack of it could be partially alleviated by the complete freedom to manage one's own property during their lifetime. There was no special legal form, however, for binding a master. The best proof that there was at first no such thing as manumission was that these methods were used to achieve the same goal that the law offered: a testament, an action, or a census.

The Roman citizen had a right to claim whatever they took from him, just like the shellfish that belonged to no one and was found on the beach. However, if the land lay beyond the Roman territory, while the citizen could take practical possession, they were not considered its rightful owner in a legal sense. This was because the individual citizen was not entitled to expand the boundaries of their community. Character of the Roman Law

We have derived our outline of these institutions mainly from the earliest recorded Roman common law, prepared about half a century after the abolition of the monarchy. The existence of these institutions during the regal period is doubtful for certain details, but their overall presence cannot be denied. The burgess and the client stood on an equal footing in their dealings. State treaties conceded a comprehensive equality of rights to both parties, including women, who were placed on a level with men in terms of legal capacity, although they were restricted in action. The boy had not yet grown up when he was granted complete control over his estate, and everyone who could dispose of property was as sovereign in their own sphere as the state was in public affairs.

The input "Servius ad Aen" appears to be a fragment of a Latin phrase, possibly from Virgil's Aeneid, but it lacks sufficient context to make meaningful corrections. i The king's privilege to ride in a chariot within the city is most simply explained by supposing that he alone was entitled to do so. The mancipatio must be far more ancient; for it primarily applies only to objects that are acquired by grasping with the hand, and thus in its earliest form would have belonged to the epoch when property consisted essentially of slaves and cattle. The process of acquiring certain objects through mancipation is considered a Servian innovation. The mancipation itself, along with the use of the balance and copper, predates these developments. The universal form of purchase, known as mancipatio, originally occurred in all cases even after the Servian reform. However, a later misunderstanding led to the rule that certain articles had to be transferred through mancipatio alone, excluding other items from this transfer. 5

The tutelary spirit, which presided over an individual's act, lasted no longer than that act itself. The tutelary spirit of the individual man lived and died with the man, and eternal duration belonged only to divinities of this sort, for similar acts and similarly constituted men were constantly coming into existence anew. To Jupiter, all the days of full moon are sacred, except for the wine-festivals and other specified days. The 21st of May is dedicated to his counterpart, the "bad Jovis". To Mars belongs the new-year on the first day of March, and generally the great warrior-festival in this month which derived its very name from the god. This festival, introduced by horse-racing (equirria), had during March its principal solemnities on the days of the shield-forging (Mamuralia, March 14), the armed dance at the Comitium (quinquatrus, March 19), and the consecration of trumpets (tubilustrium, March 23). When a war was to be waged, it began with this festival. After the close of the campaign in autumn, a further festival of Mars took place, marking the consecration of arms (Armilustrium), which occurred on October 19.

Among the other festivals, those related to the culture of corn and wine hold the first place. Pastoral feasts play a subordinate part. So after the completion of field work and the fortunate gathering of their produce, double festivals were celebrated in honor of Ceres and Saturn. The first festival took place immediately after the completion of cutting, on August 21 (Consualia), and August 25 (Opiconsiva). The second festival occurred in the middle of winter, when the blessings of the granary were especially manifest, on December 15 (Consualia) and December 19 (Opalia). Between these two latter days, the old arrangers of the festivals inserted a celebration of seed-sowing, known as Saturnalia, which took place on December 17. In like manner, the festival of Must or of Healing (Meditrinalia, October 11), so called because a healing virtue was attributed to the fresh must, was dedicated to Jovis as the wine-god after the completion of the vintage. The original reference of the third wine-feast (Vinalia, August 19) is unclear.

The representation of handicraft and art in this cycle is attributed to Vulcanus, the god of fire and smith's work. He is also associated with two festivals: Volcanalia on August 23, which honors him, and tubilustrium on May 23, a ceremony dedicated to the consecration of trumpets. Additionally, Carmentis, possibly revered as the goddess of spells and song, is also linked to the festival of Carmentalia on January 11 and 15, although her original role may have been that of a protector of births. This table is complete for the immovable public festivals; and although by the side of these standing festal days there certainly occurred from the earliest times changeable and occasional festivals, this document, in what it says as well as in what it omits, opens up an insight into a primitive age otherwise almost wholly lost to us. While abstraction, which lies at the foundation of every religion, sought to rise to broader and more expansive concepts, it endeavored to penetrate deeper into the essence of things. In contrast, the forms of the Roman faith remained at a remarkably low level of comprehension and understanding. In the case of Greece, every influential motive quickly expanded into a variety of forms, gathering around it a circle of legends and ideas. In contrast, the fundamental thought in Rome remained stationary, retaining its original rigidity.

It had its "bad god" (-Ve-diovis-), its apparitions and ghosts (-lemures-), and afterwards its deities of foul air, fever, diseases, and perhaps even theft (-laverna-). However, it was unable to evoke the mysterious awe that has always driven human hearts or fully grasp the incomprehensible and malignant elements in nature and humanity. In the ranking of the gods, however, these spirits of the house and field occupied the lowest place rather than the highest. It was not the broadest and most general concept, but the simplest and most individual one, that the pious heart found most nourishment. This indifference to ideal elements in the Roman religion was accompanied by a practical and utilitarian tendency. The table of festivals, which had previously been discussed, illustrates this aspect clearly. Increase in substance and prosperity through husbandry, herding, seafaring, and commerce was what the Romans sought from their gods. This aligns with the idea that the god of good faith (deus fidius), the goddess of chance and fortune (fors fortuna), and the god of trade (mercurius) emerged from everyday interactions, although they were not present in ancient festivals.

We have already explained how the amalgamation of the Hill-community with that of the Palatine gave rise to the duplication of the Roman Mars, and thereby to the introduction of a second priest of Mars—the flamen Quirinalis—and a second guild of dancers—the salii colligii. A college was probably one of the twelve "field-brethren" (fratres arvales) who invoked the "creative goddess" (dea dia) in May to bless the growth of the seed; although it is doubtful whether they enjoyed that peculiar consideration at this period, which they later received during the empire.

The Lupercalia festival was celebrated for the protection of flocks and herds in honor of Faunus by the Quinctian clan and the Fabii, who were associated with them after the admission of the Hill-Romans. In February, it became a genuine shepherd's carnival, where the Luperci would jump about naked while wearing a girdle made from goatskin. They would also whip those they met with thongs. The necessary and stated observances due to the gods of the community were entrusted once for all by the state to fixed colleges or regular ministers. The expense of sacrifices, which was presumably not inconsiderable, was covered partly by the assignment of certain lands to particular temples, partly by the collection of fines. It cannot be doubted that the public worship of the other Latin community, and presumably also of the Sabellian community, was essentially similar in character. Those who had business with a god resorted directly to the god, rather than through a priest.

It was natural that the worshipper of the god should regularly consult such men of skill and listen to their advice. This led to the formation of corporations or colleges of men specially skilled in religious lore, a thoroughly national Italian institution, which had a far more important influence on political development than individual priests and priesthoods. The science of things divine and human." The rudiments of spiritual and temporal jurisprudence, as well as historical recording, originated from this college. They held the same position internationally as the Pontiffs did in relation to religion and law. As a result, they were authorized to interpret the law, even if they were not empowered to enforce it.

But even with great renown, these colleges were never forgotten—least of all for those that held the highest position—that their duty was not to command, but to offer skilled counsel, not to seek the answers of the gods directly, but to interpret and explain them to those who sought knowledge. Character of the Cultus:

The Latin worship was grounded essentially on man's enjoyment of earthly pleasures, and only to a subordinate degree on his fear of nature's wild forces. It consisted pre-eminently in expressions of joy, including lays and songs, games, dances, and above all, banquets. In Italy, as in many other agricultural communities where vegetable-based diets were common, the slaughter of cattle became a dual celebration: both a domestic occasion and a ritual act of devotion. A pig, being the traditional centerpiece of such feasts, was considered the most suitable offering to the gods due to its prevalence at these gatherings.

The god of the Italians was, as we have already stated, primarily an instrument for helping him achieve substantial earthly goals; this shift was influenced by his inclination towards the tangible and the real, and is equally evident in the veneration of saints among the modern Italian population. The original Roman worship did not feature images of the gods or separate houses dedicated to them. Although the god was worshipped in Latium at an early period, likely in imitation of the Greeks, with an image, a small chapel (aedicula) was built for him. However, such figurative representation was considered contrary to the laws of Numa and was generally viewed as an impure and foreign innovation. The Latin religion, reduced as it was to ordinary perception, was intelligible to everyone and accessible to all; thus the Roman community preserved the equality of its citizens. In contrast, Hellas, where religion reached the highest level of thought, suffered from the blessings and curses of an aristocracy of intellect from the earliest times.

The Latin religion, like every other, has its origin in the effort of faith to comprehend the infinite. It is only to a superficial view that this depth is misunderstood, for its transparent spirit world appears shallow at first glance. It is time therefore to desist from that childish view of history which believes that it can commend the Greeks only at the expense of the Romans, or the Romans only at the expense of the Greeks. We should allow both civilizations to stand on their own merits, just as we do with different types of trees - some strong like oak and others delicate like a rose. In doing so, we must recognize that the unique strengths of each civilization are inextricably linked to their respective weaknesses. The deepest and ultimate reason for the diversity between the two nations lay beyond doubt in the fact that Latium did not, and Greece did, come into contact with the East during the season of growth.

The language of the Roman gods was largely confined to Yea and Nay or at most, to making their will known by casting lots, a method that originated in Italy. From ancient times, although not until they received an impulse from the East, the more talkative Greek gods imparted actual utterances of prophecy. The gods associated with sailors, Castor and Polydeuces, were also known as Pollux in Roman form. Additionally, Hermes (Roman Mercurius) was the god of traffic, while Asklapios or Aesculapius was the god of healing. These deities became familiar to the Romans early on, although their public worship did not begin until a later time. These individual cases of derivation from abroad were of secondary moment, while the remains of the natural symbolism of primeval times, such as the legend of the oxen of Cacus, had virtually disappeared.

In contrast to the tranquil world of departed "good spirits" that the Latins had imagined, the Etruscan religion presented a hellish realm where the poor souls were tormented by mallets and serpents. The conductor of the dead, a savage figure resembling an old man with wings and a large hammer, conveyed them to this underworld. This figure later appeared in the gladiatorial games at Rome as a model for the costume of the person who removed corpses from the arena. The association of torture with this condition of the shades had become so entrenched that a redemption was even provided for it. This redemption came after making certain mysterious offerings, and as a result, the poor soul was transferred to the society of the gods above. How deeply repugnant this juggling was to the Roman character is shown by the fact that even when people came later to employ Etruscan lore in Rome, no attempt was made to naturalize it; during our present period, the Romans were probably still content with their own traditions and the Greek oracles.

Respecting the intellectual value that may once have belonged to this Etruscan cosmogony and philosophy, it's challenging to form a judgment. They seem to have been marked from the start by a sense of fatalism and a lackluster approach to numbers. To make him god of the sun and of the year is less justifiable than it might seem. The fact that the month bearing his name was originally the eleventh, not the first, suggests a different origin for its naming. It appears that this month got its name from the circumstance that, after the rest of winter had passed, the cycle of fieldwork began anew at this time.

It was, however, a matter of course that the opening of the year should also fall within the sphere of Ianus, particularly after Ianuarius was appointed at its head. 5 The former stood on the same footing as the constitution of ten curies and the Flamines, Salii, and Luperci, ancient heirlooms of the Latin stock. In contrast, the Duoviri sacris faciundis and other colleges, such as the thirty curies and the Servian tribes and centuries, originated in Rome and remained confined there. In the case of the second college, the pontifices, Rome's influence likely led to the introduction of their name into the general Latin scheme, rather than an earlier designation. Alternatively, a hypothesis supported by philology suggests that pons originally signified "way" in general, and therefore pontifex meant "constructor of ways." "The statements about the original number of augurs are varied."

There were no purely pastoral tribes in Italy during historical times. However, various races throughout history combined pastoral husbandry to some degree, depending on the local environment, with the cultivation of the land being an integral part of their practices. That which is gained by war may be wrested from the grasp of war again, but it is not so with the conquests made by the plough. While the Romans lost many battles, they scarcely ever ceded Roman soil when making peace. This result was due to the tenacity with which farmers clung to their fields and homesteads. System of Joint Cultivation
We have already indicated that in the earliest times, the arable land was cultivated in common. Probably, this was done by several clans, each of which tilled its own land and then distributed the produce among the various households belonging to it.

The culture of the olive was introduced to Italy by the Greeks at a later time. The olive was first planted on the shores of the western Mediterranean at the end of the second century BC. This view is supported by the fact that the olive branch and the olive were considered less important than the juice of the grape in Roman rituals. Every ninth day, and therefore on an average four times a month, the farmer went to town to buy and sell and conduct his other business. But rest from labor took place only on festival days, specifically in the holiday month after winter sowing (-feriae sementivae-): during these set times, both the plough and its owner rested, as did the farmer's slave and ox.

The law aimed to alleviate the burden of landed property with debt by implementing two measures. Firstly, for debts secured over land, it ordained the provisional transfer of ownership of the pledged object from the debtor to the creditor. Secondly, in cases of simple loans, the rigour of the proceedings led to swift bankruptcy. However, this latter outcome was achieved only partially. The fact that such larger properties existed to a considerable extent cannot be doubted from the early development of the equites. This can be easily explained partly by the distribution of clan lands, which in itself could not but lead to the emergence of a class of larger landowners due to the necessary inequality in the numbers of persons belonging to the several clans and participating in the distribution. Additionally, the abundant influx of mercantile capital to Rome contributed to this phenomenon. Farming on a large scale in the proper sense, implying a significant establishment of slaves, did not exist during this period.

The recipient retained his occupancy as long as the grantor was pleased. However, this meant the recipient had no legal recourse to protect himself from eviction if the grantor chose to terminate his tenancy at any time. The introduction of such an assignment was not dependent on the abolition of the system of common tillage. In fact, after this abolition, individuals could grant dependents a joint use of their lands, just as clans had done previously. This state of affairs was indeed connected to the Roman clientship, which was not personal but rather involved the client and his clan entrusting themselves for protection and fealty to the patron and his clan. His house was in the countryside. In the city, he had only a lodging for the purpose of attending to his business there, and perhaps to enjoy the fresher air that prevailed during the hot season.

Above all, however, these arrangements provided a moral basis for the relationship between the upper class and the common people, and thus significantly reduced its dangers. In all cases where an immigrant nation has not immediately reduced its native population to slavery, slaves seem to have existed initially in very small numbers, and as a result, free laborers appear to have played a significantly different role in the state than they later did. In ancient Greece, day labourers (theoi) occupied the place of slaves in various instances during the earlier period. In some communities, such as those of the Locrians, there was no slavery throughout historical times.

He had land and cattle, a wife and child, like any tenant. After being freed through manumission, he gained the chance to work towards his freedom. It provided subsistence, albeit scantier in proportion, for as many families as the intermediate and smaller properties. The landlords, occupying a relatively elevated position, supplied the community with its natural leaders and rulers. In turn, the agricultural and unpropertied tenants furnished the genuine material for Roman colonization policy. This was crucial, as the state could only provide land to those who had none; however, it could not impart the spirit and energy needed to work the land to someone who knew nothing of agriculture. The right of pasturage on public domains may have had some relation to the possession of land, but no connection in law could ever have existed between particular hides of land and a definite proportional use of the common pasture. Property could be acquired even by metoikoi, but the right to use the common pasture was only granted exceptionally to them by royal favour.

At this time, however, public land seemed to hold only a subordinate place in the national economy. The original common pasture was likely limited in extent, and the conquered territory was mostly allocated to individual clans or distributed among them at a later date. While agriculture was the chief occupation in Rome, handicrafts accompanied it. This was to be expected given the early development of urban life in a city like Rome, which was an important center for the Latin people. The oldest lays of Rome celebrated not only the mighty war-god Mars, but also the skilled armourer Mamurius, who understood the art of forging for his fellow-citizens shields similar to the divine model shield that had fallen from heaven; Volcanus the god of fire and the forge already appears in the primitive list of Roman festivals. Thus in the earliest Rome, as everywhere, the arts of forging and wielding the ploughshare and the sword were closely intertwined. There was no arrogance towards handicrafts, a trait they would later exhibit.

After the Servian organization, however, imposed the duty of serving in the army exclusively on freeholders, the industrial classes were excluded not by any law, but practically as a result of their general lack of a freehold qualification. They were denied the privilege of bearing arms, except for special subdivisions chosen from carpenters, coppersmiths, and certain classes of musicians who were attached to military organizations within the army. This may have been the origin of the subsequent habit of undervaluing manual arts and the position of political inferiority assigned to them. The life of the Roman people was largely unrecorded in terms of their occupations. This establishment of copper as a general equivalent recognized throughout the whole peninsula, as well as the simplest numeral signs of Italian invention, is noteworthy. The mention of the Italian duodecimal system below(17) and its connection to this earliest international intercourse of the Italian peoples while they still controlled the peninsula can be seen as evidence of their interactions with other nations.

We cannot determine how far the development of handicrafts advanced before the separation of the stocks. It is uncertain whether the Italian fullers, dyers, tanners, and potters received their impulse from Greece or Phoenicia, or if they had an independent development. However, it is certain that the goldsmith trade in Rome, which existed since ancient times, could only have arisen after transmarine commerce began and ornaments of gold found some sale among the peninsula's inhabitants. Similar in significance to the derivation of words related to articles used in eating and drinking are those relating to oil, jugs (amphoreus, ampulla), feasting (komazo, comissari), a dainty dish (opsonion, opsonium), dough (maza, massa), and various names of cakes (glukons, lucuns; plakons, placenta; turons, turunda). Conversely, Latin names for dishes (patina, patanei) and lard (arvina, arbinei) have been adopted into Sicilian Greek. We deduce a similar inference from the numerous nautical terms borrowed from the Greek, although it is remarkable that the chief technical expressions in navigation—the sail, mast, and yard—are pure Latin forms. Additionally, we observe the recurrence in Latin of Greek designations for letters (e.g., epistolei, epistula), tokens (tessera, derived from tessara), balances (stateir, statera), and earnest-money terms (arrabon, arrabo, arra). Conversely, we note the adoption of Italian law-terms in Sicilian Greek and the exchange of coin proportions and names for weights and measures, which will be discussed further.

The worship of the god Mercury was also influenced by Greek conceptions. His annual festival appears to have been fixed on the Ides of May, as the Hellenic poets celebrated him as the son of the beautiful goddess Maia. Italy derived its luxury items from the East in ancient times, similar to imperial Rome. From this state of affairs regarding commodities in demand and the equivalents to be offered in return, we have already explained why Italian traffic took on a form so different from that which it displayed in Etruria.

The oldest tombs constructed in Greek style, but with an extravagance characteristic of the Greeks, can be found at Caere. In contrast, the Latin land exhibits only slight ornaments for the dead of foreign origin, except for Praeneste, which appears to have occupied a unique position and was closely connected with Falerii and southern Etruria. The tombs in this region lack luxury items typical of earlier times. Interestingly, among the Sabellians, a simple turf is often sufficient as a covering for the dead. The most ancient coins, dating back to a time not much later than those of Magna Graecia, belong to Etruria and specifically to Populonia. During the entire regal period, Latium was limited to using copper by weight, and had not even introduced foreign coins. The instances where such coins are found are extremely rare. g  The contrast between them in this respect is shown by the fact that luxury articles manufactured according to Greek models in Etruria found a market in Latium, particularly at Praeneste, and even in Greece itself. In contrast, Latium hardly ever exported anything of this kind.

An ancient connection with Attica is indicated by the Attic clay vases, which are abundant in more recent Etruscan tombs. It is possible that they had been introduced at this time for purposes other than tomb decoration. In contrast, Tyrrhenian bronze candlesticks and gold cups were early favorites in Attica. The coins indicate a definite intercourse. The silver pieces of Populonia were minted after the pattern of a very old silver piece stamped on one side with the Gorgoneion, while the other side presented an incuse square. This design has been found at Athens and along the ancient amber route in the Posen district. It is likely that this coin was struck under Solon's order in Athens. The Etruscans had also established dealings with the Carthaginians. It is likely that these connections continued even after the formation of the Etrusco-Carthaginian maritime alliance, which would have strengthened their ties with the Carthaginians.

We must not attribute too much importance to this Phoenician trade. In particular, it is essential not to overlook the fact that the alphabet, along with the other influences that shaped and developed native culture, was introduced to Etruria by the Greeks, rather than the Phoenicians. The Greek alphabet was introduced to the Etruscans by the Chalcidico-Doric colonies in Sicily or Campania. The variation between this and the Latin alphabet derived from the same quarter is not insignificant. Despite both peoples drawing from the same source, they adopted it at different times and locations. The Siceliot movement was far less active than expected. This is evident from the fact that all Greek words introduced to Latium in earlier times exhibited Doric forms. For example, the names Aesculapius, Latona, Aperta, and machina are notable examples of this phenomenon.

Had dealings with the originally Ionian cities, such as Cumae and the Phocaean settlements, been on a similar scale to those they had with the Sicilian Dorians, Ionic forms would at least have appeared along with the others; although certainly Dorism early penetrated into these Ionic colonies themselves, and their dialect varied greatly. The transmarine traffic conducted on the trader's own account must therefore have fallen into the hands of the great landholder, as he alone possessed the vessels for it and had the necessary articles in his produce for export. In fact, the distinction between a landed and a moneyed aristocracy was not known to the Romans of earlier times. The great landholders were both speculators and capitalists. In cases where commerce is very energetic, such as this combination could not have been maintained; however, as the previous representation shows, while there was a comparatively vigorous traffic in Rome due to the concentrated trade of the Latin land, Rome was by no means essentially a commercial city like Caere or Tarentum. Instead, it was and remains the centre of an agricultural community.

Moderation must be exercised when forming such estimates, as we are dealing with questions of averages and a method of husbandry that is neither systematic nor based on large capital. In these cases, however, it was the intention not to create new farms with the allotments, but rather, as a rule, to add new parcels to the existing farms from the conquered lands. The Latin farmer's family may have survived on two jugera of land. The main support for this claim comes from the writings of Varro, who argued that a small plot of land could be sufficient for a family to live off.

The apparent increase in yield can be attributed to the fact that the Romans harvested and planted wheat that was already partially processed, whereas they left the spelt intact in its husk. For the same reason, barley is now sown twice as thickly as wheat, and yields twice as much grain by measure, but less after deducting the husks. Compared to wheat, yields are more than double when considering gross values. With equally good soil, the crop can be tripled. However, by specific weight, before shelling, the yield is not much above 50%, but after shelling (as "kernel"), it's less than half.

The oldest Roman foot has disappeared; that which we know, and which was in use at a very early period among the Romans, was borrowed from Greece. The names of vessels have come from Greek terms either by corruption (e.g., amphora, modius) or by translation (e.g., acetabulum). Conversely, zesteis is a corruption of sextarius.

The systems of notation between the two neighboring nations eventually became assimilated after adopting the Roman system in Etruria. The mode of reckoning used in Latium, as well as similar modes employed by other cultures, likely included counting days in two distinct ways. First, days were counted backward from the phase that was next expected to occur, rather than forward from the most recent occurrence. Second, days were grouped into lunar weeks, which varied in length between 7 and 8 days on average, with an average of 7 3/8 days per week. Additionally, lunar months were also used, but their duration varied between 29 and 30 days. The average duration of a synodic month was approximately 29 days, 12 hours, and 44 minutes. It wasn't until afterwards that they divided the day into four portions, one for each quarter, and it was much later when they began to use the division into hours. This explains why even closely related stocks differed in their method of determining the start of the day; the Romans placed it at midnight, while the Sabellians and Etruscans set it at noon.

Later, but still at an early period, undoubtedly previous to the operation of Greek influences, the duodecimal system (as we have already stated) was developed in Italy. It derived its origin from the observation that the solar period was equal to twelve lunar periods. Consequently, it was applied in the first instance to the reckoning of time. This view is supported by the fact that individual month names, which could only have originated after the month was viewed as part of a solar year, showed similarities among the various branches of the Italian stock. In contrast, there was no similarity between the Italian names and those of ancient Greece. It is not improbable that the problem of laying down a practical calendar corresponding to both the moon and the sun had already engaged the minds of men in Italy before the time of their contact with the Greeks. This problem, comparable to the quadrature of the circle, was only recognized as impossible and abandoned after many centuries.

There was a recurring series in regular succession. In the intervening year, a nameless "labour-month" (mercedonius) was added at the end of the year, namely. February 29th The underlying motive behind this unusual calendar reform appears to be rooted in the perceived benefits of odd numbers. This notion is largely based on the oldest form of the Greek year, but its deviations from that original structure reveal significant influences from Pythagoras' teachings, which were prevalent in Lower Italy at the time. The Roman calendar, designed to harmonize with both the sun and moon's cycles, failed to do so in reality. Unlike its Greek model, which closely followed the lunar cycle, the Roman calendar only approximated it. The Roman calendar could only follow solar seasons through frequent arbitrary excisions, suggesting that it did not accurately track them. It is unlikely that the calendar was handled with greater skill than its original arrangement.

It would appear that the Greek trieteris was likely introduced early on from Lower Italy, at least into Latium, and possibly among other Italian regions. It then underwent further subordinate changes in the calendars of various cities. For measuring periods over a year, regnal years of kings could be used. However, it's uncertain if this method of dating, commonly used in the East, was ever employed in Greece or Italy during earlier times. On the other hand, the intercalary period, recurring every four years, and the census and lustration of the community connected with it, appear to have suggested a reckoning by Olympiads in plan, similar to the Greek method. However, this method early lost its chronological significance due to the irregularity as to when the census was held at the right time.

The Italians did not surpass the Hellenes in developing their own unique art form. Although we may find evidence of attempts at such development in Italian numerals, and possibly also in the ancient Italian practice of drawing lots using wooden tablets, independent of Hellenic influence. The Romans were familiar with forms like "-id://", "-id:P", and "-id:SIGMA", but they eventually gave way to later forms such as "-id://", "-id:R", and "-id:S". This shift can be attributed to the Latins' use of the Greek alphabet for an extended period, possibly in writing their native language or Greek. It is incorrect to assume that drawing conclusions about the early practice of writing in Etruria based on the more recent Greek alphabet found in Rome is valid. The fact that the Greek alphabet used in Rome differs from the older Etruscan script suggests that writing was likely practiced earlier in Etruria than in Rome.

This reform evidently arose among the western Etruscans. It found no reception beyond the Apennine Mountains but was adopted by all the Sabellian tribes, particularly among the Umbrians. The alphabet experienced various fortunes in its further course, connected to the different stocks of the Etruscans along the Arno and around Capua, as well as the Umbrians and Samnites. In some cases, the mediae were entirely or partially lost, while elsewhere new vowels and consonants emerged. While the Etruscans appear to be the instruments in diffusing the alphabet throughout the north, east, and south of the peninsula, the Latin alphabet was confined to Latium. It maintained its form with few alterations; only the letters gamma and kappa gradually became coincident in sound. This led to one of the homophonous signs (kappa or zeta) disappearing from writing.

What he saw, however, was probably a copy restored after the fire, assisted by a Latin exemplar. It was unlikely that engraving on metal had been practiced at that time. They even scratched or painted on leaves, inner bark, or wooden tablets. Afterwards, they also did so on leather and linen. The former received their alphabet from the Etruscans rather than the Romans. This is likely due to the assumption that they already possessed the alphabet before migrating along the Apennine ridge. Consequently, both the Sabines and Samnites brought it with them from their homeland to their new settlements. The media disappeared in all Etruscan dialects, while the Umbrians lost "id:gamma", the Samnites lost "id:d", and the Romans lost both "id:gamma" and "id:d". The latter also posed a threat to merge with "id:r". The converse occurred in the case of the sibilants. The Etruscan retained three signs: "id:z", "id:s", and "id:sh". In contrast, the Umbrian rejected the last sign but developed two new sibilants. The Samnite and Faliscan languages, like the Greek, confined themselves to just two signs: "id:s" and "id:z".

It is evident that the more subtle distinctions of sound were deeply felt by those who introduced the alphabet, men of culture and masters of two languages. However, once the national script became wholly detached from its Hellenic mother-alphabet, the mediae and tenues gradually converged, leading to a disarray of sibilants and vowels – transpositions or rather destructions of sound. The first of these is entirely foreign to Greek. They substituted for the sign of "--id:i" the simple stroke "--id:I", which was far more usual. At no very late date, it became at least so general that the broken "--id:iota S" everywhere disappeared, although individual communities retained the "--id:s" in the form "--id:/\/\" alongside of the "I". --III We find this feature in Athens and Boeotia, but Corinth and its dependent communities achieved the same goal by adopting a semicircular shape for the --"id:gamma" instead of the traditional hook-shaped design. --IV

The older form of "id:r" and "id:P" did not disappear earlier than the older form of "id:l". This alteration should be placed later. --V All these technical improvements are of a similar nature and have a comparable historical value. They share the common trait that they emerged at distinct times and locations, and subsequently followed their unique paths of dissemination and evolved into distinctive forms. In dealing with different proposals for modifying the alphabet, various districts followed an eclectic approach, resulting in one proposal being adopted here and another there. This eclecticism is instructive in the history of the Greek alphabet, as it demonstrates how certain groups of Greek lands exchanged improvements in handicraft and art, while others did not reciprocate.

For the four modifications of the alphabet above described, which concern the Italian Greeks (the fifth being confined to Asia Minor), the first three were already carried out before the alphabet passed to the Etruscans and Latins. The differentiation between --"id:p" and --"id:r" had not yet taken place in Etruria, but it had begun at least when the Latins received it. As a result, the Etruscans do not know the form -"id:R" for -"id:r", whereas among the Faliscans and Latins, there is one exception: the Dressel vase (xiv). The attempt to show its presence in the syllabarium of the latter is at any rate mistaken. The syllabarium can only take notice of the Etruscan letters that were afterwards in common use, and these do not include the koppa. Moreover, the sign placed at the close cannot have any value other than that of the -f, which was the last letter in the Etruscan alphabet. This letter could not be omitted from a syllabarium showing the variations of the alphabet from its model. The code's origins are beyond doubt. However, it is clear that the scholars who wrote about it did not refer to the original text, but rather to a later official document written after the Gallic conflagration. This is evident from the story of the Tables, which underwent reproduction at that time.

Thus, "id:C" represents Gaius; "id:CN" represents Gnaeus; while "id:K" stands for Kaeso. While in this sense no person is without poetry and music, some nations have received a pre-eminent endowment of poetic gifts. The Italian nation, however, was not and is not one of these.

He is thoroughly at home in architecture, painting, and sculpture. During the epoch of ancient culture, he was the best disciple of the Hellenes. In modern times, he has become a master of all nations. It is remarkable, however, that in the most ancient religious usages, dancing was far more prominent than song. They too were never wanting at a sacrifice, at a marriage, or at a funeral. By the side of the primitive public priesthood of the "leapers," there was ranged an equal antiquity although of far inferior rank, the guild of the "pipers" (-collegium tibicinum). Their true character as strolling musicians is evinced by their ancient privilege: maintained even in spite of Roman police strictness, they were allowed to wander through the streets at their annual festival, wearing masks and full of sweet wine.

While dancing presents itself as an honourable function, music is one subordinate but still necessary. Public corporations were instituted for both. Poetry, however, appears more incidental and indifferent, whether it arose independently or served as a complement to the dancers' movements. The whispers and pipings of the favourable spirit (from faunus, meaning "favourable one") in the grove were reproduced for men by those who had the gift of listening to him, using rhythmically measured language (from canere, meaning "to sing"). In this way, alternative chants arose, such as those later known as Fescennine songs, along with elements of popular comedy. These were skillfully planted in an environment well-suited to their development, characterized by a keen sense of the outward and comic aspects, as well as a fondness for gesticulation and masquerade, which have long been hallmarks of Italian character.

The comedies, once more, if it is permissible to mention them, were performed during this period and continued to be improvised for a long time afterwards. The measure, accompaniment of music, and choral dancing. The fundamental elements of national music and choral dancing in Latium are lost to us, but we can infer that they were established during this period. One aspect that has survived is the Latin pipe, a short and slender instrument with only four holes. Its name suggests it was originally made from the light thighbone of an animal.

Masks
The masks used in later times for the standing characters of the Latin popular comedy, or Atellana, were cleverly and strikingly compared to the two servants, Pantalone and Dottore, in the Italian comedy of Pulcinello. These masks belonged to the earliest Latin popular art. The same may be affirmed of the knowledge of reading and writing, which is closely connected to the knowledge of Greek. The culture of the ancient world, however, was not based on the knowledge of foreign languages or on elementary technical accomplishments. It was an extraordinary Thanksgiving festival celebrated in honor of Capitoline Jupiter and the gods who dwelled alongside him. The festival was typically held in pursuit of a vow made by the general before battle, and as such was usually observed upon their return home in autumn.

A festive procession proceeded toward the Circus, situated between the Palatine and Aventine hills, and was equipped with an arena and seating for spectators. In front, all the boys of Rome, arranged according to their social class, rode on horseback and walked on foot; next came the champions and groups of dancers, each accompanied by their own music. The priests then appeared, carrying vessels of frankincense and other sacred objects. Finally, the biers were brought forth bearing images of the gods themselves. First, war-chariots ran, each carrying a charioteer and a combatant. Next came the combatants who had leaped off; then horsemen, each riding one horse and leading another by hand (-desultor-); finally, champions on foot, naked to their girdles, measured their strength in racing, wrestling, and boxing. The Roman festival of victory or city-festival was one such event. The other public festivals of Rome were likely similar in nature, though perhaps less extensive in terms of resources.

The Greek term "stadion" (Doric "spadion") was transferred to the Latin language at a very early period, retaining its meaning as "-spatium". There exists an express statement that the Romans derived their horse and chariot races from the people of Thurii. However, another account suggests they originated from Etruria. While they were intended to be competitions of burgesses, it is clear that they were so at first in Rome. However, over time, they evolved into contests between professional riders and professional boxers. The initial condition for participating in the Greek festal games was proof of free and Hellenic descent. Nevertheless, the Roman festivals soon fell into the hands of freedmen, foreigners, and even those who were not born free.

The circle of fellow competitors had become a crowd of spectators, and the wreath of the victorious champion, which had been justly called the badge of Hellas, was rarely mentioned in Latium. In ancient Greece, music, singing, and dancing continued unchanged, mirroring their origins in Latium. Respectable occupations that brought honor to the burgess and his community remained a priority. However, as time passed, the more affluent burgesses in Latium increasingly distanced themselves from these frivolous pursuits. This trend intensified as art became more publicly displayed and was influenced by foreign ideas. The use of the native pipe was sanctioned, but the lyre remained despised. The national amusement of masks was allowed, while foreign amusements were regarded with indifference and considered a disgrace.

While the fine arts in Greece became increasingly common among the Hellenes both individually and collectively, this led to the development of a universal culture. However, they gradually lost significance in Latium, where their thoughts and feelings were no longer a part of the people's consciousness; as they degenerated into insignificant handicrafts, the idea of a general national culture never occurred to anyone. It is remarkable that at the Etruscan national festival, where all twelve cities were represented by a federal priest, games similar to those held in Roman city-festivals were given. However, we can no longer answer the question this suggests: how far the Etruscans surpassed the Latins in achieving a national form of fine art that exceeded the individual communities' achievements. In Etruria, a foundation was likely laid even in early times, laying the groundwork for an insipid accumulation of learned material, particularly of theological and astrological nature. This led to the development of antiquarian dilettantism, which flourished amidst general decay. As a result, the Tuscans, along with the Jews, Chaldeans, and Egyptians, were recognized as primitive sources of divine wisdom.

What are called Cyclopean ring-walls frequently occur in Italy, especially in Etruria, Umbria, Latium, and Sabina. They decidedly belong to the most ancient buildings of Italy, although the majority of those now extant were probably built at a much later age. Some of them, however, date back to the seventh century of the city's history. The Latin and Sabellian cultures continued to adhere to the traditional practice of assigning a consecrated space rather than a dwelling to their gods or spirits. In contrast, the Etruscan civilization had begun to transform its dwellings into temples, mirroring the human dwelling-house, and also built a sepulchral chamber for the spirit. It is unlikely that these arts truly gained a place in Italy during the Roman monarchy; it was only in Etruria, where commerce and piracy early gave rise to a great concentration of wealth, that art or handicraft—if the term be preferred—obtained a foothold in the earliest times. There is no single aspect of Italian art that has not been influenced by ancient Greek art. The legend that traces the manufacture of painted clay figures, the most ancient form of art in Italy, to three Greek artists - Eucheir, Diopos, and Eugrammos - is largely warranted. However, it is uncertain whether this art originated directly from Corinth or was introduced to Tarquinii.

The Etruscan lapidaries adhered to the form of the scarab beetle, which was originally Egyptian. However, the scarab bee was also used as a model for carving in ancient Greece in early times. g A stone bearing an ancient Greek inscription has been discovered in Aegina. It is possible that the Etruscans acquired it from the Greeks. The three forms of art, which were practiced in Etruria at least in later times very extensively, but in Greece only to a limited extent, included tomb-painting, mirror-designing, and engraving on stone. These have been found on Grecian soil only in Athens and Aegina. If we are to form a judgment about the artistic endowments of Italy's different nations, we already perceive at this stage that while the Etruscans developed art earlier and produced more impressive workmanship, their works lack the appropriateness and utility of those from the Latins and Sabellians in terms of spirit and beauty.

The use of polygonal wall masonry varied across regions. In Latium and the surrounding countryside, it was a common feature. However, in Etruria, it was less prevalent, with even the walls of Caere being built using rectangular blocks rather than polygonal ones.  g It was a remedy for gout. While fasting, one would think of another person. Three times, nine would be uttered, touching the earth at the same time, and then spit: "I think of you, mend my feet." The god of sowing, Saturn, received his name from the same root. His feast, the Saturnalia, was certainly a sort of carnival, and it is possible that the farces were originally exhibited chiefly at this feast.

Vates probably denoted the "leader of the singing" in its initial usage. In later times, it approximated to the Greek concept of --propheiteis--; as a word belonging to religious ritual. Even when used to describe a poet, the term retained the notion of a divinely-inspired singer—the priest of the Muses. 16 Blocks of tufo, composed of hewn blocks, are typically rectangular in shape, averaging 60 centimeters (2 Roman feet) in height and width. The length varies from 70 centimeters to 3 meters. Without mortar, they are laid together in several rows, alternating between the longer and narrower sides, with the outermost side facing outward. It was, however, the grand distinction of the endeavours after reform and the revolutions in Rome, that there was no attempt either to impose limitations on the community as such or even to deprive it of corresponding organs of expression. On the contrary, the attack was wholly directed against the form in which the community was represented.

Not only in Rome, but also among other Latins, Sabellians, Etruscans, and Apulians, and generally throughout all Italian communities, as well as in those of Greece, we find that the rulers for life of an earlier epoch were superseded in later times by annual magistrates. For whatever the history of the expulsion of the last Tarquinius, "the proud," may have been interwoven with anecdotes and spun out into a romance, it remains unaffected by such embellishments.

The royal power was not abolished. The fact that an "interim king" (interrex) was nominated upon occurrence of a vacancy demonstrates this. When two supreme powers faced each other, a colleague intervened, prohibiting what the other had commanded. The law, however, threatened the magistrate, who did not allow due process to the provocation, with no other penalty than infamy. This was essentially nothing but a moral stain, and at its worst, it merely disqualified the infamous individual from giving testimony. When a consul acted within the old regal jurisdiction, he could commit an injustice, but he committed no crime and therefore was not subject to the penal judge's authority.

The king was free to appoint deputies, but he had never felt obligated to do so. In contrast, the consuls exercised their authority by delegating power in a manner distinct from the king's approach. This was indeed not the inferior magistracy itself, at least not in the sense associated with the magisterial position. The commissioners did not emanate from the choice of the community; however, it became the starting-point for the institution of subordinate magistrates, which were later developed in various ways. The power to decide civil cases was taken away from the supreme court. This decision was made because the king's right to transfer an individual case to a deputy was changed into a duty for the consul. The consul had to settle the rightful claim of the parties involved and the object of the lawsuit, then refer the matter to a private person who would be chosen by him and provided with instructions.

The right of nomination was materially restricted in favor of the burgesses. The consul was bound to obtain the assent of the burgesses for the successors he designated, and subsequently, could only nominate those individuals chosen by the community. The nomination of ordinary supreme magistrates was undoubtedly transferred to the community through this binding right of proposal. However, in practice, there remained a significant difference between the right of proposal and the right of formal nomination. Change in the Nomination of Priests

The nomination of priests, a prerogative of kings, was not transferred to consuls. Instead, colleges of priests filled vacancies within their own ranks. The Vestals and single priests were nominated by the pontifical college, which also exercised paternal jurisdiction over priestesses of Vesta.

This separation of supreme authority in sacred matters from civil power—the "king for sacrifice" having neither civil nor sacred powers, but only a title conferred upon him—and the semi-magisterial position of the new high priest, which contrasted sharply with the character typically associated with the priesthood in Rome, forms one of the most significant and important peculiarities of this state-revolution. The aim was to impose limits on the powers of magistrates primarily in the interest of the aristocracy. The consul was distinguished from the ordinary burgess solely by the purple border of his toga. Unlike the king, who occasionally appeared in public in his chariot, the consul was required to conform to the general rule and walk on foot within the city like every other citizen. The dictator was not subject to these limitations, which applied only to the ordinary president of the community.

The consuls, like the kings before them, were the supreme administrators, judges, and generals. In a religious context, it was not the rex sacrorum (who was merely nominated to preserve the title) but the consul who performed prayers and sacrifices for the community, and in its name, sought the will of the gods with the aid of those skilled in sacred lore. The exclusion of this multitude from the public assembly, although it bore part of the public burdens, might be tolerated as long as that public assembly itself had no very material share in the working of the state machine. As long as the royal power by its high and free position remained almost equally formidable to both the burgesses and the metoeci, equality of legal redress was maintained in the nation. The appointment of magistrates, previously determined by the community's election, was now subject to confirmation or rejection by the patrician senate.

In cases of appeal, confirmation alone was never deemed necessary, as far as we know. The matter at stake was the pardon of the guilty, which was granted by the sovereign assembly of the people. Once this pardon was issued, any attempt to cancel it was entirely out of the question. The Roman Senate had acted from an early time, although not exclusively or primarily, as a state council. Even in the time of kings, it was likely that non-senators could participate in the assembly without being deemed unconstitutional. Now, for such discussions, the patrician senate would be joined by a number of non-patricians who were added to the roll. The consul in office was no more ranked as a member of the senate than the king. In taking the votes, he did not include his own.

The selection of members for both the narrower patrician senate and those added to the roll fell to the consuls, just as it had formerly fallen to the kings. However, the nature of the case implied that while the king still had some measure of regard for the representation of various clans in the senate, this consideration was of no account when it came to the plebeians, among whom clan organization was only imperfectly developed. As a result, the relation between the senate and clan organization became increasingly irrelevant. It seems to have become early custom not to fill senators' places immediately upon their falling vacant. Instead, the senate's roll is revised and completed during the census, typically every four years. This practice also imposed an important limitation on the power granted for selecting officials.

To look merely at the direct innovations, or perhaps just the change in the duration of the supreme magistracy, would be entirely to miss the broader significance of the first Roman revolution. Its indirect effects, however, were by far the most significant and likely exceeded even what its authors had initially envisioned. The exclusion of the plebeians from all public magistracies and public priesthoods, while they were admissible to the position of officers and senators, and the maintenance, with perverse obstinacy, of the legal impossibility of marriage between old burgesses and plebeians, further reinforced the patriciate's perception of an exclusive and wrongly privileged aristocracy. To this epoch therefore we may trace back -- in the views and feelings of the people -- both the invidiousness of the distinction between patricians and plebeians, and the strict and haughty line of demarcation between Roman citizens and aliens.

The former civic distinction was transitory, whereas the latter political one was enduring. The notion of political unity and growing national prestige, thus instilled within the nation's core, was expansive enough initially to erode and eventually sweep away petty distinctions. The profound and practical veneration for authority, shared by Romans like other people of political capacity, led to the development of a remarkable rule in Roman constitutional and private law. This rule stated that every command issued by a magistrate, not grounded on a law, was at least valid during their term of office, even if it expired with the end of that tenure. The president's annual change of office led to a broad scope of action. The fact that this development was now devoid of any practical significance became clear: if the consul made an error in deciding on a case during their term, their successor could initiate a new trial of the matter.

The government of the patriciate period, however, was necessary for the development of these consequences of the new republicanism. As posterity felt its effects, the revolution likely seemed different to the contemporary world at first. The non-burgesses gained burgess rights, and the new burgess body acquired comprehensive prerogatives. However, the patrician senate's right of rejection limited their freedom of movement in crucial matters. Although they couldn't thwart the collective body's will, they could practically delay and cripple it. The senate appointed for life inevitably acquired great influence, chiefly by virtue of its title to advise the magistrate in all matters. This broadened the original patrician senate to include both patricians and plebeians. As a result, the legal relations between the senate and annual rulers became inverted. The senate assumed substantial powers of government, while the former ruler became a president who acted as chairman and executed its decrees.

In every proposal submitted to the community for acceptance or rejection, a practice had been established: previously consulting the entire senate and obtaining its approval, although not constitutionally required, was deeply ingrained through tradition and custom. This approach was not taken lightly or without reluctance. The Plebeian Opposition

It is true that the immediate effect of the revolution was to establish an aristocratic government. Nevertheless, this is not the whole truth.

The majority of our contemporaries likely believed that the revolution had brought only a more rigid despotism to the common people. However, we who come after them see in that very revolution the seeds of young liberty. The metoeci had been politically insignificant, while the old burgesses held all the power. Now that the former were integrated into the community, the latter were marginalized; for, even if there was still room for full civil equality, it is the initial breach, not the occupation of the last post, that determines the fall of the fortress. The Roman community dated its political existence from the beginning of the consulate with justice.

While however the republican revolution may be justly called a victory of the common people, the revolution even in this respect did not bear the character that is commonly designated as democratic. In a regal government, personal merit alone may be more likely to gain influence and consideration than in a patriciate system. While a complete legal equality therefore had existed within the old burgess body, the new burgess body or former metoeci came to be divided in this manner into a number of privileged families and a multitude kept in a position of inferiority. While laying the axe to the root of the old burgess-body and their clan-nobility, and laying the basis for a new burgess-body, the preponderance in the latter rested on land ownership and age. The first signs of a new aristocracy began to emerge, primarily based on the actual consideration in which families were held—the future nobility.

The day of retirement was governed by it, except when a consul was elected specifically in place of one who had withdrawn; in this case, the substitute succeeded to the rights and subsequently to the term of the person he replaced. During the earlier period, supplementary consuls were only present when one of the consuls had withdrawn. It wasn't until the later ages of the republic that pairs of supplementary consuls appeared. The official year of a consul typically consisted of an equal portion of one civil year. 5 It may not be superfluous to remark that the iudicium legitimum, as well as the quod imperio continetur, rested on the authority of the directing magistrate. The distinction lay solely in the circumstance that the imperium was limited by law in the former case and unrestricted in the latter. 16

Governing through the magistracy that had been reduced to serve them, preponderating in the Senate, holding sole possession of all public offices and priesthoods, armed with exclusive cognizance of both human and divine matters, and familiar with the entire routine of political procedure, influential in the public assembly through a large number of devoted followers attached to various families, and finally entitled to examine and reject every decree of the community—the patricians might have long preserved their practical power because they had abandoned their claim to sole legal authority at the right time. During the first period following the expulsion of the kings, various measures were implemented that appeared to be aimed at winning the favor of the common people for the governance of the nobility, particularly on economic grounds. The rise of capitalist power. The reduction of port dues, while popular with the general public, primarily benefited the wealthy merchants.

As however, apart from the conversion of public land into private property or its assignment, Roman law knew no fixed rights of usufruct on the part of individual burgesses to be respected like those of property. It depended solely on the pleasure of the king, as long as the public land remained such, to grant and define its joint enjoyment. He frequently made use of his right or power in favour of plebeians. This was simply the precarium already described, applied to the state domains. It may have been in use as a public land arrangement prior to its assignment being carried out. Now, however, this occupation tenure became permanent, but as was natural, only privileged persons or their favorites participated. The tenth and fifth were collected with the same negligence as the grazing money.

The latter course was probably the most common as well as the most pernicious. For while utter ruin might be averted from the individual, this precarious position of the farmer, dependent at all times on the mercy of his creditor—a position in which he knew nothing of property but its burdens—threatened to demoralize and politically annihilate the whole farmer-class. The immediate crisis, however, did not arise from those who felt the disabilities of their order, but rather from the distress of the farmers. It seemed as though a college rule had been introduced not for the protection of the people, but to facilitate breaches of faith and despotism. They endured, however, what could not be changed.

There was, however, no division of powers. The power of the tribunes primarily involved the right to stop administration and judicial action at their discretion. They could exempt individuals bound to military service from duty, prevent or cancel legal proceedings against debtors, and halt criminal investigations until they were resolved. This included the authority to arrest accused persons while investigations were pending. The tribune, in virtue of his judicial office, may summon any burgess, particularly the consul in office, to answer for himself. If he refuses to comply voluntarily, the tribune should seize him, place him under arrest during an investigation, and allow him to post bail. Alternatively, the tribune can sentence him to death or impose a fine.

Lynch justice was once unquestioned, but it was later recognized as a legitimate form of self-help through law. After the tribunate of the plebs gained legal recognition, it became an accepted practice. The former was exempt from jurisdiction under Roman state law as long as he held office.
