After relinquishing his position, however, he could be held accountable for his actions through a legal process. Ultimately, the decision rested with members of his own profession and the broader community that shared their interests. In the tribunician jurisdiction, a new power emerged, one that could potentially challenge the authority of the supreme magistrate even during their term in office. On the other hand, this power was exclusively wielded against the noble burgesses by non-noble individuals. Furthermore, its oppressive nature lay in the fact that neither the crime nor its corresponding punishment were formally defined by law.

In civil jurisdiction, the plebeian institutions interfered only so far as to withdraw the nomination of jurors from the consuls and have decisions pronounced by the "ten-men-judges" (iudices), also known as the decemvirs, who were specifically designated for that purpose. It was a necessary preliminary to the legal recognition of the plebs that the tribunes could not be hindered from having their successors elected by the assembly and from procuring the confirmation of their criminal sentences by the same body. The Icilian law (262) further guaranteed this right, threatening severe punishment on anyone who interrupted a tribune while speaking or ordered the assembly to disperse. Thus was the tribune of the people appointed as a shield and protection for the individual, and as leader and manager for all. He was provided with unlimited judicial power in criminal proceedings, which enabled him to emphasize his authority. Furthermore, he was declared inviolable (-sacrosanctus-), meaning that anyone who laid hands on him or his servant would be considered guilty of a serious offense, punishable by death.

The appeal from a consul to a tribune and the tribune's right of intercession against the consul were identical in nature to an appeal from one consul to another and the consular intercession in opposition. Both cases were straightforward applications of the principle that when two equal authorities disagree, the veto prevails over the command. They shared also a peculiar collegiate arrangement, in which the full powers of the office rested with each individual consul and each individual tribune. In cases where conflicts arose within the college, they did not count votes, but gave Nay precedence over Yea. As a result, when a tribune forbade something, the veto of an individual was sufficient, even if his colleagues opposed him. Conversely, when he brought an accusation, any one of those colleagues could thwart it. Both consuls and tribunes had full and coordinate criminal jurisdiction. The consuls exercised it indirectly, while the tribunes exercised it directly. The consuls were necessarily patricians; the tribunes were necessarily plebeians.

The tribunate, if it did not directly contribute to the political equalization of the orders, served as a powerful weapon in the hands of the plebeians who sought admission to the offices of state. The wealthy plebeians had just as much interest in these abuses as the patricians. If

Many families of burgesses are said to have migrated and sought more peaceful abodes in neighboring communities. We may well believe it. Impeached by the tribunes, his life was in peril. It is said that he left the city, but only to return at the head of a Volscian army. When he was on the point of conquering the city of his fathers for the public foe, the earnest appeal of his mother touched his conscience. He thus expiated his first treason by a second, and both were ended by death. A band of political refugees, led by a Sabine chief named Appius Herdonius, surprised the Capitol in 294. They rallied the slaves, and only after a violent conflict was it resolved when the Tusculans arrived to lend their aid, allowing the Roman burgess-force to defeat the Catilinarian group.

The introduction of the plebeian assembly of tribes and the placement of the plebiscitum on a level with formal law sanctioned by the whole community are two key arrangements. The former is certainly attributed to the proposal of Volero Publilius, the tribune of the people, in 283 BC. This assembly was modeled after that of the curies but was actually composed of the independent middle class. The majority of freedmen and clients were excluded because they were not landowners, while the larger landholders did not hold a dominant position as they once had in previous centuries. After two successful terms as consul, in his third year of office (268), he presented a plan to the citizens to measure the public domain and lease parts of it for the public treasury's benefit. A portion would also be allocated to support those in need.

The nobles rose as one man. The wealthy plebeians joined them. However, the common people were displeased because Spurius Cassius sought, in accordance with federal law and fairness, to allocate his share among the Latin confederates. This step marked a significant advancement toward full political equality; it was not overly costly, considering that the suspension of both the tribunate of the people and the right of appeal occurred during the decemvirate's duration, and the decemvirs were strictly prohibited from violating the sworn liberties of the community. An embassy was sent to Greece to retrieve the laws of Solon and other Greek laws. It was only upon its return that the decemvirs were selected for the year 303.

Although they were at liberty to elect plebeians, the choice fell on patricians alone. So powerful was the nobility still that it wasn't until a second election became necessary in 304 that some plebeians were chosen - the first non-patrician magistrates the Roman community had ever seen. The people definitely renounced the tribunate, which was brought to this point by the decemvirate and could only be regained through illegal means.

The most remarkable feature was the exclusion of appeal to the tributain capital causes, while the privilege of appeal to the centuries was guaranteed. This is explainable due to the circumstance that the penal jurisdiction was actually usurped by the plebs and its presidents. Consequently, the tribunate's authority coincided with the tribunician process for capital cases, whereas it seems there was an intention to retain the aedilian process of fines. They delayed taking action: under the pretext that the laws were not yet ready, they themselves prolonged their magistracy after the expiry of their official year. This was possible because Roman constitutional law allowed for an extraordinary call to revise the constitution without being bound by the term set for its ending. The moderate section of the aristocracy, led by the Valerii and Horatii, attempted to compel the abdication of the decemvirate in the senate. However, Appius Claudius, the head of the decemvirs, a former rigid aristocrat who had begun to adopt demagogic and tyrannical tendencies, gained control in the senate, and the people acquiesced.

Thereupon, Lucius Siccius Dentatus, Rome's bravest man, who had fought in numerous battles and bore forty-five honourable scars, was discovered dead in front of the camp, foully murdered, according to accounts, at the behest of the decemvirs. While people were amazed at the unprecedented deed, they surrounded the dead body of the fair maiden. The decemvir then ordered his lictors to bring the father and the bridegroom before his tribunal, in order to demand an explanation from them for their rebellion against his authority. Protected by a furious multitude, the father and the bridegroom of the maiden made their escape from the lictors of the despot. Meanwhile, in Rome, the senate trembled as the pair presented themselves, accompanied by numerous witnesses of the fearful deed, to one of the two camps.

The impeachment of the decemvirs ended with the execution of the two most guilty men, Appius Claudius and Spurius Oppius, who took their own lives in prison. The remaining eight were banished from the state, and their properties were confiscated by the government. The decemvirate was the third great victory of the plebs after the abolition of the monarchy and the establishment of the tribunate of the people. The opposition to this institution and its leader, Appius Claudius, was understandable. If the nobility succeeded in setting aside the decemvirs, it is certainly conceivable that after their fall, the plebs might once again assemble in arms to secure the results of both the earlier revolution of 260 and the latest movement. The Valerio-Horatian laws of 305 can only be understood as a compromise in this conflict.

It was decreed by the centuries that, in the future, every magistrate — and thus also the dictator — would be required to grant the right of appeal upon their nomination. Anyone who nominated a magistrate under different terms would have to pay the penalty for their offense with their own life. The tribunician right of intercession had also extended to the decrees of the senate as a collective body, after the latter had become both a deliberative and decretory board. This shift likely occurred first with a plebiscitum intended for the entire community; (12) it was natural that there should be conceded to the tribunes a certain participation in the discussions of the senate-house. The plebeian aediles were established following the model of patrician quaestors, just as the plebeian tribunes followed the model of patrician consuls. This is evident both in their criminal functions, where the distinction between the two magistracies appears to have been based on their tendencies rather than their powers, and in their responsibility for managing the archives.

For those who benefited from the privileges at the heart of the agitation, it was likely that they felt it was not a suitable moment to press their case for participating in the magistracies. This was especially true given that they were viewed as inferior in other respects. The fact that the exclusive financial power of the senate was being challenged made it seem unlikely that they would be able to assert their claim at this time. The government did not take steps to achieve direct political equality among the social classes during the first 50 years of the republic. Beyond doubt, from the very first, a portion of the leading plebeian families had attached themselves to the movement. This was partly due to a sense of what was owed to fellow-members of their order and partly because they recognized that concessions to the multitude were inevitable in the issue. If turned to account, these concessions would result in the abrogation of exclusive rights held by the patriciate, thereby giving the plebeian aristocracy a decisive preponderance in the state.

A crisis arose among the parties following the collapse of the decemvirate. The throwing open of marriage and magistracies -- military tribunes with consular powers. Nothing shows so clearly the defencelessness of the clan-nobility when opposed to the united plebs, as the fact that the fundamental principle of the exclusive party -- the invalidity of marriage between patricians and plebeians -- fell at the first blow scarcely four years after the decemviral revolution. Of greater political importance than the refusal of the -ius imaginumand of the honour of a triumph was the circumstance that the exclusion of plebeians sitting in the senate from debate necessarily ceased for those of their number who, as designated or former consuls, ranked among the senators whose opinion had to be asked before the rest. It was certainly of great importance for the nobility to admit the plebeian only to a consular office, not to the consulate itself.

The censorship at this time did not possess the same level of importance and moral authority as it was later perceived to be. The nobility, despite the most stubborn resistance, suffered a series of losses without end. As their power waned, so did their frustration, ultimately leading to further decline. Spurius Maelius, a wealthy plebeian, sold corn during a severe dearth in 315 B.C. at prices that embarrassed and annoyed the patrician prefect of grain, Gaius Minucius.

In this way, it became possible that although the eligibility of plebeians had been established by law as early as 333 for the quaestorship, it was only in 345 that the first plebeian attained the position. Similarly, patricians almost exclusively held the military tribunate with consular powers until 354. The suffering farmers. During these political struggles and social questions, issues had lain dormant, or were discussed at least with less energy. The plebeian aristocracy seized control of the tribunate, using it to further their own interests. Neither the issue of land ownership nor any reforms to the credit system received serious consideration. Despite this, new lands were being acquired, while impoverished and decaying farmers remained a significant problem.

Instances of assignations did occur, particularly in recently conquered border territories, such as those of Ardea in 312, Labici in 336, and Veii in 361. However, these instances were more related to military grounds than providing relief for farmers, and they were not carried out to a sufficient extent. A patrician like Cassius, distinguished by military renown and personal valour, was Marcus Manlius, who saved the Capitol during the Gallic siege. He emerged as a champion of the oppressed people, united with them through shared experiences in war, but also harboured bitter hatred for his rival, the celebrated general and leader of the optimates, Marcus Furius Camillus. While attempts at reform were halted, social disorders intensified. On one hand, domain possessions continued to expand due to successful wars. On the other hand, debt and poverty spread more widely among farmers, particularly as a result of the severe war with Veii (348-358) and the burning of the capital during the Gallic invasion (364).

It is true that, during the Venetian War, it became necessary to extend the term of service for soldiers and keep them under arms not only during summer, but also throughout winter. When farmers, foreseeing their economic ruin, were on the point of refusing consent to the declaration of war, the senate resolved to make an important concession. It was only when the state chest was empty that a general contribution (tributum) was imposed due to lack of pay, and in this situation, it was viewed as a forced loan which was later repaid by the community. It was not for nothing that the plebeian nobility had adopted the popular cause. They combined their efforts into a single proposal for a law, and after a lengthy struggle lasting eleven years, the senate finally gave its consent. The law was then passed in the year 387.

When Marcus Furius Camillus, the former champion of the clans, founded a sanctuary of Concord at the foot of the Capitol, upon an elevated platform where the senate frequently met, above the old meeting-place of the burgesses, the Comitium, we gladly cherish the belief that he recognized in this legislation the close of a dissension only too long. The patrician spirit remained true to its nature. Of little avail were the complaints raised by the patrician presidents of the senate regarding the participation of plebeians in its debates. It became a settled rule that no longer did the patrician members, but those who had attained to one of the three supreme ordinary magistracies—the consulship, praetorship, and curule aedileship—should be summoned to give their opinion, regardless of class order, while senators who had held none of these offices still took part merely in the division.

The right, in fine, of the patrician senate to reject a decree of the community as unconstitutional—a right, however, which in all probability it rarely ventured to exercise—was withdrawn from it by the Publilian law of 415 and by the Maenian law. This latter law effectively precluded the senate from raising constitutional objections when candidates were listed or a proposed law was introduced, effectively rendering its consent a prior announcement. While the nobility still retained its comprehensive privileges, it was excluded by law from the tribunate, the plebeian aedileship, the second consulship, and censorship. It was also barred from participating in the votes of the plebs, which were legally equivalent to those of the entire body of burgesses. The Roman clan nobility did not disappear simply because it had lost its original meaning.

The haughtiness of the Ramnians survived for centuries despite losing their class privileges. After striving "to rescue the consulate from plebeian filth," they eventually became convinced that such a feat was impossible. In a fit of spite, they displayed their aristocratic spirit. A characteristic instance of this sort occurred some years after the passing of the Ogulnian law in 458. The capricious actions of this sort were of little consequence, and the majority of the clans kept themselves detached from this miserable policy of pettiness; however, it left a lingering sense of discontent on both sides. The struggle of the common people against the clans was a necessary political and moral imperative, but the futile attempts to prolong the conflict – the aimless battles of the rear guard after the battle had been won, as well as the petty squabbles over rank and status – needlessly agitated and disrupted the public and private life of Roman society.

The preservation and increase of the middle class, and particularly that of the farmers, was a concern for every patriotic Roman statesman. The new domain arrangement granted the right to drive considerable flocks and herds on public pastures, and allowed for occupying domain land not laid out in pasture up to a maximum fixed on a high scale. This concession gave the wealthy an important share in the produce of the domains, potentially even exceeding proportionate levels. The latter regulation also conferred upon the domain tenure a legal sanction, although it remained liable to pay a tenth and could be revoked at pleasure, as well as the system of occupation itself. We do not intend to raise any doubts about whether the regulations of the Licinian laws, as they existed, provided substantial benefits for the small farmer and the day labourer.

It is, however, unfair to reproach these sincere efforts to address the poverty of the middle class with their insufficiency. The notion that employing partial measures against radical evils is futile, as they only partially address the issue, is a misguided article of faith often espoused by those lacking in courage or conviction. However, this stance remains absurd. The numerous and large colonies founded to secure that sovereignty, most of which were established in the 5th century, provided land for a portion of the agricultural working class, offering them farms of their own. Those who remained at home received relief from the surplus.

The earlier small holdings were likely lost beyond recovery. However, the increasing average of Roman prosperity likely led to the conversion of former larger landholders into farmers, thereby adding new members to the middle class. If the distress of the farmers had not been alleviated and their grievances remained unresolved, it is clear that by the end of this period, the Roman middle class would have been in a significantly better position than they were during the first century following the expulsion of the kings. In regard to this imposing republican equality, we must not overlook the fact that it was largely formal. An aristocracy of a distinct stamp emerged from it or was inherent to it from its inception.

Appius Claudius, a patrician, and Manius Curius, a farmer, were bitter enemies. However, they joined forces through wise counsel and decisive action to defeat King Pyrrhus. Meanwhile, Gaius Fabricius, as censor, imposed penalties on Publius Cornelius Rufinus for his aristocratic views and behavior. Despite this, Rufinus continued to support the claim of a second consulate for himself due to his proven military prowess. The shape taken by the new government was described amidst these changes. After the political abolition of the nobility, the three elements of the republican commonwealth—the burgesses, the magistrates, and the senate—stood in their respective positions towards one another. In this manner provision was made for the majority of the freeholders in the tribal assemblies, while for the centuriate assemblies, wealth had already tipped the balance.

The powers of the burgesses increased during this period, exhibiting a tendency to expand their authority in a gradual manner. During this period, the burgesses did not interfere in administration on the whole. However, their right to declare war was maintained as reasonable, and it was held to extend to cases where a prolonged armistice ended instead of a peace expiring, marking the beginning of a new war. The government sometimes left decisions to the people in difficult cases. For example, when Caere sued for peace, the people had declared war against it before any fighting began (401). Another instance occurred when the senate was hesitant to reject the peaceful request from the Samnites (436).

It is therefore quite easy to understand how the assemblies of the burgesses, which had great practical importance during the first two centuries of the republic, gradually became a mere instrument in the hands of the presiding magistrate. In truth, this was a very dangerous instrument because the magistrates who presided were numerous, and every resolution of the community was regarded as the ultimate legal expression of the will of the people. The partition and weakening of consular powers were significant outcomes of the struggle between the old and new burgesses. The reduction in the power of magistrates was a crucial consequence of this conflict. The consuls, although they were in law wholly and everywhere coordinated, naturally from the earliest times divided between them in practice the different departments of duty.

It became customary for the Senate to define annually the spheres of duty. While it did not directly allocate them among the coordinate magistrates, it exerted significant influence on their personal allocation through advice and requests. The senate undoubtedly obtained a decree from the community, definitively settling the issue of distribution. Although, by definition, as an extraordinary magistracy, it had in reality from the start, its special functions were limited to law. In contrast, the consulate possessed a more distinct character.

Brave officers were frequently released from these rules. A notable example is Quintus Fabius Rullianus, who served as consul five times over the course of twenty-eight years. Another example is Marcus Valerius Corvus, who held six consulships: his first at age 23, his last at 72, and throughout three generations, he protected his countrymen and struck fear into the hearts of their enemies. The Tribunate of the People as an Instrument of Government

As the Roman magistrate evolved from an absolute lord to a limited commissioner and administrator of the community, the tribunate of the people underwent a similar transformation. This shift occurred internally, rather than externally. They stood on an equality with the consuls from the beginning. In the early stages of conflicts between the orders, they acquired the right to initiate legislation, like the consuls. It was only when the orders were finally equalized that they received a position of equality with the consuls as part of the governing authority alongside the senate.

If they were precluded from the right of voting, this was simply an application of the general principle of Roman state-law. Those who were not called to act should give counsel, and accordingly, the whole of the acting magistrates possessed only a seat, not a vote, in the council of the state during their year of office. But concessions did not rest there. But faith in an ideal, which is the foundation of all power and impotence in democracy, had come to be closely associated with the tribunate of the plebs among the Romans. We need not recall the case of Cola Rienzi to see that, although the advantage to the multitude might be unsubstantial, it could not be abolished without a formidable convulsion of the state. For the moment, however, and for a long time to come, the aristocracy was so absolutely powerful and completely possessed control over the tribunate. No trace at all could be met with of a collegiate opposition on the part of the tribunes to the senate. The government easily overcame the occasional forlorn movements of opposition that now and then proceeded from individual tribunes, usually by means of the tribunate itself.

The composition in reality was the Senate. It governed the Commonwealth, with little opposition, following the equalization of the orders. The number of magistrates was insufficient to maintain the senate at its normal strength of 300. It was essential that the number did not drop below this threshold, particularly since the list of senators coincided with that of jurors. The Roman state made some approach to, although it did not reach, the great institution of modern times, representative popular government. The aggregate of non-debating senators furnished a compact mass of members capable of forming and entitled to express an opinion, but voting in silence.

While the burgesses gained the appearance, the senate gained the substance of power — a decisive influence over legislation and official elections, and complete control of the state. The aristocracy's preponderating and skilled influence on elections led them to choose candidates acceptable to the government. In managing current affairs, the supreme governing corporation did not intervene in judicial and military administration. The Roman aristocracy possessed sufficient political acumen to resist converting control of the commonwealth into a guardianship for individual officials, thereby preserving the instrument as a functional entity.

The free action of burgesses should be arrested and subdued. The magistrates should be reduced to serving as presidents of its sittings and executive commissioners. A corporation for offering advice should seize the inheritance of both authorities sanctioned by the constitution, becoming, albeit in modest forms, the central government of the state. These were steps towards revolution and usurpation. It seems ii For the same reasons that led to a later period placing the praetorship alongside consulship common to both orders, even during the consular tribunate, plebeian members of the college were kept aloof from jurisdiction. This arrangement prepared the way for the subsequent actual division of jurisdiction between consuls and praetors. 2 This claim may be made as an excuse for the nobility, that after they neglected the right moment for this purpose at the abolition of the monarchy, they were no longer able to rectify the situation.

Anyone who compares the consular Fasti before and after 412 will have no doubt about the existence of the law respecting re-election to the consulate; for, while before that year a return to office, especially after three or four years, was common, afterwards intervals of ten years and more became frequent. Exceptions, however, do occur in large numbers, particularly during the most severe years of war. The fall of the Etruscan power marked a significant turning point in Roman history. The Etruscans, who had previously dominated the region, were eventually surpassed by the Celts. This shift was accompanied by the rise of the Etrusco-Carthaginian maritime supremacy. The Romans' external history and that of Italy are closely intertwined with this period.

Massilia, despite facing continual and severe struggles, managed to maintain her independence. The seaports of Campania and the Volscian land fell under Etruscan control, as did those in Corsica after the Battle of Alalia. In Sardinia, the sons of Carthaginian general Mago laid the foundation for their house's greatness and their city's by completing the conquest of the island around 260. In Sicily, while the Hellenic colonies were engaged in internal conflicts, the Phoenicians retained control of the western half without significant opposition. But now that the entire Tuscan league, seizing on the confusion and the weakness of the Roman state following the expulsion of the Tarquins, launched a more vigorous attack under King Lars Porsena of Clusium, it no longer faced the usual resistance.

The Etruscan army, which after the fall of Rome had penetrated into Latium, had its victorious career checked in the first instance before the walls of Aricia by the well-timed intervention of the Cumaeans who hastened to the aid of the Aricines. Anaxilas, ruler of Rhegium and Zancle, had already closed the Sicilian straits against their privateers by means of a standing fleet. Soon afterwards, in 280, the Cumaeans and Hiero of Syracuse achieved a decisive victory near Cumae over the Tyrrhenian fleet, to which the Carthaginians vainly attempted to render aid. This victory is celebrated by Pindar in his first Pythian ode. An Etruscan helmet still exists, which Hiero sent to Olympia, inscribed with: "Hieron son of Deinomenes and the Syracusans to Zeus, Tyrrhenian spoils from Kyme." Dionysius of Syracuse achieved extraordinary successes against the Carthaginians and Etruscans, placing Syracuse at the head of Greek cities in Sicily. In contrast, Doric Tarentum rose to pre-eminence among Italian Hellenes after Achaean Sybaris fell around the time of Rome's expulsion of its kings (243). Hiero had occupied the island of Ischia after his victory at Cumae, thereby interrupting communication between the Campanians and the northern Etruscans.

Although Etrusco-Carthaginian piracy was not wholly repressed, Antium had a significant presence of privateers until the beginning of the 5th century. The powerful city-state of Syracuse formed a strong bulwark against the allied Tuscans and Phoenicians. After the failure of the Attic expedition, Syracuse became the first Greek maritime power. The men who led the state aspired to control Sicily and Lower Italy, as well as both Italian seas. In contrast, the Carthaginians saw their dominance in Sicily threatened and were determined to subjugate the Syracusans and conquer the entire island. Capua, the capital, fell in 330. The Tuscan population there was soon after the conquest largely displaced by the Samnites.

The Hellenes maintained their hold on Neapolis, particularly, possibly with the assistance of the Syracusans. In contrast, the Etruscan name in Campania faded from historical records, except for some isolated Etruscan communities that continued to exist in a state of desolation. Events that were even more significant occurred at roughly the same time in Northern Italy. The attachment to their native soil was lacking in the Celts. In contrast, Italians and Germans were characterized by a desire to settle in towns and villages, which subsequently gained size and significance among the Celts at an earlier stage than it did in Italy.

They were accustomed to challenge a single enemy to fight, both during war and in times of peace. In battle, they would engage each other in elaborate armor, their lives hanging in the balance. One of these nephews, Sigovesus, crossed the Rhine and advanced towards the Black Forest. The second, Bellovesus, crossed the Graian Alps. The Po Valley

The Celts, accompanied by the Ligures, displaced the Etruscan tribes, taking control of each location until the entire left bank of the Po River fell under their dominion. After the fall of the rich Etruscan town of Melpum, presumably located in the region of Milan, the Celts who had already settled in the Po Valley basin united with newly arrived tribes. The Romans then crossed to the right bank of the river and began to press upon the Umbrians and Etruscans in their original abodes. During this truce, the troubles of Etruria grew more severe, and Celtic arms approached the settlements that had previously been spared on the right bank of the Po.

The conquest of Veii was a pivotal event in Roman history. The war against the Veientes, Capenates, and Falisci was marked by a lengthy siege, one that has been likened to the legendary Trojan War, which supposedly lasted for ten years. However, the reliability of this evidence is questionable. It was the first time a Roman army stayed in the field from summer to winter, year after year, until its objective was achieved. The contemporary attacks of the Celts would alone suffice to explain the non-intervention of the northern communities. However, it is affirmed that this inaction was primarily occasioned by internal factions within the league of Etruscan cities, and particularly by the opposition encountered from the aristocratic governments of other cities, which retained or restored the regal form of government, as exemplified by the Veientes.

Despite being isolated and forsaken, Veii fell (358) after a valiant resistance led by Marcus Furius Camillus, who first revealed to his countrymen the brilliant and perilous path of foreign conquest. The Volsinii, who had remained quiet due to federal indecision during the crisis of Veii, took up arms after its capture. However, they agreed to peace after a few years (363). The Celts attacked Rome. The Battle of the Allia marked a turning point as it seemed that the two peoples, through whose cooperation Etruria's existence was threatened, were about to destroy each other. However, the reviving power of Rome would be crushed by foreign invaders.

The Celtic swarms, which had crossed the river after the fall of Melpum, rapidly overflowed into northern Italy—not merely the open country on the right bank of the Po and along the shore of the Adriatic, but also Etruria to the south of the Apennines. A few years later, Clusium, situated in the heart of Etruria (Chiusi, on the borders of Tuscany and the Papal State), was besieged by the Celtic Senones. The Etruscans were humbled, and the Tuscan city in its straits invoked aid from the destroyers of Veii.

They despatched envoys. The Brennus, also known as the "king of the army," led the Gauls in breaking the siege of Clusium. The entire Celtic force, numbering around 70,000 soldiers, turned against Rome. These barbarians, however, were men whose courage despised death. Their mode of fighting was as novel to the Italians as it was terrible. Like sword-wielding warriors, the Celts charged with fierce ferocity into the Roman phalanx, shattering it at the first impact.

The city of Rome was left undefended after the battle. The remaining forces were insufficient to hold the walls, so the enemy marched in three days later. They had no knowledge of the art of besieging. The blockade of the steep citadel rock was tedious and difficult because subsistence for the great host could only be procured by armed foraging parties. The citizens of the neighbouring Latin cities, particularly the Ardeates, frequently attacked the foragers with courage and success. And yet, despite an enactment in Rome that exempted no one from military service on occasion of a Celtic invasion, or when dates were counted from the city's conquest, or when the event was widely reported throughout the civilized world and even recorded in Greek annals, the Battle of the Allia and its aftermath remain largely without lasting impact.

The conflict between the Gauls and the Romans was not like that between Rome and Etruria or Rome and Samnium, a collision of two political powers that affected and modified each other. It may be compared to natural catastrophes, after which an organism, if it is not destroyed, immediately resumes its equilibrium. Further conquests of Rome in Etruria - South Etruria. The Tuscans, who had taken advantage of the Celtic attack on Rome to assail Veii, had accomplished nothing, as they appeared in insufficient force; the barbarians had scarcely departed when the heavy arm of Latium descended on the Tuscans with undiminished weight. After the Etruscans had been repeatedly defeated, the whole of southern Etruria remained in the hands of the Romans. They formed four new tribes in the territories of Veii, Capena, and Falerii. The northern boundary was secured by establishing fortresses at Sutrium and Nepete.

In Caere, a city nearest to the Romans, which suffered the heaviest retribution, was compelled to cede half its territory to Rome. With the diminished domain left to it, Caere withdrew from the Etruscan league and entered into a relationship with Rome, becoming a subject of the Roman state. Not long afterwards, Falerii, which had preserved its original Latin nationality even under Tuscan rule, abandoned the Etruscan league and entered into a perpetual alliance with Rome. As a result, the entire southern region of Etruria became subject to Roman supremacy. In the Alps, the extent to which Celtic settlers intermingled with earlier Etruscan or other populations remains unclear due to insufficient information. However, the Raeti people in modern-day Grisons and Tyrol can be considered a likely Etruscan stock.

While sharing more or less of an Etruscan character to mark these regions, it was confined to isolated remnants and fragments of their earlier power. The Etruscan nation no longer reaped the benefits of such gains as were still acquired by individuals through peaceful commerce or maritime warfare. Etruria Proper at Peace and on the Decline. Thus the Etruscans, after losing their possessions in Campania and the entire region north of the Apennines and south of the Ciminian Forest, were left with very narrow boundaries. Their period of power and ambition had come to an end forever. Wealth became concentrated in the hands of a few nobles, who owned vast tracts of land. The masses were left impoverished. As social revolutions arose from this situation, they only increased the suffering of the people, whom they sought to alleviate. With the central power powerless, no solution remained for the distressed aristocrats. g In 453, the city was captured by the Romans in Arretium, while in 488 it was taken over in Volsinii. However, to seek Roman assistance, the Romans intervened, ultimately putting an end to the disorder and eliminating the remaining vestiges of independence.

c Herodotus also wrote about Hatrias, which is located at the mouth of the Po River and the sea that washes its shores. This hypothesis commends itself to acceptance from a geographical perspective. The facts presented by history may be brought into harmony with it, since what has hitherto been regarded as Graeco-Italian civilization could have been Graeco-Celtic Italian instead. We know little about the earliest stage of Celtic culture. Linguistic investigation has not made as much progress as would warrant the inclusion of its findings in the early history of the people. 8

In a manner similar to Wickham and Cramer's investigations, it is clear that Bellovesus' line of march did not follow the route over the Cottian Alps (Mont Genevre) and through the territory of the Taurini, but rather lay over the Graian Alps (the Little St. Bernard Pass). The fluctuations caused by the revolution in Rome on the Roman-Latin confederacy are attested by the legend of the victory at Lake Regillus, a battle unusually vivid and varied in its accounts. 258 The Romans are said to have gained the upper hand over the Latins with the help of the Dioscuri, and even more so with the renewal of the perpetual alliance between Rome and Latium by Spurius Cassius in his second consulate in 261. The Roman Republic and the Latin Confederacy.

Even in the founding of federal fortresses or the so-called Latin colonies, most colonists were Romans. Although they were converted from Roman burgesses into members of an allied community, the newly planted township often retained a preponderant attachment to its original mother-city. The remodelling of the arrangements of the several communities belonging to the Latin confederacy, following the pattern of the consular constitution in Rome, can be characterised as an innovation. Although different communities may have independently abolished royalty, the similarity in the appellation of new annual kings in Roman and other Latin commonwealths, and the widespread adoption of the collegiate principle, suggest an external connection.

After the expulsion of the Tarquins from Rome, the Latin communities' arrangements were likely revised in line with the consular constitution. We have already mentioned that the Etruscans remained in possession of supremacy over Latium for only a short time, and that relations soon returned to their position during the regal period. However, it was not until more than a century after the expulsion of the kings from Rome that any real extension of Roman boundaries took place in this direction. The object was attained even more fully by the accession of the Hernici to the league of the Romans and Latins in 268, an accession that isolated the Volscians completely. This move provided the league with a bulwark against the Sabellian tribes dwelling on the south and east. Consequently, it is clear why this small people obtained the concession of full equality with the two others in counsel and in distribution of spoils.

The first notable success, after those mentioned earlier, was the foundation of Circeii in 361. As long as Antium and Tarracina were free, it could only have held communication with Latium by sea. Attempts were often made to occupy Antium. One was temporarily successful in 287, but the town regained its freedom in 295. It wasn't until after the Gallic conflagration that the Romans gained a decided superiority in the Antiate and Pomptine territories, following a thirteen-year violent war (365-377). Saticum, located near Antium, was inhabited by a Latin colony in 369. It is likely that Antium also became a Latin colony around the same time, as did Tarracina. The Pomptine territory was secured with the founding of the fortress Setia in 372, which was later strengthened in 375. The area was then divided into farm allotments and burgess districts starting from 371 onwards.

After this date, the Volscians still perhaps rose in revolt, but they waged no further wars against Rome. The main cause, however, of the internal breaking up of the league was the very subjugation of their common foe; forbearance ceased on one side, and devotedness on the other, from the time when they thought they had no longer need of each other. No concerted revolt took place, and Rome mastered the individual towns without much trouble.

Tusculum was forced to give up its political independence in 373 and join the burgess-union of Rome as a subject community. This allowed the town to retain its walls and limited self-administration, including magistrates and its own burgess-assembly. However, its burgesses as Romans did not have the right to elect or be elected — a unique instance where an entire burgess-body was incorporated into the Roman commonwealth as a dependent community. Closing of the Latin Confederation

In manifest connection with this crisis in relations between Rome and Latium, the Latin confederation closed around 370. It is unclear whether this event was a result or a cause of the revolt of Latium against Rome, as described earlier. Isolation of later Latin cities was a respected private right, but it did not apply to all places where Latin privileges were granted after 370. Instead, these cities were kept separate from the federal association and from each other, also as respected private rights. Each of them was allowed to have reciprocal trade and commerce with Rome, as well as marriage. However, no such reciprocal arrangements were permitted with other Latin communities.

If in after times two aediles, entrusted with the police-supervision of markets and highways and the administration of justice in connection therewith, appeared alongside the two praetors as necessary elements of the Latin magistracy, the institution of these urban police functionaries, which evidently took place at the same time and at the instigation of the leading power in all federal communities, could not have preceded the establishment of the curule aedileship in Rome, which occurred around 387. Domination of the Romans; Exasperation of the Latins. The collision between the Romans and the Samnites occurred after the fall of Veii and the conquest of the Pomptine territory. Rome, feeling powerful enough, tightened its grip on its hegemony and reduced the Latin cities to a position so dependent that they became completely subject. In 406, the Carthaginians, following a commercial treaty with Rome, pledged not to harm the Latins who were under Roman rule. The maritime towns of Ardea, Antium, Circeii, and Tarracina; if any one of the Latin towns were to fall away from the Roman alliance, the Phoenicians would be allowed to attack it. However, in the event of conquering it, they would be bound not to destroy it, but to hand it over to the Romans.

After the overthrow of the northern Volscians, no considerable people in the first instance opposed the Romans in the south. Their legions, unchecked, approached the Liris. The fall of Etruscan power at the end of the third century and the decline of Greek colonies in the fourth century created an opportunity for them to expand westward and southward. As a result, one Samnite host after another marched down to and crossed the southern Italian seas. While Dionysius wrested control from the fleets of Magna Graecia, one Greek city after another was occupied or destroyed by the Italians.

Even that city, however, had to constantly fight for its existence against the Lucanians, and was forced to seek alliances and mercenaries from the mother country of Greece. There was no single leading community, and thus no policy of conquest. While the conquest of the Veientine and Pomptine territories was a real enlargement of power for Rome, Samnium was weakened rather than strengthened by the rise of the Campanian cities and the Lucanian and Bruttian confederacies. Every swarm that had sought and found new settlements thereafter pursued its own path.

Relations between the Samnites and the Greeks were complex. The Samnite tribes occupied a vast territory that seemed disproportionate to their numbers, yet they displayed little inclination to fully assimilate into the region. The larger Greek cities, such as Tarentum, Thurii, Croton, Metapontum, Heraclea, Rhegium, and Neapolis, although weakened and often dependent, still existed. The Hellenes were tolerated in the open country and smaller towns, including Cumae, Posidonia, Laus, and Hipponium, which remained Greek cities even under Samnite rule. Nola, Nuceria, and Teanum, despite having a predominantly Samnite population, adopted Greek customs and a Greek civic system. In reality, the native cantonal form of government was no longer viable in this new context.

In this city, recruiting officers encountered a vast gathering of disheartened citizens. Meanwhile, Capua struggled to fend off the relentless Samnite attacks, prompting young Campanians to flock together in large numbers as self-appointed condottieri, particularly drawn to Sicily. These soldiers of fortune had a profound impact on Italy's destiny. Their influence is a defining aspect of Campanian life, comparable to the gladiatorial sports that originated or flourished in Capua. Gladiators made their appearance at banquets, with their numbers corresponding to the rank of the guests invited.

This degeneracy of the most important Samnite city, closely connected with Etruscan habits that lingered there, must have been fatal for the nation as a whole. Although the Campanian nobility knew how to combine chivalrous valour and high mental culture with deep moral corruption, it could never become what the Roman nobility was to the Latin people. The old national writing they had brought with them from the north was abandoned by the Lucanians and Bruttians, and exchanged for Greek. In Campania, the national alphabet developed itself under the influence of the Greek model into greater clarity and delicacy. The Samnite stock was dispersed and divided. In Samnium proper, the confederacy had preserved the manner and valor of their ancestors intact. However, this made them entirely disparate from the other Samnite tribes and towns.

The further course of events can no longer be ascertained in detail. We discover only that Rome and Samnium came to an agreement, whether after a campaign or without the intervention of war, which left Capua at the Romans' disposal, Teanum in the hands of the Samnites, and the upper Liris in those of the Volscians. The consent of the Samnites to ally with Rome is explained by the energetic exertions made by the Tarentines during this period, who sought to distance themselves from their Sabellian neighbors. To the terms of the treaty, several ancient Latin cities were admitted, including Laurentum, Tibur, and Praeneste. However, these cities were required to cede parts of their territories to Rome.

The principle of isolating communities from one another, which had already been established in regard to the places founded after 370, was thus extended to the entire Latin nation. The two important coastal towns, Antium and Tarracina, were occupied with Roman full-burgesses, restricted to a communal independence within narrow limits, following the model of Ostia. In contrast, previous burgesses had their landed property largely confiscated in favour of Roman colonists, who also adopted any retained land into the full burgess-union. Tusculum was a model for the establishment of Roman burgess-communities, including Lanuvium, Aricia, Momentum, and Pedum. The walls of Velitrae were demolished. Its senate was ejected en masse and deported to the interior of Roman Etruria. The town was likely made a dependent community with Caerite rights. Of the land acquired by the Romans, a portion—the estates, for example, of the senators of Velitrae—was distributed among Roman citizens. This distribution was connected to the establishment of two new tribes in 422.

The same treatment was measured out some years after to the Volscian Privernum. Its citizens, supported by Vitruvius Vaccus, a bold partisan belonging to Fundi, had the honour of fighting the last battle for the freedom of this region. The struggle ended with the storming of the town in 425 and the execution of Vaccus in a Roman prison. The Samnites' Inaction

As a matter of course, the Samnites could not behold the threatening progress of the Romans with satisfaction. They probably put obstacles in its way; however, they neglected to intercept the new career of conquest while there was still perhaps time to do so.

( I rearranged them for better readability.) They appear indeed in accordance with their treaty with Rome to have occupied and strongly garrisoned Teanum. In earlier times, the city had sought help against Samnium from Capua and Rome; however, in later struggles, it emerged as a bulwark of the Samnite power on the west.

The dictator of Caere was to be explained in accordance with Livy. ix The Roman Senate was forbidden from holding magistracies beyond the age of 43. All these Latin magistracies substantially coincide in reality, as well as in their names, with the arrangement established in Rome following the revolution. 9 The country of the Aequi encompasses not only the valley of the Anio above Tibur but also the territories of the later Latin colonies Carsioli, situated on the upper part of the Turano, and Alba, located on the Fucine lake. Additionally, it includes the district of the later municipium of the Aequiculi, which is essentially the remaining territory of the Aequi after the Romans subjugated them and assigned most of their land to Roman or Latin colonists, leaving municipal independence intact. 11

To all appearances, Velitrae, though situated in the plain, was originally Volscian, and thus became a Latin colony. In contrast, Cora, located on the Volscian mountains, was also originally Latin. 12 The Latin Confederation closed, and the list largely agrees with what can be determined about the state of the league shortly after Ardea's accession. If we assume this, then the list contains all the colonies sent out up to 372, including Satricum, which was destroyed in 377, and Velitrae, whose Latin rights were divested in 416. The only two missing are Suessa Pometia, beyond doubt destroyed before 372, and Signia, likely due to a discrepancy in Dionysius' text, where twenty-nine names are mentioned but one has been lost between --SEITINON-- and --SIGNINON--. In harmony with this view, there are no Latin colonies listed that were founded after 372. Additionally, all places like Ostia, Antemnae, and Alba, which were incorporated into the Roman community before 370, have been excluded from the list. In contrast, cities such as Tusculum, Lanuvium, and Velitrae, which were incorporated later, are still included in the list.

The narrative is filled with detailed accounts of battles, as well as intertwined stories such as that of the praetor of Setia, who broke his neck on the steps of the senate-house after being audacious enough to seek the consulship. Additionally, there are various anecdotes derived from the surname of Titus Manlius, and lengthy, though sometimes suspicious, archaeological digressions. Struggle of the Italians against Rome

Wars between the Sabellians and Tarentines. Archidamus, king of the Molossians, allied himself with the Tarentines to resist Roman expansion. While the Romans were engaged in battles along the Liris and Volturnus rivers, other conflicts arose in the south-eastern region of the peninsula. Alexander initially held the upper hand; he captured Heraclea from the Tarentines, re-established Thurii, and it appears that he attempted to rally the other Italian Greeks under his protection against the Tarentines. Concurrently, he sought to establish a peace between them and the Sabellian tribes.

There was a significant change during that brief period. While the Greeks and Samnites were at odds with each other, Rome had quickly risen to a dominant position that no single people in the peninsula could challenge, threatening to make them all subjects of her rule. The Coalition of Italians against Rome. After the fall of Etruscan power and the weakening of Greek republics, the Samnite confederacy emerged as the next most considerable power in Italy, alongside Rome, and was simultaneously the one most closely and immediately threatened by Roman encroachments.

The foremost place and the heaviest burden in the struggle for freedom and nationality fell to Italy. After Cumae and Capua had become Roman, nothing so naturally suggested itself to the Romans as the subjugation of the Greek city Neapolis, which ruled over the Greek islands in the bay—the only town not yet brought under Roman control. The Tarentines and Samnites, informed of the Roman scheme to obtain possession of the town, resolved to preempt them. The Tarentines, though somewhat tardy, were not far off from executing this plan, whereas the Samnites actively contributed a substantial garrison to it.

After it had lasted a while, the Campanian Greeks grew weary of the disturbance to their commerce and the foreign garrison; and the Romans, whose efforts were directed at maintaining states of the second and third rank through separate treaties, isolated them from the coalition forming soon after, hastened to offer them favourable terms as soon as the Greeks agreed to negotiate. These terms included full equality of rights, exemption from land service, equal alliance, and perpetual peace. After the Neapolitans had rid themselves of the garrison by stratagem, a treaty was concluded. The Sabellian towns south of the Volturnus, including Nola, Nuceria, Herculaneum, and Pompeii, allied with Samnium at the start of the war. However, their vulnerable position and Roman machinations soon led them to join forces with Rome. The Romans attempted to sway the optimate party in these towns through every means of persuasion and self-interest, drawing on the precedent set by Capua. As a result, these towns declared themselves either for Rome or neutral shortly after Neapolis fell. The people with true instinct were in favour of joining the Samnites; however, an alliance with the Samnites required peace with Tarentum. Many governing lords of Lucania were not willing to suspend their profitable pillaging expeditions. As a result, the Romans succeeded in concluding an alliance with Lucania—a valuable agreement that provided employment for the Tarentines, thereby leaving Rome's full power against Samnium.

If they wished to arrive in good time, they had to take the only route available: through the heart of the enemy's territory. From there, the Roman road was built along the Appian Way, continuing from Capua to Beneventum and then to Apulia. The Romans, who had entered the valley unopposed, found its outlet blocked by abattis and strongly fortified. On their return march, they discovered that the entrance was also blocked, while the crests of the surrounding mountains were occupied by Samnite forces. After the Roman generals agreed to these terms, they gave six hundred hostages chosen from the cavalry for execution. They also pledged their own word and that of all their staff-officers on oath to the same effect. The Roman army was dismissed uninjured but disgraced. The Samnite army, drunk with victory, could not resist the desire to subject their hated enemies to the disgraceful formality of laying down their arms and passing under the yoke.

If every person justly reckons it a point of honour to tear up treaties by force that are disgraceful, how could honour require patient adherence to a convention like the Caudine, to which an unfortunate general was morally compelled, while the sting of recent disgrace was keenly felt and the nation's vigour remained unimpaired? The Romans' victory did not bring about the peace that Samnium's enthusiasts had foolishly hoped for from Caudium. Instead, it led to war after war, fueled by exasperation on both sides. The opportunity for peace was lost due to a breach of solemn engagement, military honour being disgraced, and comrades being abandoned. The Roman officers who had surrendered were not received by the Samnites, partly because they were too generous to exact revenge on those unfortunate individuals, partly because doing so would have acknowledged the Roman claim that the agreement only applied to those who had sworn to it, and not to the entire Roman state.

The Romans formed a junction again under the walls of Luceria. The siege was prosecuted with greater zeal because the Roman equites were held captive there. The Apulians, particularly the Arpani, lent the Romans important assistance in the siege, especially by procuring supplies. The Samnites had fought valiantly for the relief of the town but were ultimately defeated. As a result, Luceria surrendered to the Romans. The Samnites allied with the Nucerians in 438, soon followed by the Nolans. The Sorani then drove out the Roman garrison from their upper Liris territory (439). Meanwhile, the Ausonians were preparing to revolt and threatened the strategic city of Cales. In Capua, a faction opposed to Rome was gaining momentum.

Sora was immediately attacked by the Romans. Fregellae, which after the catastrophe of Caudium had fallen into the hands of the party adverse to Rome, became their chief stronghold in the district on the Liris. In the eighth year following its occupation by the Samnites (441), Fregellae finally fell. Two hundred citizens, including the chief members of the national party, were taken to Rome and publicly beheaded in the Forum as an example and warning to patriots who were actively working against Rome everywhere. To secure and permanently command the conquered territory, several new fortresses were built in the region between 440-442. These included Luceria in Apulia, which received a permanent garrison of half a legion due to its isolated location; Pontiae (the Ponza islands), established to secure the Campanian waters; Saticula on the Campano-Samnite frontier, serving as a bulwark against Samnium; and Interamna near Monte Cassino, and Suessa Aurunca (Sessa) on the road from Rome to Capua.

Since the constitution of Tarentum, which had been adopted from the old Doric fashion and was originally strictly aristocratic, had undergone significant changes to become a complete democracy, a vibrant life of singular activity had emerged in that city, where mariners, fishermen, and artisans made up the majority of its inhabitants. After the Caudine catastrophe, when the Romans and Samnites stood opposed in Apulia, they had sent envoys to both sides to lay down their arms. It was no doubt a difficult and dangerous thing for Tarentum to be entangled in such a war; for the democratic development of the state had directed its energies entirely to the fleet. The fleet, resting upon the strong commercial marine of Tarentum, held the first rank among the maritime powers of Magna Graecia. However, the land force, on which they were dependent in this case, consisted mainly of hired soldiers and was sadly disorganized.

Unlike the Samnites, who had maintained an unequal struggle for eighteen years, three powerful Etruscan towns—Perusia, Cortona, and Arretium—agreed to a separate peace after their first defeat. This peace lasted for 300 months (444). The following year, when the Romans defeated the other Etruscans near Perusia, the Tarquinienses also agreed to a peace of 400 months (446). As a result, the other cities ceased their involvement in the conflict, and a temporary ceasefire took place throughout Etruria. The last campaigns in Samnium. While these events were taking place, the war had not been suspended in Samnium. Two Roman consular armies penetrated into Samnium. One, under Tiberius Minucius, followed by Marcus Fulvius after his fall, came from Campania through mountain passes. The other, under Lucius Postumius, marched from the Adriatic up to Biferno and entered Samnium. There, they united in front of Bovianum, the capital, securing a decisive victory. Statius Gellius, the Samnite general, was taken prisoner, and Bovianum was captured by storm.

He was not in a hurry to undertake a war from which he might expect more blows than booty, but preferred to make common cause with the Lucanians against Metapontum. He made himself comfortable in that city while discussing an expedition against Agathocles of Syracuse and liberating the Sicilian Greeks. Upon its conclusion, the Samnites made peace. When Rome began to take a more serious interest in the south-eastern peninsula, it sent a Roman force to collect contributions in the territory of the Sallentines. In 447 BC, this force was actually sent by order of the government to reconnoiter the area. Meanwhile, the Spartan condottiere, accompanied by his mercenaries, sailed to Corcyra Island, which provided an ideal base for pirate expeditions against Greece and Italy. Soon after 451, even a brief incursion by Cleonymus, who had landed in the Sallentine region, was repelled by the inhabitants with Roman assistance.

The first and main object was not so much to compel southern Italy as quickly as possible to recognize formally the Roman supremacy, but rather to supplement and complete the subjugation of central Italy. The way had been prepared by the military roads and fortresses already established in Campania and Apulia during the last war. This preparation enabled the separation of northern and southern Italians into two distinct groups, cut off from direct contact with each other militarily. The northern road, later known as the Flaminian, followed the line of the Tiber River. It led through Ocriculum, an ally of Rome, to Narnia, the ancient Umbrian fortress of Nequinum, which the Romans renamed when they established a military colony there in 455 BCE. The southern road, also known as the Via Valeriana, ran along the shores of Lake Fucine by way of the fortified cities of Carsioli and Alba.

The Celts in northern Italy were just beginning to stir again after a long period of suspended warfare. Several Etruscan communities there remained at odds with the Romans, and brief armistices were frequently followed by violent yet indecisive conflicts. The war thus began anew. While a second army fought in Etruria, the main Roman army marched through Samnium and forced the Lucanians to make peace and send hostages to Rome. The Tuscan states had entered into negotiations for peace with Rome.

The Romans captured strongholds in Samnium, weakening the Samnite faction's hold on Lucania. They were unable to stop the army led by Egnatius from departing. The rendezvous of the Italians was Umbria, towards which the roads converged from the Gallic, Etruscan, and Sabellian territories. The consuls also moved their main force towards Umbria, partly along the left bank and partly along the right bank of the Tiber. At the same time, the first reserve made a movement towards Etruria, in an attempt to recall any remaining Etruscan troops from the main scene of action for the defence of their homes.

The fugitive soldiers rallied. The bravest threw themselves after their leader into the hostile ranks, either to avenge him or to die with him. Just at the right moment, Consul Lucius Scipio, despatched by Rullianus, appeared with the Roman reserve on the imperiled left wing. Campania, which the Samnites had overrun during the Etruscan war, was quickly re-occupied by the Romans. The Romans conquered and stormed the strongholds where the Samnites had taken refuge.

Despite this great defeat, the Confederates continued to resist the enemy's increasing superiority for years, employing unparalleled perseverance in their mountain strongholds. They still managed to achieve several isolated advantages. At the close of the Samnite wars, the Roman domain expanded north to the Ciminian Forest, east to the Abruzzi and Adriatic, and south as far as Capua. The two advanced posts, Luceria and Venusia, were established along lines of communication with their opponents, effectively isolating them on every side. We must be cautious not to pursue the connection between events in the west and those in the east of Italy too far. 2
