CHAPTER VII Struggle between Pyrrhus and Rome, and Union of Italy

Relations between the East and West

After Rome had acquired undisputed mastery over the world, the Greeks would often annoy their Roman masters by asserting that Rome's greatness was due to the fever from which Alexander of Macedonia died at Babylon on June 11, 331 BCE. As it was not too agreeable for them to reflect on the actual past, they were fond of allowing their thoughts to dwell on what might have happened if the great king had turned his arms towards the west and contested Carthaginian supremacy by sea with his fleet, and Roman supremacy by land with his phalanxes.

The king's fate remained a mystery, as he died without influencing the western world. His unfulfilled ambitions were lost forever, entombed alongside him. In general, the raising of mercenaries was a matter of logistics. Sparta, although it regularly supplied captains to the Tarentines for their Italian wars, was as little involved in hostilities with the Italians as the German states were in the North American war of independence. During this conflict, the German states sold the services of their subjects to the Union's opponents. Alexander's work survived him, despite his early demise. Pyrrhus witnessed firsthand the ruin of his ambitions before death claimed him.

The struggle between phalanxes and cohorts, between a mercenary army and a militia, between military monarchy and senatorial government, between individual talent and national vigor — this struggle between Rome and Hellenism was first fought out in the battles between Pyrrhus and Roman generals. Though the defeated party often appealed anew to the arbitration of arms, every succeeding day of battle confirmed the decision. But while the Greeks were defeated in both battle and senate, their superiority was not diminished on every other field of competition; and these very struggles already foreshadowed that Rome's victory over the Hellenes would be distinct from her victories over Gauls and Phoenicians. It is only when the warrior's charm, like Aphrodite's, begins to take effect, that the true nature of battle is revealed – not in the heat of combat, but after the fight has ended. Character and Earlier History of Pyrrhus

King Pyrrhus was the son of Aeacides, ruler of the Molossians around Janina. After Alexander's death, he was spared as a kinsman and faithful vassal. As a result, he became entangled in Macedonian family politics. He lost his kingdom first and then his life (441).

His son, then six years old, was saved by Glaucias, ruler of the Illyrian Taulantii. During the conflicts over Macedonia's possession, he was restored to his hereditary principality by Demetrius Poliorcetes when still a boy (447). However, he lost it again after a few years due to the opposing party's influence (about 452), and began his military career as an exiled prince in the train of Macedonian generals. In the confusion that arose regarding the succession to the Macedonian throne after Cassander's death in 457, the Epirote extended his dominions step by step. He gained control over the regions on the Ambracian Gulf, including the important town of Ambracia, and the island of Corcyra. Furthermore, he even secured a part of the Macedonian territory. Despite being outnumbered, he successfully challenged King Demetrius, earning the admiration of the Macedonians themselves. For the free farmers of Macedonian soil, though diminished and impoverished, they did not share in the moral decay and valour decline caused by the Diadochi's government in Greece and Asia. Pyrrhus emerged as a suitable king, for he, like Alexander, maintained an open heart towards all human sympathies within his household and among his closest friends, avoiding the regal bearing of an Oriental sultan that was deeply detested by the Macedonians; and, similarly to Alexander, was widely regarded as the first-rate tactician of his era. Pyrrhus, unable to exercise sovereignty over Macedonia with the consent of the Macedonians, was too powerless and perhaps too high-spirited to force himself on the nation against its will. After reigning for seven months, he left the country to its native misgovernment and returned home to his loyal Epirots.

His views therefore turned overseas. The kings, who were quarreling over the possession of Macedonia, agreed on one thing: they were willing to aid their dangerous rival's voluntary departure. He knew this because his loyal war comrades would follow him wherever he went. The people who took part in the Roman campaign against the Samnites during the war effectively halted the actions of the Tarentines, contributing significantly to the outcome. As a result, the Romans granted them control over the Greek cities within their territory.

They had concluded a peace, and in concert with the Bruttians, subsequently subdued the cities in sequence. The Lucanians and Bruttians, having been cheated out of their share by more powerful allies, entered into negotiations with the opposition party among the Samnites and Tarentines to form a new Italian coalition. When the Romans sent an embassy to warn them, they detained the envoys in captivity and launched a new attack on Thurii (around 469). At the same time, they extended invitations not only to the Samnites and Tarentines but also to northern Italians, including the Etruscans, Umbrians, and Gauls, to join them in their struggle for freedom. While her enemies were negotiating alliances, settling treaties, and collecting mercenaries, Rome was at work.

In cases where a people primarily relied on livestock, mass expulsions were feasible. The expulsion of the Senones from Italy likely contributed to the gathering of Gallic forces that subsequently flooded the regions of the Danube, Macedonia, Greece, and Asia Minor. In the following year, the Boii again risked a general engagement near Populonia, but with no better luck. Frustrated, they abandoned their allies and negotiated a separate peace with the Romans in 472 BCE. The Gauls, the most formidable member of the league, were conquered in detail before the league was fully formed. This allowed Rome to focus its attention on Lower Italy, where a contest had not been carried out with much vigor during the years 469-470.

A furious mob attacked the Roman warships, which were suddenly assaulted in a pirate-like manner. The ships put up a fierce resistance but ultimately fell to the attackers. Five of the ships were captured, and their crews were either executed or sold into slavery. The Roman admiral was also killed in the battle. The Tarentines, seeking further retribution after their heroic feat, attacked Thurii, the Roman garrison, which surrendered in the winter of 472-473 due to surprise. The Tarentines then inflicted severe punishment on the Thurines, who had been abandoned by Tarentine policy to the Lucanians and were forced into surrender as a result of their defection from the Hellenic party to the barbarians. The barbarians, however, acted with a moderation that, considering their power and the provocation they had received, excited astonishment.

A Roman embassy arrived in Tarentum (473) and simultaneously presented these proposals. To bolster their argument, a Roman army led by Consul Lucius Aemilius marched into Samnium. The principal persons captured, however, were released without ransom. The hope was not abandoned that the pressure of war would give the aristocratic party ascendancy in the city, bringing about peace. Pyrrhus promised to stay in Italy only as long as was necessary. He likely intended for his own judgment to determine when he would be required there.

While the Tarentine envoys—the chiefs of the war party—were absent in Epirus, the state of feeling in the city, now hard-pressed by the Romans, underwent a change. The king arrived at the start of the year 474, following him. The citizens' initial joy at Milo's arrival, which freed them from guarding duties, gave way to reluctant obedience when faced with the king's demands. It was essential to intimidate those who had been neglecting their responsibilities, lest they face the threat of death.

The main force was destined for Lower Italy. Its departure was hastened as much as possible to reach Pyrrhus while still within the territory of Tarentum, thereby preventing him and his forces from forming an alliance with the Samnites and other south Italian levies that were in arms against Rome. Pyrrhus was not given to him. While the Campanians harbored a national hatred for the Romans, this animosity undoubtedly contributed to the outbreak of military insurrection. However, it was impossible for Pyrrhus, who had crossed the sea to shield and protect the Hellenes, to receive troops as allies who had slaughtered their Rhegine hosts in their own homes. The Romans succeeded by means of a weak corps that advanced along the Lucanian frontier and the garrison of Venusia, preventing the Lucanians and Samnites from uniting with Pyrrhus. Meanwhile, the main force – four legions, accompanied by a corresponding number of allied troops – marched against Pyrrhus under the consul Publius Laevinus. Pyrrhus, however, took command of his infantry and launched a new and more decisive battle.

Then Megacles, one of the best officers of the king, fell. Because he wore the king's armor on this hotly contested day, the army mistakenly believed the king had fallen for the second time. The ranks wavered, and Laevinus, already confident of victory, threw his entire cavalry onto the flank of the Greeks. The Roman loss was great. The victors found 7,000 Romans dead or wounded on the battlefield and took 2,000 as prisoners. The Romans reported their own losses, which likely included those who were carried off the field with injuries, at around 15,000 men. Venusia, though now entirely surrounded by enemies, remained steadfast in her allegiance to Rome.

The object of the mission had failed, and the dexterous diplomatist, instead of producing an effect by his oratorical art, was himself impressed by such manly earnestness after so severe a defeat. He declared at home that every citizen in that city seemed like a king; in truth, the courtier had gained a glimpse of a free people. Pyrrhus marched against Rome. After learning that the negotiations had been severed, he swiftly advanced into Campania, intending to cooperate with the Etruscans, disrupt Rome's alliances, and intimidate the city itself. At the herald's summons to enroll in the room of the fallen, young men immediately after the battle of Heraclea pressed forward in crowds to enlist. With the two newly formed legions and a corps withdrawn from Lucania, Laevinus, stronger than before, followed the march of the king.

No army crossed his path; but everywhere the towns of Latium closed their gates against him. Laevinus followed him from Campania with measured step. Meanwhile, the consul Tiberius Coruncanius, who had just concluded a seasonable peace with the Etruscans, brought up a second Roman army from the north. In Rome itself, the reserve was preparing for battle under the dictator Gnaeus Domitius Calvinus. Pyrrhus anticipated the Romans on the second day as they occupied the intersecting terrain. He thereby secured the plain without loss, allowing him to assemble his phalanx without disturbance. The gesture of the guard attached to the Roman chariots served as a signal for universal flight. However, this did not result in the loss of many lives, as the adjacent camp accepted the fleeing soldiers.

The Roman account of the battle alone mentions that during the principal engagement, an Arpanian corps detached from the Roman main force had attacked and set fire to the weakly-guarded Epirot camp. However, even if this were correct, it would not justify the Romans' assertion that the battle remained undecided. Pyrrhus felt that matters were as they stood: weary of his victories and despising his allies, he merely persisted because military honor demanded he not leave Italy until he had secured his clients from barbarian attack. After the death of Agathocles in 465, the Greeks of Sicily lost their leading power.

In the various Hellenic cities, demagogues and tyrants who were unable to govern effectively were constantly replacing one another. Meanwhile, the Carthaginians, the ancient rulers of the western region, expanded their territory without interference. After Agrigentum had surrendered to them, they believed that the time had come to take final steps towards the end they had long envisioned for centuries and to bring the entire island under their control. They then turned their attention to attacking Syracuse. The Carthaginian fleet sailed from there for Syracuse and blockaded the city by sea. At the same time, a strong Phoenician army began the siege by land.

The two consuls of 476, Gaius Fabricius Luscinus, and Quintus Aemilius Papus, both experienced generals, had begun the new campaign with vigour. Despite the Romans' history of defeat in this war, it was not they who were weary but rather the victors, who longed for peace. The struggle, however, was not terminated as quickly as might have been expected. This was largely due to its nature as a warfare of mountain skirmishes and sieges. Additionally, it is likely that the Romans' exhaustion played a role, as evidenced by the significant decrease in the burgess-roll from 473 to 456. In 477, a desultory war was waged in Samnium. An ill-conceived attack on fortified positions resulted in heavy Roman casualties. The conflict then shifted to southern Italy, where the Lucanians and Bruttians were defeated.

The consul succeeded at length by a stratagem in inducing the city to march out, and then he possessed himself of the undefended town. This success, however, advanced the main objective by little. Even apart from the fact that siege-warfare had been revolutionized by Philip of Macedonia and Demetrius Poliorcetes, the Romans faced a decided disadvantage when matched against an experienced Greek commandant. A strong fleet was necessary for such an enterprise. Although the Carthaginian treaty promised Roman support by sea, the affairs of Carthage in Sicily were not in a condition to enable her to grant that support.

It was evident that with the possession of Lilybaeum and the departure of the king, the Carthaginians' position in the island would remain nearly unchanged as it had been before Pyrrhus' landing. The Greek cities, left to themselves, were powerless, and the lost territory could be easily regained. Ignorance and shortsightedness in later times condemned this step, but it was really as necessary as it was, given the island's resources, an accomplishment easily achieved. The master of Ambracia, Tarentum, and Syracuse could not dispense with a naval force. He needed a fleet to conquer Lilybaeum, protect Tarentum, and attack Carthage at home as Agathocles, Regulus, and Scipio had done before or afterwards.

All cities refused to send money and troops to the absent king upon hearing the news. The brilliant state collapsed even more quickly than it had risen, largely due to the king's own actions, which eroded the loyalty and affection of his subjects, essential for any commonwealth. The people were unwilling to renounce their freedom, even temporarily, in order to preserve their national identity. He arrived in Tarentum, as reported, with an army of 20,000 infantrymen and 3,000 cavalry. The division of the army, intended to attack the Romans from the flank, became disoriented during its nighttime march through the woods and failed to arrive at the decisive moment. Following a fierce battle, the elephants ultimately decided the outcome in favor of the Romans. This time, however, they did so because the archers stationed to protect the camp had thrown them into confusion, causing them to attack their own people.

Although after the king's departure, the peace party regained control in the city. Milo, who commanded there on behalf of Pyrrhus, rejected their suggestions and allowed citizens who were favourable to Rome, who had built a separate fort for themselves in Tarentum's territory, to conclude peace with Rome as they pleased, without opening his gates. When Pyrrhus died, a Carthaginian fleet entered the harbour. Milo observed that the citizens were about to surrender the city to the Carthaginians. To prevent this, he handed over the citadel to Roman consul Lucius Papirius. By doing so, he secured his own safe passage and that of his troops. After the experiences of Philip before Perinthus and Byzantium, Demetrius before Rhodes, and Pyrrhus before Lilybaeum, it is doubtful whether the strategy of that period could compel the surrender of a well-fortified town, especially one that was freely accessible by sea. It is also unclear how different the outcome might have been had Tarentum become as significant to the Phoenicians in Italy as Lilybaeum was in Sicily.

What was done, however, could not be undone. In this instance, Rome vindicated her own rights and upheld the general cause of the Hellenes against the barbarians. On the other hand, Rhegium, despite the mutineers' long and obstinate resistance, was stormed by the Romans in 484. The survivors of the garrison were punished and executed in the public market at Rome, while the old inhabitants were recalled and, to the extent possible, reinstated in their possessions.

The construction of new fortresses and roads led to further conflicts with small tribes whose territory was subsequently reduced. The Picentes, a group of whom were relocated to Salernum, clashed with the Sallentines near Brundisium. Meanwhile, the Umbrian Sassinates, who appeared to occupy Ariminum's territory after the Senones' expulsion, also engaged in hostilities. During periods of crisis, such as the expulsion of kings, internal disturbances within the Romano-Latin confederacy, and unhappy wars with the Etruscans and Celts, Rome's focus on the Mediterranean region waned. As a result of Rome's increasing efforts to subjugate the Italian continent, its naval power stagnated. The Romans bound themselves, for no concessions from Tarentum are mentioned, to avoid navigating the waters east of the Lacinian peninsula. This stipulation barred them from the eastern part of the Mediterranean Sea. The Roman fortification of the coast was a disaster comparable to the defeat on the Allia. The Roman senate viewed it as such and took advantage of the favourable turn that Italian relations assumed soon after the conclusion of humiliating treaties with Carthage and Tarentum, using all available energy to improve its depressed maritime position.

After that, when the Greek cities in southern Italy, led by Neapolis, joined Rome's client list in 428, the warships each city pledged to supply as part of their contribution to the alliance with Rome, formed at least a new nucleus for a Roman fleet. Their relations with Carthage during the last Italian war have been previously explained, which reveals traces of such views. King Pyrrhus compelled the two great cities once more—to conclude an offensive alliance for the last time—but the lukewarmness and faithlessness of that alliance, the Carthaginians' attempts to establish themselves in Rhegium and Tarentum, and the Romans' immediate occupation of Brundisium after the war's termination, demonstrate how much their interests already came into collision.

The votive offering sent by Rome to Delphi, following its conquest of Veii, was preserved there in the treasury of the Massaliotes. After the capture of Rome by the Celts, a collection was held in Massilia for those affected by the fire. The city chest took the lead. In return, the Roman Senate granted commercial advantages to Massilian merchants. At the celebration of the games in the Forum, they were assigned a position of honor (-Graecostasis-) alongside the senators' platform. These towns retained the restricted self-administration, which under their earlier legal position belonged to them even after their admission into the Roman burgess-union. It was they more than the maritime colonies that furnished the model for the special commonwealths within the body of full Roman burgesses, thus shaping the Roman municipal organization over time.

The Roman comitia's legal position was governed by decrees, while the Roman praetor issued additional rules. However, these rules were likely derived from earlier arrangements. The Latins, however, were not those who had fought in the battles of Lake Regillus and Trifanum.

The Latin people, originating from the Roman citizenry and regarding themselves as equal to it in every aspect, had already begun to feel discontent with their subordinate federal rights and sought full equality. The senate had exerted itself to curtail Latin communities as much as possible, limiting their rights and privileges, with the aim of converting their position from that of allies to subjects. This was feasible only if it did not compromise the existing wall of partition between them and the non-Latin communities of Italy. We have already described the abolition of the League of Latin Communities itself, as well as the loss of their former complete equality of rights, and the most important political privileges they once held.

As long as Rome remained one of many urban communities in Italy, its status as the first may have been disputed. However, admission to the unrestricted Roman franchise was widely regarded as a significant advantage for the admitting community. The acquisition of this franchise by non-citizens was facilitated in every way and often imposed upon them as a penalty. The Roman community had become the sole sovereign, with all other communities serving as its subjects. This marked a significant shift in the state of affairs. The Roman community began to guard its franchise jealously, putting an end to the old full liberty of migration in the first instance. Despite this, statesmen of that period were wise enough to keep admission to the Roman franchise legally open at least to men of eminence and capacity within the highest class of subject communities.

The Latins were thus made to feel that Rome, after having subjugated Italy mainly through their aid, had now no longer needed them as it did before. Others, such as those with Neapolis, Nola, and Heraclea, were granted rights that were more comprehensive. In contrast, the Tarentine and Samnite treaties approximated to despotism. Dissolution of National Leagues-- Furnishing of Contingents

As a general rule, it may be taken for granted that not only the Latin and Hernican national confederations were legally dissolved or at least reduced to insignificance. The same applies to all such confederations subsisting in Italy. In particular, the Samnite and Lucanian leagues were also affected. In general, no Italian community was allowed the right of acquiring property or intermarriage with any other group. Even joint consultation and resolution with another community were not permitted.

The Latin communities, similar to this contingent, appear to have had their numbers definitively established. However, if necessary, the leading community could make an additional request. It was not without design that the supply of most costly war requisites devolved chiefly on Latin or non-Latin federate communities. The Greek cities largely maintained the war marine. In cavalry service, allies were required to furnish a proportion three times as numerous as Roman burgesses. However, in infantry, the principle remained that the contingent of allies should not exceed the burgess army for an extended period. The immediate circle of the ruling community was expanded, partly through the settlement of full burgesses and partly through the conferment of passive burgess rights, as far as was possible without completely decentralizing the Roman community, which was an urban entity intended to remain so.

Remodelling of Constitutions in Italian Communities

The remodelling of constitutions in each dependent community followed a similar pattern, where the constitution was adapted from the Roman model. A government consisting of wealthy and respectable families was established, which naturally opposed the multitude due to its material interests and desire for local power tied to Roman support. The Roman senate sided with the old burgesses, and when the city refused to submit, they destroyed the communal constitution of Volsinii, which was already in operation. They also razed the old capital of Etruria, demonstrating to the Italians a palpable proof of Rome's mastery. On that account, lastly, while the duty of bearing arms was partially devolved on the subjects, the ruling burgesses were by no means exempt from it. It is probable that the latter were proportionally far more numerous than the body of the allies. In that body, again, the Latins as a whole were likely to be subject to greater demands than the non-Latin allied communities.

The various nations inhabiting those lands were probably first led to feel and own their unity, partly through their common contrast to the Greeks, partly and mainly through their common resistance to the Celts. For although an Italian community may now and then have made common cause with the Celts against Rome, it employed the opportunity to recover independence. However, in the long run, sound national feeling necessarily prevailed. The Romans led the great national struggle on one hand, compelling the Etruscans, Latins, Sabellians, Apulians, and Hellenes within the described bounds to fight under their standards. On the other hand, this unity, previously undefined and latent, gained firm consolidation and recognition in state law. The name "Italia," originally confined to modern Calabria in Greek authors of the fifth century, such as Aristotle, was extended to the entire land. The Celtic cantons beyond the Apennines could not be considered part of the -togati-, despite possibly having been among Rome's clients.

The circumstance that Mago did not land at Ostia is to be explained not by any foresight, but simply by the fact that Latium was not threatened by Pyrrhus and therefore did not need Carthaginian aid; and the Carthaginians certainly fought for Rome in front of Rhegium. 6 It seems more likely that Demetrius Poliorcetes attempted to suppress piracy in the Tyrrhenian Sea, which he had never seen. It is not inconceivable that the Antiates, as Roman citizens, defied the prohibition for a time and continued their old trade in an underhanded manner. 20 The twelve most recent of these cities—Ariminum, Beneventum, Firmum, Aesernia, Brundisium, Spoletium, Cremona, Placentia, Copia, Valentia, Bononia, and Aquileia—are those referred to here; and since Ariminum was the oldest of these and the town for which this new organization was primarily established, it is likely that the name of the colonies was derived from Ariminum.

CHAPTER VIII: Law, Religion, Military System, Economic Condition, Nationality

The development of law within the Roman community during this period was marked by significant material innovations. One of the most important was the peculiar control that the community itself and its office-bearers began to exercise over the manners and habits of individual burgesses. The root of the matter lies in the authority of the magistrate to impose fines on property for offenses against public order. In the case of fines exceeding two sheep and thirty oxen, or those commuted to money under the decree of the people in 324, where cattle-fines were valued at more than 30 pounds, the decision passed soon after the expulsion of the kings by way of appeal into the hands of the community. Most of the merits of the Twelve Tables, which coincide with the laws of Solon and therefore may be considered as in substance innovations, include securing the right of free association and the autonomy of societies that originated under it. The enactment that forbade ploughing up boundary markers is another example. Additionally, the mitigation of the punishment for theft, so that a thief not caught in the act could release himself from the plaintiff's suit by paying double compensation, is also noteworthy.

In the law of marriage, civil marriage was permitted. Although the full marital power was associated with both a true civil and a true religious marriage, the permission for a connection rather than marriage, formed without that power, constituted an initial step towards relaxing the full power of the husband. If the accused heard their case (quaestio) and was condemned by the magistrate, they could appeal to the burgesses. The magistrate would then proceed with a further hearing (anquisitio) in their presence. After discussing the matter three times before the community, the decision would be confirmed or rejected by the burgesses at the fourth diet. The Roman criminal procedure was devoid of principle and had degenerated into a tool for political parties. This lack of excuse is particularly evident since the procedure, which was specifically designed for political crimes, was also applied to other serious offenses like murder and arson.

We are less able to trace the progress of the religious conceptions of the Romans. They generally adhered to the simple piety of their ancestors, keeping an equal distance from both superstition and unbelief. Two youths of superhuman size and beauty were said to have been seen fighting on the battlefield among the Romans. After the battle, they watered their steeds in the Roman Forum near the Fountain of Luturna, announcing the great victory. This legend bears a distinctly un-Roman stamp and is believed to have been modelled on the appearance of the Dioscuri as early as possible, similar down to its details, in the famous battle fought about a century earlier between the Crotoniates and Locrians at the River Sagras. The Delphic Apollo was consulted, as was usual among all peoples influenced by Greek culture. After special successes, such as the capture of Veii, he presented a tenth of the spoils. A temple was also built for him in the city.

In Etruria, while the nation stagnated and decayed in political nullity and indolent opulence, a theological monopoly of the nobility emerged, characterized by fatalism, wild mysticism, and mendicant prophecy. The Manipular Legion played a significant role in the military system. Its unique structure, which included an entrenchment of camp, cavalry, officers, and strict military discipline, was designed to enhance its effectiveness on the battlefield. Training and classes for soldiers were also crucial components of the Manipular Legion's strategy. The legion's value lay in its ability to adapt quickly to changing circumstances, making it a valuable asset in any military campaign. A complete revolution occurred during this epoch in the military system, marking a significant turning point in its development. The phalanx, as if it were a single mighty lance, was hurled at once upon the enemy. In contrast, the new Italian legion featured smaller units that existed within the phalanx system but were strategically separated in the order of battle.

The close square was divided, as we have said, into two equally strong halves. Each of these halves was separated in the direction of its depth into three divisions: the hastati, principes, and triarii. The latter were probably divided into four sections, each with a moderate depth. Along the front, the divisions were broken up into ten bands, or manipuli, leaving a noticeable gap between every two divisions and every two manipuli. It is essential that this became a matter of significance. Previously, both subalterns and staff officers were consistently appointed by the general. However, following 392, some of these positions were filled through election by the burgesses. The old military discipline remained unchanged. While the Hellenic phalanx had developed close formations and the Oriental squadrons of horse were armed with bows and light missile spears at a distance, the Roman combination of heavy javelins with swords produced similar results to those achieved in modern warfare by introducing bayonet-muskets. The volley of javelins prepared the way for the sword encounter, much like a volley of musketry now precedes a charge with the bayonet.

The further traditions associated with the Samnite and Pyrrhic wars are neither sufficiently accredited nor can they be duly arranged. However, it is probable that prolonged Samnite mountain warfare had a lasting influence on the individual development of the Roman soldier. The struggle against one of the first masters of war, belonging to Alexander's school, likely improved the technical features of the Roman military system. The revival of the Latin farmer class, which emerged in the 5th century, was largely driven by large land assignments and incorporations. Additionally, a decline in interest rates and an increase in the Roman population contributed to this phenomenon. This revival had a profound impact on the development of Roman power. It is unclear how far this method of husbandry had spread by this time. However, the history of the wars with Hannibal suggests that it was not yet widespread enough to be considered a standard practice or to have significantly impacted the Italian farming community.

Where it came into vogue, however, it destroyed the older clientele based on the precarium; just as the modern system of large farms was largely formed by the suppression of small holdings and the conversion of hides to fields. In Italy, excluding Greek cities and the Etruscan city of Popolonia, there was no coinage during the first three centuries of Rome. Initially, cattle were used as a medium of exchange, followed by copper by weight. The circumstances in central Italy led to the adoption of copper instead of silver as the metal for their coinage. The unit of coinage was primarily based on the previous unit of value, the copper pound. As a result, they cast their coins rather than stamping them, since no die would have been sufficient for pieces so large and heavy.

In transmarine commerce, the relations we previously described between Sicily and Latium, Etruria and Attica, the Adriatic and Tarentum, were essential for subsistence during this epoch. Although facts of this class are typically mentioned without a specific date, they have been grouped together to provide a general overview under the first period. The statements made apply equally to the present. As the striking of Etruscan silver money after an Attic standard and the penetration of Italian and especially Latin copper into Sicily attest to two former routes of trade, the equivalence between the silver money of Magna Graecia and the copper coinage of Picenum and Apulia forms, with numerous other indications, evidence of active trade between the Greeks of Lower Italy, particularly the Tarentines, and the east Italian seaboard. It was from the Hellenes that the Italians derived this custom of embellishing tombs. However, while the moderate means and fine discernment of the Greeks confined the practice to narrow limits, it was stretched in Italy by barbaric opulence and extravagance far beyond its original and proper bounds.

It is a significant circumstance that in Italy, extravagance is met only in the lands with a Hellenistic semi-culture. It is worth noting that Latium, despite being relatively close to Greece, as well as Etruria and Campania, maintained a strong connection with the Greeks, yet largely avoided sepulchral decorations. Although Rome probably preserved a certain outward simplicity longer than Capua and Volsinii, its commerce and trade—on which, along with agriculture, her prosperity from the beginning rested—must not be regarded as inconsiderable.

Development of Rome as a great city. While neither an opulent urban middle class nor a strictly closed body of capitalists emerged in Rome, it was constantly acquiring more and more the character of a great city. The increase in manumissions must have kept pace with the growth of Roman commercial and industrial activity. Following in the steps of Claudius, the Roman senate built a network of roads and fortresses around Italy, a formation already described,(39) which is essential for any military state from the Achaemenid dynasty to the creation of the road over the Simplon. Without it, no military hegemony can exist.

Statues of famous men from the past, including kings, priests, and heroes of a legendary period, were erected on the Capitol. These statues honored individuals who had made significant contributions to Rome's history. Honorary columns and monuments dedicated to notable figures such as Pythagoras and Alcibiades were also placed in prominent locations. The statues commemorated great leaders like those who conquered the Veientes, Latins, and Samnites, as well as envoys who lost their lives while carrying out their duties. Additionally, they recognized wealthy women who had donated their property to public causes. Monuments dedicated to the decemvirs, who were said to have interpreted Solon's laws, were also erected in the Forum. In 485, all local mints were restricted to issuing small coins. A general standard of currency applicable to all Italy was introduced, and the coining of currency was centralized in Rome. Capua alone retained its own silver coinage, struck in the name of Rome, but after a different standard. The historian does not need to delve into the intricate details of every event that occurred during a particular era. However, by focusing on isolated pieces of information from various sources, he can identify the most significant transformations that took place in Italy's national life during that time period.

The power of circumstance is greater than even the most powerful government. The Latin language and customs spread rapidly throughout Italy, soon dominating the region and undermining the other Italian nationalities. Apulia is the most remarkable region in Italy, which after the 5th century BC adopted Greek culture and language, gradually shedding its ancient Roman dialect. In all branches of Rome's development during this epoch, we find evidence of Greek influence: legislation, coinage, religion, and the formation of national legend. From the fifth century onwards, particularly after the conquest of Campania, the Greek impact on Roman life grew steadily.

We must therefore place their introduction in this period at the latest. The Romans erected statues of the wisest and bravest Greeks in the Roman Forum, under the command of Pythian Apollo during the Samnite Wars. This inference is suggested by what Livy says. As to the reorganization of the colony of Antium twenty years after it was founded, it is self-evident that while the Romans might impose on the inhabitants of Ostia the duty of settling all their lawsuits in Rome, the same course could not be followed with townships like Antium and Sena. 14

R i The author of the Licinian agrarian law likely owned extensive lands, but it is possible that this story was fabricated to explain the origin of his cognomen (Stolo). 27 The Roman community's Thanksgiving Festival, which had been organized previously under Greek influence, was initially an extraordinary event. The ludi maximior Romani, (1) acquired during this period, now has a longer duration and greater variety in its amusements. On other days, the multitude were probably left mainly to entertain themselves. Musicians, dancers, rope-walkers, jugglers, jesters, and others would not fail to make an appearance on the occasion, whether hired or not. Around 390, a significant change occurred, which likely connected with the extension of the festival that took place around the same time.

That matters might not be carried too far, however. A fixed sum of £200,000 was once appropriated from the exchequer for all festival expenses, and this amount remained unchanged until the time of the Punic Wars. While taking part in masked farces with stereotyped characters, which formed the usual native amusement, was looked upon as an innocent youthful frolic, appearing on a public stage for money without a mask was considered directly infamous. The singer and poet were placed on this level with the rope-dancer and harlequin. The police magistrates sat in judgment on the performance after its conclusion. On that occasion, wine flowed as abundantly for those who had acquitted themselves well as stripes fell to the lot of the bungler. However, it was only the urban magistrates who were legally entitled to inflict bodily chastisement and imprisonment on any actor at any time and place.

Of works composed during this period, posterity could point to nothing but a sort of Roman "Works and Days" - counsels of a farmer to his son. The Pythagorean poems of Appius Claudius were also notable, marking the first commencement of Roman poetry in the Hellenic style. The calendars were subsequently understood as Fasti, a term that exclusively applied to court days. This arrangement was likely adopted soon after the abolition of the monarchy, as an official record of the annual magistrates was a matter of pressing practical need to authenticate the order of succession in official documents.

The list of presidents we now possess is based on contemporary and credible records, which have been supplemented by family pedigrees of the nobility in collateral matters and genealogical statements. But besides this, the list of annual magistrates was adjusted to match the calendar years so that a pair of magistrates were assigned to each calendar year. Where the list did not suffice, intercalary years were inserted, which are denoted in the later (Varronian) table by the figures 379, 383, 421, 430, and 445. The idea naturally suggested itself that, along with the names of the magistrates, the most important events occurring under their magistracy might be noted. From such notices appended to the catalogue of magistrates, the Roman annals arose. This approach is similar to how the chronicles of the Middle Ages emerged from marginal notes attached to the table of Easter.

Before the eclipse of the sun was noticed under the 5th day of June in 351, it is likely that this refers to the eclipse on the 20th of June in 354. No solar eclipse was recorded from observation in the later chronicle of the city: its statements about the numbers of the census only become credible after the beginning of the fifth century. The cases of fines brought before the people and the prodigies expiated on behalf of the community appear to have been regularly introduced into the annals only after the second half of the fifth century began. To all appearance, the institution of an organized book of annals and the revision of the earlier list of magistrates, which we have just explained, took place in the first half of the fifth century. The revision involved making the list a year-calendar by inserting intercalary years chronologically necessary. Even after it became a practically recognized duty of the pontifex maximus to record year after year campaigns and colonizations, pestilences and famines, eclipses and portents, the deaths of priests and other men of note, new decrees of the people, and the results of the census, these records were still far removed from the character of real historical writings.

Such chronicles, however, did not exist merely in Rome. Every Latin city possessed its annals as well as its pontifices, as is clear from isolated notices relative to Ardea, Ameria, and Interamna on the Nar. From the collective mass of these city-chronicles, some result might perhaps have been attained similar to what has been accomplished for the earlier middle ages by comparing different monastic chronicles. The leading families, however, were meticulous about creating genealogical charts, which were crucial from a legal perspective. They also commissioned a family tree to be painted on the walls of the entrance hall as a lasting tribute. Above all, the sacred fig tree and other notable objects in the city were associated with a multitude of sextons' tales. These stories were similar to others that emerged over a thousand years later, also originating from the same site, which eventually gave rise to the Mirabilia Urbis.

The Aemilii, such as Calpurnii, Pinarii, and Pomponii, claimed descent from the four sons of Numa: Mamercus, Calpus, Pinus, and Pompo. Furthermore, they also traced their lineage to Mamercus, son of Pythagoras, who was renowned for his exceptional oratory skills. Despite the evident Hellenic influences present throughout these ancient records, it is reasonable to categorize them as national in nature, at least to some extent. This designation is justified by two primary factors: Rome's origins and its primary focus on forming connections between Rome and Latium rather than with Greece. It seldom follows a simple course. For Italy, the legends of Herakles and the Argonauts held less significance. However, Hecataeus, who was acquainted with the Pillars of Herakles after 257 BCE, is notable for his account of the Argo's journey from the Black Sea to the Atlantic Ocean, then to the Nile River, and finally back to the Mediterranean.

The new Trojan tale soon became intertwined with the earlier legend of Odysseus as it spread throughout Italy. It was he who represented Aeneas as first founding Lavinium with its shrine of the Trojan Penates, and then founding Rome. He must also have intertwined the Tyrian princess Dido with the legend of Aeneas, for it is through him that Dido became the founder of Carthage, and Rome and Carthage are said to have been founded in the same year.

The story could not have originated from Latium; it was likely the product of the old gossipmonger's imagination. It is true that these same gods might not be seen by anyone for centuries afterwards; however, Timaeus was one of the historians who were most fully informed about things beyond human knowledge. The origins of the Greek fable about Italian affairs, which first emerged in Sicily, are unclear.

While engaged in this historical composition, or what was known among the Hellenes, it focused on its own manner on prehistoric times of Italy, leaving the contemporary history of Italy largely untouched—a circumstance as significant as the sunken state of Hellenic history. It may be assumed that, from the start of this period, the more significant senate decrees were regularly written down, even if not those of the people. This was a matter of dispute during the early conflicts between the orders regarding their preservation. Opinions on table of formulae for actions while the mass of written legal documents grew, but the foundations of jurisprudence in the proper sense remained firm. This attempt, however, to give formal shape to a science that was yet to recognize itself, stood for a long time completely isolated.

The knowledge of law was a means of recommendation to the people and a way to attain offices of state. This can be easily understood, even if the story about Publius Sempronius Sophus, the first plebeian pontifex, and Tiberius Coruncanius, the first plebeian pontifex maximus, received their priestly honors due to their knowledge of law is likely a conjecture of posterity rather than a tradition. The real genesis of the Latin language was anterior to this period, and it is evident that even at its commencement, the Latin language was substantially an accomplished fact. This is supported by the fragments of the Twelve Tables, although they have been largely modified by their semi-oral tradition. Philology began at this epoch with the treatment of native languages using a rational method.

The rod played its part in both instruction and military discipline, as well as in policing. The instruction of this epoch cannot, however, have passed beyond the elementary stage. There was no material shade of difference, in a social respect, between the educated and the uneducated Romans. They wished to substitute for the previous calendar based on the old Attic calendar of the octaeteris. The contemporary calendar retained the lunar month of 29 1/2 days but assumed a solar year of 365 1/4 days instead of 368 3/4. As a result, without altering the length of the common year of 354 days, it intercalated, not as formerly 59 days every 4 years, but 90 days every 8 years. The Romans intended to modify the calendar while retaining its current structure. In the two intercalary years of a four-year cycle, they planned to shorten the two February months by seven days each. This change would result in February having twenty-two and twenty-one days, respectively, instead of the usual twenty-nine and twenty-eight days during non-intercalary years.

too we do not find phenomena of real originality; however, if the impress of borrowing, which Italian plastic art bears throughout, diminishes its artistic interest, a historical interest all the more lively gathers around it. On one hand, it preserves the most remarkable evidence of an international intercourse that has largely disappeared, and on the other hand, amidst the near-total loss of the history of non-Roman Italians, art is almost the sole surviving index of the living activity displayed by the different peoples of the peninsula. No trace has been preserved of any advance in architecture among the Etruscans during this period. We find among them neither any really new reception nor any original creation, except for the magnificent tombs. g The so-called Tomb of Porsena at Chiusi, as described by Varro, vividly recalls the grandeur of the Egyptian pyramids. The Arch in Latium, during the first century and a half of the republic, it is likely that they followed the established path. It has already been stated that the practice of art declined rather than increased with the introduction of the republic.

It is well established that at the dawn of Hellenic architecture, the Hellenes were not yet familiar with the arch. Consequently, they relied on a flat ceiling and a sloping roof for their temples. However, it is possible that the arch was a later innovation among the Hellenes, potentially derived from more advanced mechanical principles. The Greek tradition attributes its origin to the natural philosopher Democritus (c. 460-370 BCE). We have already observed that these, although only younger sisters of architecture, began to develop themselves at least in Etruria, even during the Roman regal period. However, their principal development in Etruria and Latium belongs to the present epoch, as is evident from the fact that in those districts wrested by the Celts and Samnites from the Etruscans in the fourth century, there is hardly a trace of Etruscan art. Etruscan artists created colossal bronze statues, some reaching heights of over 50 feet. In Volsinii, a site considered the Etruscan equivalent of Delphi, around 2,000 bronze statues were discovered dating back to approximately 489 BCE.

Anyone who has seen the rich and elegant gold decorations of the South Etruscan tombs will have no difficulty in believing the statement that Tyrrhenian gold cups were valued even in Attica. Etruria also practiced gem-engraving, albeit in varying forms. In Velitrae, a place where it is likely that only the Volsci retained their unique language and customs after leaving their homeland, terracotta figurines have been discovered. These artifacts showcase distinctive and characteristic artistic styles. It is a fact less known, but not less certain, that Latium, like Etruria, possessed artistic taste and practical skill despite being inferior in terms of art's abundance and massiveness.

The beautiful group of a she-wolf with twins is undoubtedly linked to similar Greek designs. However, its origin is certain: it was produced either in Rome or by Romans, if not in Rome itself. It's worth noting that this design first appeared on Roman silver coins minted for Campania. In the region of Calydon, it is believed that a unique type of figured earthenware was developed shortly after its establishment. This distinctive pottery featured the names of the craftsmen and the location where it was made. It was widely traded throughout the area, extending even to Etruria. This, however, does not exclude Greek masters who had also worked for Rome.

The rigid adherence to traditional styles in older branches of art was even more pronounced than the inferior handling of subsequent branches, which emerged after those earlier ones. Notably, the poor treatment of sculpture in stone and copper-casting in coins reveals how swiftly the Etruscan artistic spirit dissipated. The southernmost Etruscan cities, Veii, Caere, and Tarquinii, were considered the primitive and chief seats of Etruscan art in Roman tradition. In contrast, the northernmost town, Volaterrae, with its largest territory, stood apart from art. While a Greek semi-culture prevailed in South Etruria, Northern Etruria was characterized by a marked absence of culture. It does not appear that Latin art put forth its freshest vigour at Rome; rather, the Roman asssesand Roman denarii far surpassed in fineness and taste of workmanship by the Latin copper. The rare Latin silver coins and masterpieces of painting and design belong chiefly to Praeneste, Lanuvium, and Ardea.

This accords entirely with the realistic and sober spirit of the Roman Republic, as previously described—a spirit that cannot have been equally intense elsewhere in Latium. The reason why these precise numbers suggested themselves is apparent from the similar adjustment.

Our surviving inscriptions do not generally precede the age of rhotacism. Only isolated traces of the older -s sound remain, such as -honos-, -labos, and -honor-, alongside -labor-. Similarly, recent feminine -praenomina-, -Maio- (equivalent to -maios- or -maior-) and -Minoin epitaphs have been discovered at Praeneste. 27 Linguistic pedagogue and grammarian are related nearly as elementary teachers and teachers of languages with us; the latter designation belonged by earlier usage only to the teacher of Greek, not to a teacher of the native tongue. Litteratus is more recent, and it denotes not a schoolmaster but a man of culture. 28 The true centre of the former lay in the east, while that of the latter was in the region of the Mediterranean. Wars and migrations may have altered the line of demarcation, but they did not change the fact that a deep sense of diversity has always existed between the IndoGermanic peoples and the Syrian, Israelite, and Arabic nations. The noble and enduring creations in the field of intellect that originated from the Aramaean race do not primarily belong to the Phoenicians. Faith and knowledge in a certain sense were the especial property of the Aramaean nations. They first reached the Indo-Germans from the east. In contrast, Phoenician religion did not hold an independent rank among the Aramaean family, nor did their science and art.

During the most prosperous times of Sidon and Tyre, the land of the Phoenicians was a point of contention between the powers that ruled over the Euphrates and the Nile. It was sometimes under Assyrian control and sometimes under Egyptian rule. Although not the earliest settlement of the Phoenicians in this region, it was originally a dependency of the adjoining Utica, the oldest Phoenician town in Libya. However, it soon surpassed its neighbours and even its motherland due to the exceptional advantages of its location and the energetic efforts of its inhabitants. Carthage paid rent, even during times of prosperity, for the land occupied by the city to the native Berbers, a tribe known as the Maxyes or Maxitani. Despite being protected by the sea and desert from eastern attacks, Carthage acknowledged the supremacy of the great king and occasionally paid tribute to him in order to maintain its trade connections with Tyre and the East.

The Carthaginians undertook the task. After long and obstinate wars, they established a limit to the advance of the Cyrenaeans. As a result, Hellenism was unable to establish itself west of the desert of Tripoli. Carthage was no longer just a commercial center; it sought to dominate Libya and a portion of the Mediterranean. It had to do so because it couldn't avoid it. The Phoenician civilization flourished in Libya, much like the Greek civilization did in Asia Minor and Syria, following Alexander's conquests, though to a lesser extent.

The period in which this transformation of Carthage became the capital of Libya is unclear, as the change undoubtedly occurred gradually. The naval power of Carthage was significantly greater than that of Libya. During the same period, Carthage's maritime and colonial empire had expanded to become almost as powerful as Libya's. While the natives retreated into the mountainous interior of the island to escape bondage as agricultural serfs, similar to the Numidians in Africa who withdrew to the desert borders, Phoenician colonies were established at Caralis (Cagliari) and other strategic locations. The fertile coastal districts were then cultivated by Libyan farmers. Sicily. The straits of Messana and the eastern half of the island fell to the Greeks at an early period. However, the Phoenicians, aided by the Carthaginians, retained control over the smaller adjacent islands, including Aegates, Melita, Gaulos, and Cossyra. Notably, the settlement in Malta was particularly prosperous. The Phoenicians also maintained a strong presence on the west and north-west coast of Sicily, from which they could communicate with Africa via Motya and later through Lilybaeum, as well as with Sardinia via Panormus and Soluntum. After the further advance of the Greeks was checked, a state of comparative peace had prevailed in the island. Even the campaign undertaken by the Carthaginians at the instigation of the Persians against their Greek neighbours on the island did not permanently interrupt this peace. It persisted until the Attic expedition to Sicily (339-341).

When the Etruscan power fell, as it often did in such forced alliances, Carthage had barely mustered all its strength to prevent this collapse. Following the failure of Alcibiades' grand projects, Syracuse emerged as the undisputed leader of Greek naval power, not only seeking dominance over Sicily and lower Italy but also control over the Tyrrhenian and Adriatic seas. The peace, which was concluded after Mago's victory at Kronion in 371 BCE, and which subjected the Greek cities of Thermae (formerly Himera), Segesta, Heraclea Minoa, Selinus, and a portion of Agrigentum's territory up to the Halycus, was regarded by both powers as only a temporary arrangement. Both sides continually sought to dispossess each other of the island. The balance inclined more and more towards the Carthaginians, who were typically the aggressors. Although they did not maintain Roman steadfastness in pursuing their objective, they conducted their attack with greater method and energy than the Greek city, which was already rent and worn out by internal factions defending itself. The Carthaginian fleet dominated the entire western Mediterranean after that failure, with no rival. Their efforts to capture Syracuse, Rhegium, and Tarentum demonstrated the scope of their power and objectives.

Isocrates, a contemporary of Aristotle, states that the Carthaginians had an oligarchical government at home but a monarchical system in the field. This suggests that the office of the Carthaginian general could be described as a dictatorship by Roman writers. However, it is likely that the geronts (or gerusiasts) attached to him exercised significant control over his power, limiting its scope even after he relinquished his position. The combination however of several offices in one person was not unusual among the Carthaginians, and it is not therefore surprising that often the same person appeared as both general and sheriff. We know that holding the quaestorship granted admission into the body of judges, but the candidate still had to be elected by certain self-electing boards of five (Pentarchies). The judges, although presumably chosen by law for year-to-year terms, practically remained in office for a longer period or even for life. This is why they are usually called "senators" by the Greeks and Romans.

Citizens in Carthage did not have their attitudes restricted like those in Sparta. Instead, they were largely passive bystanders in state affairs. However, this lack of influence is not entirely accurate. In Carthaginian elections to the gerusia, a system of open corruption was prevalent. The people's input was sought only after the nomination of a general had been proposed by the gerusia. In other matters, the gerusia would consult the people if they could not reach an agreement or if it deemed necessary. After the termination of the Hannibalic war, it was even enacted, on the proposal of Hannibal, that no member of the council could hold office for two consecutive years. This introduced a complete democracy, which under existing circumstances was the only means of saving Carthage if there was still time to do so. Science and art in Carthage, as in Rome later, were largely influenced by Greek culture. However, this influence did not go unnoticed.

In Carthage, the wealthy elite held more power than the landowners. In contrast, Rome at this time saw the landed class dominate over the moneyed interests. While the farmers of Carthage were typically large landlords and slave-holders, in ancient Rome during this period, most citizens, known as burgesses, worked their own fields personally. The majority of Rome's population owned property, making them conservative. In contrast, the majority of Carthage's population lacked property, rendering them more open to the influence of wealth and the demands of democratic reformers. In Carthage, all the opulence characteristic of powerful commercial cities was already prevalent. The manner and law enforcement of Rome, however, retained at least an external semblance of the severity and frugality of ancient times.

The Carthaginian senate was based on a jealous control of administration by the government, representing exclusively the leading families. Its essence lay in mistrust of all above and below it, making it uncertain whether the people would follow its lead or fearful of potential usurpations by the magistrates. Rome had granted citizenship to one district after another, making it legally accessible to the Latin communities. In contrast, Carthage maintained its exclusivity from the start, refusing to allow dependent districts to harbor any hopes of eventual equality. Rome, as a rule, did not wholly take away independence from its subject communities. Instead, it imposed a fixed tribute on none. Carthage, however, despatched her overseers everywhere and loaded even the old-Phoenician cities with a heavy tribute. In contrast, her subject tribes were practically treated as state-slaves.

The Carthaginian government's well-calculated mildness and the Sicilian Greeks' unhappy distractions created an environment where a party truly friendly to the Phoenicians existed in Sicily. Even after the island fell under Roman rule, Philinus of Agrigentum wrote the history of the great war with a distinctly Phoenician spirit. At the beginning of the fifth century, Rome had sent to the field a burgess army equally strong. After the great expansions of the burgess domain during that century, the number of full burgesses capable of bearing arms must have at least doubled. It was the same with the cognate races of both communities. The Latins rendered service to the Romans equal to that provided by their own troops. In contrast, the Libyphoenicians were as little adapted for war as the Carthaginians, and they were even less desirous of it. As a result, they too disappeared from the armies. The towns bound to furnish contingents presumably redeemed their obligation by paying a sum of money. Not only did a dangerously long interval elapse, but when mercenaries were required, it took time to prepare them before the Roman militia could take the field. The main issue was that there was little to hold together the armies of Carthage except for military honour and personal gain, whereas the Romans were united by their shared heritage and loyalty to their common fatherland.

They bestowed great care on those elements that among the ancients represented modern artillery—the construction of machines, in which the Carthaginians regularly surpassed the Siceliots, and the use of elephants, after these had replaced earlier war-chariots. In the casemates of Carthage, there were stalls for 150 elephants. While Carthage made every effort to develop artificial means of attack and defense with its intellect and wealth, it was unable to adequately address the fundamental needs of a land army of its own and a symmachy based on self-sufficiency. When Agathocles had shown the way, a Roman general could follow the same course. In Italy, the entrance of an invading army marked the beginning of war, whereas in Libya, it led to a siege. Unless special accidents occurred, even the most courageous forces would eventually yield.

He was still a young man, born in 505, and now, therefore, in his twenty-ninth year. His life had already been one of manifold experience. As a young boy, he accompanied his father to the camp, where he quickly demonstrated his abilities. Although his youth had been spent in the camp, he possessed culture belonging to the Phoenicians of his time. After becoming a general, he made significant progress in Greek under the guidance of his confidant Sosilus of Sparta, enabling him to compose state papers in that language.

He then led the cavalry under his brother-in-law, Hasdrubal, and demonstrated exceptional personal bravery, as well as leadership skills. The voice of his comrades now called upon him—the young, tried general—to lead the chief command. He could now fulfill the plans devised by his father and his brother-in-law, who had lived and died for them. Every page of the history of this period attests his genius in strategy; and his gifts as a statesman were, after the peace with Rome, no less conspicuously displayed in his reform of the Carthaginian constitution. His unparalleled influence as a foreign exile was also evident in the cabinets of the eastern powers.

The rupture between Rome and Carthage was resolved by Hannibal immediately after his appointment in the spring of 216, not 534. The year 534 is actually the birth year of Charlemagne, a king of the Franks who ruled much of Western Europe during the Middle Ages. He sent a summary notification to Carthage that the Saguntines were making aggressive movements towards the Torboletes, subjects of Carthage, and he must therefore attack them. He then began the siege of a town in spring 535, which was allied with Rome, marking the start of war against Rome. All respectable men disapproved of an attack made without orders. There was talk of disavowing the daring officer.

Saguntum defended itself, much like Spanish towns do when defending themselves. If the Romans had shown even half the energy their clients were capable of, and hadn't wasted time during the eight-month siege on petty warfare with Illyrian brigands, they might have spared themselves the shame of failing to deliver on their promise of protection. After the destruction of Saguntum, a Roman embassy arrived at Carthage and demanded the surrender of the general and the gerusiasts present in the camp. The Roman spokesman interrupted an attempt at justification, gathered his robe, and declared that he carried both peace and war within it. He offered the gerusiasts a choice between them. The gerusiasts, mustering courage, replied that they left this decision to the Romans. When he proposed war, they accepted it in the spring of 536. To ensure the fidelity of the latter, the general, who knew the men with whom he had to deal, gave them a proof of his confidence by granting them a general leave of absence for the entire winter. Unlike Phoenician patriotism, which was narrow-minded and exclusive, he promised the Libyans on oath that they would be granted citizenship of Carthage if they returned to Africa victorious.

This mass of troops, however, was only intended for the expedition to Italy. The immediate territory of Carthage was comparatively weakly garrisoned because the capital provided sufficient resources in case of need. Similarly, a moderate number of infantry sufficed for the present in Spain, where new levies could be easily procured. In contrast, a large proportion of arms specifically African – horses and elephants – were retained there. The country, situated between the Ebro and the Pyrenees, remained free. Its tribes, allied with the Romans by nature, had also received assurances of swift assistance from Roman envoys, similar to those given to the Saguntines.

Catalonia may be reached by sea from Italy in a shorter time than from Cartagena. If the Romans had started like the Phoenicians did in April, after the formal declaration of war that took place, Hannibal might have encountered the Roman legions on the line of the Ebro. Although Hannibal encountered strong resistance on the Ebro, it came only from the natives. Given the circumstances, time was more valuable to him than the lives of his men. After several months, he overcame the opposition with a loss of one-fourth of his army and reached the Pyrenees. Fortunately for Hannibal, opposite to the point at which he intended to cross, there lay only a general levy of the Celts. The consul, accompanied by his army of 22,000 infantry and 2,000 horse, was still in Massilia, four days' march downstream.

A strong division under Hanno, son of Bomilcar, proceeded by forced marches up the stream until they reached a suitable point for crossing. They found it undefended and located two days' march above Avignon. On the morning of the fifth day after reaching the Rhone, and the third day following Hanno's departure, smoke signals from the detachment risen on the opposite bank reached Hannibal, signaling his long-awaited opportunity for crossing. Scipio held councils of war in Massilia regarding the best way to occupy the Rhone ferries. He was not swayed by the urgent messages sent by Celtic leaders.

This detachment found the entire enemy's army already transported to that bank, where they occupied themselves in bringing over the elephants which alone remained on the right bank of the stream. After it had warmly engaged some Carthaginian squadrons in the district of Avignon, merely for the purpose of enabling it to complete its reconnaissance—the first encounter between the Romans and Phoenicians in this war—it hastily returned to report at headquarters. The Romans had abandoned their allies for the third time due to pure negligence. This was not only a mistake but also a result of passing from mistaken slackness to mistaken haste. Furthermore, by continuing to attempt to rectify the situation without any prospect of success, they allowed the real means of correcting their error to slip away. Hannibal's Passage of the Alps

After crossing the Rhone, Hannibal addressed his troops in a great assembly and explained the purpose of his expedition. He brought forward Magilus, the Celtic chief from the valley of the Po, who had arrived via an interpreter to address the army. Meanwhile, Hannibal's march through the passes of the Alps proceeded without obstruction.

It was a fortunate coincidence, but not a motive influencing the decision of Hannibal, that the Celtic tribes allied with him in Italy inhabited the country up to the Little Starnberg. Bernard, while the route by Mont Genevre would have led him initially into the territory of the Taurini, who had been in a state of feud with the Insubres since ancient times. The reason for this movement was that the nearest route would have led them through an impracticable and poor mountain country, while the island was level and extremely fertile, and was separated by but a single mountain wall from the valley of the upper Isère. Hannibal learned the state of affairs early enough to avoid a surprise. He encamped at the foot of the pass until after sunset, when the Celts dispersed to their homes in the nearest town. Seizing the opportunity, he took control of the pass that night. This allowed him to reach the summit. However, on the extremely steep path leading down from the summit to Lake Bourget, his mules and horses slipped and fell. When he finally arrived on the plain after much loss, Hannibal immediately attacked the nearest town to chastise and terrorize the barbarians, and also to make up for his losses by recovering as many surrendered animals and horses as possible.

After a day's rest in the pleasant valley of Chambery, the army resumed its march up the Isère. When the troops finally reached the foot of the Alps, at the point where the path left the Isère, they followed the brook Reclus as it wound its way through a narrow and difficult defile up to the summit of the Saint. The militia of the Ceutrones appeared simultaneously on both flanks of the army: in the rear, and on the rocky outcrops that surrounded the pass to the right and left. Their aim was to intercept the train and baggage. He thwarted the enemy's plan, despite being unable to stop them from advancing up the mountain slopes in parallel with the infantry, and causing significant casualties by throwing or rolling down rocks.

But the confidence of the general remained ever the same; numerous stragglers rejoined the ranks. The friendly Gauls were near, and the watershed was reached. As the mountain-pilgrim gazed down the descending path, a sense of relief washed over them. With renewed courage, they prepared for their final and most daunting challenge: the downward march. The army was not significantly affected by the enemy, but the advanced season, which was already early September, caused similar troubles during the descent as it had during the ascent due to the attacks from the neighboring tribes. The horsemen, through diligent effort, created a path for horses and beasts of burden. It wasn't until after another three-day labor with regular breaks that the half-starved elephants could finally be led across. In this way, the whole army was reunited after a four-day delay. After another three days' march through the valley of Doria, which was ever widening and displaying greater fertility, the inhabitants of the region—the Salassi, clients of the Insubres—welcomed the Carthaginians as their allies and deliverers. The army arrived in the plain of Ivrea around mid-September, where exhausted troops were quartered in the villages to recover from their hardships through good nourishment and a fortnight's rest.

However, even in our fragmented and confused accounts (those of Fabius being the most important), Polybius wrote. iii 8; Appian If Hannibal had reached Italy nine days earlier, he would have arrived around the middle of September. This would leave space for the events that took place from then until the Battle of the Trebia at the end of December. iii and in particular for the transportation of the army destined for Africa from Lilybaeum to Placentia. Of the two Carthaginian squadrons bound for Italy and Sicily, one was dispersed by a storm, while the other failed to surprise Lilybaeum. The latter had then been defeated in a naval engagement off that port.

The summer passed away in the conquest of Melita, while pursuing the enemy's squadron. He had expected to find it on the Lipari islands, but instead, it made a descent near Vibo (Monteleone) and pillaged the Bruttian coast. Additionally, he sought information about a suitable spot for landing on the African coast. As a result, the army and fleet remained at Lilybaeum until orders from the senate arrived, instructing them to return with all possible speed to defend their homes. While the two great Roman armies, each equal in number to Hannibal's, remained at a great distance from the valley of the Po, the Romans were unprepared for an attack in that region. The praetor Lucius Manlius, who held the chief command at Ariminum, hastened with his single legion to relieve the blockaded colonists. However, he was surprised in the woods and had no course of action left but to establish himself on a hill and submit to a siege by the Boii until a second legion sent from Rome under the praetor Lucius Atilius arrived to relieve the army and town, and temporarily suppress the Gaulish insurrection.

The Numidian horsemen sent in pursuit of the abandoned camp, but they lost valuable time plundering and setting fire to it. The position taken by Scipio and the Cenomani threat to Insubrian borders prevented the main body of Gallic insurgents from joining the enemy directly. This allowed the second Roman army, which had arrived at Ariminum from Lilybaeum, to reach Placentia without significant obstacles, uniting with the Po Valley army in the process. It was clear as day that it was now December, and under the course proposed, Rome might gain victory, but not with Consul Tiberius Sempronius at its helm. This was due to his sole command following Scipio's injury, whose term of office was set to expire in a few months.

A general engagement came soon after on a raw and rainy day. The Roman infantry proved itself worthy of its name: at the beginning of the battle, it fought with decided superiority against the enemy's infantry. Even when the repulse of the Roman horse allowed the enemy's cavalry and light-armed troops to turn their attacks against the Roman infantry, the latter, although ceasing to advance, obstinately maintained its ground. The wings of the Roman army and the rear ranks of its centre were broken up and scattered by this attack. Meanwhile, the first division, consisting of 10,000 men, formed a compact array that broke through the Carthaginian line. It then made a passage for itself obliquely through the midst of the enemy, inflicting great loss on the opposing infantry, particularly on the Gallic insurgents.

Although the loss in the battle fell chiefly on the Celtic insurgents, many veteran soldiers of Hannibal died later from illnesses caused by that harsh and wet winter. One elephant survived. He led a large force of Gallic rebels, comprising over 60,000 infantrymen and 4,000 cavalrymen from Celtic tribes. Hannibal's mode of carrying on the war was necessary because it was the only element that could counteract the numerous disadvantages he faced. His military genius was crucial, but it only shone in its full light when he consistently outmaneuvered his opponents with unexpected combinations. He was undone if the war became static.

That was the aim dictated to him by right policy, because, though a mighty conqueror in battle, he saw clearly that on each occasion he vanquished generals rather than cities, and that after each new battle, the Romans remained just as superior to the Carthaginians as he himself was personally superior to Roman commanders. Hannibal camped at Fiesole, while Gaius Flaminius waited at Arezzo for the roads to become passable, so he could block them. After the Roman defensive position had thus been turned, the best course for the consul would have been to wait until the second army arrived, as Hannibal could no longer be faced in open battle.

He himself, however, judged otherwise. He was a political party leader who gained distinction through his efforts to limit the senate's power.
