Indignant at the government due to aristocratic intrigues against him during his consulship, he became scornfully defiant of tradition and custom. His blind love for the common people equaled bitter hatred for the nobles' party. Additionally, he was driven by a fixed idea that he was a military genius. The Carthaginian loss of 1,500 men again fell mainly on the Gauls. Immediately after the battle on the Trasimene lake, the cavalry of the army of Ariminum under Gaius Centenius, 4000 strong, which Gnaeus Servilius had sent forward for temporary support, was surrounded by the Phoenician army. The cavalry was partly slain and partly made prisoners.

Quintus Fabius, the dictator, united the two additional legions formed in Rome with the army of Ariminum. As Hannibal marched toward the Roman fortress of Luceria, he encountered the Roman standards on his right flank near Aecae. His predecessor's course was followed by their leader, however. During the march of the Carthaginian army, the dictator followed along the heights. His soldiers were condemned to watch helplessly as the Numidian cavalry plundered their faithful allies across the land, while villages burned throughout the plain.

When Hannibal began his retreat, Fabius intercepted his route near Capua. He strongly garrisoned the town on the left bank of the Volturnus and occupied the heights on the right bank with his main army. A division of 4,000 men camped on the road itself, leading along by the river. Hannibal ordered his lightly armed troops to climb the heights that rose immediately alongside the road. He then instructed them to drive before them a number of oxen with lit faggots on their horns, making it seem as if the Carthaginian army was marching off into the night by torchlight. Hannibal then marched unopposed in a north-easterly direction. He took a widely circuitous route after traversing and laying under contribution the lands of the Hirpinians, Campanians, Samnites, Paelignians, and Frentanians without resistance. With rich booty and a full chest, he arrived once more in the region of Luceria just as the harvest was about to begin.

Hannibal led two-thirds of the army away daily to gather supplies, while he kept the remaining troops in place to defend the camp and the detachments that were sent out. The Roman army, having recently been divided into two separate corps, was again split. Furthermore, the two sections were assigned to leaders whose military strategies were starkly at odds with one another. The senate, therefore, sought to nominate suitable consuls. However, this attempt only served to fuel suspicion and obstinacy.

The Battle of Cannae took place while preparations for the next campaign were underway in Rome. The war had resumed in Apulia. Hannibal wished nothing so much as a battle. Not merely for the general reasons previously discussed, but especially because the wide Apulian plain enabled him to fully exploit the superiority of his cavalry. Additionally, providing supplies for his large army would soon become extremely difficult due to the proximity of an enemy twice as strong and fortified by a chain of fortresses. The leaders of the Roman forces had also made up their minds on the general question of giving battle. They approached the enemy with that view. However, the more sagacious of them saw Hannibal's position and were disposed accordingly to wait in the first instance and station themselves in the vicinity of the enemy, compelling him to retire and accept battle on a less favourable ground.

The democratic consul disapproved of such military pedantry. He believed that men should take the field to engage in battle, not simply stand guard. Accordingly, he ordered his troops to attack the enemy wherever and whenever they were found. A division of 10,000 men was left behind in the principal Roman camp. They were tasked with capturing the Carthaginian encampment during the conflict and intercepting the enemy's army as it retreated across the river. Hannibal positioned his infantry in the shape of a crescent, with the Celtic and Iberian troops in their traditional armor forming the front line. The Libyans, armed in the Roman style, took up positions on either flank, creating a defensive perimeter around the center.

After a brief skirmish between the light troops, the entire line was soon engaged. Meanwhile, on the right wing, fortune had turned against the Romans. After a brave resistance, the Roman horse gave way, and those who were not cut down were chased up the river and scattered across the plain; Paullus, wounded, rode to the center to turn or, if not, share the fate of the legions.

Hasdrubal, having defeated the wing of Paullus, regrouped his cavalry and led it to attack the enemy's center from behind, targeting the wing of Varro. The support that seemed the most secure, namely the sending of reinforcements from Spain, had been thwarted by the boldness and firmness of the Roman general, Gnaeus Scipio. After Hannibal's passage of the Rhone, Scipio had sailed for Emporiae. He became master of the coastline between the Pyrenees and the Ebro first, and then, after conquering Hanno, he also controlled the interior.

In the following year (537), he completely defeated the Carthaginian fleet at the mouth of the Ebro. After his brother Publius, who was the brave defender of the valley of the Po, joined him with a reinforcement of 8,000 men, he crossed the Ebro and advanced as far as Saguntum. Hasdrubal had indeed attempted to cross the Pyrenees in the following year (538), after receiving reinforcements from Africa, as ordered by his brother. However, the Scipios opposed his passage of the Ebro River and defeated him nearly simultaneously with Hannibal's victory at Cannae. Now, therefore, support for Hannibal could no longer be expected from Spain.

It was not until after the Battle of Cannae that Demetrius of Pharos presented his proposal to Philip, who was finally willing to listen. The proposal involved ceding Macedonia's Illyrian possessions to the kingdom, a move that had been necessary in the first place to wrest them from the Romans. It was only then that the court of Pella came to terms with Carthage. Alliance between Carthage and Syracuse in Sicily was formed under King Hiero. During years of peace, he maintained a policy of neutrality, as far as it was safe to do so. He had shown a disposition to accommodate the Carthaginians during perilous crises following the peace with Rome, particularly by sending supplies of corn. In the autumn of 538, death removed the old man after a reign of 54 years.

The position of the Roman fleet at Lilybaeum, already beset by a second Carthaginian squadron stationed near the Aegates, became critical. Meanwhile, the force in readiness at Rome for embarkation to Sicily had to be redirected due to the defeat at Cannae. Capua and most of the communities in Lower Italy passed over to Hannibal. Above all came the decisive fact that now at length the fabric of the Roman confederacy began to be unhinged, after it had survived unshaken the shocks of two severe years of war. He found himself obliged to have one of the leaders of the aristocratic party in Capua, Decius Magius. Even after the Phoenicians entered, he obstinately contended for a Roman alliance. However, this led to his own downfall as he was seized and conveyed to Carthage. This act served as a demonstration of the small value of liberty and sovereignty that had been recently assured to the Campanians by the Carthaginian general.

The Phoenicians and Bruttians united against Croton, forcing it to surrender. The city's inhabitants were taken to Locri, while Bruttian settlers established a key naval base there. It seems there is nothing provided for me to correct. To be perceived as having done so, and to have actually done it, without any justifiable complaints or reservations, is the greatest glory that can be bestowed upon the Roman Senate.

The senate maintained its firm stance, while messengers from all sides rushed to Rome to report on the loss of battles, the defection of allies, and the capture of key positions. They also requested reinforcements for the Po Valley and Sicily at a time when Italy had been largely abandoned and Rome was almost defenseless. Appius Claudius and Publius Scipio the Younger, two skilled military tribunes, gathered the remaining survivors from the battlefield at Canusium. If Placentia lay on the right bank of the Trebia where it meets the Po, and if the battle was fought on the left bank, with the Roman encampment pitched on the right—both points disputed but undeniable—the Roman soldiers must have crossed the Trebia to capture Placentia and gain access to the camp. On the other hand, the passage near Placentia was accomplished after the pursuit had slackened. The corps was several miles distant from the field of battle and had arrived within reach of a Roman fortress. It is possible that a bridge led over the Trebia at that point, and the pontoon on the other bank was occupied by the garrison of Placentia. It is evident that the first passage was just as difficult as the second was easy. Polybius, a military judge, remarks on the corps of 10,000, stating that in close columns, they made their way to Placentia.

xxii At the beginning of May, she had to enter into it. Northern Italy
CHAPTER VI
The War under Hannibal from Cannae to Zama
The Crisis

Hannibal's aim in his expedition to Italy was to break up the Italian confederacy. After three campaigns, that goal had been achieved as far as it was possible to attain. They joined the conqueror of Rome after the cause seemed fairly lost. However, they felt that the question had shifted from liberty to simply exchanging one Italian leader for a Phoenician master. It was not enthusiasm, but desperation, that led the Sabellian communities into the arms of the victor.

With true instinct, after the disastrous day of Cannae, the Senate and the people turned their attention to this brave and experienced officer, and immediately entrusted him with the supreme command. Although far above fifty, he still radiated the fervor of a youthful soldier. Just a few years prior, he had, as a general, defeated the mounted commander of the enemy—the first and only Roman consul to accomplish such a feat of arms. His life was consecrated to two divinities: Honour and Valour. He erected a splendid double temple at the Capene Gate in their honour. While the merit of rescuing Rome from this extreme danger belonged to the Roman citizens collectively, it was pre-eminent among them to the senate. Yet, no single man contributed more to the success of the common enterprise than Marcus Marcellus.

The council and citizens of Carthage were not comparable to the senate and people of Rome. The threat to Carthage, following Regulus' first campaign, was far more pressing than that faced by Rome after Cannae. Despite this, Carthage had made a stand and emerged victorious. Instead of sacrificing practicable and important successes for the sake of such empty demonstrations, Hannibal immediately proceeded to Capua before the Romans could throw in a garrison. By his advance, he had induced this second city of Italy, which had hesitated for so long, to join him. With the two legions saved from Cannae, Marcus Marcellus marched to Teanum Sidicinum, where he was joined by troops available from Rome and Ostia. He advanced, while the dictator Marcus Junius slowly followed with the main army, which had been hastily formed, as far as the Volturnus at Casilinum, with the aim of saving Capua. In Campania, Nuceria, Acerrae, and Casilinum, after an obstinate siege that lasted into the following year (539), Hannibal also conquered the key city of Volturnus. The severest punishments were inflicted on the senates of these towns for their allegiance to Rome.

The Romans in Campania not only maintained what they had, but also recovered Compulteria and other smaller towns. Meanwhile, loud complaints were heard from the eastern allies of Hannibal. Tiberius Gracchus led his forces, while the two other Roman armies in Campania made preparations to launch an attack on Capua the following spring. After the initial victory at Cannae had faded, the peace party in Carthage, always eager to sacrifice its country for political gain, found allies in the shortsightedness and laziness of its citizens. When the general appealed for more decisive support, they responded with a half-hearted, half-malicious reply: "We don't need your help since you're already the victor." This response did little to assist Rome, much like the Roman Senate itself. If

The position of Hasdrubal in Spain after the Battle of the Ebro was critical. The supplies of money and men that the victory of Cannae had prompted the Carthaginian citizens to provide were largely spent on Spain, with little improvement in the region's situation. The ratification of the alliance between Macedonia and Rome was delayed, primarily due to the capture of Macedonian envoys on their return journey by Roman warships. The war had come to a stand in Italy. Nothing was done by Carthage to hasten the movement of new armies or ships towards the site of conflict. It had formed no part of Hannibal's original plan to excite a war on the island. However, partly through accident and chiefly due to the boyish vanity of the imprudent Hieronymus, a land war had broken out there. This was likely because Hannibal had not planned it, and as a result, the Carthaginian council took great interest in it.

After Hieronymus was killed at the end of 539, it seemed highly uncertain whether the citizens would continue with the policy he had pursued. But amidst the dreadful confusion in the city, which after Hieronymus' death was alternately agitated by efforts to re-establish ancient freedom for the people and by the coups de main of numerous pretenders to the vacant throne, while the captains of foreign mercenary troops were the real masters of the place, Hannibal's emissaries found opportunity to frustrate the projects of peace. They stirred up the multitude in the name of liberty. Descriptions of the fearful punishment the Romans were said to have inflicted on the Leontines, who had just been re-conquered, awakened doubts among the better portion of the citizens about whether it was too late to restore their old relations with Rome. Meanwhile, numerous Roman deserters among the mercenaries, mostly runaway rowers from the fleet, were easily persuaded that a peace between the citizens and Rome would be their death warrant.

No course was left to the consul except to undertake a siege. However, the skilled defense, led by the Syracusan engineer Archimedes, a renowned mathematician, distinguished himself particularly well. This compelled the Romans, who had besieged the city for eight months, to convert the siege into a blockade by both sea and land. Carthaginian Expedition to Sicily

The Carthaginian troops destroyed the conquest of Syracuse. Meanwhile, Carthage, which had previously only supported the Syracusans with its fleets, dispatched a strong land army under Himilco to Sicily after learning of their renewed uprising against the Romans. The army landed at Heraclea Minoa without interruption and immediately occupied the important town of Agrigentum. With the help of some reinforcements that arrived from Italy, he maintained his position on the island and continued the blockade of Syracuse.

In 542, the besiegers of Syracuse successfully scaled a portion of the extensive outer walls during a festival, allowing them to penetrate into the suburbs that stretched from the island and the city proper on the shore to the interior. The fortress of Euryalus, located at the westernmost edge of the suburbs, safeguarded both this location and the main road connecting it to Syracuse. It was subsequently severed and soon after fell. Fate turned the city's special defense against it, becoming a means of its own destruction. The Roman army, led by Marcellus, camped in the suburbs and suffered little damage. In contrast, the Phoenician and Syracusan camps were ravaged by fevers.

Marcellus not only tarnished his military honor by allowing a general looting of the wealthy mercantile city, during which Archimedes and many other citizens were killed. The Roman Senate ignored the complaints presented by the Syracusans regarding Marcellus' celebrated victories, refusing to return stolen property to individuals or restore freedom to the city. Syracuse and the towns previously dependent on it were classified among the communities tributary to Rome. Tauromenium and Neetum alone received the same privileges as Messana, while Leontini's territory became a Roman domain, and its former owners became Roman lessees. As a result, no Syracusan citizen was allowed to reside in the "island," which was the part of the city that commanded the harbour. The relations that prevailed between Hannibal and the Carthaginian council were similarly repeated on a smaller scale.

After the whole of Sicily was thus subdued, the Romans restored tranquility and order to the island. Antiochus the Great, a natural ally of Philip, was fortunate in obtaining peace from the indolent Philopator after the decisive victory of the Egyptians at Raphia in 537. Although Philip was superior to each of his opponents, he repelled their attacks on all sides with great energy and personal valor. However, he wasted much of his time and strength on a futile defense.

He decided to build 100 ships of war only towards the end of the war, a decision he should have made from the start. However, it appears that the order was never fully carried out. The Aetolians, to whom their Roman allies attached great importance, suffered greatly from the war. After the petty king of the Athamanes was won over by Philip, the interior of Aetolia was left vulnerable to Macedonian raids. The Aetolians were no longer free. If they concluded peace with Philip at their own risk, it would be unfavorable. The Romans were not inclined to make peace, especially after the favorable turn of events in Spain and Italy, which made war less likely. The Aetolians bore the burden of a war that was largely fought with just a few ships. The Aetolians ultimately decided to heed the advice of the mediating cities. Despite the Romans' efforts to thwart this plan, a peace treaty was negotiated during the winter of 549.

The war with Philip could not have been carried on by the Romans without considerable exertions of their own after the withdrawal of the Aetolians. It appeared more convenient to them to terminate it also by a peace, whereby the state of things before the war was substantially restored, and Rome retained all her possessions on the coast of Epirus, except for the worthless territory of the Atintanes. The Romans enjoyed superior status generally. This was due in part to their initial arrival in Spain as liberators of the land from Phoenician rule, and also because of the fortunate choice of their leaders and the stronger core of reliable troops they brought with them. It is hardly possible, however, with the very imperfect and chronologically confused accounts that have been passed down to us, to gain a satisfactory understanding of a war conducted in such a manner.

The barrier of the Pyrenees was steadfastly maintained, and the attempt to re-establish communication by land between the commander-in-chief of the enemy and his headquarters was sternly repulsed. A Spanish New Rome had been created, modeled after the Spanish New Carthage, through Tarraco's comprehensive fortifications and harbour works. In 539, Roman armies had already fought successfully in Andalusia. Their expedition there was repeated in the following year, 540, with even greater success. While the Scipios nearly dislodged the Carthaginians from Spain, they knew how to raise a formidable enemy in western Africa himself, in the person of powerful West African prince Syphax, ruler of the modern provinces of Oran and Algiers. He formed alliances with the Romans (around 541). The Scipios, who during the absence of the enemy's general, had to plunder and gain partisans in the Carthaginian territory, found themselves unexpectedly assailed by forces so superior that they were forced to retreat behind the Ebro or call upon the Spaniards.

While Gnaeus, with his corps containing a third of the Roman and all the Spanish troops, lay encamped opposite Hasdrubal Barcas, the latter had no difficulty in inducing the Spaniards in the Roman army to withdraw by offering them a sum of money. This perhaps did not seem a breach of loyalty to them, as they were motivated by their own interests rather than any sense of duty to their paymaster. The second Roman corps, led by Publius, was fiercely attacked by the combined forces of Hasdrubal, son of Gisgo, and Mago, as well as Massinissa's cavalry units. After Publius had fallen, Gnaeus, who was slowly retreating, had with great difficulty defended himself against a single Carthaginian army. However, he soon found himself suddenly assailed on all sides by three enemy forces, and his retreat was cut off by the Numidian cavalry.

The Carthaginians who had crossed the river were driven back, and the line along the Ebro River was maintained until Rome could send a new army and a new commander. He was an able officer, but a harsh, irritable, unpopular man who lacked skill in renewing old connections or forming new ones. He also struggled to capitalize on the injustice and arrogance with which the Carthaginians treated friends and foes in Further Spain after the death of Scipio. As an officer, Marcus Marcellus rendered no greater service to his country than any other officer. As a politician, although not perhaps fully conscious of the unpatriotic and personal character of his policy, he injured his country at least as much as he benefited it by his military skill.

As the assault raged on the landward side, Scipio dispatched a division with ladders across the shallow bank. Fortunately, Neptune himself appeared to guide them, leading them to an undefended section of the wall where they could establish a foothold. Success thus crowned the bold enterprise. It was bold because Scipio knew Hasdrubal Barcas had received orders from his government to advance towards Gaul and was fulfilling them. The weak division left behind on the Ebro was not in a position to seriously oppose that movement, even if Scipio's return was delayed. The battle took place at Baecula, where the Romans claimed victory and took 10,000 captives. However, Hasdrubal's goal was largely achieved despite losing a significant part of his army.

Spain Conquered by Mago Goes to Italy. After Hasdrubal Barcas was removed, the two generals who remained in Spain decided to retire for the time being. Hasdrubal, son of Gisgo, went to Lusitania, while Mago headed to the Baleares. Until new reinforcements arrived from Africa, they left the light cavalry of Massinissa alone to wage a desultory warfare in Spain, as Muttines had done so successfully in Sicily. Hasdrubal abandoned the idea of maintaining an open field, and distributed his troops among the Andalusian cities. During this year, Scipio was able to storm only one city, Oringis. Like Wellington in similar circumstances, Scipio arranged his Spanish troops to prevent them from participating in the battle, while throwing his Roman troops into the fray first.

For a moment it seemed as if, after the Romans had entered upon the Carthaginian heritage, they had sufficiently undeceived the expectation cherished here and there among the Spaniards that, following the close of the Phoenician rule, they would get rid of their Roman guests and regain their ancient freedom. A general insurrection against the Romans in Spain was imminent, with former allies of Rome likely to take the lead. The Phoenician settlement of Gades, the oldest and last remaining possession of the Phoenicians in Spain, was surrendered to its new rulers under favorable terms. Spain was converted from a Carthaginian into a Roman province after thirteen years of struggle. The conflict with the Romans continued for centuries through repeated insurrections that were consistently suppressed, but never fully defeated. At this time, there was no enemy standing in opposition to Rome.

The Italian War: A Continued Struggle

While the war had ended in Sicily under Marcellus, in Greece under Publius Sulpicius, and in Spain under Scipio, the conflict persisted unabated in the Italian peninsula. After the Battle of Cannae had been fought, its effects on loss or gain became apparent with the passing of time. At the start of 540, the fifth year of the war, the Roman and Phoenician forces' dispositions were as follows: North Italy was reoccupied by the Romans after Hannibal's departure. Three legions protected the region: one stationed in Celtic territory and another in Picenum, with the third serving as a reserve.

Tiberius Gracchus led a force of four legions against Gnaeus Pompeius, who was stationed with his main army at Arpi. The two armies clashed in Apulia, near the fortified cities of Luceria and Beneventum. In the land of the Bruttians, where the inhabitants had thrown themselves entirely into the arms of Hannibal, and where even the ports—excepting Rhegium, which the Romans protected from Messana—had been occupied by the Phoenicians, there was a second Carthaginian army under Hanno. Philip had no choice but to wait for either his brother's promised invasion of England or the arrival of his other brothers from Spain. In the meantime, he should strive to keep himself, his army, and his loyal supporters safe from harm and maintain a positive atmosphere.

Hannibal arrived in good time to protect its capital, which he prevented from being invested. However, he was unable to dislodge any of the Campanian towns held by the Romans from their strong Roman garrisons, or to prevent Casilinum, a key town that secured his passage over the Volturnus, from falling into the hands of the two consular armies after an obstinate defense. Meanwhile, the Bruttian army of the Carthaginians under Hanno had various encounters with the Roman army of Apulia. Tiberius Gracchus sustained the struggle with good results. After a successful combat not far from Beneventum, in which slave legions pressed into service distinguished themselves, he granted liberty and burgess rights to his slave-soldiers in the name of the people. The Romans acquired Arpi in 541. The following year, the Romans recovered the city, which had been occupied by a Carthaginian garrison. After the Roman soldiers entered the town, its citizens joined forces with them to resist the garrison.

The two consuls then invested the town. Tiberius Gracchus stationed himself on the Appian Way to prevent Hannibal from approaching to relieve it. However, that brave officer fell as a result of the shameful stratagem devised by a perfidious Lucanian. His death was equivalent to a complete defeat, for his army, mostly composed of slaves he had manumitted, dispersed after the fall of their beloved leader. The Romans, entrenched in their camps and fortifications, remained stationary, observing from their ramparts as the Campanian horsemen clashed with the Numidian squadrons on one side and the other. Hannibal could not have contemplated such an assault. He foresaw that his advance would soon attract the other Roman armies to Campania, driven by the scarcity of supplies in a region that had been systematically exploited before their arrival.

He gave the Campanians information of his intention and exhorted them to hold out. With this done, he began by relieving the army from Capua and then set off towards Rome. Hannibal had never expected to surprise Rome by a coup de main, such as Scipio soon afterwards executed against New Carthage. He had even less considered a siege in earnest; his only hope was that part of the besieging army from Capua would march to Rome, giving him an opportunity to break up the blockade. He left shortly afterwards.

The Romans believed that Hannibal's withdrawal was a miraculous intervention by the gods, who had used portents and visions to force the wicked man to leave. However, in reality, it was the Roman legions' inability to stop him. At the spot where Hannibal had approached closest to the city, near the Capene gate on the Appian Way, the Romans built an altar to "Rediculus Tutanus," the god who "turned back and protected." In fact, Hannibal's retreat was part of his plan, and he directed his march towards Capua. The citizens themselves would have pronounced their own sentence if they had not been put to death by the Romans. The citadel of Tarentum was severely affected by famine after the Tarentino-Carthaginian squadron closed the harbour. However, as the Romans with their more powerful fleet were able to cut off supplies from that squadron itself, and the territory commanded by Hannibal barely sufficed to maintain his army, the besiegers on the side next the sea suffered equally to those inside the citadel. Eventually, they abandoned the harbour.

In the next year (544), the Italian war was prosecuted more remissly than before by the Romans. Although Marcus Marcellus had resumed command of the main army after the close of the Sicilian war, he focused on besieging fortresses in the interior and had indecisive conflicts with the Carthaginians. They put to death all of the garrison or of the citizens they could find, and pillaged their houses. Thirty thousand Tarentines were sold into slavery, and three thousand talents (730,000 pounds) were sent to the state treasury. Hannibal was driven back. The death of Marcellus followed. After Hannibal had thus lost his most important acquisitions and found himself hemmed in by degrees to the south-western point of the peninsula, Marcus Marcellus, who had been chosen consul for the next year, hoped that, in connection with his capable colleague Titus Quintius Crispinus, he would be able to terminate the war by a decisive attack.

While conducting a routine reconnaissance mission in the district of Venusia, both consuls were unexpectedly ambushed by a contingent of African cavalry. The danger that had once threatened the state's very existence seemed to have vanished, but this only made the Romans feel the weight of their endless war more heavily. After the Battle of Cannae in 538, a special bank commission (tres viri mensarii) was appointed, composed of men highly respected, to form a permanent and cautious board of supervision over public finances during this challenging period.

They soon found that supplies from contractors on credit were insufficient; therefore, they had to take them. This was done despite their questionable conduct because they needed the supplies. However, the scandalous malversation eventually led the aediles to make an example of some of the worst offenders by impeaching them before the people. The owners of the slaves who had been armed by the state and freed after the Battle of Beneventum responded to the bank's commission, which offered them compensation. They agreed to accept the offer, allowing it to remain in place until the end of the war. Latium now began to waver.

Nero's stubborn campaign ended in defeat, as he failed to capture Hannibal. However, the Carthaginian general was able to escape with some losses, employing a clever flanking maneuver that allowed him to bypass his pursuers and reach Apulia unimpeded. The two armies lay inactive, face to face, when a dispatch from Hasdrubal was unexpectedly intercepted by the outposts of Nero. Hasdrubal intended to take the Flaminian road. In essence, his plan was to follow the coastline initially and then turn inland at Fanum, crossing the Apennine Mountains towards Narnia, where he hoped to encounter Hannibal.

Nero immediately ordered the reserve in the capital to proceed to Narnia, where the point he had selected would serve as the junction of the two Phoenician armies. Meanwhile, the division stationed at Capua marched towards the capital, and a new reserve was formed there. Hannibal retired to the Bruttian territory. The day after the battle, Nero began his campaign, and after a 14-day absence, he again encountered Hannibal in Apulia, where no news had reached him, and he remained inactive. It forms a brilliant proof of Hannibal's strategic talent as well as the incapacity of the Roman generals opposing him, that after this he was still able for four years to maintain control in the Bruttian country, and that all the superiority of his opponents could not force him either to fortify himself within fortresses or to embark. It is true that he was obliged to retire farther and farther, not so much in consequence of the indecisive engagements with the Romans, as because his Bruttian allies had become increasingly troublesome, and at last he could only rely on the towns that his army garrisoned.

The senate was not favourable to a methodical conduct of the war. The party that supported this approach was unwilling to consider an African expedition as long as Hannibal remained in Italy. Similarly, the majority was not inclined to support the young general. Both sides displayed enough wisdom not to push matters too far. The majority decided not to refuse Scipio his desired commission, as he had previously shown respect for the supreme governing board in form and had submitted himself to the senate's decree beforehand.

The Africans could no longer hold the field after two consecutive defeats. An attack on the Roman naval camp by the Carthaginian fleet was not unsuccessful, but far from decisive. The capture of Syphax, which Scipio's good fortune brought about, greatly outweighed this attempt. As a result, Massinissa became to the Romans what Syphax had been to the Carthaginians at first. Negotiations for Peace

After such defeats, the Carthaginian peace party, which had been silenced for sixteen years, was finally able to rise again and openly rebel against the government of Barcides.

The patriotic party held sway among the citizens. It was decided that the opposition would be allowed to negotiate for peace, while preparations were made for a final and decisive effort. Hannibal, arriving without the protection of the armistice, but solely through his rapidity of movement and good fortune, reached Leptis without hindrance. He was the last of the "lion's brood" to return home after a thirty-six-year absence, treading once more on his native soil. He had left it, even as a young boy, to embark on a heroic career that was ultimately unfulfilling. He had set his sights westward, intending to return home from the east, and had achieved a significant military victory around the Mediterranean Sea.

The Carthaginian general attempted to negotiate better terms with the Romans in a personal meeting. However, Scipio, who had already reached the extreme limit of concession, could not agree to further yield after the armistice was breached. It is unlikely that Hannibal's actions were motivated by anything other than demonstrating to the public that the patriots were not entirely opposed to peace. After this day, folly alone could counsel a continuation of the war on the part of Carthage. His position in Rome was not suitable for being a favorite of the people after the victory of Zama. He was not seriously concerned about being recalled — even before the victory, there had been an attempt to replace him, which the Senate had referred to the Burgesses and they had decisively rejected.

The Carthaginian city never, after its hands were thus tied and a powerful neighbour was placed beside it, made even an attempt to withdraw from Roman supremacy. It was certainly less likely to enter into rivalry with Rome. Everyone who cared to know understood that the war just terminated had been undertaken primarily by Hannibal rather than Carthage. Moreover, it was absolutely impossible to revive the gigantic plan of the patriot party. Scipio thought otherwise. We have no reason to assume he was motivated by vulgar motives, rather than the noble and magnanimous impulses that were part of his character. It was not the consideration of his own possible recall or the mutability of fortune that prevented the self-reliant and confident hero, with whom everything had hitherto succeeded beyond belief, from accomplishing the destruction of the unhappy city. Fifty years later, his adopted grandson was commissioned to execute this very deed, which might have been equally well accomplished now.

The Second Punic War ended thus, having devastated the lands and islands from the Hellespont to the Pillars of Hercules. The Romans achieved sovereignty over Italy because they sought it. The hegemony, and the sovereignty that arose from it, extended over the Mediterranean territories to a certain extent, which was largely handed over to the Romans by circumstance, rather than any deliberate intent to claim it. Out of Italy, the immediate results of the war were:

1. The conversion of Spain into two Roman provinces.
2. The union of the hitherto dependent kingdom of Syracuse with the Roman province of Sicily.
3. The establishment of a Roman protectorate over the most important Numidian chiefs instead of a Carthaginian one.
4. The conversion of Carthage from a powerful commercial state to a defenceless mercantile town.

The entire soil, except for the possessions of foreigners or those who were well-disposed towards Rome, was declared by the senate to be public domain. It was then allocated to small landowners on temporary leases. The position in which matters stood during the Macedonian war was marked by the senate's anxious carefulness in watching Italy. The senate despatched reinforcements from Rome to key colonies, including Venusia in 554, Narnia in 555, Cosa in 557, and Cales shortly before 570. The number of people who died due to war and famine among the Italian population is evident from the case of the Roman burgesses, whose numbers decreased by nearly one-fourth.

The senate's reduction was starkly evident after the Battle of Cannae, when it dwindled to just 123 members. However, through an extraordinary appointment process, the number was gradually restored to its original complement of senators. The coexistence of people from different nations alongside one another in harmony and friendship, despite maintaining an attitude of mutual hostility, which seems to be the goal of modern-day national life, was a concept alien to ancient times. The league of the cantons was plagued by intestine discord. The Boii and Insubres quarrelled, while the Cenomani withdrew from the national league and purchased their pardon from the Romans through a shameful betrayal of their countrymen. During a battle where the Insubres clashed with the Romans on the Mincius, the Cenomani attacked from behind, helping to destroy their allies and comrades in arms.

The Insubres, humbled by their defeat at Comum, agreed to negotiate a separate peace. The Romans demanded the cession of half the territory; the demand could not be refused. Even within the diminished district left to the Boii, they soon disappeared and amalgamated with their conquerors. After the Romans had thus cleared the ground for themselves, they reorganized the fortresses of Placentia and Cremona. The colonists who had been in great part swept away or dispersed by the troubles of the last few years were replaced with new settlers. The road from Rome to Arretium or the Cassian way was taken in charge and constructed anew by the Roman community, likely around 583. In 567, the track from Arretium over the Apennines to Bononia, as far as the new Aemilian road, had been put in order, providing a shorter route between Rome and the fortresses on the Po River.

The expedition of Tiberius Gracchus against the Sardinians in 577 was held in remembrance. It was not primarily because it brought peace to the province, but rather because he claimed to have slain or captured around 80,000 islanders. He also dragged large numbers of slaves from the island to Rome, which led to the phrase "as cheap as a Sardinian" becoming a proverb. The Carthaginians sent embassy after embassy to Rome. The Romans told them to either allow them to defend themselves by arms, or to appoint a court of arbitration with power to enforce their decision, or to regulate the border anew so they could finally know how much they would lose; otherwise it was better to make them Roman subjects at once than to gradually hand them over to the Libyans. The Roman commissions appeared in Africa, but after a thorough investigation, they reached no conclusion. Alternatively, during negotiations in Rome, the envoys of Massinissa claimed to lack instructions, and the matter was postponed.

Hannibal's reform of the Carthaginian constitution was a significant step forward. However, his flight from Carthage marked a turning point in his life. The Roman government, just then on the point of beginning its critical war with the great king of Asia, observed the progress of these events with apprehension; it was no imaginary danger that the Carthaginian fleet might land in Italy and a second war under Hannibal might spring up there. While the Roman legions fought in Asia Minor. The Romans should not be criticized for sending an embassy to Carthage in 559 BCE, as it was likely intended to negotiate Hannibal's surrender. Irritation in Rome towards Carthage arose from a grave responsibility. The Roman government bore a greater burden, stemming from its persistent suspicion and persecution of the city following Hannibal's removal.

The parties continued as before. However, after the departure of the extraordinary individual who had nearly altered the course of history, the patriotic faction held little significance in Carthage compared to Aetolia or Achaia. The Phoenicians' power was waning in the land they had chosen, much like it had in their ancestral homeland. A new nation emerged alongside them. The northern coast of Africa has been inhabited since ancient times, and it remains inhabited today by the people known as Shilah or Tamazigt. The Greeks and Romans referred to them as Nomades or Numidians. e The Berbers, also known as the pastoral people, are referred to by the Arabs as Berbers. At times, they are also designated as "Shepherds" (Shawie).

In the Carthaginian period, tribes that did not live immediately around Carthage or on the coast maintained their independence. They also retained their pastoral and equestrian lifestyle, similar to the Atlas region's inhabitants today. Although they were familiar with the Phoenician alphabet and civilization, instances of Berber sheiks' sons being educated in Carthage and marrying into Phoenician noble families did occur. His wide dominions were mainly given to Massinissa; although Vermina, son of Syphax, by humble petition recovered a small portion of his father's territory from the Romans, he was unable to deprive the earlier ally of the Romans of his position as the privileged oppressor of Carthage. The king, like his predecessors, cultivated the land extensively, spreading it across the kingdom. He then distributed large tracts of land among his children as their inheritance. As a result, his people started settling down and adopting farming practices.

It is true that there were not many wanting the rudiments of a national Iberian civilization, although of its special character it is scarcely possible for us to acquire any clear idea. There existed a tradition among the Turdetani (round Seville) that dated back to ancient times, which included a metrical book of laws containing 6,000 verses and historical records. Regardless, this tribe was described as the most civilized of all Spanish tribes, and at the same time, the least warlike. It is worth noting that they regularly conducted their wars using foreign mercenaries. Warm baths became a popular practice in Italy even during this time among the locals.

Roman money was current in Spain far earlier than elsewhere, particularly in Italy. It was also imitated in Spanish coins, which can be attributed to the country's rich silver mines. The use of Spanish denarii with Iberian inscriptions dates back to 559. The start of their coinage can be traced no further than this period, as the design is clearly inspired by the earliest Roman denarii. In the southern and eastern provinces, the culture of the natives had prepared the way for Roman civilization and rule. However, in contrast, the western and northern regions, as well as the interior, were inhabited by numerous tribes that were largely uncivilized. These tribes knew little about any form of civilization, with the use of gold and silver still unknown in places like Intercatia until around 600 years ago.

Twenty years after the close of the Second Punic War, the small Celtiberian community of Complega, located near the sources of the Tagus River, sent a message to the Roman general. They demanded that for every man who had fallen in battle, he should provide them with a horse, a mantle, and a sword. If no recruiting officer appeared, they met on their own initiative in small groups, with the aim of raiding the more tranquil districts and even capturing and occupying towns, much like the Campanians. In Spain, there was no serious war. However, as little peace as there was, it was not a lasting one. The Spaniards, as Caesar later observed, were never truly at rest during times of peace or engaged in intense conflict when they should have been.

He could only deal with it through palliative measures, as a comprehensive Latin colonization, the only truly effective solution, was not in line with Rome's overall strategy during this time. The Romans maintained a standing army in Spain. Cato Gracchus. The territory acquired by the Romans in Spain during the second Punic War was divided into two provinces from its inception. The first province, formerly Carthaginian, encompassed the present-day districts of Andalusia, Granada, Murcia, and Valencia. The second province, along the Ebro River, comprised modern-day Aragon and Catalonia, which served as the fixed quarters of the Roman army during the last war. The Romans sought to gradually subjugate the interior regions corresponding to the two Castiles, which they encompassed under the general name of Celtiberia. They were content with checking the incursions of the inhabitants of the western provinces, particularly those of the Lusitanians in modern Portugal and Spanish Estremadura, into Roman territory. The Romans did not yet come into contact with the tribes on the north coast, including the Callaecians, Asturians, and Cantabrians.

The territories thus won, however, could not be maintained and secured without a standing garrison. The governor of Hither Spain had no small trouble every year with the chastisement of the Celtiberians. Similarly, the governor of the more remote province found similar employment in repelling the Lusitanians. It was necessary to maintain a Roman army of four strong legions in Spain, numbering around 40,000 men, year after year. Additionally, the general levy was frequently called upon in the districts occupied by Rome to reinforce the legions. It was of great importance for two reasons: it was in Spain first, marking the beginning of continuous military occupation of the land; and it was there that the service acquired a permanent character.

The new generals, with good reason, refused to allow the relief of the entire corps. The men rebelled, threatening that if they were not granted their discharge, they would take it by themselves. There was a temporary ceasefire following the peace treaty with Carthage in 553 BC, but it lasted only briefly. It was necessary to take up the war in earnest. Although the able praetor Quintus Minucius had already overcome the initial threat, the senate decided in 559 to dispatch the consul Marcus Cato to Spain in person.

A pitched battle took place between the insurgents and the consular army. After an obstinate conflict, man-to-man, the Roman military skill ultimately decided the day with its last reserve. The entire region of Hither Spain subsequently submitted. Nevertheless, this submission was not genuine, as soon as news reached the consul's return to Rome, the uprising resumed. But the rumor was false; after Cato had quickly subdued the communities that had revolted for the second time and sold them en masse into slavery, he decreed a general disarmament of the Spaniards in the Hither province and ordered all native towns from the Pyrenees to the Guadalquivir to tear down their walls on one day.

In 563, a Roman army was forced to abandon its camp due to heavy losses and return by forced marches into the more tranquil districts. It was not until after a victory won by Praetor Lucius Aemilius Paullus in 565 and another even more significant one gained by Praetor Gaius Calpurnius beyond the Tagus over the Lusitanians in 569 that a period of quiet ensued for some time. In Hither Spain, the Romans' nominal rule over the Celtiberian tribes was strengthened by Quintus Fulvius Flaccus, who after a great victory in 573 compelled at least the adjacent cantons to submission. His successor, Tiberius Gracchus, achieved lasting results not only through his military victories, which reduced three hundred Spanish townships, but also by his ability to adapt to the views and habits of the simple yet proud nation.

His name was held in grateful remembrance by the Spaniards, and comparative peace thereafter reigned in the land. The northern frontier was secure once more, following the receding tide of the Gallic invasion. The border guards maintained control over the Illyrian tribes with ease, barring any significant threats during normal periods. In steadfast resistance to the public enemy under whatever name, in unshaken fidelity towards their native country and hereditary government, and in persevering courage amidst the severest trials, no nation in ancient history bears a closer resemblance to the Roman people than the Macedonians. The almost miraculous regeneration of the state after the Gallic invasion is a testament to the honour of its leaders and the people they led.

None indeed of the states founded by the successors of Alexander were free from such attempts, or from the other horrors that absolute monarchy in degenerate times brings. However, in the kingdom of Asia, these evils were more injurious than elsewhere, as they often led to the separation of particular portions from the empire for longer or shorter periods. The remissness and indolence of its rulers led to paralysis in Egypt more than in Macedonia and Asia. In contrast, when wielded by capable leaders, such as the first Ptolemy and Ptolemy Euergetes, the state machine proved extremely useful. While an opponent, even at the height of their career, was hardly in a position to seriously threaten Egypt, which was almost inaccessible by land, the Egyptians were able by sea to establish themselves not only in Cyrene but also in Cyprus and the Cyclades, along the Phoenico-Syrian coast, on the entire south and west coast of Asia Minor, and even in Europe, specifically in the Thracian Chersonese.

The mutual relations of the three great states are evident. The maritime power, which ruled the coasts and monopolized the sea, could not but direct its further efforts towards the weakening of the two great states on the mainland and protecting the several minor states after the first great success—the political separation of the European from the Asiatic continent. Meanwhile, Macedonia and Asia regarded each other as rivals, but their common adversary was Egypt, and they should have combined against it. These fierce barbarians were a general terror to the surrounding nations, including those of Asia. Even the great kings of Asia feared them, for they had defeated numerous Asian armies that had fallen to the Celts and King Antiochus I.

The Romans provided significant financial support to the kings of Syria, with the debt repayment later becoming a condition for peace. The republics of Epirus, Acarnania, and Boeotia had close ties to Macedonia. The Acarnanians were particularly connected, as Macedonian protection allowed them to escape the destruction threatened by the Aetolians. The energy of the Northern Greek character remained unbroken there, despite having degenerated into a reckless impatience with discipline and control.

Sparta, Elis, Messene. The policy of the weaker states in the Peloponnesus, Messene, and Sparta was determined by their ancient enmity to the Achaean League. This enmity was fostered by disputes over their frontiers, and they tended towards the Aetolian and anti-Macedonian factions, as the Achaeans sided with Philip. Three cities, in particular, enjoyed their full freedom after Alexander's death. Byzantium, mistress of the Bosporus, became wealthy and powerful due to transit dues and its significant corn trade with the Black Sea. Cyzicus, on the Asiatic side of the Propontis, maintained close ties with the court of Pergamus as the daughter and heiress of Miletus. Rhodes was the last and most prominent of these cities.

The Rhodians, who immediately after Alexander's death had expelled the Macedonian garrison, secured the carrying trade of all the eastern Mediterranean by their favourable position for commerce and navigation. Their well-handled fleet, as well as the tried courage of the citizens in the famous siege, enabled them to become prudent and energetic representatives of a neutral commercial policy when occasion required. They kept themselves as far as possible aloof from land warfare, despite having acquired considerable possessions on the opposite coast of Caria. When war was unavoidable, they waged it through mercenaries. This league upheld the freedom of cities against monarchical interests; while wars raged around their walls, public spirit and civic prosperity found shelter in comparative peace within, and art and science flourished unimpeded.

We have already set forth to some extent the origin of this interference and the course of the first Macedonian War (540-549). We have also pointed out what Philip might have accomplished during the Second Punic War, and how little of all that Hannibal could reasonably expect and count on was actually fulfilled. No one can deny that he possessed sagacity and resolution, but these were combined in a singular manner with procrastination and supineness. This combination may be partly explained by the fact that he was called to an absolute sovereign position at the age of eighteen. His ungovernable fury towards anyone who dared counter his autocratic course with argument or advice drove away all independent counselors from him. Like Philip, the kings began the war not merely without cause but even without pretext. "The allies, however, had made their calculations correctly, especially Philip."

While the allied fleet gathered on the Aeolian coast, Philip ordered a part of his fleet to capture Chios and Samos. He appeared in person before Pergamus, but his investment was in vain. He had to settle for traversing the level countryside and leaving behind the remnants of Macedonian valor on the temples he destroyed throughout the land. Attalus, one of the leaders of the allies, was cut off from his fleet and forced to abandon ship at Erythrae. Theophiliscus of Rhodes, a man driven by public spirit who had played a crucial role in deciding the question of war, died the day after the battle due to his wounds.

While the fleet of Attalus returned home, the Rhodian fleet remained temporarily at Chios. Meanwhile, Philip, who falsely claimed the victory for himself, continued his voyage and turned towards Samos to occupy the Carian towns. The victory, claimed by both sides, appears to have been this time gained by the Macedonians. While the Rhodians retreated to Myndus and then to Cos, the Macedonians occupied Miletus. A squadron under Dicaearchus the Aetolian also controlled the Cyclades. Philip meanwhile prosecuted the conquest of the Rhodian possessions on the Carian mainland, and of the Greek cities. If he had been disposed to attack Ptolemy in person, or preferred to confine himself to acquiring his own share in the spoil, he would now be able to think of an expedition to Egypt.

They might have cut off the king's retreat and forced him to spend the winter in Caria. However, given the situation back home, especially the threat of Aetolian and Roman intervention, it was imperative for him to return. It has often been asserted that following the conquest of the West, they immediately set about subjugating the East. A more serious consideration would lead to a fairer judgment. The senate would have gladly seen Macedonia humbled. However, that humiliation would be too dearly purchased if it came at the cost of a land war carried on in Macedonia with Roman troops. After the Aetolians withdrew, the senate voluntarily concluded peace at once on the basis of the status quo. It is therefore far from made out that the Roman government concluded this peace with the definite design of beginning the war at a more convenient season. It is very certain that, at the moment, from the thorough exhaustion of the state and the extreme unwillingness of the citizens to enter into another transmarine struggle, the Macedonian war was in a high degree unwelcome to the Romans.

The fall of Egypt and the humiliation, perhaps subjugation, of Rhodes would have inflicted deep wounds on the trade of Sicily and Italy. Rome could not remain a quiet spectator while Italian commerce with the East became dependent on the two great continental powers. The government was unable to find a plausible excuse for the war; one that would justify it to the people, despite having underestimated the value of providing a legitimate reason for hostilities, as Philip had done. The support that Philip was alleged to have granted to the Carthaginians after the peace with Rome could not be proven.

But Rhodes and Pergamus, which naturally did not fail to request Roman aid, were formally the aggressors. Despite Alexandrian ambassadors' pleas with the Roman senate to take guardianship of the boy king, Egypt showed little enthusiasm for invoking direct Roman intervention, which would have resolved its immediate difficulties but also opened up the eastern sea to the great western power. Antiochus did not break off his alliance with Philip, nor did he give the Romans the definite explanations they desired. In other respects, however, whether from remissness or influenced by the declarations of the Romans that they did not wish to interfere in Syria, he pursued his schemes in that direction and left things in Greece and Asia Minor to take their course. The war resumed in the spring of 554.

The fleet that commanded the Aegean Sea after the withdrawal of the weaker Macedonian squadron was led by Philip. He confined his naval operations to maintaining garrisons on three of the Cyclades: Andros, Cythnos, and Paros, and fitted out privateers. The allies may have arrived in time to aid the Abydenes, who bravely defended themselves. However, they failed to act, and as a result, the city eventually fell, with most able-bodied warriors having fallen during the battle before the walls. After the capitulation, a large portion of the inhabitants fell by their own hand. The mercy of the victor consisted in allowing the Abydenes a term of three days to die voluntarily.

Before Abydos. The Roman embassy, having concluded its business in Syria and Egypt, met with the king and presented the proposals entrusted to them by the Senate. The king should not wage aggressive war against any Greek state. He should restore the possessions he had taken from Ptolemy and agree to arbitration regarding the injuries inflicted on the Pergamenes and Rhodians. The object of the senate, which sought to provoke the king to a formal declaration of war, was not gained. The Roman ambassador, Marcus Aemilius Lepidus, obtained only a polite reply from the king, who excused what the envoy had said because he was young and handsome, and a Roman. The occasion for declaring war, which Rome desired, had been provided by another source.

The Athenians, driven by their own vanity, had put to death two Acarnanians who had unintentionally stumbled upon their secrets. All the burgess troops, on the other hand, who had been under arms since the Hannibal war, were discharged. Volunteers alone were to be enrolled for the Macedonian war, but they were, as was later discovered, largely coerced into service—a fact that in the autumn of 555 sparked a dangerous military revolt in the camp of Apollonia. Philip had offended many among the Achaeans by murdering Aratus. However, this act also laid the groundwork for a more liberal development of the confederacy.

While the rest of the ships took their station for the winter at Corcyra, a division under Gaius Claudius Cento proceeded to the Piraeus to provide aid to the hard-pressed Athenians. This principal attack from the west was to be supported by three subordinate attacks: one on the north by an invasion of the Dardani and Illyrians; another on the east by a combined fleet of Romans and allies assembling at Aegina; and if successful, the Athamanes and Aetolians would advance from the south. After Galba had crossed the mountains pierced by the Apsus (now known as the Beratind), he marched through the fertile plain of Dassaretia and then entered the mountain range that separated Illyria from Macedonia.

Philip's army, after being joined by the corps detached to occupy the northern passes, numbered approximately 20,000 infantry and 2,000 cavalry. The Roman army was nearly as strong. The Macedonians, however, had a great advantage. Fighting in their native land and well-acquainted with its highways and byways, they had little trouble in procuring supplies of provisions. Since they had encamped so close to the Romans, the latter could not venture to disperse for extensive foraging. The consul repeatedly offered battle, but the king persisted in declining it. The combats between the light troops, although the Romans gained some advantages in them, produced no significant change.

Although Philip had instituted levies as large as possible in his own dominions, he had enlisted Roman deserters and other mercenaries. However, he was unable to bring more than a single army into the field, which he confronted with the consul in person. Furthermore, to form this army, he had been forced to leave the northern passes in the Pelagonian territory undefended. These now suddenly joined the league against Macedonia. In conjunction with the Athamanes, they penetrated into Thessaly. Meanwhile, the Dardani and Illyrians overran the northern provinces. The Roman fleet under Lucius Apustius appeared in the eastern waters, having departed from Corcyra. There, the ships of Attalus, the Rhodians, and the Istrians joined it. Return of the Romans

Although Philip's army was no longer able to prevent the advance of the Romans after this unfortunate action, the latter were themselves afraid to encounter further unknown dangers in an impassable and hostile country. They returned to Apollonia, having laid waste to the fertile provinces of Upper Macedonia—Eordaea, Elymaea, and Orestis. The Roman fleet made little progress; it drove out the Macedonian garrison from Andros, punished Euboea and Sciathus, and then launched attacks on the Chalcidian peninsula, which were ultimately repelled by the Macedonian garrison at Mende.

The Roman troops, after an extremely troublesome campaign, stood in autumn precisely on the spot where they had started in spring. It was only due to the timely intervention of the Aetolians and the unexpected success of the battle at the pass of Eordaea that not a single man of their entire force would have seen the Roman territory again. Philip's fourfold offensive had failed everywhere in its objective. Not only did he clear his entire dominion of the enemy in autumn, but he was able to make an attempt – which, however, miscarried – to capture the strong town of Thaumaci, situated on the Aetolo-Thessalian frontier and commanding the plain of the Peneius River. The latter, however, were not anxious to urge the great king at this time to take action. They sent envoys, who in fact secured an evacuation of the territories of Attalus.

It would have been better for Rome and Greece if the choice had fallen on a leader less swayed by Hellenic sympathies. A general dispatched to that region should be one who is not easily bribed by flattery or stung by sarcasm. Such a person would not overlook the poor state of the constitutions in the Hellenic states, even amidst reminiscences about their literature and art. They would treat Hellas fairly, without setting unattainable ideals for the Romans to pursue. The new commander-in-chief immediately held a conference with the king, as the two armies faced each other in silence. The Romans, following their initial victory, swiftly invaded Thessaly, which was quickly overrun due to its open terrain. However, the fortified towns, allied with Macedonia and receiving support from Philip, resisted fiercely or held out against the superior force – particularly Atrax on the left bank of the Peneius River, where the phalanx formation served as a defensive bulwark in place of the wall.

The fleet, which had again been joined by Rhodian and Pergamene ships, had hitherto been employed in capturing and pillaging two smaller towns in Euboea: Eretria and Carystus. However, both were subsequently abandoned and reoccupied by Philocles, the Macedonian commandant of Chalcis. After the deputies from the towns closest to Macedonia — Dyme, Megalopolis, and Argos — had left the diet, it resolved to join the coalition against Philip. The Macedonian garrison, numbering around 1,300 strong and mainly composed of Italian deserters, defended the city with determination. However, Philocles also arrived from Chalcis with a force of approximately 1,500 men, which not only helped Corinth but also invaded Achaean territory and, in conjunction with citizens sympathetic to Macedonia, seized Argos. Philip hoped to win over Nabis after the Achaeans joined the Roman coalition, as Nabis had previously been an ally of the Romans. His main reason for joining the Roman alliance was his opposition to the Achaeans; he had been at war with them since 550.

The Roman senate had long been united in its opinion that Macedonia must relinquish all her possessions abroad. When Philip's ambassadors appeared in Rome, they were asked if they had full powers to renounce Greece, particularly Corinth, Chalcis, and Demetrias. The ambassadors replied that they did not have such powers, and the negotiations were immediately terminated. It was then resolved that the war would be prosecuted with vigor. To secure Greece, all states except the Acarnanians and Boeotians were now in arms against him. The garrison of Corinth was augmented to 6,000 men. He himself, straining the last energies of exhausted Macedonia, enrolled children and old men in the ranks of the phalanx, bringing an army of about 26,000 men into the field, of whom 16,000 were Macedonian. The great difficulties of provisioning the army in a hostile and largely desolate country, which had often hindered its operations, were now to be alleviated by the fleet accompanying the army along the coast and carrying supplies sent from Africa, Sicily, and Sardinia.

The decisive blow came sooner than Flamininus had hoped. Philip, impatient and confident as he was, could not wait to engage the enemy on the Macedonian frontier. After assembling his army at Dium, he advanced through the pass of Tempe into Thessaly, where he encountered the enemy's advancing army in the district of Scotussa. The Macedonian and Roman armies, reinforced by contingents of the Apolloniates and Athamanes, received support from Cretans sent by Nabis and a strong band of Aetolians. The two forces were nearly evenly matched, with approximately 26,000 men on each side; however, the Romans held an advantage in cavalry.

In front of Scotussa, on the plateau of Karadagh, a gloomy day with rain gave way to an unexpected encounter between the Roman vanguard and that of the enemy. The enemy's forces occupied a high and steep hill named Cynoscephalae, situated between the two camps. Nicanor, on the other wing, saw the king give the order to attack. He immediately ordered the remaining half of the phalanx to advance with all haste. This movement threw the phalanx into confusion. As the first ranks rapidly followed the victorious right wing down the hill, they were further disordered by the uneven terrain. Meanwhile, the last files were just reaching the height. As a fierce battle raged around him, a determined Roman officer rallied twenty companies and launched a bold attack on the advancing Macedonian wing, which had pushed deep into pursuit of the Roman left flank, forcing the Roman right to fall back. From the complete breaking up of the two phalanxes, it is clear that the Macedonian loss was significant. The exact number is uncertain, but it is believed to be around 13,000, comprising both prisoners and fallen soldiers. However, the majority were killed, as Roman soldiers were unfamiliar with the Macedonian sign of surrender, which involved raising the sarissae.

Philip escaped to Larissa, burned all his papers to avoid compromising anyone, then evacuated Thessaly and returned home. As the Macedonians suffered this great defeat, they faced other discomfits at every point they still held. In Caria, Rhodian mercenaries defeated the Macedonian corps stationed there, forcing it to surrender in Stratonicea. The Corinthian garrison was decisively beaten by Nicostratus and his Achaean allies, with heavy losses. Leucas in Acarnania fell after a heroic resistance. The flourishing city of Lysimachia on the Thracian Chersonese had already been destroyed by the Thracians at the end of the war.

He lost all his foreign possessions in Asia Minor, Thrace, Greece, and the islands of the Aegean Sea; he retained Macedonia proper undiminished, except for some unimportant tracts on the frontier and the province of Orestis, which was declared free—a stipulation that Philip felt very keenly, but the Romans could not avoid prescribing, as it was impossible to leave him free to dispose of subjects who had once revolted from their allegiance. Philip entered into an alliance with the Romans, which required him to send a contingent whenever requested. In fact, Macedonian troops soon fought alongside Roman legions. Greece was free. After Macedonia had been reduced to complete political nullity, it was left with only enough power to defend its borders against the barbarians. Steps were then taken to manage the remaining possessions that had been ceded by the king.

The Achaean confederacy was the best-treated among the Greek states, despite being the last to join the coalition against Philip. This was likely due to their perception that joining a well-organized and respected federation would be an honourable decision. All the possessions of Philip in the Peloponnese and on the Isthmus, and consequently Corinth, were incorporated into their league. But after various attempts to induce Nabis to yield, Philip had already surrendered the city of Argos to him as part of the Achaean league. In the end, Flamininus was left with no choice but to declare war on the stubborn petty leader, who relied on the long-standing animosity between the Aetolians and Romans, as well as Antiochus's advance into Europe, and refused to return Argos.

Even when he himself had achieved the first successes of the Roman army and fleet, he resolved to yield and accept the relatively favourable terms of peace proposed by Flamininus. However, "the people," meaning the group of robbers Nabis had settled in Sparta, were apprehensive of a reckoning after their victory and were deceived by false information about the nature of the peace terms and the advance of the Aetolians and Asiatics. As a result, they rejected the Roman general's offer of peace, leading to the struggle beginning anew. The Achaeans, although by this arrangement they gained the accession of the free Laconians as well as Argos, were yet far from content. They had expected that Nabis would be superseded and the emigrants would be brought back, and that the Achaean symmachy would be extended to the whole Peloponnesus. Unprejudiced individuals would recognize that Flamininus handled these challenging matters fairly and justly, given the circumstances in which two opposing political factions, equally culpable of unfairness and injustice, stood against each other. The restitution of the emigrants and the complete restoration of a government that had been set aside for twenty years would only have substituted one reign of terror for another. The expedient adopted by Flamininus was the right one, simply because it failed to satisfy either of the extreme parties.

The Boeotians openly displayed their Macedonian tendencies even after the expulsion of the Macedonians from Greece. Following Flamininus' decision to allow their countrymen who served Philip to return home at their request, Brachyllas, a staunch supporter of Macedonia, was elected president of the Boeotian confederacy. This move irritated Flamininus in every way. The Boeotians, friendly to Rome, who knew what awaited them after the Roman departure, decided to kill Brachyllas. Flamininus, whose permission they thought necessary, did nothing to stop them. They then sought to negotiate. Flamininus actually gave up on intervening on behalf of the Achaeans and Athenians, exacting only a moderate fine from those who were at fault; and although the Macedonian faction continued to hold sway in the small province, the Romans dealt with their childish resistance by exercising superior power. The work was completed in the spring of 560. Flamininus once again gathered the deputies of all Greek communities at Corinth and urged them to use their newfound freedom rationally and moderately. In return for the Romans' kindness, he requested that they send him the Italian captives who had been sold into Greece during the Hannibal War within thirty days.

In Boeotia, for instance, Rome allowed, if not instigated, a political murder. The Romans had resolved to withdraw their troops from Greece and, consequently, could not prevent the Greeks who were friendly to Rome from seeking their remedy in the usual manner of the country. After Ptolemy III's death in 221, the opportune moment for conquering Egypt had finally arrived. With this in mind, Antiochus formed an alliance with Philip V and launched a campaign against Coele-Syria, while Philip simultaneously attacked the cities of Asia Minor.

He himself was not probably in earnest with the conquest of Egypt proper, which was more easily talked of than achieved. However, he contemplated the subjugation of Egypt's foreign possessions one by one, and immediately launched attacks on those in Cilicia, Syria, and Palestine. Difficulties with Rome This enterprise had an indirect bearing on the Romans. They had initially demanded that Philip withdraw his garrisons from Asia Minor and allow the Rhodians and Pergamenes to retain their territory, as well as preserve the former constitution of the free cities. Now, they were forced to watch as Antiochus took control of these territories in Philip's stead. Attalus had requested military aid from the Romans as early as 55 BC. He sought assistance against Antiochus, who had occupied his territory, while Attalus' own troops were engaged in the Roman war.

The king discussed his undisputed claim to the ancient kingdom of Lysimachus, which had been conquered by his ancestor Seleucus. He explained that his role was not to acquire new territories but rather to maintain the integrity of his inherited domains. The king declined Roman intervention in his disputes with cities in Asia Minor that were under his jurisdiction. He also promised to restore the provinces taken from his son-in-law, which was later confirmed by Egypt, although possibly without justification. In any case, the land remained under the control of the Syrian kingdom. He offered to restore to Eumenes, who had succeeded his father Attalus on the throne of Pergamum in 557, the towns taken from him, and to give him one of his daughters in marriage, provided he abandoned the Roman alliance. He bestowed a daughter on Ariarathes, king of Cappadocia, as a gift. He also won over the Galatians with gifts, while using his military might to subdue the Pisidians, who had been in constant revolt, along with other smaller tribes.

Their policy is characteristically expressed in the reply, which they gave soon after to Flamininus, when he requested a copy of the declaration of war against Rome. He promised to deliver it to him in person, once the Aetolian army camped on the Tiber. They actually succeeded in inducing the simple obstinacy of Nabis to break loose and rekindle the flame of war two years after Flamininus's departure in the spring of 562. However, in doing so, they missed their aim. After this laudable project of the Aetolians had failed, it instead had a unifying effect on almost the whole Peloponnesus in favor of the other party. The Roman faction fared little better at Chalcis, as they called upon the citizens of Eretria and Carystus in Euboea, who were favorable to Rome, to provide timely assistance against the Aetolians and the Chalcidian exiles.

The Carthaginians would have had no involvement with Hannibal - such a man was too great for court intrigue; and after trying various absurd expedients, they attempted to implicate the general whose name instilled fear in Roman children, alongside the Roman envoys. However, they failed to persuade Antiochus the Great, who, like many insignificant monarchs, excessively boasted about his independence and was easily swayed by the fear of being ruled, into believing that he should not be overshadowed by such a renowned figure. Egypt sided with Rome and provided support in supplies and troops. The Romans rejected this offer. Philip therefore placed his entire force with great enthusiasm at the disposal of the Romans.

The commander-in-chief was Manius Acilius Glabrio, a man of humble origin, but an able general feared by both his soldiers and the enemy. The admiral was Gaius Livius. Among the military tribunes were Marcus Porcius Cato, who had conquered Spain, and Lucius Valerius Flaccus, who did not disdain re-entering the army as a simple war-tribune after having held consulship. Only half of the Aetolian contingent was pleased to comply with this order. The remaining 2,000 men marched towards the neighbouring town of Heraclea, where they played a minor role in the battle by attempting to surprise and plunder the Roman camp during its progress. Even the Aetolians posted on the heights discharged their duty of watching with remissness and reluctance. Their post on the Callidromus allowed itself to be surprised by Cato. The Asiatic phalanx, which the consul had assailed in front, dispersed when the Romans hastened down the mountain and fell upon its flank.

Antiochus had made no provision for any case and had not considered retreat. The army was destroyed, both on the battlefield and during its flight. A small band of soldiers managed to reach Demetrias, while the king himself fled to Chalcis with a group of 500 men.
