All Greeks who had sided with Antiochus hastened to make peace; the Epirots humbly asked for pardon for their ambiguous conduct, while the Boeotians surrendered at discretion. The Eleans and Messenians, the latter after some struggle, eventually submitted to the Achaeans. Even the Aetolians, when their corps was shut up in Heraclea, had been compelled after obstinate resistance to capitulate. They attempted to make peace with the sorely provoked Romans; however, the Roman consul's stringent demands and a consignment of money arriving from Antiochus emboldened them once more to break off negotiations and sustain a siege in Naupactus for two months.

A more serious war was impending in Asia—a war which appeared of a very hazardous character on account not so much of the enemy as of the great distance and insecurity of communications with home. Despite this, owing to the short-sighted obstinacy of Antiochus, the struggle could only be terminated by an attack on the enemy in his own country. The Roman fleet, tasked with disrupting communication between Greece and Asia Minor during the campaign in Greece, had been successful in seizing a strong Asiatic transport fleet near Andros by the time of the battle at Thermopylae. The fleet was then employed in making preparations for the Romans' crossing into Asia the following year, with its primary objective being to drive the enemy's fleet out of the Aegean Sea. The Roman fleet was joined by 25 ships from Rhodes, ensuring its superiority in those waters.

Neither party was busy during the winter in preparing for the next campaign. Antiochus was determined to prevent the Romans from crossing into Asia. He made zealous naval preparations, employing Polyxenidas to fit out and augment the fleet stationed at Ephesus, and Hannibal to equip a new fleet in Lycia, Syria, and Phoenicia. Additionally, he collected a powerful land army in Asia Minor from all regions of his vast empire. When the Roman fleet arrived, partly from Cane and partly from the Hellespont, it was soon joined by twenty new ships from Rhodes at Samos. Polyxenidas was forced to seal himself inside the harbour of Ephesus once again.

A division meanwhile proceeded to Patara on the Lycian coast, partly to relieve the Rhodians from the troublesome attacks coming from that quarter, and chiefly to prevent the hostile fleet, for which Hannibal was expected, from entering the Aegean Sea. When the squadron sent against Patara achieved nothing, Lucius Aemilius Regillus, the new admiral who had arrived with 20 warships from Rome and relieved Gaius Livius at Samos, was indignant. He proceeded to Samos with his entire fleet; however, his officers struggled to convince him during their voyage that the primary objective was not the conquest of Patara but rather control of the Aegean Sea. They eventually managed to persuade him to return to Samos. On the mainland of Asia Minor, Seleucus had begun the siege of Pergamon. Meanwhile, Antiochus with his main army ravaged the Pergamene territory and the possessions of the Mytilenaeans on the mainland. They hoped to crush the hated Attalids before Roman aid arrived.

The fleet led by Hannibal, after being delayed by constant westerly winds, finally attempted to reach the Aegean. However, at the mouth of the Eurymedon, off Aspendus in Pamphylia, it encountered a Rhodian squadron under Eudamus. In the ensuing battle between the two fleets, the excellence of the Rhodian ships and naval officers proved decisive, carrying the victory over Hannibal's tactics despite his numerical superiority. In the Aegean Sea, the Romano-Rhodian fleet at Samos was weakened after detaching the Pergamene ships to the Hellespont to support the land army that had arrived. It was then attacked by Polyxenidas' fleet, which now numbered nine more sail than its opponents. Inscription in Saturnian verse over the temple of the Lares Permarini, built in the Campus Martius, commemorated a Roman victory for many centuries. It proclaimed to the Romans that the Asiatic fleet had been defeated before King Antiochus' eyes and that of his entire army. The inscription stated that the Romans "settled the mighty strife" and subdued the kings. The enemy's ships no longer ventured onto the open sea, and made no further attempts to obstruct the crossing of the Roman land army.

They had lost a significant amount of time, partly due to their encounters with the Aetolians and their ongoing campaign, which ultimately led the army to arrive at the Thracian Chersonese around the same time as the Battle of Myonnesus. After disembarking on the Asian shore, the Romans paused for several days to rest and wait for their leader, who was delayed due to religious obligations. Ambassadors from the great king then arrived at their camp to negotiate a peace treaty. The great king attempted to purchase peace from his adversary after the oriental custom of offering large sums of money. He proposed that he would give half of his annual revenue in exchange for a lasting peace. The great king was rewarded by the proud burgess for restoring his son, who had been taken captive, and received friendly advice to make peace on any terms.

The king's resolve to prolong the war and lure the enemy into a trap by retreating into the interior made a favorable outcome still possible. In the second division, heavy cavalry (the Cataphractae, a type of cuirassiers) were stationed on the flanks. Next to these, in the intermediate division, Gallic and Cappadocian infantry were deployed. In the centre, the phalanx formation was established, armed after the Macedonian style, with 16,000 soldiers, considered the flower of the army. However, due to the narrow space, they had to be drawn up in double files, 32 deep. The king sued for peace and accepted the terms proposed by the Romans, which, as was customary, mirrored those presented before the battle. The agreement included the cession of Asia Minor.

He himself was soon afterwards slain by the indignant inhabitants of Elymais at the head of the Persian Gulf, on account of pillaging the temple of Bel, with the treasures of which he had sought to replenish his empty coffers. The Roman government, having achieved victory, had to organize the affairs of Asia Minor and Greece. The burgesses of Pergamum, under the vigorous guidance of their presidents who had thereby become hereditary princes, had rid themselves of the unworthy yoke. The fair afterbloom of Hellenic art, which had recently emerged afresh from the soil, had grown out of these last Hellenic wars sustained by a national public spirit.

There is no warrant for the former objection, as directed against this movement in particular. It was, on the contrary, a necessary consequence of this policy, following the Roman state's interference in Hellenic affairs, which it had done. The missiles of Roman slingers and archers, which often tipped the balance against the Celts unfamiliar with such weapons, much like firearms have in more recent times turned the scale against savage tribes, forced the heights. The Celts then succumbed to a battle that bore parallels to others on the Po and Seine rivers, but this event stands out as singular, mirroring the emergence of this northern race among Greek and Phrygian nations. The state of Syria was thus completely dislodged from the west both by land and sea. This was a significant indication of the feeble and loose organization of the kingdom of the Seleucidae, that it alone among all the great states conquered by Rome never again sought a second appeal to the decision of arms after its initial conquest.

Cappadocia Ariarathes, king of Cappadocia, whose land lay beyond the boundary set by the Romans for their protectorate, escaped with a fine of 600 talents. This amount was later reduced to half its original value on the intercession of his son-in-law Eumenes. Phocaea also received its territory and freedom as compensation for its capitulation, despite being plundered by the Roman fleet. There was a dispute between Eumenes and Antiochus over Pamphylia. They disagreed about its location in relation to the prescribed boundary, with some arguing it fell entirely within the designated area and others claiming it extended beyond it.

After the Roman fleet had made an expedition to Crete and had accomplished the release of the Romans sold into slavery, the fleet and land army left Asia at the end of summer 566; on which occasion the land army, marching through Thrace, suffered greatly due to the general's negligence as it was attacked by barbarians along the route. The Roman consul Marcus Fulvius Nobilior landed in Rome. He arrived among the legions in the spring of 565. After fifteen days' siege, he gained possession of Ambracia through a capitulation honourable for the garrison. Meanwhile, the Macedonians, Illyrians, Epirots, Acarnanians, and Achaeans attacked the Aetolians simultaneously. The Aetolians lost all cities and territories held by their enemies, particularly Ambracia, which later became free and independent following a plot hatched in Rome against Marcus Fulvius. They also gave up Oenia to the Acarnanians, as well as Cephallenia.

The inhabitants of Same, fearing they would be displaced from their strategically located town by a Roman colony, rebelled after their initial surrender. A four-month siege ensued, but the town ultimately fell, and its entire population was sold into slavery. In Thrace, the interior remained under Macedonian protection. The coastal towns and islands of Thasos and Lemnos, however, were effectively controlled by Philip, as was the Chersonese, which was specifically granted to Eumenes. It became clear that Eumenes had received possessions in Europe, primarily so that he could maintain control over both Asia and Macedonia in times of need. The exasperation of a proud and chivalrous king was natural. It was not a hindrance, but rather an unavoidable political necessity that led the Romans to take this course.

They had, in the course of the war with Antiochus, gratified their long-cherished wish to bring the whole Peloponnesus into their confederacy. First Sparta, then Elis, and after the expulsion of the Asiatics from Greece, Messene joined the alliance. The Achaeans, tormented by their insatiable thirst for aggrandizement, refused to relinquish their hold on the town of Pleuron in Aetolia, which they had occupied during the war. Instead, they made it an involuntary member of their confederacy. They purchased Zacynthus from Amynander, the lieutenant of the last possessor, and also expressed interest in acquiring Aegina. There was a profound sense of injustice and a deeper feeling of sorrow in the fact that Rome, despite its sincere efforts to establish freedom and gain the gratitude of the Greeks, instead brought only chaos and reaped only scorn.

Every one was constantly listening to learn the sentiments of Rome, the liberal man no less than the servile. They thanked heaven when the dreaded decree was not issued; they were sulky when the senate told them it was in their best interest to yield voluntarily, lest they be compelled. In doing so, they did what they had to do, if possible, in a way that was offensive to the Romans, "to save face." They reported, explained, postponed, evaded, and ultimately yielded with a patriotic sigh. Four years after Sparta's nominal admission to the confederacy, matters escalated into open war. Nabis' policy of granting citizenship to slaves was reversed, and they were sold back into slavery. The city used the proceeds to build a colonnade in Megalopolis. The old system of property in Sparta was reinstated, and Achaean laws replaced those of Lycurgus. The walls were demolished. The senate bore the Achaean candour's sarcasm with exemplary composure, manifesting a culpable indifference as the worst outrages were committed.

There was joyous celebration in Achaia when, following the restoration, news arrived from Rome that the Senate had criticized it, but did not repeal it. There was no state that the Romans would have deemed worthy of fear. It is more certain that, while the Roman senate deemed it beneath its dignity to have the old man hunted out in his last asylum, Flamininus, whose restless vanity sought after new opportunities for great achievements, undertook on his own part to deliver Rome from Hannibal as he had delivered the Greeks from their chains.

When he was born, Rome was struggling for control of Sicily; by then, he had lived long enough to see the West conquered and his own last battle with the Romans against their ships. He was forced to remain a spectator as Rome dominated the East, just like a tempest that overpowers an uncrewed ship. The charges of corruption and embezzlement, directed against him and even more so against his brother Lucius, were baseless accusations that failed to explain the intensity of the animosity; however, it was characteristic of the man to instead defend himself by presenting his account-books, rather than destroying them as he did in the presence of the people and his accusers. He then invited the Romans to accompany him to the temple of Jupiter to celebrate the anniversary of his victory at Zama. Genuine pride protects the heart, while arrogance lays it open to every blow and every sarcastic remark, ultimately corroding even a noble-spirited individual.

They then obtained a guarantee of their constitution and letters from the kings in Corinth. Flamininus also sent them similar letters. The only detail we can glean from these letters is that the embassy was deemed successful by the decree. We certainly find that approximately 500 farmers from Syria made their tax payments in Alexandria. The dispute likely occurred without affecting the rights of sovereignty, as the dowry of Cleopatra was a charge on those revenues. This circumstance may have led to the subsequent disagreement. 4 CHAPTER X The Third Macedonian War

Philip of Macedonia was greatly dissatisfied with the treatment he received from the Romans following the peace treaty with Antiochus. This dissatisfaction did not dissipate, and the subsequent events only served to fuel his anger towards Rome.

Philip was allowed to retain what he had taken from the Aetolians by the Romans. However, in Thessaly, the Magnetes alone had formally joined the Aetolians. The towns Philip had wrested from the Aetolians in two other Thessalian confederacies—the narrower sense of Thessaly and Perrhaebian—were demanded back by their leagues, as Philip had only liberated these towns, not conquered them. The Athamantes also believed that they might desire their freedom; and Eumenes demanded the maritime cities which Antiochus had possessed in Thrace, specifically Aenus and Maronea. However, in the peace treaty with Antiochus, the Thracian Chersonese was expressly promised to him alone. Philip, however, was by no means the man to endure this affliction with Phoenician patience.

Passionate as he was, he had been more indignant with his faithless ally than with the honourable antagonist after his defeat. He had long been accustomed to pursue a policy not driven by Macedonian interests but by personal ambition. Seeing the war with Antiochus as an excellent opportunity for instant revenge against the ally who had deserted and betrayed him, he had seized the chance. The Romans utilized the Attalids to reconstruct the kingdom of Lysimachus in all essential points, following the destruction of which had been the most significant achievement of the Macedonian rulers after Alexander. They placed alongside Macedonia a state that equaled it in power and was also a client of Rome. In particular, his submissiveness towards Rome, which allowed him to purchase the time necessary for his objectives, proved a severe test for the fierce and haughty man. Nevertheless, he courageously endured it, despite his subjects paying dearly for the suppression of his resentment, including the unfortunate Maronea.

With this purpose in view, they made it clear that the senate forgave the father on behalf of the son; naturally, this led to dissensions within the royal household, and the king's elder son, Perseus, who was the offspring of an unequal marriage but had been destined for succession by his father, sought to undermine his brother as a rival. Philip, a king even as a boy, enjoyed good fortune during the first twenty years of his reign. However, destiny soon spoiled him. Perseus ascended to the throne in his thirty-first year. Like when he was still a boy, he had played a part in the unhappy war with Rome and had grown up under the pressure of humiliation. As a result, he inherited not only the kingdom from his father but also his troubles, resentments, and hopes. It is a significant indication of character that after defeat, the father first hastened to destroy the papers in his cabinet that might compromise him. In contrast, the son took his treasure chests and embarked on a different path.

He followed the same path in Macedonia that Augustus later adopted when he rebuilt the foundations of the Roman Empire. It is strange that Philip did not adopt the Roman style of organizing his army, like Hannibal. However, we can understand this decision when we recall the high esteem in which the Macedonians held their phalanx, an infantry formation often defeated, yet still widely regarded as virtually unbeatable. The plan was foiled by the determined resistance of the Dardani and their allied tribes. The Bastarnae were forced to retreat, and the entire horde was swept away as they attempted to return home due to the melting of the ice on the Danube.

As a matter of course, the entire national party among both Asiatic and European Greeks had become Macedonian in nature. This was not due to isolated acts of unrighteousness by the Roman deliverers, but rather because the restoration of Hellenic nationality by a foreign power involved a contradiction in terms. Now that it was too late, everyone realized that the most detestable form of Macedonian rule was less evil for Greece than a free constitution born from the noblest intentions of honourable foreigners. The Roman envoys at the Achaean diet as early as 581 stated plainly that an alliance with Perseus was equivalent to breaking off the alliance with Rome. Even among the patriotic party, who had not agreed with those social movements nor reached as high as the longing for prudent neutrality, they had no idea of throwing themselves into Perseus' arms. Moreover, the opposition party there, now brought about by Roman influence, had attached itself absolutely to Rome.

Carthage, Genthius of Illyria, Rhodes, and the free cities of Asia Minor, along with Byzantium, had previously been very friendly with Perseus. They offered the Romans their warships, but the Romans declined. Antiochus IV Epiphanes was a king of the Seleucid Empire who ruled from 175 to 164 BCE. He is known for his persecution of Jews in Judea and his desecration of the Temple in Jerusalem, which led to the Maccabean Revolt. The king of Syria, styled as "the God, the Brilliant Bringer of Victory," took action, distinguishing himself from his father, who was known as the Great. He focused on capturing the Syrian coast during this war, successfully separating it from the weak and ineffective Egypt. The Romans invaded Thessaly. The consul left a strong division in Illyria to harass Macedonia from the west, while with the main force he began his campaign from Apollonia towards Thessaly.

The Romans refused his request. They had never concluded peace following a defeat, and in this case, the conclusion of peace would have meant the loss of Greece. The Roman commander, despite his lax and unsuccessful management of the war, was unaware of how to launch a successful attack. His army wandered aimlessly through Thessaly, failing to achieve any significant accomplishments. Perseus, though a skilled soldier, did not possess the same military acumen as his father. Having prepared for a defensive campaign, he found himself ill-equipped to adapt when circumstances shifted, leaving him feeling incapacitated.

Discharges and furloughs could be purchased, so the divisions never reached their full strength. In the summer, the men were quartered, while the officers plundered in larger quantities, and the common soldiers did so on a smaller scale. The military outcome of the initial two campaigns was inconclusive, whereas the political repercussions left a lasting negative impact on the Romans. Their exceptional achievements in the east were largely due to their reputation for moral integrity and stability, which contrasted sharply with the scandals surrounding Hellenic governance. A handful of resolute men might have blocked the route, making retreat impossible. Even after the passage, with the Macedonian main force in front and the fortified mountainfortresses of Tempe and Lapathus behind him, wedged into a narrow plain on the shore without supplies or foraging options, his position was no less desperate than when he had been similarly surrounded during his first consulate in the Ligurian defiles, which later bore his name.

He advanced without hindrance, but was forced to turn back after four days' march due to lack of provisions. When the king regained his senses and hastened to resume his position, the Roman army would have been in great danger, had it not been that the impregnable Tempe surrendered at the right moment and handed over its stores to the enemy. The Roman army remained in the farthest corner of Thessaly for the rest of the summer and winter. The crossing of the passes was a success, marking the first substantial achievement in the war. However, it was not due to the Roman army's ability, but rather the Macedonian general's blundering efforts. Lucius Aemilius Paullus was the son of the consul Lucius Aemilius Paullus who fell at Cannae. He belonged to the old nobility but lived a humble life, which affected his performance in the comitia. In contrast, he excelled on the battlefield, particularly in Spain and Liguria.

Perseus is driven back to Pydna. The new general's arrival at Heracleum prompts him to order a surprise attack on the ill-guarded pass at Pythium by Publius Nasica. Meanwhile, skirmishes between the outposts in the channel of the river Elpius keep the Macedonians occupied, forcing them to retreat to Pydna. There was an eclipse of the moon on September 4, 586, or June 22 in the Julian calendar. A scientific Roman officer announced beforehand that the eclipse might not be a bad omen, providing a means to determine the date. However, the outposts accidentally clashed while watering their horses after midday. Both sides immediately decided to engage in battle, which had originally been intended for the following day. They were scarce in position when the formidable phalanx attacked them. The general, who had witnessed many hard fights, later admitted that he had trembled.

The war was at an end, on the fifteenth day after Paullus had assumed command; all Macedonia submitted within two days. He then wrote to the consul, but the letter was not received because he had declared himself king in it. Perseus died a few years later as a state prisoner at Alba, near the Fucine lake. His son later earned a living in the same Italian town as a clerk.

The empire of Alexander the Great came to an end, having conquered and culturally assimilated the eastern regions, 144 years after the death of its founder. The state most affected by this change in the balance of power was the kingdom of the Attalids, which had been created and supported by Rome to maintain control over Macedonia. After the destruction of Macedonia, the kingdom was no longer relevant. The king, after paying his respects to the consul, took the Pergamene fleet home at the same time as the Roman fleet entered its winter quarters.

The Pergamenes gained little territory from the spoils of Macedonia. After their victory over Antiochus, the Romans, having preserved the form of respect for Philip, were now inclined to harm and humiliate. The king was long silent. At last, he spoke, saying he desired nothing more and set sail again. The first dissensions with Rome arose from the rising of the Lycians, who were handed over to Rhodes after Antiochus' defeat. The Lycians claimed they were not subjects but allies of the Rhodians, and this was accepted by the Roman senate, which intervened to clarify the meaning of the peace treaty.

In a republic governed by primary assemblies, various caprices are possible. However, an insane intervention by a commercial city, which could only have been resolved after the fall of the Pass of Tempe became known in Rhodes, requires special explanation. The key to it lies in the well-documented account that consul Quintus Marcius, known for his "new-fashioned diplomacy," requested civilities from the Rhodian envoy Agepolis at Heracleum. Following the occupation of Tempe's pass, he made an underhanded appeal to mediate a peace. The Roman Republic's shortsightedness and arrogance ultimately led to its downfall. The Rhodians, believing the Romans had lost all hope, sought to play a mediating role among four major powers simultaneously. They established communication with Perseus, but Rhodian envoys with Macedonian leanings revealed more than they should have, drawing unwanted attention.

The alliance requested by the allies was initially denied, but it was reinstated in 590 following intense appeals. The war appears to have been begun by Egypt. King Antiochus Epiphanes gladly embraced the opportunity to once again attempt to achieve the traditional goal of the Seleucid policy, which was the acquisition of Egypt, while the Romans were engaged in Macedonia. The king of Egypt at that time, Ptolemy VI Philometor, son of Cleopatra, had not yet reached adulthood when he was advised by incompetent counselors. Following a significant victory on the Syro-Egyptian border, Antiochus was able to enter his nephew's territories in the same year that Roman legions arrived in Greece (583).

The Syrian king was disturbed by events in his own kingdom. Upon returning from Egypt, he discovered that the brothers had reached a mutual agreement while he was away. He then resumed the war against both. As he lay before Alexandria, not long after the battle of Pydna in 186 BCE, the Roman envoy Gaius Popillius, a harsh and rude man, arrived. He conveyed to him the senate's command: restore all that you had conquered and vacate Egypt within a set term. There was a Macedonian party in every city. Trials for high treason began in all parts of Greece.

The Romans likely established the practice of deporting people to Italy, at least in part, to avoid such atrocities. The vanquished foe held at least an equal position with Roman diplomatists, often better than their faithful allies. In contrast, defeated opponents were reinstated, while those who attempted to reinstate themselves were humbled – as the Aetolians, Macedonia after the Asiatic war, Rhodes, and Pergamum learned through experience. It is true that the former view naturally suggests itself: Sallust is right when he makes Mithradates say that the wars of Rome with tribes, cities, and kings originated from one prime cause - an insatiable longing for dominion and riches. However, it is an error to give forth this judgment as a historical fact, influenced by passion and the event.

That they did not exhibit moderation after victory, especially in Italy's interest, is clear. The retention of Spain, the guardianship of Africa, and the half-fanciful plan to bring liberty to Greece were grave errors from an Italian perspective. The causes of these errors lay on one hand in a blind fear of Carthage, and on the other in an even blinder enthusiasm for Hellenic liberty. During this period, the Romans showed little inclination towards conquest, instead displaying a prudent fear of it. The ancient world knew nothing of a balance of power among nations. As a result, every nation that had achieved internal unity sought to either directly subdue its neighbors or make them harmless. The Hellenic states followed the former approach, while Rome employed the latter method. This effort ultimately led to subjugation.

The legal dissolution of the Boeotian confederacy, however, did not occur at this time, but rather after the destruction of Corinth. vii 6) 4 No gold coins from the four Macedonian kingdoms are extant. It is likely that the gold mines were closed, or the extracted gold was melted down to produce bars. During a brief period, when they were likely depleted (596-608), the number of them is surprisingly high, suggesting that the mines were extensively mined or that the old royal currency was reissued in significant quantities. 7

We have already indicated that the plebeian party contained within it the character from the first as well as, and in some sense more decisively than, the patriciate. For while an absolute equality of rights prevailed among the old body of burgesses, the new constitution established a distinction between the senatorial houses, who were privileged in both burgess rights and burgess usufructs, and the mass of other citizens. Upon receiving the text, I found several errors that needed correction. Here is the revised version:

Immediately after the abolition of the patriciate and the formal establishment of civic equality, a new aristocracy and its corresponding opposition emerged; we have already demonstrated how the former merged with the fallen patriciate and how this led to the first movements of the new party of progress being intertwined with the last movements of the old opposition between the social classes. The formation of these new parties began in the 5th century, but they assumed their definitive shape only in the following century. But the freedom hitherto enjoyed in the commonwealth was undermined, and the foundation for future revolutions was laid during this epoch. The development of Rome would remain imperfect if we fail to consider the strength of the underlying forces, the gradual growth of power, and the ominous signs that foretold its impending collapse.

The result of the Licinian laws in reality was almost equivalent to creating a class of peers. The nobility was not content with its honorary privileges, which were matters of comparative indifference. Instead, they strove for separate and sole political power and sought to convert the most important institutions of the state—the senate and the equestrian order—from organs of the commonwealth into organs of the plebeio-patrician aristocracy. This enabled us to understand in some degree why the equites during the Sicilian war refused to obey the order of Consul Gaius Aurelius Cotta, instructing them to work at the trenches alongside legionaries. When Cato was commander-in-chief of the army in Spain, he found himself compelled to issue a severe reprimand to his cavalry.

The innovation was met with much censure, even from the ruling class, as it was deemed invidious and useless. This stance contradicted the efforts of the more prudent portion of the aristocracy to maintain their exclusive government under the guise of civil equality. Admission to the Roman Senate was generally open to members of the ruling families regardless of ability. In contrast, the poorer and humbler ranks of the population were completely barred from accessing government offices. Additionally, all Roman citizens who were not part of the hereditary aristocracy were effectively excluded from holding the two highest magistracies: consulship and censorship. However, if you'd like me to suggest some improvements:

After Manius Curius and Gaius Fabricius, there is no instance that can be pointed out of a consul who did not belong to the social aristocracy. It is likely that no such instance occurred.

It was already a singular event when Paullus, who had been named commander-in-chief against Perseus, instead of tendering his thanks in the usual manner to the citizens, declared to them that he presumed they had chosen him as general because they considered him the most capable of command. He then requested them accordingly not to help him to command, but to remain silent and obey. The management of finances in the Roman community did not progress significantly during this period. Their revenue was clearly on the rise. The Roman state received considerable sums from various sources during this period. Notably, the spoils of war with Antiochus yielded 200 million sestertii (2,000,000 pounds), while the war with Perseus brought in 210 million sestertii (2,100,000 pounds). The latter sum was the largest single influx into the Roman treasury.

The provinces, with Sicily perhaps excepted, likely cost nearly as much as they yielded. The expenditure on highways and other structures increased in proportion to the expansion of territory. Additionally, the repayment of advances received from freeholder burgesses during times of severe war formed a burden for many years on the Roman treasury. The state's performance was generally satisfactory as regards its revenue farming and supply contracts for buildings. This can be gauged from the fact that the senate resolved in 587 to cease operating the Macedonian mines that had fallen to Rome. The reason for this decision was that the lessees of the minerals would either plunder the local population or cheat the treasury. Despite appearances, it is clear that even under the intense pressure of war, this era fell short of the previous epoch's last section in terms of public infrastructure development. Notably, there were no new aqueducts built in Rome between 482 and 607.

The treasure of the state undoubtedly increased; by the last reserve in 545, when they found themselves under the necessity of laying hands on it, amounted only to 164,000 pounds (of gold); whereas a short time after the close of this period, around 597, the treasury was filled with approximately 860,000 pounds in precious metals. However, I must inform you that If you provide the actual text with errors or inaccuracies, I would be happy to assist you in correcting it. Although some communities, such as Velitrae in the Volscian territory, Teanum and Cumae in Campania, may have retained their earlier legal status, it is generally considered that this franchise of a passive character has been superseded.

Only a few communities in this category, such as Neapolis, Nola, Rhegium, and Heraclea, had remained steadfast on the Roman side throughout the war's vicissitudes. As a result, they retained their former rights as allies unchanged. The majority of the other communities were forced to accept a revised treaty that disadvantaged them. The reduced position of non-Latin allies is attested by the emigration from their communities into the Latin. In 577, the Samnites and Paelignians applied to the senate for a reduction of their contingents. Their request was based on the fact that during late years, approximately 4,000 Samnite and Paelignian families had migrated to the Latin colony of Fregellae. The Latins, which term now denoted the few towns in old Latium that were not included in the Roman burgess-union, such as Tibur and Praeneste, the allied cities placed on the same footing with them, such as several of the Hernican towns, and the Latin colonies dispersed throughout Italy, were still at this time in a better position. It cannot be shown that even after Italy's complete subjugation, a single Italian community exchanged its position as an ally for the Roman franchise. In reality, none did so after that date. Even the transition of individual Italians to the Roman franchise was largely confined to magistrates from Latin communities and, by special favour, non-burgesses who were admitted to share it at the founding of burgess-colonies. It cannot be denied that these changes, both de facto and de jure, in the relations of Italian subjects, exhibit at least an intimate connection and consistency.

The situation of the subject classes had deteriorated in proportion to the previous gradations. While the government had previously attempted to soften the distinctions and provide means for transition between them, it now abandoned the intermediate links and broke down the connecting bridges. As within the Roman burgess-body, the ruling class separated itself from the people, withdrawing uniformly from public burdens and taking for itself the honors and advantages. In turn, the burgesses asserted their distinction from the Italian confederacy, excluding it more and more from joint rule, while transferring a double or triple share of common burdens to it. The abolition of the passive burgesses cannot be censured in itself. The motive that led to it is likely related to another issue that should be discussed separately; however, the loss of an intermediate link resulted from this abolition.

The disappearance of the distinction between the Latin and other Italian communities was far more fraught with peril. However, such distinctions, which imply a gradation of classes among the subjects, could not afford even a remote compensation for the earlier contrast between cognate and alien Italian subjects. The proposal made in the senate after the battle at Cannae, to grant Roman citizenship and a seat in the senate to two men from each Latin community, was made at an unseasonable time and rightly rejected. However, it reveals the apprehension with which the ruling community viewed the relations between Latium and Rome.

The fact that they still maintained moderation in the imposition of burdens was of little consequence, as compared to the conversion of their sovereignty into a right that yielded profit. The fall was the same, whether one apple was taken or the entire tree was plundered. Cato, as governor of Sardinia, appeared in the towns under his authority on foot, accompanied by a single servant who carried his coat and sacrificial ladle. Upon returning home from his Spanish governorship, he sold his war-horse before departing, for he did not consider himself entitled to burden the state with the costs of transporting it. The Roman governors, although few of them exhibited conscientiousness to an extreme degree, like Cato, made a lasting impression on their subjects, particularly on the frivolous and unstable Greeks. This was largely due to their old-fashioned piety, the reverence with which they conducted themselves at meals, and the gravity of their demeanor. Their administration of office and justice was also notable for its uprightness, especially in dealing with the worst elements among the provincials - the Roman revenue-farmers and bankers who preyed on them.

It was a rare occurrence—and even rarer because the government adhered strictly to its old principle of not paying public officials—that a governor returned with clean hands from his province. This was particularly noteworthy, as Paullus, the conqueror of Pydna, had done so without accepting any payment. The liberties which Roman magistrates allowed themselves to take at the close of this period not only with unhappy subjects but also with dependent free-states and kingdoms are illustrated by the raids of Gaius Volso in Asia Minor, and above all by the scandalous proceedings in Greece during the war with Perseus. Control over the governors was a key aspect of the Senate's supervision of the provinces and their respective governors. The government should not have been caught off guard by such events, as it had failed to implement any meaningful checks on the abuses of power by this erratic military administration. Although according to the universal but more questionable rule that allows no complaint to be brought against a commander-in-chief during their term of office, the Roman governor could normally be held accountable only after the mischief had been committed. However, they were still subject to both criminal and civil prosecution.

The opposition relied on the support of the public assembly, just as the nobility relied on the senate. To comprehend the opposition, we must first describe the Roman citizenry during this time, examining its spirit and its role within the commonwealth. The machinery, however, by means of which the burgesses intervened in public affairs became increasingly unwieldy. The circumstances that arose from their own efforts far exceeded their ability to manage them. We have already stated that in the course of this epoch, most of the former communities of passive burgesses, as well as a considerable number of newly established colonies, received the full Roman franchise. At the close of this period, the Roman burgess-body occupied Latium in its widest sense, including Sabina and a part of Campania. It stretched from Caere on the west coast to Cumae on the south coast, covering most of the region. The only cities not included within this district were Tibur, Praeneste, Signia, Norba, and Ferentinum.

To some extent, the unwieldiness of a civic community was remedied by the deputy judges previously mentioned for the purposes of justice and administration. The maritime and new Picenian and TransApennine colonies already exhibited the first signs of the system that would later organize smaller urban communities within the great city-commonwealth of Rome. The government, whether due to a lack of judgment, negligence, or any malicious intent, no longer enrolled new communities into the franchise after 513 newly established election districts. Instead, it included them along with other communities in the existing old districts. As a result, each tribe gradually came to be composed of different townships scattered throughout the entire Roman territory. It was foolish and utterly ridiculous to leave the decision of the highest and most difficult questions that the power ruling the world had to solve to a well-disposed but fortuitous concourse of Italian farmers. The nomination of generals and the conclusion of treaties of state should be finally judged by people who understood neither the grounds nor the consequences of their decrees.

Thus, for instance, old penny collections, previously held mainly for religious purposes and at the burial of men of merit, were now used by lords of high standing. This was first done by Lucius Scipio in 568, on the occasion of a popular festival he had planned, to collect contributions from the public on extraordinary occasions. The senators began to collect regular tribute from their clients under this name. Corruption in the spoil was not limited to the forum, but it also spread to the camp.

The decline of the warlike spirit among the military discipline and martial spirit of the burgesses can be traced in the campaigns against Perseus. The conversion of war into a traffic in plunder led to a significant spread of cowardice, which was manifest almost scandalously during the insignificant Istrian War in 576. During the Hannibal War (546 BCE), censors took action against those who evaded military service due to their social status, as recorded in the equestrian census. The pursuit of titles and insignia is a stark indicator of the decline of genuine pride and honor across all social classes.

There was a need for acquiescence, but those generals who had in vain sought or had no hope of gaining approval from the senate or the burgesses, marched in triumph to the Alban Mount at least as early as 523. While formerly the commander-in-chief had reckoned it an honour to serve next year on the staff of his successor, the fact that consular Cato took service as a military tribune under Tiberius Sempronius Longus (560) and Manius Glabrio (563), was now regarded as a demonstration against the new-fashioned arrogance. Although the two tendencies cannot be wholly separated, they go hand in hand in various respects. It is necessary to consider them separately.

Cato, the last statesman of note, belonged to an earlier system that confined its ideas to Italy and was opposed to universal empire. As a result, he was later regarded as a model of a genuine Roman in the antique style; he may be considered more accurately the representative of the opposition of the Roman middle class to the new Hellenistic-cosmopolitan nobility. Under the leadership of Flaccus, and following the traditional practice of serving his fellow citizens and the commonwealth in counsel and action, Cato rose to the consulship and achieved a triumph, and eventually became censor. He publicly presented his list of grievances against his noble colleagues, one by one. He was not particularly meticulous in providing evidence, nor did he show any restraint when it came to those who had personally wronged or provoked him.

In 570, Cato and his like-minded patrician colleague, Lucius Flaccus, sought censorship. Beforehand, they announced their intention to thoroughly purify the burgess-body through all its ranks in office. Despite the nobility's efforts, these two men were elected by the burgesses, who had been deterred by the nobles' exertions. The great purge then took place, removing among others the brother of Africanus from the equites and the brother of the Greeks' deliverer from the senate. On the other hand, the serious emergencies of war led the Roman government to make an attempt — fortunately unsuccessful — to recruit their armies in the Oriental style from the slave market. This compelled them to modify the qualifications previously required for service in the burgess army. The formal retention of earlier rates, despite an overall increase in men's disposable income, inherently entailed an expansion of voting rights in a democratic context.

The total number of divisions remained unchanged. In the past, as we have stated, the 18 equestrian centuries and the 80 from the first class held a majority in the 193 voting centuries. However, following the reformed arrangement, the votes of the first class were reduced to 70. As a result, under all circumstances, at least the second-grade centuries came to vote. From the time that non-freehold burgesses had been enrolled among the tribes, they too came into being during the centuries. While restricted to the four urban divisions in the comitia tributato, they formally enjoyed the same rights as freehold burgesses. However, it is likely that the censorial prerogative intervened in the composition of the centuries, granting non-freehold burgesses enrolled in rural tribes a preponderance in the centuriate assembly. The levelling spirit of the party of progress suggested the abolition of distinctions within the middle class. At the same time, the chasm between burgesses and non-burgesses was both widened and deepened.

Whether the disease could be remedied at all by human skill remains fairly open to question. The Roman reformers of this period seem to have been good citizens rather than good statesmen, and they conducted the great struggle between old civism and new cosmopolitanism on their part in a somewhat inadequate and narrow-minded manner. In his caustic manner, the old man describes these fops, who were modeled after the Greek orators of the agora. They dealt in jests and witticisms, sang and danced, and were ready for anything. In his opinion, such a person was good for nothing but to exhibit themselves as harlequin in a procession and engage in conversation with the public - he would sell either his talk or his silence for a bit of bread. The reformers insisted above all else on moral reform. In contrast, demagogues prioritized limiting the government's powers and expanding those of the burgesses.

In 538, following the Battle of Cannae, the government appointed a dictator who held active command. However, during more peaceful periods, they were unable to nominate another dictator. At various times, including the most recent instance in 552, a dictator might be appointed following an earlier suggestion from the town's representatives. However, the position gradually became obsolete due to its informal nature. It was therefore significant, although not of great political importance, that at this time (before 542), while election into the colleges remained on its former footing, the designation of the presidents — curiones and pontifices — was transferred from the colleges to the community.

In this instance, with a genuine respect for traditional Roman practices, it was decided to err on the side of caution and only allow a small number of tribes to participate in the electoral process, thereby excluding the general population. Spurius Cassius had likely proposed the same idea two hundred years earlier; however, the two measures shared similarities but differed fundamentally. Cassius presented a matter affecting the community when it was still in power and self-governing, whereas Flaminius submitted a question of state to the primary assembly of a great empire. Publius Scipio, although setting a fashion among the nobility for arrogance, title-hunting, and client-making, also sought support for his personal policy of opposition to the senate. He charmed the multitude with the dazzling effect of his personal qualities and bribed them with his generosity of grain. In the legions, he courted their favour by any means necessary, regardless of right or wrong. Above all, he won the loyalty of his clients, both high-ranking and lowly, who personally adhered to him.

While they did not stop the evil at its source, it was to little purpose that those who were better disposed listened with anxiety to the growing flood and worked at building dikes and dams. To the later generations who had survived the storms of revolution, the period that followed the Hannibal War appeared as the golden age of Rome. And Cato was seen as the model of the Roman statesman. During no epoch did the Roman constitution remain formally so stable as in the period from the Sicilian War to the Third Macedonian War, and for a generation beyond it. However, the stability of the constitution was here, as everywhere, not a sign of the health of the state, but rather an indication of impending sickness and revolution.

These insignia likely belonged to the nobility from their earliest appearance. e To the agnate descendants of curule magistrates, though their decorations followed a similar pattern, they eventually expanded to include a broader range of individuals over time. Also, as was the case with the silver decorations, which remained a symbol of nobility even during the Second Punic War. xxvi In ancient Rome, the purple border of the boys' toga was initially reserved for the sons of curule magistrates.
