Hasdrubal, however, was forced to yield to his antagonist's demands. He agreed to surrender deserters, return exiles, deliver arms, and have his troops march off under the yoke. As part of the agreement, Hasdrubal would pay 100 talents annually for the next fifty years, equivalent to 24,000 pounds.

The circumstance that everything which they could ask was guaranteed, except for the city, and that nothing was said regarding stopping the embarkation of the troops for Africa, revealed the Roman intentions very clearly. The Senate acted with fearful harshness, yet it did not assume a semblance of concession. The Carthaginians, however, remained unmoved; no statesman possessed the authority to sway the volatile masses of the city either towards determined resistance or unconditional surrender. When they heard simultaneously about the dreadful decree of war and the tolerable demand for hostages, they promptly agreed to the latter and held onto hope, despite lacking the courage to fully grasp the implications of surrendering themselves beforehand to the arbitrary will of a mortal enemy.

The Carthaginians understood that the generals would not and could not accept this request, as it had been rejected once before. The consuls, however, were convinced by this refusal in their natural assumption that after the initial outbreak of despair, the defenceless city would eventually submit. As a result, they postponed the attack. This promontory, at its narrowest point, is approximately two miles wide and lies entirely on a flat surface. It then expands towards the gulf and terminates there at the foot of the Jebel-Khawi and Sidi bu Said mountains, with the plain of El Mersa stretching between them. The city of Carthage lay on the southern portion of the peninsula, ending at the height of Sidi Bou Said.

The old city, suburb, and necropolis occupied the entire breadth of the promontory on its side next to the gulf. They were accessible only by the two highways leading to Utica and Tunis along that narrow strip of land, which although not enclosed by a wall, provided an advantageous position for armies taking their stand under the protection of the capital, with the aim of protecting it in return. Manius Manilius, who commanded the land army, pitched his camp opposite the wall of the citadel. Lucius Censorinus stationed himself with the fleet on the lake. The besieged succeeded in filling up a great part of the breach during the night, and their sortie damaged the Roman machines to such an extent that they were unable to operate the following day.

The heat of summer and the departure of Censorinus, a more capable general, contributed to the diseases that spread in the camp. Massinissa's ill-humour and inaction, as he was not pleased to see the Romans taking the spoils he had long desired, further hindered their progress. The king's death at the age of ninety soon after (end of 605) brought an end to the Roman offensive operations. While the course of the war was unglorious for the general and the army, the military tribune Scipio distinguished himself brilliantly. After fulfilling Massinissa's dying charge, he divided his kingdom among his three sons: Micipsa, Gulussa, and Mastanabal. He then brought a capable cavalry leader to the Roman army in Gulussa, thereby addressing the serious lack of light cavalry that had been felt for some time.

Clupea repulsed him, forcing him to retreat in disarray from Hippo Diarrhytus after losing the entire summer before it and having his siege engines destroyed twice. Once this threat was neutralized, the general moved on to Piso's camp to take command of the army and bring it back to Carthage. Meanwhile, Scipio confined the besieged within the city itself, cutting off all communication with the outside world.

Scipio therefore constructed a stone mole, 96 feet broad, running from the tongue of land between the lake and the gulf into the gulf, so as to close the mouth of the harbour. While the Roman labourers were constructing the mole, work continued day and night for two months in the Carthaginian harbour, with even the deserters unable to discern the designs of the besieged. As the Romans finished building the barrier across the harbor entrance, fifty Carthaginian triremes and various boats and skiffs set sail from the same harbor into the gulf. Meanwhile, the enemy were attempting to close the old harbor mouth towards the south. The Carthaginians had created a new outlet by constructing a canal in an easterly direction, which proved impassable due to the sea's depth at that location.

They gained enough time to close the breach. Scipio, however, re-established the machines and set fire to the wooden towers of the enemy. This allowed him to gain possession of the quay and the outer harbor. The capture of the city was a result of the fearlessness with which the mighty agencies had labored in the work of destruction during the interval. Hasdrubal, who sought to vaunt and gormandize, appeared soon after the Roman army's arrival in the spring of 608 to attack the inner town. The senate ordered the general to level the city of Carthage and its surrounding suburb of Magalia with the ground. He was also instructed to do the same with all townships that had been under Carthaginian control up until that point. Afterward, he was to plow over the site of Carthage to formally bring an end to the city's existence.

Scipio, who had been destined for a nobler role than that of an executioner, gazed at his work with horror; instead of feeling triumphant, he was consumed by a sense of foreboding about the consequences of his actions. The real gainers, however, were the Roman merchants, who flocked to Utica as soon as Carthage lay in ashes. From this new base, they began to turn a profit not only from the Roman province but also from the Numidian and Gaetulian regions, which had previously been closed to them. Neither the commissions sent by the senate nor the foreign arbiters, such as Scipio Aemilianus, were able to establish any tolerable order.

He turned to Demetrius Soter, the king of Syria, his mother's brother. With Thracian support, Philip the so-called invader conquered Macedonia. Although defeated at first, he gained one victory over the Macedonian militia in the district of Odomantice beyond the Strymon River, followed by another on the west side of the river, which gave him control over all of Macedonia. Philip fled to the chieftain Byzes in Thrace. Metellus followed him and, after a second victory, obtained his surrender.

The possessions in Epirus, formerly detached from Macedonia after the first Roman victories, included the Ionian islands and the ports of Apollonia and Epidamnus. These had been under the jurisdiction of Italian magistrates until now, when they were reunited with Macedonia. As a result, Macedonia extended its reach north-west to a point beyond Scodra, where Illyria began. It had no longer been a unified kingdom, but rather a province, retaining its communal and local organization, albeit under an Italian governor and quaestor. Their names appeared on the native coins alongside that of the country. A few years after the subjugation of pseudo-Philip, another pretended son of Perseus, Alexander, raised the banner of insurrection on the Nestus. He had collected 1600 men in a short time. However, the quaestor Lucius Tremellius mastered the insurrection without difficulty and pursued the fugitive pretender as far as Dardania.

The bitterest abettors of the now dominant party were Lyciscus the Aetolian, Mnasippus the Boeotian, Chrematas the Acarnanian, and Epirot Charops. The infamous Epirotian was even forbidden by honourable Romans from entering their homes. One after another, they descended into the grave. Another generation grew up, where old recollections and antagonisms had faded. The senate replied as usual that it would send a commission to investigate the matter. However, instead of providing this response, the envoys falsely claimed in Achaia and Sparta that the senate had already made a decision in their favor. Diaeus, the instigator of all this mischief, zealously led the war, while at the same time he gave assurances of the full loyalty of the Achaean league to the dreaded commandant of Macedonia.

When the Achaean deputies learned of this, they rushed immediately to the market-place without even hearing the Romans' demands to an end, and communicated them to the multitude; whereupon the governing and governed rabble with one voice resolved to arrest at once all Lacedaemonians present in Corinth, for it was said that Sparta had brought on this misfortune. The arrest took place in a tumultuous manner, with possession of Laconian names or shoes being sufficient grounds for imprisonment. In fact, they even entered the dwellings of Roman envoys to seize Lacedaemonians who had taken shelter there. Hard words were uttered against the Romans, although they did not lay hands on their persons. He did so; but after Caesar and the Lacedaemonian envoys had waited there for a long time in vain for the Achaeans, Critolaus finally appeared alone and informed them that the general assembly of the Achaeans was solely competent in this matter. He said it could only be settled at the diet, or in other words, within six months.

The declaration of Critolaus, that they wished the Romans to be their friends but not their masters, was received with great delight. When the members of the diet wished to intervene, the mob protected the man who had won its support, and applauded his witty remarks about the high treason of the wealthy and the need for a military dictatorship, as well as the mysterious hints regarding an impending uprising by countless peoples and kings against Rome. The loss in prisoners and the dead was considerable. Critolaus was never heard of again after the battle. Diaeus, who after the fall of Critolaus resumed command, summoned all men capable of bearing arms to the isthmus. He ordered 12,000 slaves, natives of Greece, to be enrolled in the army. The rich were asked for advances, and the ranks of those who supported peace were thinned by bloody prosecutions.

The war accordingly ended, and after the same style. Meanwhile, the Achaeans, emboldened by a successful attack on the too incautious Roman outposts, offered battle to the Roman army, which was about twice as strong, at Leucopetra on the isthmus. All the cities submitted without opposition; and even the impregnable Corinth, into which Mummius had hesitated for three days because he feared an ambush, was occupied by the Romans without a blow.

Mummius rejected the proposal to throw down the statues of Philopoemen, the founder of the Achaean patriotic party. The fines imposed on the communities were intended for the injured Greek cities, and most of them were later remitted. The property of those who had parents or children was not sold on public account; instead, it was handed over to their relatives. If we review the whole catastrophe, an impartial historian must acknowledge—what the Greeks of this period themselves candidly confessed—that the Romans were not to blame for the war, but rather their own foolish perfidy and feeble temerity compelled the Roman intervention. The abolition of mock sovereignty among leagues and all vague, pernicious dreams connected with them was a blessing for the country. The government of the Roman commander-in-chief in Macedonia, though falling short of what was desired, was still far better than the previous confusion and misrule of Greek confederacies and Roman commissions.

For the Roman wholesale trade, however, Delos was of greater importance; it became a Roman free port as early as 586, attracting a significant portion of Rhodes' business, and now entered Corinth's heritage in a similar manner. In Asia Minor, following the Seleucid defeat, the kingdom of Pergamus emerged as the dominant power. We have previously discussed how the senate sought to humiliate and weaken this ally after the third Macedonian war by abandoning diplomatic efforts.

King Prusias II, surnamed the Hunter, fought in the Bithynian War. Roman intervention saved him -- although not until he had been besieged in his capital. A first warning given by the Romans had remained unattended to, and was even scoffed at by Prusias (598-600). Whether it was the intense animosity towards his subjects that had tormented the last Attalid during life, or if his decision to leave his kingdom to the Romans was simply a further acknowledgment of Rome's practical superiority, remains unclear. The commander, consul, and pontifex maximus Publius Licinius Crassus Mucianus, one of the wealthiest and most cultivated men in Rome, equally distinguished as an orator and jurist, was about to besiege the pretender in Leucae. However, during his preparations for this purpose, he allowed himself to be unexpectedly defeated by a less-regarded opponent, who then made him a prisoner in person by a Thracian band.

Aristonicus, the victor, was not long in enjoying his triumph when he was attacked by Marcus Perpenna, the successor of Crassus. His army was defeated, and he himself was besieged and captured in Stratonicea. He was subsequently executed in Rome. The subjugation of the last towns that still offered resistance was completed, along with the definitive regulation of the country, following the sudden death of Perennius, who had been succeeded by Manius Aquillius. Beyond the Halys Cappadocia, after King Ariarathes V Philopator (591-624) had largely maintained his position with the support of the Attalids, he resisted his brother and rival Holophernes, who was backed by Syria. This followed substantially the Pergamene policy, respecting both absolute devotion to Rome and a tendency towards adopting Hellenic culture.

King Pharnaces I had extended his dominion far beyond the Halys River to Tius, located on the border of Bithynia. He particularly acquired the wealthy city of Sinope, formerly a Greek free city, which became the royal residence of the kings of Pontus. It was under this hypothesis alone that we could explain how Mithridates, who had ostensibly earned his reputation for bravery in the war against Aristonicus, actually received Great Phrygia from the Roman general after the dissolution of the Attalid kingdom. The kingdom of Pontus at this time extended in the direction of the Caucasus and the sources of the Euphrates. Its western boundary is not precisely known, but it appears to have included the region around Enderes and Divirigi, commonly referred to as Lesser Armenia. In contrast, Greater Armenia and Sophene formed distinct and independent kingdoms.

Syria and Egypt were in the peninsula of Asia Minor. Rome thus substantially conducted the government, although much was done without or in opposition to her wishes, yet determined on the whole the state of possession. The wide tracts beyond the Taurus and the Upper Euphrates, as far down as the valley of the Nile, were mainly left to themselves. In the aftermath of Antiochus Epiphanes' death in 590, Demetrius, also known as Soter, the son of Seleucus IV, who was living as a hostage at Rome, and Antiochus Eupator, a minor son of the late king Antiochus Epiphanes, contested for the throne. In contrast, Ptolemy Philometor (573-608), the elder brother of the two who had jointly ruled since 584, was forced to flee the country in 590 by either his younger brother Ptolemy Euergetes II or the Fatid king. In Syria, Demetrius, who had the better title, was set aside, and Antiochus Eupator was recognized as king. The senate entrusted the guardianship of the royal boy to Roman senator Gnaeus Octavius. As expected, he governed in Rome's interest, reducing the war-marine and army of elephants in accordance with the 565 treaty. He was on the path to completing the country's military ruin.

Soon after, a war broke out between the kings of Egypt and Cyrene over the possession of the island of Cyprus. The Senate had initially assigned the island to the elder king, then to the younger one, before it finally remained with Egypt in opposition to the most recent Roman decision. The Roman government, in the height of its power and during a period of great internal and external peace, was mocked by the powerless kings of the East. Its authority was undermined, with its officials being killed. Cyprus was retained by Egypt despite a Senate decree to the contrary. Upon Philometor's death in 608, Euergetes succeeded him and reunited the divided kingdom. The Senate then permitted this arrangement without opposition.

India, Bactria After such occurrences, the Roman influence in these countries was practically shattered. Events pursued their course there without the help of the Romans. However, it is necessary for a right understanding of the sequel that we should not wholly omit to notice the history of the nearer and more remote east. In Egypt, surrounded by its natural barriers, the status quo resisted change. In Asia, both to the west and east of the Euphrates River, the people and states underwent significant transformations, largely due to this temporary lapse in Roman oversight. Beyond the great desert of Iran, Alexander the Great's kingdom had arisen not long after him. The kingdom of Palimbothra was established under Chandragupta (also known as Sandracottus) on the Indus River. To its north lay the powerful Bactrian state on the upper Oxus River. Both regions were formed from a mixture of national elements, with the easternmost offshoots of Hellenic civilization.

The decline of the Kingdom of Asia began to the west, where the kingdom of Asia emerged. Although diminished under Antiochus the Great, it still extended from the Hellespont to the Median and Persian provinces, encompassing the entire basin of the Euphrates and Tigris rivers. The matter came to the senate; and, as it was just at that time with good reason indignant at Demetrius Soter and apprehensive of a combination between the Attalids and Seleucids, while the establishment of a power intermediate between Syria and Egypt was for the interest of Rome, it made no difficulty in recognizing the freedom and autonomy of the insurgent nation. Nothing was done by Rome for the Jews except what could be done without personal exertion. Despite a clause in the treaty between the Romans and the Jews that promised Roman aid if they were attacked, and an injunction addressed to the kings of Syria and Egypt not to march their troops through Judaea, it was left entirely to the Jews themselves to defend their territory against the Syrian kings.

The Parthians, also known as the Parthwa, emerged as a distinct people within the vast Persian Empire. Initially, they were found in the southern region of modern-day Khorasan, east of the Caspian Sea. It wasn't until after 500 BCE that they began to take shape under Scythian influence. e The Turanians were a princely race of the Arsacids who ruled as an independent state. It was not until about a century after their emergence from obscurity that they became known. The Syrian empire was in a state of utmost disorganization at that time. This was largely due to the failure of Antiochus Epiphanes' Hellenizing attempts, as well as the troubles surrounding his succession after death. The provinces in the interior were already beginning to break away from Antioch, while the coastal region remained under its control.

As long as the country between Ragae and Persepolis to the Mediterranean obeyed the king of Antioch, Rome's power extended to the border of the great desert; the Parthian state could never take its place among the dependencies of the Mediterranean empire, not because it was so powerful, but because it had its centre far from the coast, in the interior of Asia. Since the time of Alexander, the world had been dominated by the Occidentals. The east, however, was seen as a mere extension of this dominance, much like how America and Australia later became integral to European expansion under Mithradates' rule. Maritime relations with piracy are a significant aspect of this era. Despite the scarcity of information, it can be inferred that the absence of a dominant naval power had a profound impact on maritime relations during this time period.

While a century previously the Romans had come forward with a special and salutary decision, they had introduced their supremacy in the east through a maritime police that was energetically handled for the general good. However, the complete nullity of this police at the beginning of this period betrays the fearfully rapid decline of the aristocratic government. Civil wars that persisted despite Roman efforts to achieve peace transformed one thriving town after another on the ancient island into rubble. The inhabitants of the island roamed as robbers at home and abroad, both on land and sea. The island became a recruiting ground for surrounding kingdoms after evil was no longer tolerated in the Peloponnesus, making it the true seat of piracy. For example, during this time, the island of Siphnus was thoroughly pillaged by a fleet of Cretan corsairs.

The responsibility for guarding the Asian and African borders was transferred to the subjects. What could not be transferred, such as defending the frontiers in Italy, Macedonia, and Spain, was handled with the utmost ineptitude. CHAPTER II
The Reform Movement and Tiberius Gracchus

The Roman government experienced a period of relative calm for a whole generation following the Battle of Pydna. This tranquility was occasionally disrupted, but overall, it marked a significant era in Roman history. Every neighbour whom she might have feared was politically annihilated. The men who had been reared under the old order of things in the severe school of the Hannibalic war still echoed with its mighty epoch as long as they survived. Death called one after another, until even the voice of the last veteran, Cato, ceased to be heard in the senate-house and the Forum.

The prizes were many, but those truly worth having were scarce. The competitors ran, as a Roman poet once said, as if they were racing across a track that widened at the starting point and then narrowed down. The first requirement for a public career was powerful connections. As a result, that career began, not in the camp as it once had, but in the antechambers of influential men. The burgesses became accustomed to the notion that, as a result of their past conflict with Perseus, they were lawfully exempt from paying direct taxes, regardless of any future advances requested by the community.

The commonwealth was becoming increasingly unstable, both politically and morally, on the brink of complete disorganization. The latter had already waged a war against the farmer-class on a great scale for centuries. This conflict seemed destined to end in the destruction of both the farmers and the entire commonwealth, but it was halted without being properly resolved due to the successful wars and the widespread distribution of land domains that these wars facilitated. The capitalists kept pace with the times. Capital, while waging war against labour or, more accurately, against the liberty of the person, had always done so under the strictest form of law. No longer did it wage its campaigns in an unseemly manner that converted a free man into a slave due to debt. Instead, throughout history, capital had acquired slaves who were bought and paid for. The former usurer of capital now appeared as the owner of industrial plantations.

Just as the nobility posed a greater threat than the patriciate, for the former could not be easily overthrown like the latter, which could be dismissed with a change in the constitution; similarly, this new power wielded by capital was more perilous than that of the fourth and fifth centuries, because no effective measures could be taken against it through changes to the law. While captives taken in war and hereditary transmission of slavery kept up the stock of slaves during the earlier period, this system of slavery was based on the methodically-prosecuted hunting of humans. Due to the little regard given to their lives or propagation, the slave population constantly dwindled. Even wars that supplied fresh masses to the slave market were insufficient to cover the deficit. The king of Bithynia declared himself unable to furnish the required contingent after about 650 of his able-bodied men had been forcibly taken away by the revenue-farmers.

At the great slave market in Delos, where slave dealers from Asia Minor sold their wares to Italian speculators, a large number of slaves were disembarked on one day. It is reported that as many as 10,000 slaves arrived in the morning and were all sold by evening. This demonstrates both the enormous quantity of slaves delivered and the fact that despite this, the demand still outstripped the supply. The Roman economy of the sixth century was based, as with other large undertakings of antiquity, on the use of slaves. In whatever direction speculation applied itself, it reduced man to a beast of burden. We have already discussed the armed and mounted slave-herdsmen in Italy's vast pastoral ranges;(9) and this type of pastoral husbandry soon became a favourite object of Roman speculation, as seen in provinces like Dalmatia, which was hardly acquired when Roman capitalists began to raise cattle there on a large scale after Italian methods.

The plantation system was far worse in every respect. Slaves were cultivated in the fields, often branded with iron, who toiled under overseers during the day. At night, they were shackled and imprisoned together in a common, often underground, facility for laborers. Italy, however, still remained substantially exempt from this worst form of slavery. Although in Etruria, where the plantation system first emerged in Italy, it existed most extensively for at least forty years afterwards. It is extremely probable that even now, serfdom did not exist; yet Italian agriculture at this epoch was still chiefly carried on by free persons or at any rate by non-fettered slaves, while the greater tasks were frequently let out to contractors.

In another district of Sicily, Cleon, a Cilician slave, had previously been a daring bandit in his native land. He followed the example set by others and occupied Agrigentum. When the leaders reached a mutual understanding, they gained various minor advantages before ultimately defeating the praetor Lucius Hypsaeus in person and his army, which consisted mainly of Sicilian militia. They then captured his camp. The Romans were compelled to send consuls and consular armies to Sicily for three successive years (620-622). After several undecided and unfavorable conflicts, the revolt was eventually subdued by the capture of Tauromenium and Enna.

While the task of escaping the proletariat's demands can be overwhelming and often surpasses the power and wisdom of a government, its suppression through police measures is relatively easier for a larger commonwealth. The consequence of such connivance was that Publius Rupilius, the consul, ordered all those who came into his hands to be crucified, numbering around 20,000 men. The landlord was required by law to hire a number of free laborers in proportion to the number of his slave laborers. Now at the suggestion of the government, a Punic treatise on agriculture was translated into Latin for the use and benefit of Italian speculators.

After the founding of Luna in 577, there was no further assignment of land for a long time. The only exception was the isolated institution of the Picene colony at Auximum (Osimo), established in 597. After the conquest of the Boii and Apuani, no new territory was acquired in Italy except for the far-from-attractive Ligurian valleys. Consequently, there existed no other land available for distribution, leaving only the leased or occupied domain-land, which was equally unappealing to the aristocracy now as it had been three hundred years prior. The farmer would often forcibly evict his family from their farm while he worked in the fields. He would then use the concept of "accomplished facts" to coerce them into submission. The landlords mainly employed slaves instead of free laborers, as the former could not be called away for military service like the latter. This reduced the free proletariat to a similar level of misery as the slaves.

It could be said aloud in the market of the capital that the beasts had their lairs. But for the burgesses, nothing was left but the air and sunshine. Those who were styled as masters of the world now had no land to call their own. From the end of the Hannibal War until 595, the number of burgesses steadily increased. This growth can be attributed to the continuous and considerable distribution of domain land. However, after 595, when the census revealed 328,000 capable men, a regular decline became apparent. The numbers were 324,000 in 600, 322,000 in 607, and 319,000 in 623 – an alarming result for a time of profound peace at home and abroad. He loved the chase. As a youth of seventeen, after serving with distinction under his father in the campaign against Perseus, he had asked as his reward the free range of the deer forest of the kings of Macedonia, which had been untouched for four years. He was especially fond of devoting his leisure to scientific and literary pursuits.

Although not strictly an author, he yet committed to writing his political speeches, esteemed like the letters of his adopted sister, the mother of the Gracchi. These were considered masterpieces of model prose by later writers. He took pleasure in surrounding himself with the better Greek and Roman litterati—a plebeian society regarded with suspicion by those colleagues in the senate whose noble birth was their sole distinction. A man morally steadfast and trustworthy, whose word was good with friend and foe. He shunned buildings and speculation, living simply; in matters of money, he acted not merely honourably and disinterestedly, but also with tenderness and liberality, which seemed singular to the mercantile spirit of his contemporaries. It was chiefly due to his assistance that Lucius Cassius, an able man of old Roman austerity and uprightness, was enabled to carry through his law regarding voting, despite the strongest opposition from the Optimates. His law introduced vote by ballot for those popular tribunals that still handled the most important part of criminal jurisdiction. In a similar vein, though he had not elected to participate in youthful indiscretions, he nonetheless subjected several of the most culpable members of the aristocracy to trial in his mature years.

With a small circle of friends, he held a middle ground between the aristocrats, who never forgave him for advocating the Cassian law, and the democrats, whom he neither pleased nor sought to please. Solitary during his life, he was praised by both parties after his death: sometimes as the champion of the aristocracy, and at other times as the promoter of reform. As governor of the province of Ebro, he rendered a permanent service to his country through his bravery and integrity. His mother Cornelia was the daughter of the conqueror of Zama. She had chosen his former opponent as a son-in-law simply because of that generous intervention. Cornelia herself was a highly cultivated and notable woman. After the death of her much older husband, she refused the hand of the king of Egypt and raised her three surviving children in memory of her husband and father.

Appius Claudius, a respected man in the senate who had already served as consul (611) and censor (618), censured the Scipionic circle for abandoning their scheme to distribute domain-lands with the fervor characteristic of the Claudian house. He did so with greater bitterness, likely due to his personal conflict with Scipio Aemilianus during his own candidature for censorship. Quintus Metellus, the conqueror of Macedonia and the Achaeans, shared similar sentiments. However, he was not widely respected solely due to his military prowess, but rather for embodying the traditional discipline and mannerisms of old, which were evident in both his private and public life. A collegium of three men, who were regarded as ordinary and standing magistrates of the state, was entrusted with the work of resumption and distribution. This task was later supplemented by the responsibility of legally determining what land belonged to the state and what was privately owned.

War was thus declared against the great landholders, who, like three centuries ago, had found substantial support in the senate. Once again, after a lengthy period, a single magistrate emerged as a vocal opponent of the aristocratic government. The aristocracy made no secret that, while they would acquiesce in the law because they could not do otherwise, the officious legislator should never escape their vengeance. The announcement by Quintus Pompeius that he would impeach Gracchus on the very day of his resigning his tribunate was one of the worst threats thrown out against the tribune. He was said to have introduced further popular measures, including reducing the length of service, expanding the right of appeal, eliminating the exclusive privilege of senators to serve as civil jurors, and granting Italian allies Roman citizenship.

The senate assembled in the temple of Fidelity, near the temple of Jupiter. The bitterest opponents of Gracchus spoke in the sitting. When Tiberius moved his hand towards his forehead, it signified to the people that his head was in danger. Amidst the wild tumult, it was said that he was already summoning the people to adorn his brow with the regal chaplet. But in his flight, he fell on the slope of the Capitol and was killed by a blow to the temples from the bludgeon of one of his furious pursuers — Publius Satureius and Lucius Rufus afterwards contested the infamous honor. Before the statues of the seven kings at the temple of Fidelity, three hundred others were slain; not one by iron weapons. The prolonged conflict between parties that lasted for over a century during the initial social upheaval ultimately set the stage for an even more devastating crisis.

Nasica, against whom the multitude breathed vengeance above all others, had at least the courage openly to avow his deed before the people and defend it. He was despatched to Asia under honourable pretexts and soon afterwards (624) invested with the office of Pontifex Maximus during his absence. Gaius Laelius played a part in the investigations against those who supported Gracchus; Publius Scaevola, who had tried to prevent the murder, later defended it in the senate. When Scipio Aemilianus, after his return from Spain, was publicly challenged to declare whether he approved or disapproved the killing of his brother-in-law, he gave an ambiguous reply: so far as Tiberius had sought the crown, he had been justly put to death. The territory of Capua and its neighboring towns, which had been annexed as a domain in 543, had largely fallen into private ownership by the time the following period of unrest began.

It is clear, then, why the most distinguished and patriotic men, even among those of the conservative party, such as Gaius Laelius and Scipio Aemilianus, approved and desired the distribution of their estates. In the voting assemblies, known as comitia, while they did not strictly focus on qualification, it was primarily the burgesses who appeared in these gatherings. In contrast, the popular assemblies, or contiones, allowed every man to participate, regardless of his background, including Egyptians, Jews, streetboys, and slaves. Scipio Aemilianus was a rare leader who commanded respect without seeking it. His brother-in-law's death had a profound impact on him, and he wore his grief like a badge of honor. "He who considers Italy his mother rather than his stepmother should remain silent." "I am certain you do not believe I will be intimidated by those who were once bound and sold into slavery." The rusty machinery of the comitia should not be used for elections and legislation.

Cato and those who shared his views never brought up such questions during the previous period. Instead, they only discussed them within the senate. For that reason, contemporaries of Gracchus, members of the Scipionic circle, described the Flaminian agrarian law of 522 as the first step in that fatal career, marking the beginning of Rome's decline. For that reason, they allowed the author of the domain distribution to fall, and saw in his dreadful end a deterrent against similar attempts in the future. Meanwhile, they maintained and utilized the domain distribution itself, which he had carried through – a testament to the dire state of affairs in Rome, where honest patriots were forced into the horrible hypocrisy of condemning the wrongdoer while benefiting from their wrongdoing. In place of Tiberius Gracchus, Publius Crassus Mucianus was appointed, who was the father-in-law of his brother Gaius. Following Mucianus' fall in 624 and Appius Claudius' death, distribution was managed jointly by young Gaius Gracchus and two prominent leaders of the movement party: Marcus Fulvius Flaccus and Gaius Papirius Carbo.

The census, published in 623, actually took place around 622. It revealed no more than 319,000 burgesses capable of bearing arms. Six years later, at 629, the number increased to 395,000, a rise of 76,000, largely due to the allotment commission's efforts for Roman burgesses. The decision rested with the middle party. It was this party that, following Gracchus' fall, had allied itself with his supporters to shield reforms from the oligarchy. Now, in conjunction with the oligarchy, it alone could establish a limit on reforms. What assassin's hand had done during the night slain the first statesman and the first general of his age was never discovered. It does not become history to repeat the reports handed down from the contemporary gossip of the city, nor is it necessary to set about the childish attempt to ascertain the truth from such materials.

Conscious of the best intentions and lacking common abilities, he was condemned to witness the ruin of his country before his eyes. He was forced to suppress every sincere attempt to save it, as he realized that such efforts would only exacerbate the evil; he was thus bound by the necessity of condoning atrocities like Nasica's, while also defending the work of the victim against their murderers. The scheme had been frustrated by Scipio's resistance at that time. Some years later, it was reintroduced and implemented, albeit with limiting clauses. The principal object of the party was to revive the action of the allotment commission, which had been practically suspended. The leaders seriously discussed removing the obstacles imposed by the Italian allies, who were granted the rights of citizenship, and the agitation took on this main direction. He stood almost alone. Carbo had changed his colors and now became a zealous aristocrat. Gaius Gracchus was absent as quaestor in Sardinia. The project was frustrated by the resistance not just of the senate, but also of the burgesses, who were little inclined to extend their privileges to wider circles.

Flavius left Rome to take command against the Celts. His Transalpine victories paved the way for the democratic ambitions that followed, as he simultaneously distanced himself from the conflicts with his own allies who had been provoked by him. Gaius Gracchus returned to Rome. He shared the same aversion to coarse pleasures and pursuits as the latter. A man of refined culture and bravery, he had distinguished himself in battle. Initially, he fought alongside his brother-in-law at Numantia, before later serving in Sardinia.

The young man's self-possession and composure under pressure, as he carried out his numerous laws, demonstrated his genuine statesmanly talent. His passionate devotion, even to the point of death, was a testament to the loving nature of his noble mind. The discipline of suffering he had endured, combined with his forced reserve over the past nine years, strengthened his resolve and determination. The suppressed anger burning within him now flared with intense passion against the party that had ravaged his nation and killed his brother. This distribution, which consequently excluded the burgesses living outside the capital, was designed to bring the burgess-proletariat of the capital into dependence on the leaders of the movement-party. It aimed to shift the burgess-proletariat's reliance from the aristocracy to the party leaders, thereby providing a new master of the state with both a bodyguard and a firm majority in the comitia.

While these enactments were mainly designed to secure for the new chief of state control over the capital and the state through the city-proletariat, they also aimed to provide the fullest possible control over the citadel machinery and the ability to strike fear into the senate and magistrates in times of need, the legislator simultaneously sought to address the existing social ills. Gaius did not proceed further in this matter because the domain land taken into possession by Roman burgesses was already substantially distributed. The question regarding the domains enjoyed by the Latins could only be raised anew in connection with the difficult question about the extension of Roman citizenship.

When the occupation of Spain led to the service becoming permanent, it was first legally enacted that anyone who had spent six consecutive years in the field gained the right to be discharged. However, this discharge did not exempt them from being recalled for further duty. In the burgess-tribunals, it had been a rule, especially in strictly political processes, that the accused remained at liberty during his trial. By surrendering his burgess-rights, he could save at least his life and freedom. The fine laid on property, as well as civil condemnation, might still affect even those who were exiled. On the other hand, judicial commissions, which originated from civil procedure, probably could not at the outset touch the liberty or life of a citizen. However, they could only pronounce a sentence of exile. This, which had previously been a mitigation of punishment awarded to one found guilty, now became a formal penalty. Involuntary exile, like voluntary exile, left the person banished to his property, so far as it was not exhausted in satisfying claims for compensation and money-fines.

The elevation of the equestrian order was a significant development during this period. Gracchus, who relied heavily on the support of the common people, implemented various reforms that improved their economic situation. At the same time, he worked tirelessly to dismantle the power and influence of the aristocracy. Those members of senatorial families who had not entered the senate did not cease to serve as equites and consequently bear the name. In fact, the burgess-cavalry, properly so called—the eighteen equestrian centuries—were chiefly filled by the young senatorial aristocracy from the censors' selection. This order of the equites, meaning those who were wealthy merchants, came into contact with the governing senate in various ways. The insignia of the Equites were the external privileges that distinguished men of equestrian census from the rest of the multitude. These included a golden finger-ring instead of an ordinary ring made of iron or copper, and a separate place at the burgess-festivals. The origin of these privileges is not certain, but it is not improbable that they were first conferred by Gaius Gracchus.

Jury Courts

While a gold mine was thus opened for the mercantile class, and the members of the new partnership constituted a great financial power, even imposing on the government—a "senate of merchants"—a definite sphere of public action was at the same time assigned to them in the jury courts. The field of criminal procedure, which came before the burgesses, was among the Romans from the earliest times. It was further narrowed by Gracchus; most processes, both those related to public crimes and civil causes, were decided either by single jurors or by commissions that were either permanent or extraordinary. The substantial effect of this was that, while there had been only two authorities in the state - the government as the administering and controlling authority, and the burgesses as the legislative authority - now the moneyed aristocracy was united into a compact and privileged class on the solid basis of material interests. It also formed part of the state as a judicial power, taking its place almost on an equal footing by the side of the ruling aristocracy.

The function of administration had hitherto belonged exclusively to the senate. Gracchus took it away, settling key administrative questions through comitial laws or, in effect, through tribunician dictation. He also restricted the senate's involvement in current affairs as much as possible and assumed control over business in a comprehensive manner. He prohibited, under severe penalties, the appointment of extraordinary commissions for high treason by decree of the senate, a measure he apparently introduced with the renewal of the law. It was an act of revenge that gave retroactive effect to the previous law, forcing Publius Popillius, the aristocrat who had become a target for the democrats after Nasica's death, which occurred during this time, to flee into exile.

But of great importance was Gaius' scheme to strengthen the senate by 300 new members, roughly equal in number to what it had previously held. These new members were to be elected from the equestrian order by the comitia, a peer-based assembly modeled after the most comprehensive style. This would have significantly reduced the senate's dependence on the chief of state. Character of the Constitution of Gaius Gracchus:

This was the political constitution that Gaius Gracchus proposed and largely implemented during his two-year term as tribune (631-632), with no notable opposition and without needing to resort to force to achieve his goals. If Gracchus, as his words and works plainly testify, aimed at the overthrow of the senate's government, what other political organization remained possible, after overthrowing the aristocratic government, in a commonwealth that had outgrown primary assemblies and lacked parliamentary government.

Dreamers such as his predecessor might call this into question, but Gaius Gracchus was a statesman. Though the formal shape he had inwardly projected for his great work has not been handed down to us, it may be conceived of in various ways. Nonetheless, he was undoubtedly aware of what he was doing. It is undeniable that his entire legislation was tainted by conflicting objectives. On one hand, it sought to benefit the public, while on the other hand, it served the personal interests and even fueled the ruler's personal vendetta. The conduct of Gaius Gracchus cannot be wholly explained by necessity alone. A consuming passion and a burning desire for revenge also played a significant role. Foreseeing his own downfall, he hurled himself into the fray, fueled by a fierce determination to strike back at his enemies.

In concert with Marcus Flaccus, who, although a consular, had again taken the tribuneship of the people, in order now to carry the law he had formerly proposed without success, he made a proposal to grant full franchise to the Latins and the former rights to their other Italian allies. The nature of this coalition and its mode of conflict are clearly and distinctly evident from a fragment of the speech that the consul Gaius Fannius delivered to the citizens in opposition to the proposal. "Do you then think," said the Optimate, "that if you confer the franchise on the Latins, you will be able to find a place in future—just as you are now standing there in front of me—in the burgess-assembly or at the games and popular amusements." The weakness of the latter lay in the fact that there was no relation of allegiance subsisting at all between the chief and the army. While the new constitution possessed all other elements of vitality, it lacked one: the moral tie between ruler and ruled, without which every state rests on shaky ground.

It then repaid its benefactor in kind, as usual, by inflicting a gentle blow when he stood for election for the third time as a candidate for the tribunate in 633. However, on this occasion, there were allegations of unjust proceedings by the tribune presiding at the election, who had been previously offended by Gracchus. While the transmarine colonies had been indirectly threatened by the allurements of Italian and African hyaenas, it was now alleged that newly-placed boundary stones of Carthage had been dug up. The Roman priests, when requested, certified that such signs and portents should serve as an express warning against rebuilding on a site accursed by the gods. The leaders of the democracy had left the Capitol and returned to their homes. Marcus Flaccus had spent the previous night preparing for the war, while Gracchus chose not to engage with his fate. Gracchus arrived alone and unarmed. Flaccus summoned the slaves to arms and took refuge in the temple of Diana, simultaneously sending his younger son Quintus to the enemy's camp in an attempt to negotiate a truce.

Marcus Flaccus fled with his eldest son to a place of concealment. They were hunted out and put to death shortly after. To gain time for him to escape, Marcus Pomponius and Publius Laetorius, two attendants, turned to face their pursuers and allowed themselves to be cut down. Gracchus, accompanied only by his slave Euporus, reached the suburb on the right bank of the Tiber. In the grove of Furrina, two dead bodies were later discovered. It appeared that the slave had killed his master before taking his own life.

The heads of the two fallen leaders were handed over to the government as required. The stipulated price was paid to Lucius Septumuleius, a man of quality, who bore the head of Gracchus. In contrast, the murderers of Flaccus, persons of humble rank, were sent away with nothing. The prosecution's war against Gracchus' partisans commenced on a grand scale. It is reported that 3,000 were strangled in prison, including Quintus Flaccus, an 18-year-old who had not participated in the conflict. He was widely mourned due to his youth and amiable nature. On the open space beneath the Capitol, where Camillus had consecrated an altar after restoring internal peace, and other shrines built on similar occasions for Concord were situated, these small chapels were demolished. The property of those killed or condemned traitors was confiscated, even to their wives' portions, and a new, splendid temple of Concord with its basilica was erected in accordance with a senate decree by Consul Lucius Opimius.

The memory of the Gracchi remained officially proscribed. Cornelia was not allowed even to don mourning for the death of her last son. However, the passionate attachment many people felt towards the two noble brothers, particularly Gaius, during their lifetime, was touching after their death in the almost religious veneration shown by the crowd, despite police precautions, to pay tribute to their memory and the places where they had fallen. It was all the more so in this present instance, because after the fall of Gaius and the sweeping and bloody prosecutions of Opimius, there existed at that moment no one who, either by blood relationship to the fallen chief of state or by preeminent ability, might feel himself warranted in attempting to occupy the vacant place. After Gaius Gracchus' death without heirs, the government of the senate resumed its place naturally; this was more so because it hadn't been formally abolished by the tribune in strict terms, but rather rendered practically ineffective through his extraordinary actions.

Publius Popillius was recalled from banishment after the enactments relating to him had been cancelled. A war of prosecution was waged against the adherents of Gracchus. In contrast, the attempt by the popular party to condemn Lucius Opimius for high treason after his resignation of office was thwarted by the government's supporters. This course was not adopted solely because the Gracchan revolution still captivated the minds of its contemporaries and protected its creations; the fostering and cherishing at least of the interests of the populace was in fact perfectly compatible with the personal advantage of the aristocracy, and thereby nothing further was sacrificed than merely the public weal. The noblest of his projects was the scheme to introduce legal equality first between Roman burgesses and Italy, and then between Italy and the provinces. By doing so, he eliminated the distinction between those who ruled and consumed, and those who served and worked, thereby solving the social question through a comprehensive and systematic emigration that is unparalleled in history.

The scheme for restoring Carthage after his death was abandoned with little difficulty by the government party, despite the individual allotments that had already been distributed being left to their recipients. The final step was taken 8 years later, in 643, when a new decree by the people converted the occupied land into rent-free private property for its former occupants. The Proletariate and the Equestrian Order under the Restoration. While the restored government was thus careful to eradicate the germs of improvement which existed in the Gracchan constitution, it remained completely powerless in the face of hostile powers that had been stirred up not for the general weal by Gracchus.

The proposal, introduced by Consul Quintus Caepio seventeen years after the establishment of the equestrian tribunals (648), aimed to reassign trials to senatorial jurymen. However, it revealed what the government desired: that it could not effectively address a matter that was not about squandering resources but rather implementing a measure in the face of an influential order. The aristocracy had been overthrown and then reinstated. As a result, they were now burdened with the weight of their own restoration. The aristocracy had formerly governed for good or ill, but for over a century without significant opposition. The crisis that had recently struck revealed to them, like a flash of lightning on a dark night, the vast chasm that lay before their feet.

The Roman aristocracy had never been so lacking in men of statesmanly and military ability as it was during this period of restoration following the Gracchan and Cinna revolutions. The Cimbrians, a nomadic people from modern-day Germany and Austria, invaded Italy in 390 BCE. The Roman Republic responded with force, but the conflict was marked by several key events that would shape its future. One such event was the Servile War, a series of rebellions led by slaves who sought to overthrow their Roman masters. These uprisings broke out annually during the early years of the Cimbrian war and were sparked by various factors, including economic hardship and social unrest. The most notable of these insurrections took place in 135 BCE, when a large group of slaves, led by a man named Eunus, rose up against their Roman owners. The rebellion was eventually put down, but it highlighted the growing tensions between the Roman elite and the increasingly discontented slave population. Despite being ultimately unsuccessful, the Servile War had significant consequences for Rome. It led to increased scrutiny of the institution of slavery and paved the way for later reforms aimed at reducing its power and influence. g Nuceria, Capua, and Thurii. The free proletarians in Sicily were little better than slaves. Their attitude during the first insurrection had demonstrated this. After the uprising was suppressed, the Roman speculators exacted revenge, reducing a significant number of the free provincials into slavery.

He occupied the undefended camp, but the slaves, despite being surprised, made a stand. Athenion

While the revolt thus spread alarmingly throughout the interior of the island, a second uprising broke out on the western coast. He secured the adherence of Greeks and Syrians, just as his predecessors had done, through prophesies and other edifying deceptions. However, skilled in war and wise as he was, he did not, like other leaders, arm the entire crowd that followed him. Instead, he formed an organized army from those capable of warfare, while assigning the rest to peaceful pursuits.

Conspiracies among the townspeople threatened to erupt within the city, as insurgent armies lay siege to its walls. Even Messana was on the brink of capture by Athenion. Aquillius Despite the difficulties faced by the government during the serious war with the Cimbri, it was unavoidable to send a second army into the field. In 651 BC, an army of 14,000 Romans and Italians, excluding the transmarine militia, was dispatched under the praetor Lucius Lucullus to the island. But the party, determined to hold out to the last man, retained the upper hand. Athenion, who had been saved in a marvelous manner, reappeared among his troops and revived their sunken courage. Above all, Lucullus showed incredible negligence, taking no step to follow up his victory. In fact, he is said to have intentionally disorganized the army and burned his field baggage, aiming to conceal the ineffectiveness of his administration and avoid being overshadowed by his successor.

Gaius Servilius, the successor of this individual, did not achieve any better outcomes. Both generals were later criminally impeached and convicted for their actions while in office, although this was not conclusive evidence of their guilt. Athenion, who after Tryphon's death in 652 was invested with sole command, stood victorious at the head of a considerable army. In 653, Manius Aquillius, who had distinguished himself under Marius the previous year in the war against the Teutones, was appointed consul and governor to lead the war effort. After two years of hard conflict, Aquillius was said to have fought in person with Athenion and killed him in single combat. The Roman general eventually put down the desperate resistance and defeated the insurgents in their final retreats due to famine.

After Massinissa's death in 605 BC, Scipio had divided the sovereign functions among his three sons: Micipsa, Gulussa, and Mastanabal. The firstborn son received the residency and state treasury, the second son was given command of war, and the third son was entrusted with the administration of justice. Now that Massinissa's eldest son, Micipsa, had died, his two brothers' demise left him as the sole ruler. A frail and elderly man, he ruled peacefully but was more inclined to devote his time to studying Greek philosophy rather than attending to matters of state. King Micipsa died soon after the war for the Numidian succession. Adherbal and Hiempsal sought to characterize their father's will as deceitful and entirely opposed to acknowledging Jugurtha's claim to a share in the kingdom. Meanwhile, Jugurtha presented himself as an usurper of the entire realm. During the ongoing debates about partition, Hiempsal was secretly murdered by hired assassins. This led to a civil war breaking out between Adherbal and Jugurtha, involving the entire Numidian region.

The division turned out in favour of Jugurtha, rather than against the commissioners. Cirta, the capital, with its port at Rusicade, was undoubtedly given to Adherbal. However, this arrangement meant that the portion allocated to him comprised mainly the eastern part of the kingdom, which consisted almost entirely of sandy deserts. In contrast, Jugurtha received the fertile and populous western half, which included what would later become Mauretania Caesariensis and Sitifensis. While the siege was underway, Jugurtha's troops clashed daily with the numerous Italians settled in Cirta, who took a more vigorous part in defending the city than the Africans themselves. The Roman senate's commission, dispatched on Adherbal's first complaint, then appeared; composed of young, inexperienced men, typical of those employed by the government in state missions during that time. It was not until the fifth month of the siege that a messenger from Adherbal stole through the enemy's entrenchments and delivered a letter from the king, full of urgent entreaties. This letter finally stirred the senate into action, prompting it to adopt a resolution: rather than declaring war as the minority had demanded, they decided to send a new embassy — one led by Marcus Scaurus, a renowned conqueror of the Taurisci and freedmen, whose presence alone would likely persuade the refractory king to reconsider his stance.

In a short time, a Roman army arrived on African ground and marched upward along the Bagradas River. They advanced into the Numidian kingdom, where towns farthest from the royal seat had already submitted voluntarily. Great Leptis, one of these towns, was among them. Meanwhile, Bocchus, king of Mauretania, who was married to Jugurtha's daughter, offered friendship and alliance to the Romans despite their familial connection. The treaty with Bocchus broke down because the king, unfamiliar with Roman customs, believed he could negotiate an advantageous treaty for the Romans without offering any concessions. As a result, he failed to provide his envoys with the customary price for Roman alliances. At the Roman headquarters, it became clear that a true peace, rather than just an armistice, could be negotiated.

The treaty was concluded, following its submission to a council of war, which obtained its consent after an irregular and brief discussion. Jugurtha surrendered at discretion, but the victor was merciful and gave him back his kingdom intact, in consideration of him paying a moderate fine and delivering up the Roman deserters and war elephants. The king later repurchased most of the latter by bargaining with individual Roman commandants and officers. The senators debated the legitimacy of the treaty, while a new consul, Spurius Postumius Albinus, fervently advocated for its cancellation, hoping that this would grant him control over the African campaign.

Discipline had largely ceased, and the spoilation of Numidian townships and even Roman provincial territory became the main concern for the Roman army during the suspension of hostilities. However, some officers and soldiers, along with their generals, had secretly formed alliances with the enemy. It is clear that such an army would be ineffective in the field. If Jugurtha had bribed the Roman general into inaction, as was later judicially established, he would have done nothing of significance. Spurius Albinus therefore was content with doing nothing.

On the other hand, his brother, who assumed command after his departure, was equally foolhardy and incapable. Aulus Postumius took it upon himself to seize the king's treasures by a bold coup de main in the middle of winter. The town of Suthul (later Calama, now Guelma), with its difficult access and even more challenging conquest, held the riches. While Africans rejoiced at the prospect of overthrowing alien domination, now suddenly revealed, it was bringing numerous tribes of free and half-free desert dwellers to the victorious king's standards. Public opinion in Italy was vehemently stirred against the equally corrupt and pernicious governing aristocracy, breaking out into a storm of prosecutions that swept away a succession of victims from the highest circles of nobility. Metellus, however, was resolved and perhaps even instructed not to terminate the war except with the unconditional subjugation and execution of the daring client prince. This was indeed the only issue that could satisfy the Romans.

Jugurtha was regarded as the deliverer of Libya from the rule of hated foreigners after his victory over Albinus. Although unscrupulous and cunning, he posed a threat to Roman stability. Even after a peace treaty, he could rekindle war in his native country at any time. Until King Jugurtha ceased to exist, tranquility would not be secured, and the removal of the African army would remain impossible. The Roman attacking column was surrounded by a swarm of enemy horses charging down from the ridge. The constant threat of hostile swarms hindered the advance, and the battle teetered on the brink of chaos. Meanwhile, Bomilcar's division held off the corps under Rufus, preventing it from rushing to aid the beleaguered Roman main army.

Jugurtha sent home a significant portion of his troops after the battle, and he limited himself to guerrilla warfare, which he handled with skill. The Romans occupied Numidia. Two Roman columns, led by Metellus and Marius, respectively, traversed the region. Although Marius was born into a humble background, he rose to become one of the top leaders after the Battle of Muthul. The two men worked together, occupying towns and forcing any place that refused to surrender to put its adult male population to death. At the same time, however, the king's most confidential counsellor, Bomilcar, was won over by Metellus. He reasonably feared that if peace were achieved, Jugurtha would hand him over to the Roman courts as the murderer of Massiva. In consideration of an assurance of impunity for the murder and great rewards, he agreed to deliver the king alive or dead into the hands of the Romans.

The state of feeling in Numidia was evident during the revolt of Vaga, the most significant city occupied by the Romans, in the winter of 646-7. On this occasion, the entire Roman garrison, including officers and men, were put to death, except for Commandant Titus Turpilius Silanus, who was later condemned to death by a Roman court-martial and executed for having an understanding with the enemy. The town was surprised by Metellus on the second day after its revolt. It was given over to all the rigour of martial law. However, if such was the temper of the dwellers who were easy to reach and comparatively submissive, living on the banks of the Bagradas, what might be expected farther inland among the roving tribes of the desert? A Numidian corps that had been fighting with the Romans in Italy for twenty years was hastily recalled to Africa. The son of Jugurtha suddenly appeared among the enemy lines. From this event, it can be inferred that Jugurtha's son wielded significant influence over his own people. Metellus followed the king through a desert. His troops carried water skins forty miles to sustain them. After reaching Thala, the city fell after a forty-day siege. However, the Roman deserters destroyed the most valuable part of the booty and burned down the building where they took their own lives following the capture of the town. More significantly, King Jugurtha escaped with his children and his treasure chest.

The African forces, intoxicated with victory, failed to capitalize on their gains. They were caught off guard as the Roman troops, reorganized overnight, launched a surprise attack during the early morning hours. Negotiations with Bocchus Strange as it may seem, we can yet understand why the Romans now, after King Bocchus had commenced the war, began to make most zealous exertions to secure his friendship. They had initially slighted him and later made little effort to pursue a closer relationship. By doing so, they gained an advantage: no formal declaration of war was ever issued by Mauretania. King Jugurtha, dressed in royal robes and shackled, accompanied his two sons into the triumphal chariot of the victor, who entered Rome on January 1. Shortly after, by his orders, the son of the desert met a tragic end in the subterranean city-prison beneath the Capitol, known as the "bath of ice" to its African captors. Marius reluctantly accepted the assumption that he was the conqueror of Numidia, but his predecessor, King Bocchus, consecrated a golden effigy at the Capitol representing Jugurtha's surrender to Sulla. This act enraged Marius, who had previously been revered for his military prowess. However, unprejudiced judges saw that the services of both Marius and Sulla overshadowed his generalship - particularly Sulla's brilliant desert expedition, which earned him recognition from the general himself and the entire army for his courage, strategic thinking, and leadership abilities.

The political issues of greater importance than this regulation of African clientship were those stemming from the Jugurthine war or its subsequent insurrection. All the evils of the government were laid bare in their entirety. It was now notorious, and by extension, judicially established, that among the governing lords of Rome, everything was for sale – even the treaty of peace and the right of intercession, the rampart of the camp and the life of the soldier. The African had merely stated the truth when he declared on his departure from Rome that, with enough gold, he would have bought the city itself. The circumstance that they were now furnished with some fresh proofs of the baseness of the restored senatorial government—a baseness only surpassed by its incapacity—might have been of importance, had there been an opposition and a public opinion with which the government would have felt compelled to come to terms.

The coming forward of Marius was significant. If the burgesses had stormed the senate-house after Albinus' defeat, it would have been a natural course. However, following Metellus' turn in the Numidian war, further criticism was unwarranted, and even less so regarding potential danger to the commonwealth. Yet, the first ambitious officer who arrived succeeded in achieving what the older Africanus had once threatened the government with, securing one of the principal military commands despite the governing body's explicit will. In the north and west of Spain, in the valleys of the Ligurian Apennines and the Alps, and in the mountains of Macedonia and Thrace, there existed tribes that were either wholly or partially free to defy the lax Roman government.

An expedition into the Alps above Nice and Antibes, directed against the Ligurian Oxybii and Decietae, was undertaken by the Romans in 600. This was partly at the request of the Massiliots, partly in their own interest. Following hot conflicts, some of which were attended with significant loss, this district of the mountains was compelled to furnish standing hostages to the Massiliots and pay them a yearly tribute. It is unlikely that during the same period, the cultivation of grapes and olives, which had thrived in this region following the example set by the Massiliots, was simultaneously prohibited for both Italian landowners and merchants within the territory beyond the Alps, dependent on Massilia. A similar character of financial speculation marks the war, which was waged by the Romans under the consul Appius Claudius in 61 against the Salassi respecting the gold mines and gold washings of Victumulae (in the district of Vercelli and Bard, and throughout the valley of the Dora Baltea). The war, though the Romans began it with a defeat, ultimately led to the subjugation of the Salassi and the transfer of the gold district to the Roman treasury.

About forty years later, the colony of Ivrea was established on the newly acquired territory, primarily to control the western route through the Alps, just as Aquileia dominated the eastern one. The Haedui, who inhabited the region around Autun, engaged in a fierce competition with each other for dominance. In contrast, the kings of the Suessiones, located in north-eastern Gaul near Soissons, formed an alliance under their protection, which included the league of the Belgic tribes, stretching as far as Britain. War with Allobroges and Arverni. The attack by Flaccus fell on the smaller tribes in the district between the Alps and the Rhone. This area was inhabited by the original Ligurians, who had intermingled with subsequent Celtic bands. As a result, a Celto-Ligurian population had emerged, comparable to the Celtiberian population in this regard.

King Betuitus, when he saw the numerous hosts of the dependent clans marching towards him on the bridge of boats thrown across the Rhone, exclaimed that there were not enough Romans to satisfy the dogs of the Celtic army. Ahenobarbus, exasperated at King Betuitus, was upset because he had persuaded the Allobroges to surrender to Maximus instead of him. Marius was regarded as the real conqueror of the Cimbri, and this was justly so. His superiority over his colleague was not merely due to his higher rank, which gave him command on the decisive day.
