CHAPTER VI The Attempt of Marius at Revolution and the Attempt of Drusus at Reform

Marius Gaius Marius, son of a poor day-labourer, was born in 86 BC at Cereatae, a village then belonging to Arpinum. After obtaining municipal rights as Cereatae Marianae, it still bears the name "Marius' home" (Casamare) today. He was raised in poverty, his circumstances so humble that they seemed to deny him access even to the municipal offices of Arpinum. He learned early what he would later practice as a general – to endure hunger and thirst, the heat of summer, and the cold of winter – and to sleep on hard ground.

In 639, he successfully attained the praetorship, an office that allowed him to showcase his military prowess as governor of Further Spain. After receiving the consulship despite the aristocracy in 647, he went on to serve as proconsul from 648 to 649 and ended the African war. He was later called upon to oversee the war against the Germans following a calamitous day at Arausio, which led to his consulship being renewed for four consecutive years from 650 to 653—a feat unprecedented in the republic's annals. During this time, he successfully defeated and annihilated the Cimbri in Cisalpine and the Teutones in Transalpine Gaul. He was not like other soldiers. He enjoyed his wine, particularly in his later years. However, he lacked the skill to host impressive feasts and had a poor cook.

The aristocratic hostility had likely led him to join the ranks of those opposing the government. He soon found himself appointed as the leader of the opposition, a position that could potentially lead to greater opportunities in the future. It was certain beyond dispute that Marius had saved Rome, and it was clear that he was the only man who could have done so. His name was on every lip, and men of quality acknowledged his services. The people adored him, not just for his virtues but also for his flaws. He was a unifying figure, admired by all for his disinterestedness and rough-around-the-edges demeanor. The multitude saw him as a second Camillus and a third Romulus, and they poured libations in his honor like the gods themselves. It was no wonder that the head of the peasant's son grew giddy at times with all this glory. He compared his march from Africa to Gaul to the victorious processions of Dionysus, who had marched across continents, and had a cup – none of the smallest – manufactured for him after the model of Bacchus'.

The better classes of society kept increasingly distant from service in the army, and the Roman and Italic middle class as a whole was disappearing. In contrast, the considerable military resources of the extra-Italian allies and subjects became available, and the Italian proletariat also provided a valuable material for military purposes. In like manner, filling up the complement of legions with properly qualified persons bound to serve proved difficult; thus, efforts required after the Battle of Arausio would have been impractical under the existing rules regarding service obligation. All distinctions of armor were set aside, and as a result, all recruits underwent uniform training.

The thirty companies of heavy infantry, each in two sections composed of 60 men in the first two and 30 men in the third, had hitherto formed the tactical unit. They were replaced by 10 cohorts, each with its own standard, and each consisting of 6 or sometimes 5 sections of 100 men apiece. This change saved 1200 men by suppressing the light infantry of the legion, but it increased the total numbers of the legion from 4200 to 5000 and then to 6000 men. The custom of fighting in three divisions was retained. Previously, each division had formed a distinct corps, but in the future, this arrangement was abandoned and the general was left to distribute the cohorts, of which he had control, in the three lines as he deemed best. Within the legion, all traces of former civic and aristocratic classifications vanished, leaving only military distinctions. However, accidental circumstances that arose decades earlier had created a privileged division within the army, separate from the legions – the elite bodyguard of the general.

Marius' introduction of the system of inland enlistment likely saved the state from military destruction. Centuries later, Arbogast and Stilicho extended its existence temporarily through the introduction of foreign enlistment. It involved a complete political revolution. The enlisted proletariat.

He was solely dependent on his pay for the moment. After discharge, there were no Hotel des Invalides or poorhouse to receive him. For the future, he could not but wish to abide by his standard and maintain it without leaving it otherwise than with the establishment of his civic status. His only home was the camp; war was his only science; his only hope was the general. When Marius gave Roman citizenship to two cohorts of Italian allies on the Raudine plain after a battle, he argued that he could not hear the voice of the law above the din of war. Men had peace, but they were not glad of it. The state of affairs was not now like it had been after the first great assault by the men of the north on Rome, when, as soon as the crisis passed, all energies were rekindled in the fresh awareness of recovered health, and had rapidly made up for what was lost through their vigorous development.

The multitude felt deeply the void left after the disappearance of those two illustrious youths who had opened the gates to revolution. In a childlike manner, it grasped at any substitute for them. This was evident in the case of Tiberius Gracchus's pretended son, who, despite being charged with fraud by his sister, the very sibling of the two Gracchi, was chosen as tribune solely due to his usurped name in 655. Anyone who looked more deeply into the difficulties of the enterprise than Marius probably did might reflect that the army, although in its transition from a militia to a body of mercenaries, was still during this state of transition by no means adapted for the blind instrument of a coup d'etat. An attempt to set aside the resisting elements by military means would have probably augmented the power of resistance in his antagonists. Marius, leader of the Popular Party, discharged the army after his victory, following the established regulations. He then proceeded with the plan laid out by Gaius Gracchus to gain control over the state by assuming its constitutional offices.

The Democratic Party, after a period of relative insignificance, experienced a sudden resurgence in political relevance. The dissatisfaction with the senatorial government was not now less than it had been; however, several hopes that had brought the Gracchi their most devoted followers had in the meantime been revealed as illusory. As a result, many minds harbored a growing suspicion that the Gracchan agitation was heading towards an outcome to which a significant portion of the discontented were unwilling to commit themselves. Marius did not disdain soliciting votes in person, and it was even said that he would purchase them. In fact, at the tribunician elections, when nine men from the government party's list were proclaimed, and the tenth place seemed already secured for a respectable man of similar complexion, Quintus Nunnius, was set upon and slain by a savage band, mainly composed of discharged soldiers who had supported Marius.

They achieved their goal, despite using the most extreme methods. This law revived plans for conquest beyond the Alps and transalpine colonization projects initiated by Gaius Gracchus and Flaccus on a large scale. This marked an initial step towards addressing the claims of Italians seeking equality with Romans, which proved challenging to fulfill despite their persistence. If the law was passed and Marius was called to carry out these immense schemes of conquest and assignation, he would become practically monarch of Rome until those plans were realized or considered indefinitely. Given their unlimited character, it may be presumed that Marius intended his consulship to be renewed annually, similar to Gracchus' tribunate.

While this was the nature of their aim, the next question related to the means by which they proposed to break down the resistance - which might be anticipated to be obstinate - of the government party. The equites were not only left in possession of the tribunals, but their power as jurors was considerably increased. This increase was partly due to a stricter ordinance regarding the standing commission, which was especially important for merchants. The ordinance aimed to prevent extortions by public magistrates in the provinces. Glaucia likely introduced this measure in this year. Additionally, a special tribunal was appointed, possibly as early as 651, on the proposal of Saturninus. This tribunal focused on investigating embezzlements and other official malversations that had occurred during the Cimbrian movement in Gaul. Although they did not despise the alliance with the equites and the proletariate of the capital, the real power behind the confederates' enforcement of their measures lay not in these groups, but in the discharged soldiers of the Marian army, who had been provided for under the colonial laws themselves after such an extravagant fashion.

The scandal was grave, but when it came to the question whether the senate would comply with the clause of the law that within five days after its passing every senator should on pain of forfeiting his senatorial seat take an oath faithfully to observe it, all the senators took the oath except for Quintus Metellus, who preferred exile. However, Saturninus and Glaucia were among the first masters and servants of the proletariat. As a result, they were not on good terms with the wealthy aristocracy, which occasionally used the rabble to keep the senate in check, but had no taste for street riots or violent outbursts.

Gaius Gracchus, recognizing that no government could be overthrown solely through the efforts of the proletariat, had particularly sought to win over the support of the property-owning classes. Those who wished to carry on his work initially aimed to reconcile the aristocracy with the bourgeoisie. Marius maintained a passive attitude despite his associates bringing forward decisive proposals, which were then carried by his soldiers after a struggle. In contrast, he did not take an active role in presenting himself at the forefront of battle. Saturninus, isolated by his own actions, was assailed and overpowered. However, two demagogues who had compromised themselves to such an extent that they could no longer retreat found themselves with no alternative but to resign their offices in the usual manner, thereby delivering themselves into the hands of their exasperated opponents, or risk seizing power for themselves, despite feeling they were unable to bear its weight.

Saturninus would again run for the tribunate of the people in 655, while Glaucia, as a praetor, would be eligible to run for consul in two years. The longing for honors once stirred within him; the more it was thwarted, the deeper it took hold of his heart. The jurors hesitated, not whether the accused was guilty or innocent, but whether his ally Saturninus or his enemy Caepio was more deserving of their hatred. Ultimately, they decided in favor of acquittal.

Even if people were not more favourably disposed towards the government itself than before, yet after having found themselves, although only for a moment, on the verge of a real mob rule, all men who had anything to lose viewed the existing government in a different light. It was notoriously wretched and pernicious for the state, but the anxious dread of the even more wretched and pernicious government of the proletariat had conferred upon it a relative value. The condemned returned to the province he was accused of plundering, where he was warmly received by all the communities with honorary delegations. He was highly regarded and loved throughout his life, and spent his remaining days in literary pursuits. This condemnation, though severe, was not the only instance of its kind. Son of the man who had thirty years earlier primarily caused the overthrow of Gaius Gracchus, and later made a name for himself as an officer by subjugating the Scordisci, Drusus was like his father in holding strictly conservative views. He had already provided practical proof of these sentiments during the insurrection of Saturninus.

More dangerous than these decided opponents was the cowardly and corrupt mass of the aristocracy, who would have preferred to plunder the provinces alone but had no objection to share the spoils with the equites. Instead of taking on the grave struggle against the haughty capitalists, they reckoned it more equitable and easier to purchase impunity at their hands through fair words or occasional prostrations, even for a substantial sum. It was only to be expected that, as the tyrannical regime rested on its support against the oligarchy, so too should the latter rest on its support against the moneyed aristocracy, which relied heavily on the paid and organized proletariat; whereas the government had previously accepted the feeding of the proletariat at the state's expense as an unavoidable necessity, Drusus now considered employing it, at least for the time being, to counter the moneyed aristocracy. Drusus had not acted until he caused the consul Philippus, who refused to back down, to be arrested and taken into custody by a bailiff.

The people celebrated the tribune as their benefactor, and received him in the theatre by rising up and applauding. However, the voting had not so much decided the struggle as it had merely shifted its focus to another ground. The opposing party had justly characterized Drusus' proposal as being contrary to the law of 656 BCE, rendering it null. It had changed, but had deteriorated rather than improved. In general, Italy's economic and social condition was not primarily dependent on political distinctions. There were allied districts, such as Umbria and Etruria, where the class of free farmers had largely disappeared. In contrast, other regions, like the valleys of Abruzzi, still maintained a tolerable presence or remained almost unaffected. A similar diversity could be observed in different Roman burgess-districts.

In Teanum Sidicinum, one of the most considerable allied towns, a consul ordered the chief magistrate to be scourged with rods at the stake in the marketplace. This punishment was imposed because the municipal officers had not driven forth the men from the public bath quickly enough, and as a result, the consul's wife expressed her desire to bathe there. While the present state of the Italian allies had been transformed from a tolerable relation of dependence into the most oppressive bondage, they were at the same time deprived of any prospect of obtaining better rights. In short, while the Italian allies had formerly stood to the Romans partly as brothers under tutelage, protected rather than ruled and not destined for perpetual minority, partly in that of slaves who were tolerably treated and not utterly deprived of the hope of manumission, they were now all subject nearly in equal degree, and with equal hopelessness, to the rods and axes of their Roman masters.

The desire for equal rights had grown from a quiet aspiration into a vocal demand, only to be met with increasingly strong rejection as it was more openly presented. They had been driven to the conviction that, while the best men of both parties acknowledged the justice and equity of their claims, these best men, aristocrats as well as Populares, had equally little power to procure an hearing for those claims with the mass of their party. A government that has endured for thirty years through revolution and restoration would likely have seen its share of changes. Despite these shifts, a shortsighted and narrow-minded approach has consistently guided decision-making.

It is unclear whether he actually led a secret league, as reported, with branches extending throughout Italy, whose members swore an oath to support Drusus and the common cause. However, even if he did not participate in such reckless and unjust actions for a Roman magistrate, it is certain that he did not stick to mere general promises. Instead, dangerous connections were formed in his name, possibly without his consent or against his will. The Italians rejoiced when they learned that Drusus had successfully passed his initial proposals with the approval of the vast majority of the Senate. Shortly after, the entire Italian community celebrated the return of the tribune, who had fallen gravely ill and was now recovering. The secret league, moreover, which was alleged to be headed by Drusus, had lost in him its actual or expected chief. However, it still existed and afforded an important nucleus for the political organization of the insurrection. Its military organization might be based on the fact that each allied town possessed its own armament and experienced soldiers.

The capital was so utterly undefended that a resolute Marsian officer, Quintus Pompaedius Silo, one of Drusus' closest friends, is said to have devised the plan to infiltrate the city at the head of a band of trusted associates, carrying swords under their clothing, and seize it by surprise. The Martians were the first to formally declare against the Romans. From that point on, the war was known as the Martian War. When we say that towns in better positions did not immediately abandon Rome after the initial shock, we do not mean that they would now, as during the Hannibal war, hold out for a long time and withstand severe defeats without wavering their allegiance to Rome; the fiery trial had not yet been fully endured.

The immediate effect of the Italian insurrection was that, just as had been the case after the defeats suffered by the government's policy in Africa and Gaul, the commencement of a warfare of prosecutions marked the beginning. By means of this, the aristocracy of judges took vengeance on those men of the government whom they, either rightly or wrongly, regarded as the primary cause of this trouble. Suspicion grew against the senators who favoured Drusus' reforms. Soon afterwards, Consul Lupus reported from the camp to the senate about the communications maintained between the Optimates in his camp and the enemy; a suspicion which was soon proven unfounded by the arrest of Marsian spies.

The Political Organization of the Insurrectionary Opposition—Rome

While the leading state was collecting its energies in anticipation of the severe war that loomed on the horizon, the insurgents faced a far more daunting challenge: establishing a viable political organization amidst the turmoil of their struggle. In the territory of the Paeligni, situated in the centre of the Marsian, Samnite, Marrucinian, and Vestinian cantons, lay the town of Corfinium. This location, in the heart of insurgent districts, was chosen as the Opposition-Rome or city of Italia. The burgesses of all insurgent communities were granted citizenship here. A forum and senate-house were established on suitable grounds. The Latin language, even then the prevailing language among the Marsians and Picentes, was used officially alongside the Samnite language, which dominated Southern Italy. The two languages were given equal status and alternated use on silver coins minted by the new Italian state, modeled after Roman designs and standards, thereby claiming Rome's centuries-long monopoly on coinage.

The imitation extended to the smallest particulars. For example, upon a victory, the title of consul or praetor held by the chief magistrate was exchanged with that of the Italian general for the title of Imperator. This new Rome, established by the insurgents, was distinguished from the original city merely by circumstance. Unlike the latter, which had developed in an urban environment and its unnatural position between a city and a state had formed naturally, the new Italia was nothing more than a gathering place for the insurgents. It was only through a fictional application of law that the inhabitants of the peninsula were designated as citizens of this new capital. The Italian polity, despite having representative senates and a diminished comitia, approximated a free state. However, neither Rome nor Italy was ever able to cross the boundary line.

Warlike preparations thus began, a few months after the death of Drusus, in the winter of 663-64, the struggle—represented by one of the coins of the insurgents—as that of the Sabine ox against the Roman she-wolf. The text appears to be grammatically correct and free of errors. Conflicts likely occurred in the north around Firmum, Atria, and Pinna, while in the south they took place around Luceria, Beneventum, Nola, Paestum, prior to and during the gathering of Roman forces along the borders of the insurgent nation.

After the southern army under Caesar assembled in the spring of 64 BC in Campania, which was largely held by Rome, Capua—a city crucial for Roman finances—along with its allied cities, received garrisons. The army then attempted to assume the offensive and provide aid to smaller divisions sent ahead to Samnium and Lucania under Marcus Marcellus and Publius Crassus. It is true that Sulla's incursion, carried out with the same artful audacity as his previous expedition to Bocchus, briefly relieved the hard-pressed Aesernians; however, they were ultimately compelled by the extremity of famine to capitulate towards the end of the year. Publius Crassus was defeated by Marcus Lamponius in Lucania. He was forced to surrender and take refuge in Grumentum, which eventually fell after a prolonged and stubborn siege.

The insurrection spread; when Mutilus advanced into Campania at the head of the Samnite army, the citizens of Nola surrendered to him their city and delivered up the Roman garrison. The commander was executed by Mutilus' orders, while the remaining men were distributed among the victorious army. Mutilus attempted to attack the Roman camp, but he was defeated. The Samnites, who were being pursued from behind by the Roman cavalry, suffered heavy casualties and left around 6,000 dead on the battlefield. It is true that not long afterwards the victorious army was attacked while crossing a river by Marius Egnatius. The ships that had been built in Thessaly, according to Sulla's orders, joined them there. With its strategic location commanding the Hellespont, Lucullus secured a safe passage for the Roman senatorial army to Asia next spring. But Sulla, cool and clear as ever, while urgently desiring a speedy settlement of Asiatic affairs due to the situation in Italy, estimated the advantages of the Cappadocian alliance for the war looming over him in Italy as very slight. He was too much of a Roman to consent to such a disgraceful and injurious concession.

Preliminaries of Delium

During the peace conferences held in the winter of 669-70 at Delium on the coast of Boeotia opposite Euboea, Sulla firmly refused to yield even a single inch of land. This stance was consistent with the traditional Roman practice of not raising demands beyond those made before battle, following victory. New Difficulties Sulla proceeds to Asia. Mithradates rejected the peace and demanded that the Romans should not insist on the surrender of war vessels, nor concede Paphlagonia. Meanwhile, he claimed that Fimbria was prepared to offer even more favorable terms. Archelaus eventually succeeded in persuading his stubborn master to agree to the treaty. As a result, he was viewed with suspicion at court, accused of having negotiated an unfavorable peace, and even suspected of treason. Eventually, he was forced to leave the country and seek refuge with the Romans, who welcomed him warmly and showered him with honors.

He himself may have realized that the political complications hindered his efforts in a most frustrating manner, rendering a task that seemed simple from a military perspective almost impossible. As a result, he was forced to settle for a peace that was far less desirable than the triumphs he had achieved. The king wanted to meet with the Roman general personally to finalize the peace treaty. It is likely that this meeting was merely a pretext for relocating the army to Asia Minor, where he could bring an end to Fimbrian resistance. Sulla, accompanied by his legions and Archelaus, crossed the Hellespont. After meeting with Mithradates on its Asiatic shore at Dardanus, he orally concluded a treaty with him. He then led his army to continue its march until it reached the camp of Fimbria at Thyatira, near Pergamus. His own camp was pitched nearby.

The spoils carried in triumph after the campaign did not reflect an unusually oppressive nature. In fact, their value was substantial, amounting to approximately 1 million pounds of precious metal. Sulla had already brought the kings of Bithynia and Cappadocia to meet the Pontic king at Dardanus. He had made them all promise to live in peace and good neighbourhood. However, on this occasion, Mithradates, the haughty king, refused to admit Ariobarzanes, who was not a descendant of royal blood, to his presence. After four years of war, the Pontic king became once again a client of the Romans. A single and settled government was re-established in Greece, Macedonia, and Asia Minor. The requirements of interest and honour were met, albeit not fully, given the circumstances. Sulla had distinguished himself both as a soldier and general, and possessed the skill to navigate the difficult balance between bold perseverance and prudent concession.

After compensating his soldiers for their winter quarters in Asia Minor's rich west, he transferred them in 1600 vessels from Ephesus to the Piraeeus in the spring of 671. From there, they traveled by land to Patrae, where the vessels awaited to take the troops to Brundisium. CHAPTER IX

Cinna and Sulla Ferment in Italy

A state of suspense and uncertainty existed in Italy when Sulla departed for Greece at the beginning of 67 BC. This had been described earlier: a half-suppressed insurrection, an army under the command of a general whose politics were highly doubtful, and a capital plagued by confusion and manifold intrigue. The victory of the oligarchy, achieved through force of arms, ultimately led to manifold discontent.

The adherents, resident in the capital, of those outlawed after the Sulpician revolution, who remained very numerous due to Sulla's remarkable moderation, laboured zealously to procure permission for the outlaws to return home. In particular, some ladies of wealth and distinction spared no trouble nor expense to achieve this goal. Cinna was admitted into the conspiracy only by an afterthought, seemingly because the intrigue, which required a consul due to the restriction of tribunician powers, saw him among the consular candidates as its most suitable instrument. Strabo, the proconsul, disagreed with the government but was not willing to join this faction.

The Forum was awash with blood on that fateful day, Octavius', which was unlike any other. The sheer scale of the carnage was staggering, with estimates suggesting a staggering ten thousand corpses littering the streets. There were no more than 500 armed men, for the most part slaves of the refugees and enlisted Numidian horsemen. However, as Gaius Marius had been willing to fraternize with the rabble of the capital in the previous year, he now ordered the field laborers who were shut up at night by the landholders to be released. He offered them arms to achieve their freedom, which they did not despise. Marius, with his band, had grown to three legions in strength. He controlled a number of warships and occupied one coastal town after another until Ostia finally fell into his hands through treachery. As a precursor to the terror that was to come, the general abandoned the city to Marius' men, who then committed massacre and pillage.

The Senate did not seek to ally itself with one usurper in hopes of escaping another. Instead, it looked for assistance elsewhere. It was a severe blow when the city of Ariminum was occupied by the insurgents after an unfavorable combat against the government's troops. This led to the interruption of vital communication between Rome and the Po Valley, where men and supplies were expected. Contagious diseases followed on the distress and committed dreadful ravages among the masses of soldiers densely crowded around the capital. The veteran army of Strabo's 11,000 men and the troops of Octavius' 6,000 men are said to have fallen victims to them.

The Roman government, bolstered by the arrival of Metellus and the death of Strabo, regained its strength and prepared for battle against the insurgents on the Alban Mount. While one party, led by the honourable but stubborn and shortsighted Consul Octavius, refused all concessions, Metellus, more experienced in war and more judicious, attempted to bring about a compromise. However, his conference with Cinna only served to inflame the passions of the extreme men on both sides: Marius labelled Cinna a weakling, while Octavius branded Metellus as a traitor. Envoys went to Cinna; but, while they foolishly made difficulties about recognizing him as consul, and Cinna in the interval thus prolonged his camp near the city gates, the desertion spread to such a great extent that it was no longer possible to negotiate any terms.

The senate surrendered unconditionally to the outlawed consul, requesting only that he refrain from bloodshed. Cinna promised this, but declined to ratify his promise with an oath. By his side during the negotiations, Marius maintained a sullen silence. The gates were closed. For five days and five nights, the slaughter continued uninterrupted. Even after that, the execution of those who had escaped or been missed became a daily occurrence. The bloody persecution persisted in Italy for months. A severe illness had taken hold of him. After being confined to bed for seven days, his mind became clouded with wild thoughts, and he imagined himself fighting in the battles of ancient Asia Minor, where Sulla's laurels were won. He died on January 13th. 668

Even after his death, individual incidents undoubtedly occurred, recalling that time of terror. Gaius Fimbria, for example, who had committed more atrocities than any other during the Marian massacres, attempted to kill Quintus Scaevola, the universally revered pontifex maximus, at Marius' funeral. Scaevola, who had been spared even by Marius, later indicted Fimbria criminally after he recovered from the wound he had received. Cinna alone possessed an absolute power in Italy and most of the provinces for nearly an uninterrupted period. His rule was unmatched by any other chief of the popular party before or after him. However, it is difficult to identify anyone whose governance was as devoid of purpose and value as Cinna's. The law proposed by Sulpicius and later adopted by Cinna himself, which granted equal suffrage to new burgesses and freedmen on par with the old burgesses, was naturally revived. It was subsequently formally confirmed by a senate decree as a valid law.

These measures, the only positive ones during the entire Cinnan government, were dictated by the circumstances and not based on any plan. A large proportion of the old burgesses were favourably disposed towards democratic views. The majority of the quiet people, who disapproved of the Marian atrocities, viewed an oligarchic restoration as the beginning of a new era of terror perpetrated by the opposing faction. Asia was under the control of Mithradates. As a result, the province of Macedonia, which was still within Sulla's authority, served as the last refuge for the exiled oligarchs.

If Lucius Valerius Flaccus, the younger, succeeded Marius after his death and was appointed consul and commander-in-chief in the east. He was accompanied by Gaius Fimbria, who although capable, was also insubordinate. The army assigned to them was significantly weaker than Sulla's forces. A letter from him reached the senate, in which he reported the war's termination and announced his return to Italy. He stated that he would respect the rights granted to the new burgesses and that while penal measures were inevitable, they would fall only on those responsible for the mischief.

Cinna was stirred from his inaction by this announcement. He had previously taken no action against Sulla, apart from placing some men under arms and gathering a fleet in the Adriatic. Now, he decided to set sail for Greece as quickly as possible. Cinna had, without concerning himself further about the senatorial decree, immediately after the sitting's conclusion, proceeded to the army and urged them to embark. While Sulla crossed into Asia and induced defection in the army of Fimbria, its leader fell by his own hand. The government in Italy used the additional year granted to it by these steps to make energetic preparations. At Sulla's landing, 100,000 men were arrayed in arms against him; later, even double that number joined them.

It is true that this army had been weaned from politics during its seven years' conflicts in Italy, Greece, and Asia. The general pardoned everything in his soldiers, including debauchery, brutality, and even mutiny against their officers. He required nothing but valour and fidelity towards him, setting before them the prospect of extravagant rewards in the event of victory. This soldierly enthusiasm is all the more powerful because it often combines the noblest and meanest passions in the same breast. While the state's edifice burned, his friends were slaughtered, his homes lay in ruins, and his family fled into exile, he stood steadfast at his post until the public enemy was vanquished and the Roman border was secure. The chief difficulty in Sulla's position lay in the fact that the faithlessness and perfidy prevalent among the new burgesses gave them every reason to suspect his personal designs, if not to doubt outright whether he could persuade his party to keep their word after the victory.

Quintus Lucretius Ofella and other suitable officers were likewise received and at once employed. Even Publius Cethegus, one of the senators banished after Sulla's Sulpician revolt, obtained pardon and a position in the army. The latter, unlike his father, who had never been an adherent of the oligarchy, had acknowledged the revolutionary government and even taken service in Cinna's army. However, this fact was not forgotten: his father had borne arms against the revolution. The former found himself assailed in various forms and threatened with the loss of his considerable wealth due to an indictment charging him to return the booty that was alleged to have been embezzled by his father after the capture of Asculum. The soldiers of both camps mingled. The Sullans, well-funded by their commander, had little trouble convincing the recruits – not too eager for war – to join them over drinks that it was better to have them as comrades than enemies; however, Sertorius warned the commander to stop this dangerous contact.

Sulla gave orders that the consul should resign his office--which he did--and be escorted by his cavalry to whatever point they desired. However, Scipio was hardly set at liberty when he resumed his dignity's insignia and began anew to collect troops, without taking any further significant action. Sulla and Metellus occupied winter quarters in Campania. Following the failure of a second attempt to negotiate with Norbanus, they continued the blockade of Capua throughout the winter. To replenish the treasury, the senate decreed the melting down of the gold and silver vessels in the capital's temples. The extent of the produce is evident from the fact that after several months of warfare, nearly 600,000 pounds (14,000 pounds of gold and 6,000 pounds of silver) remained on hand.

It was owing to anything but devotion towards the revolutionary Roman government that numerous contingents from the Oscan districts reinforced their armies. However, it was well understood that an oligarchy restored by Sulla would not acquiesce, unlike the lax Cinnan government, which had already conceded independence to these lands; and therefore, the primitive rivalry between the Sabellians and the Latins was rekindled in the struggle against Sulla. It was no wonder, therefore, that the war in this region bore a character altogether different from the conflicts elsewhere. No compromise was attempted there, and no quarter was given or taken. The pursuit went to its utmost extent. Metellus, the proconsul, advanced into Upper Italy, relying on the support of the Picene insurrection. Meanwhile, Sulla marched directly from Campania towards the capital.

His troops soon gave way; the defection of a division even during the battle hastened their defeat. Publius Antistius, a former aedile and father-in-law of Gnaeus Pompeius, was one of the two most skilled orators in the judicial courts of Rome's desolate Forum. Alongside Lucius Domitius, a consular, he stood out among many men of exceptional talent who had lost their lives during this tumultuous period. Among these notable individuals was Quintus Scaevola, the venerable pontifex maximus, who had narrowly escaped assassination by Fimbria but ultimately succumbed to his wounds in the temple of Vesta, where he was entrusted with guardianship. Metellus clashed with Carbo in Northern Italy. Carbo launched a three-pronged attack on Etruria. Meanwhile, Metellus had defeated Carbo's lieutenant Carrinas at the river Aesis (Esino), which lies between Ancona and Sinigaglia, separating Picenum from the Gallic province. When Carbo arrived with his superior army, Metellus was forced to halt further progress.

The young Pompeius and his contemporary and rival Crassus penetrated into Umbria by mountain paths from Picenum. They gained access to the Flaminian road at Spoletium, where they defeated Carbo's legate Carrinas and captured him in the town. However, on a rainy night, Carrinas managed to escape and make his way to Carbo's army despite sustaining some losses. The pitched battle fought between Carbo and Sulla in the region of Chiusi did not result in a decisive victory for either side. However, Carbo's forces had gained the upper hand, forcing Sulla to halt his advance. While the oligarchic party was concentrating all its energies on Etruria, the democracies everywhere put forth the utmost efforts to break the blockade of Praeneste.

The governor of Sicily, Marcus Perpenna, set out for that purpose. It is unclear whether he arrived at Praeneste. There were the Samnites under Pontius of Telesia, and the Lucanians under their experienced general Marcus Lamponius. They set out without being able to prevent their departure, and were joined in Campania where Capua still held out by a division of the garrison under Gutta. To the number of 70,000, they marched upon Praeneste. Sulla himself left behind a corps to confront Carbo before returning to Latium. He then took up a strategic position in the defiles near Praeneste, blocking the path of the relieving army. In vain the garrison attempted to break through the lines of Ofella. The relieving army, led by Sulla, also made no progress as they tried to dislodge him. Despite this, both sides remained firmly entrenched in their strong positions. Even after Damasippus, who had been sent by Carbo, reinforced the relieving army with two additional legions, neither side was able to gain the upper hand.

The Sullans' successes in Upper Italy led to the occupation of Etruria. However, as the war stagnated in both Etruria and Latium, a decisive outcome emerged in the valley of the Po. He encountered the enemy at Faventia and launched a late-afternoon attack with his tired troops. The result was a complete defeat, leading to the disintegration of his corps, with only about 1,000 men returning to Etruria. When Carbo received this news in the camp at Clusium, he lost his self-control. Despite having a significant force under his command, he secretly slipped away from his headquarters and set sail for Africa. After his defeat, some of his abandoned troops followed the lead of their general and returned home. Others were destroyed by Pompeius. Carrinas gathered the remaining soldiers and led them to Latium to join the army of Praeneste.

There was no change that had been made in the meantime; and the final decision drew near. Revenge alone drove this final march to Rome, the last outburst of fury among the passionate revolutionists and the desperate Sabellian nation. He warned his officers not to send the troops exhausted from the forced march immediately into battle. But Sulla considered what the night might bring for Rome and, despite the late hour, ordered the attack.

Marcus Crassus overthrew the enemy on the right wing and pursued them as far as Antemnae. This somewhat relieved the left wing, which then advanced an hour after sunset. The prisoners taken in the battle, numbering between 3,000 and 4,000, including generals Damasippus, Carrinas, and Pontius, who was severely wounded, were brought to the Villa Publica in the Campus Martius by Sulla's orders on the third day after the battle. There, they were massacred to the last man, with the sounds of clashing arms and dying groans echoing through the nearby temple of Bellona, where Sulla was holding a meeting of the senate at the time. Gaius Marius, a consul, and his son, were killed by each other with their swords.

It was natural that, after such an occurrence, the cities of newly established burgesses who had not yet suffered would continue their resistance with utmost obstinacy. As the dictator declared, Rome would not rest so long as Samnium existed. He ordered that the Samnite name be erased from the earth. This was evident in his treatment of prisoners taken before Rome and in Praeneste, where he inflicted terrible punishment on them. It appears he also launched a campaign to lay waste to the country, capturing Aesernia. The region had been transformed from a thriving and densely populated area into the arid desert it remains today. A longer resistance was offered in Etruria by Populonium and above all by the impregnable Volaterrae. The city gathered an army of four legions from the remnants of the defeated party and withstood a two-year siege conducted first by Sulla in person, then by the former praetor Gaius Carbo, brother of the democratic consul. It was not until the third year after the Battle of the Colline Gate (675) that the garrison surrendered on condition of free departure.

They were spared the difficult task of opening up the passes of the Pyrenees by force because the general sent to them by Sertorius had been killed, and his troops subsequently melted away. He thus became master of the enemy in one day; Ahenobarbus was among the fallen. With the aid of King Bogud, Hiarbas was seized and slain at Bulla, while Hiempsal was reinstated in his hereditary kingdom. A great razzia against the inhabitants of the desert, including several Gaetulian tribes recognized as free by Marius, were made subject to Hiempsal. This revived the fallen reputation of the Roman name in Africa. In forty days after Pompeius' landing in Africa, all was at an end. ) Sulla yielded, and allowed the young man to boast of being the only Roman who had become a triumphator before he was a senator. The "Fortunate" saluted the youth on his return from these easy exploits as the "Great."

Lucius Lucullus was obliged to bring up troops against the free city of Mytilene, particularly, after exhausting all gentle measures. Even a victory in the open field did not put an end to the stubborn resistance of its citizens. The Roman governor of Asia, Lucius Murena, faced new challenges as he struggled with King Mithridates. The assurances of Archelaus, who had been obliged to seek asylum with Murena, induced Murena to move his troops towards the Cappadocian Comana under the pretext that Mithradates still kept possession of the Cappadocian frontier districts.

Capture of Mytilene

A foolish feud had delayed the reduction of the Mytilenaeans. It was only after a long siege by land and sea, during which the Bithynian fleet provided good service, that Murena's successor finally captured the city by storm in 675. This text is already grammatically correct and free of spelling mistakes. However, I will make some minor adjustments to improve readability:

"After the terrible convulsions of the last few years, even this brief respite was a welcome relief." The mass of the aristocracy was certainly of opinion that now, after the death of the two revolutionary consuls, it would be sufficient to make arrangements for the ordinary supplemental election and leave it to the senate to take such steps as seemed further requisite for rewarding the victorious army, punishing the most guilty revolutionists, and possibly also preventing similar outbreaks.

Sulla was equally committed to restoring the ancient constitution as the most ardent aristocratic exile. He grasped this concept, though perhaps not fully comprehending its implications—how else could he have begun working on it? But better at any rate than his party, the enormous difficulties that attended this work of restoration. If Sulla had already carried out what he deemed necessary after the Sulpician revolution without asking much of their advice, he was now determined, under circumstances of far more severe and intense excitement, to restore the oligarchy, not with their aid but in spite of them by his own hand. Sulla, the Regent of Rome, was not now consul as he had been then. Instead, he was furnished with proconsular power, which is purely military authority. He needed an authority that closely resembled constitutional forms but was still extraordinary, enabling him to impose his reforms on both friends and foes.

He had maintained a high level of restraint even after the revolution of 1666. The second revolution, marked by atrocities and having a profound impact on him personally, did not disrupt his composure. Even after his initial successes, he had negotiated with Lucius Scipio in this spirit. However, it was the revolutionary party that had broken off these negotiations. At the last moment before their downfall, they resumed the massacres more fearfully than ever and even conspired with their sworn enemies to destroy the city of Rome. In other cases, death had already claimed many notable opponents. Only Gaius Norbanus survived, laying hands on himself at Rhodes while the ecclesiastical authorities deliberated his surrender. Lucius Scipio, whose insignificance and noble birth likely earned him leniency, was granted permission to end his days in peace at his retreat in Massilia. Quintus Sertorius, an exile wandering the coast of Mauretania, remained unaccounted for.

In like manner, the professional accusers, the worst scourge of the nobility, who made it their trade to bring men of the senatorial order before the equestrian courts, now had to suffer for it. An advocate soon asked, "How comes it that they have left us the courts, when they were putting to death the accusers and judges?" "The most savage and disgraceful passions raged unchecked for several months in Italy." At Larinum, a town welcoming new citizens and sympathetic to Marian views, Statius Albius Oppianicus, who had fled to Sulla's headquarters to escape a charge of murder, reappeared after the victory as commissioner of the regent. He then deposed the magistrates of the town, took control of his own quarters, and had those who had threatened to accuse him, along with their closest relatives and friends, outlawed and killed. The regent did not forget himself. His wife, Metella, was particularly favored, as well as other high-ranking individuals who were closely connected to him. Freedmen and loyal companions were occasionally permitted to buy without competition, while in some cases their purchase money was entirely or partially waived.

One of his freedmen purchased a property worth 6 million sesterces for 2,000 sesterces. Another subaltern acquired an estate valued at 10 million sesterces through such investments. The indignation was great and just. Even during Sulla's regency, an advocate asked if the nobility had waged civil war solely to enrich their freedmen and slaves. The oligarchic reign of terror bore a different stamp from that of the revolution. While Marius had glutted his personal vengeance in the blood of his enemies, Sulla seemed to view terrorism as an abstract concept, necessary for introducing the new despotism. He was indifferent to prosecuting and having others prosecute the work of massacre.

Maintenance of the Burgess Rights in Regulating Italy-Rome Relations

Sulla, although generally treating state acts as null during the revolution, except for current business transactions, firmly adhered to the principle laid down by the Burgess Rights. This principle held that every burgess of an Italian community was also a burgess of Rome. The distinctions between burgesses and Italian allies were abolished, as well as those between old and new burgesses with varying rights. The iron arm of the regent fell with especial weight on Etruria and Samnium, two regions that had offered serious resistance up to the end and even after the battle at the Colline gate. To repeat people in Samnium was, as we have said, not part of the regent's design.

The settlements were established after the Gracchan period, when settlers joined an existing urban community. The settlement's comprehensiveness is evident from the number of land allotments distributed, which totals 120,000. However, some portions of land were allocated to other purposes, such as the temple of Diana at Mount Tifata. The Volaterran domain and a part of the Arretine also remained undistributed. Furthermore, certain lands were taken over by Sulla's favourites, who exercised their right of occupation despite it being legally forbidden in the past but now being revived. In so far as the contrast between the soldier and the burgess was intended to remain, and did remain, in force in the Sullan colonies even after their establishment, these colonists formed a standing array of the senate. They are not incorrectly designated, in contradistinction to the older ones, as military colonies.

Sulla undoubtedly granted those exiled by the equestrian courts liberty to return, as seen in the case of Publius Rutilius Rufus,(12) who failed to take advantage of the permission, while Gaius Cotta, a friend of Drusus,(13) also did not benefit from it. However, this measure only partially compensated for the gaps created by the tumultuous and divisive reigns of terror that had ravaged the senate ranks. The burgess body remained formally sovereign; however, in relation to its primary assemblies, the regent felt it was essential to maintain the form while preventing any actual activity from taking place. Sulla dealt with the franchise itself in a contemptuous manner, making no difficulty in granting it to new burgess communities or bestowing it on Spaniards and Celts en masse. It is likely that he did so with design, as no steps were taken to adjust the burgess-roll, which was urgently needed for revision after violent revolutions, despite the government's apparent seriousness about its legal privileges.

The legislative functions of the comitia were not directly restricted. In fact, there was no need for such a restriction, as the senate's better-secured initiative meant that the people could not easily interfere with government administration, finance, or criminal jurisdiction. As a result, the legislative cooperation of the comitia was reduced to its original right: giving assent to constitutional amendments. In this latest enactment, the earlier ordinance of 412 was revived, rather than an absolute prohibition on re-election to the consulship, as had been the preferred idea of the most recent ultra-oligarchic era. On the whole, however, Sulla left the elections to take their course, and sought merely to limit the power of the magistrates in such a way that—let the caprice of the comitia call to office whomsoever it might—the person elected should not be able to rebel against the oligarchy. The consulship and praetorship, though viewed by the aristocratic regenerator of Rome with a more favorable eye than the tribunate, were not immune to the distrust that characterized oligarchy.

In the ordinary course of things, accordingly, the six fields of special jurisdiction were apportioned among the six praetors. The two judicial appointments in the capital and the four transmarine provinces fell under their purview. By virtue of their general powers, the two consuls managed the non-judicial business of the capital and exercised military command over the continental possessions. For this case in reality, a remedy was provided by the rule that there was no interregnum in the military imperium. As such, although it had its legal term, it yet remained in effect until the successor appeared and relieved his predecessor of command. Alternatively, if a successor did not appear, the commanding consul or praetor after the expiry of their term of office was bound to continue acting in the stead of the consul or praetor they replaced. The demarcation of consular and praetorian functions on each occasion belonged to the senate. The designation of individuals to assume these offices did not follow a specific pattern, but rather occurred through agreement among the relevant magistrates or by lottery.

The new arrangement limited the magistracies of the capital and governorships throughout to one year. A special enactment requiring every governor to leave their province within thirty days after their successor's arrival there is evident, particularly when considered alongside the previously mentioned prohibition on immediate re-election for the late magistrate to either the same or another public office, revealing the underlying tendency of these arrangements. Even after their victory at the Colline Gate, the Senate, seeing that the state treasury had been carried off to Praeneste, was forced to take immediate action. The Sullan Ordinances can be characterized as the first Roman code after the Twelve Tables and the first criminal code specifically issued.

The Roman polity adhered to this view with its peculiar consistency. Even in the sixth century, dependent communities in Italy were either constituted as formally sovereign states of non-burgesses or, if they obtained the Roman franchise, were deprived of properly municipal rights. This meant that even in burgess-colonies and burgess-municipia, the administration of justice and the charge of buildings devolved on the Roman praetors and censors. It is probable that its germs may be traced to exceptional regulations for the great burgess-colonies, which were founded at the end of the sixth century. Several differences between burgess-colonies and burgess-municipia tend to show that the new burgess-colony, which at that time practically replaced the Latin, had originally a better position in state-law than the far older burgess-municipium. The advantage doubtless lies in a municipal constitution approximating to the Latin, such as afterwards belonged to all burgess-colonies and burgess-municipia. It is unclear whether the Julian law, the censors of 668, or Sulla first arranged the details. The entrusting of censorial functions to the duumvirate seems to have been introduced after the analogy of Sulla's ordinance, which superseded censorship. Alternatively, it may be equally well referred to the oldest Latin constitution, in which censorship was unknown.

The civil war provided fearful evidence of this total revolution in the spirit of the army. Six generals were in command: Albinus, Cato, Rufus, Flaccus, Cinna, and Gaius Carbo. During its course, all but one of them fell by the hands of their soldiers. Sulla alone had hitherto been able to retain mastery over the dangerous crew, and that was only possible because he gave free rein to all their wild desires, a tactic no Roman general before him had ever employed. He had not, however, taken the military dictatorship solely for the purpose of making the state subject to the military, but rather for the purpose of compelling everything in the state, and especially the army and its officers, to submit once more to the authority of civil order. Gnaeus Pompeius, chosen by Sulla to conquer Sicily and Africa, was appointed as his son-in-law. After completing his mission, he received an order from the Senate to disband his army. However, he refused to obey and nearly led to open rebellion.

Although the Valerian law granted him absolute power, it also gave his ordinances the force of law. Nevertheless, he had availed himself of this extraordinary prerogative only in cases of transient importance, where participating would have compromised the senate and burgesses, especially in the case of proscriptions. When the new state of affairs seemed likely to hold its ground, and the largest and most important portion of the new arrangements had been completed, although various matters, particularly in colonization, still needed to be done, he allowed the elections for 675 to take place freely. He declined re-election to the consulship because it was incompatible with his own ordinances. At the beginning of 675, he resigned the regency, soon after the new consuls Publius Servilius and Appius Claudius had taken office. He had a deep affection for fine wine and its accompanying cups, but his passion for women remained unwavering. Even in his later years, as a former regent, he would put aside the duties of the day to take his seat at the table.

Even when regent, he gave orders while conducting the public sale of the property of those proscribed. A donation from the spoils should be given to the author of a wretched panegyric which was handed to him, on condition that the writer would promise never to sing his praises again. In a world governed by chance, wishing and striving seemed like folly. Yet, if people strived for something, it would likely be driven by chance alone. The Delphic priests informed him that they were hesitant to deliver the treasures he had requested, citing the harp of the god, which produced a distinct sound upon contact. He responded by saying they could now send the treasures with greater ease, as the god's approval was evident in his plan.

During his praetorship, which office he held after failing a previous candidature in 661, it once more chanced that in his province, the least important of all, the first victory over King Mithradates and the first treaty with the mighty Arsacids, as well as their first humiliation, occurred. Like a captain who refuses to extinguish the flames of his own burning ambition, Sulla continued to wage war against the enemy, even as the revolution raged on in Italy. The moment of his return home was overwhelming for Sulla, filled with both joy and pain. He recounts in his memoirs that on his first night in Rome, he could not sleep, and we can reasonably assume this is true.

There is no doubt about the difference between an officer who refuses the sceptre due to public spirit and one who throws it away out of a cloyed appetite; however, when it comes to the total absence of political selfishness—a quality in which Sulla excels—although this is true only in one respect—he deserves to be mentioned alongside Washington. A man of conservative views could take steps to preserve the old constitution, as Sulla did. He had forebodings that while he might build a fortress, he would be unable to establish a reliable garrison, and that the uselessness of the oligarchs would make any attempt to save the oligarchy futile. His constitution resembled a temporary dike thrown into the raging breakers. It was no reproach to the builder that some ten years later the waves had swallowed up a structure at odds with nature, which had been left undefended even by those it had sheltered.

The immoral and superficial nature of the Sullan restoration cannot be overlooked. It was not solely the statesman who had a say in condemning the actions of the past, nor could human emotions, fueled by justice, ever come to terms with what Sulla did or allowed others to do. His military discipline was lax, despite being influenced by his political needs. However, his leniency towards his political supporters was even more damaging. The saying that he was before his regency a good-natured, mild man, but when regent a bloodthirsty tyrant, carries in it its own refutation. If he as regent displayed the reverse of his earlier gentleness, it must rather be said that he punished with the same careless nonchalance with which he pardoned.

It is as if the victor considered victory itself to have no value. He seemed to anticipate the vanity and impermanence of his own achievement, much like a steward who prefers making repairs rather than tearing down and rebuilding. In the end, he was content with a makeshift solution that only concealed the flaws. It was the same temperament that led him to seek idleness again in the capital, following his brilliant successes in Africa. After gaining complete control, he found solace and rejuvenation in his Cuman villa.

He remained unchanged after his resignation, free from petulance and pretension. He was pleased to be free from public concerns, but occasionally intervened when the situation arose. Death of Sulla

Little more than a year after his retirement, in the sixtieth year of his life, Sulla died while still vigorous in body and mind. He was confined to a sick-bed for a brief period before his death, during which he even managed to write two days' worth of his autobiography. The rupture of a blood vessel ultimately carried him off. The equites and soldiers held their race of honor around the corpse as flames blazed. The ashes of the regent were laid to rest in the Campus Martius beside the tombs of ancient kings.

As the entire ancient civilization became increasingly integrated into the Roman state, its forms of universal applicability took hold. Meanwhile, nations outside of it began to shift their stance beyond the Alps and the Euphrates, transitioning from defense to aggression. While the system of collecting taxes through middlemen, particularly those who held general leases, proved costly, Rome's competitive landscape became severely hindered due to the limited subdivision of lettings and the large concentration of capital. To these ordinary burdens, however, fell the primary responsibility of adding the initial requisitions.

If the governor needed grain, ships, slaves to man them, linen, leather, money, or anything else, he was at liberty in time of war to demand such supplies according to his discretion and exigencies from subject communities or sovereign protected states. In times of peace, it was not far otherwise. These supplies were treated legally as purchases or advances, and their value was made good by the Roman exchequer. These requisitions became, in practice, one of the most oppressive burdens on provincials. The fact that compensation was usually settled by the government or governor in an unfair manner made matters worse. While fixing such rules, the pressure of those requisitions on the economy of communities and individuals in the province was undoubtedly alleviated here and there. However, it was by no means eliminated.

Sulla compelled the provincials of Asia Minor, who had gravely offended Rome, to provide forty-fold pay to every common soldier quartered among them (16 denarii per day = 11 shillings). Centurions received seventy-five-fold pay, in addition to clothing and meals, as well as the right to invite guests at pleasure. Sulla soon afterwards imposed a general contribution on client and subject communities, with no mention of repayment. Although every present given to the governor could be treated legally as a tax, and even his right to buy might be limited by law, it did not prevent him from using his public powers to do wrong. Even under these circumstances, a moderate tax in theory might become extremely oppressive in practice. It is clear that this was the case, despite the financial oppression felt by Italian merchants and bankers in the provinces being even more burdensome than the tax itself, which was marred by numerous abuses. The political hegemony, equally honourable and judicious, was still the guiding principle for the financial administration of the provinces, just as it had been in ancient Rome.

It is true, however, that this principle originated in a previous era when such exceptions were allowed to prevail. The two ancient aqueducts, the Appian and the Anio, had been in existence since 442 and 492 respectively. In 610, they were thoroughly repaired, but two new ones were also formed: the Marcian aqueduct, which remained unsurpassed for its excellence and abundance of water, and the Tepula, built nineteen years later. We cannot afford to overlook the fact that, despite the government's impressive construction projects during the first two-thirds of this period, they failed to allocate sufficient funds for other essential expenditures.

While public works that can be shown to have been constructed from the Battle of Pydna down to the time of Gaius Gracchus were numerous, there is scant mention of any other projects after 632. The notable exceptions are the bridges, roads, and drainage systems organized by Marcus Aemilius Scaurus as censor in 645. The severe storm of civil unrest and revolution, combined with a five-year deficit in the revenues of Asia Minor, posed the first significant test to Rome's finances following the Hannibal War. They were unable to withstand it. The difference between the times is perhaps most clearly marked by the circumstance that it was not until the tenth year of the Hannibal War that the reserve was touched. In contrast, the Social War was supported from its inception and when the balance in hand was exhausted after two campaigns to the last penny, the public sites in the capital were sold by auction, and the treasures of the temples were seized rather than levying a tax on the burgesses.

The storm, however, passed over; Sulla, at great cost to his subjects and Italian revolutionists, restored order to the finances by abolishing corn largesse and retaining Asiatic revenues in a reduced form, securing a satisfactory economic condition, with ordinary expenditures well below ordinary incomes. The plantations system, which by the middle of this epoch had already gained ascendancy even in particular districts of Italy, such as Etruria, had, through the co-operation of an energetic and methodical management and abundant financial resources, attained to a state of high prosperity. The production of Italian wine, particularly, was artificially promoted through the opening of forced markets in certain provinces and the prohibition of foreign wines in Italy, as seen in the sumptuary law of 593. As a result, Amian and Falernian wines gained recognition alongside Thasian and Chian wines, while the "Opimian wine" of 633, also known as the Roman vintage "Eleven," remained notable even after its last jar was exhausted.

There is nothing to be said about Trades of Italy, except that they persevered in an inaction bordering on barbarism. They destroyed the Corinthian factories, which housed many valuable industrial traditions. However, their goal was not to establish similar factories for themselves, but rather to purchase Corinthian vases made of earthenware or copper at inflated prices, along with other "antique works" found in Greek homes. The trades that were still somewhat prosperous, such as those connected with building, yielded hardly any benefit to the commonwealth. This was due to the system of employing slaves in every more considerable undertaking. For example, during the construction of the Marcian aqueduct, the government concluded contracts for both building and materials simultaneously. They hired 3,000 master-tradesmen, each of whom worked with his band of slaves to complete the contracted work.

The financial transactions, however, throughout the entire Roman state were controlled by the Romans. Every coin that circulated in Gaul, as stated in a document written shortly after the end of this period, was recorded in the accounts of Roman merchants, and thus it was undoubtedly widespread. For a long time, traffic through Corinth was conducted, but after its destruction, it passed through Delos. In this sense, Puteoli is called "Little Delos" by Lucilius, the Italian. However, following the catastrophe that occurred at Delos during the Mithridatic War (28), and from which it never recovered, the Puteolans established direct commercial connections with Syria and Alexandria. As a result, their city became increasingly the first seat of transmarine commerce in Italy. How many people were in Delos is evident from the tombstones still standing on the island. The statement also mentions that 20,000 foreigners, mostly Italian merchants, were killed there by order of Mithradates. In Africa, the Italians played a significant role. The Numidian town of Cirta was able to withstand attacks from Jugurtha due to their presence. Gaul, it is said, was filled with Roman merchants. In the case of Spain, no statements of this sort are found.

The Italian population by the census of 684 consisted of 910,000 men capable of bearing arms. To determine the number of free individuals in the peninsula, those who were accidentally overlooked in the census, including Latins living in the district between the Alps and the Po, as well as foreigners residing in Italy, must be added. Conversely, Roman citizens living abroad should be subtracted from this total. It will therefore be scarcely possible to estimate the free population of the peninsula at more than six million. It is not necessary to resort to such fallacious calculations to make the dangerous situation apparent; this is amply attested by the partial slave insurrections, and by the appeal made at the outset of every uprising addressed to the slaves to take up arms against their masters and fight for their freedom.

The silver piece retained its weight and quality for over three centuries, unchanged since its reduction in the Hannibal War from 1/72 (42) to 1/84 of a pound. The use of copper money became restricted at the start of this period, mainly for small transactions. As a result, it ceased to be used for large transactions, leading to its discontinuation around the beginning of the seventh century. The remaining copper coinage was limited to smaller denominations, such as semis (1/4 pence) and below, which could not be accurately represented in silver. During the Cinnan government, a law was enacted by the praetors and tribunes, primarily by Marcus Marius Gratidianus (45), to redeem all token money with silver. An assay office was then established.

In Africa and Sardinia, Carthaginian gold and silver coins may have circulated even after the fall of the Carthaginian state. However, no new coinage of precious metals occurred there after either the Carthaginian or Roman standard was adopted. Soon after the Romans took control, the denarius introduced from Italy became the dominant currency in transactions between the two countries. Such progress had already been made in this epoch that the denarius exclusively prevailed in the whole western division of the Roman state. For Italy, Sicily, it is expressly attested that no other silver money circulated there but the denarius. Sardinia, Africa, and used exclusively Roman silver money, as well as the provincial silver still current in Spain, were at least struck after the standard of the denarius. In areas where the number of states minting coins from ancient times and the amount of native coins in circulation were substantial, the denarius failed to gain widespread acceptance, despite being possibly declared a legal tender.

The previous monetary standard in use, such as in Macedonia, still used its Attic tetradrachmae in substance, despite partially incorporating the names of Roman magistrates into the country's name. Alternatively, a unique money standard was introduced under Roman authority, as seen in the institution of the province of Asia, where a new stater called the cistophorus was prescribed by the Roman government and subsequently struck by local capitals under Roman supervision. This diversity between the Occidental and Oriental systems of currency became historically significant: The Romanization of subject lands utilized the adoption of Roman money as a primary lever. It was not by chance that what we now call the denarius field became Latin, while the drachma field became Greek, half of the empire. Dinner had been previously described as poor. The fowl was served in its entirety to the guests, rather than just the choicest parts. Guests were expected to sample all the various dishes, rather than merely sampling them.
