Unfortunately, truth requires us to acknowledge that among all Romans of rank, only a few - no more than three - are said to have conformed to these laws; and in the case of these three, it was the law of the Stoa, not the state's law, that limited their bill of fare. It is worth considering the luxury that was increasing in defiance of these laws, particularly with regards to silver. To understand the costs incurred on these projects, we must recall that the workmanship was also paid for at substantial rates. For example, Gaius Gracchus spent fifteen times the value of silver on select items, while Lucius Crassus, consul in 59 BC, paid eighteen times the metal's worth for a pair of cups by a renowned silversmith, with the latter paying 100,000 sesterces ( equivalent to 1,000 pounds).

The impact of marriage and childrearing is evident in the Gracchan agrarian laws, which initially prioritized these aspects. Divorce, once almost unheard of in Rome, was now a common occurrence. In ancient Roman marriages, husbands would often purchase their wives. It might have been proposed to Romans of high quality in modern times that, as a way to align names with reality, they should consider introducing the concept of marriage for hire. In the capital, however, the Catonian opposition had become a mere form of words. The modern tendency bore sovereign sway, and though individuals of firm and refined organization, such as Scipio Aemilianus, knew how to combine Roman manners with Attic culture, Hellenism was among the great multitude synonymous with intellectual and moral corruption. If that's not the case, please provide the correct spelling. As the afternoon progressed, they summoned the servant and instructed him to inquire about the Comitium, specifically regarding events in the Forum: Who had spoken in favor of or opposed the new law proposal, which tribes had voted in support and which against it?

As a result of the Social War, any portion of Italian soil could belong to any Italian in full Roman ownership, and any god of an Italian temple might receive Roman gifts. In all Italy, with the exception of the region beyond the Po, Roman law had exclusive authority, superseding all other civic and local laws. Consequently, the Roman language became the universal language of business and soon also the universal language of cultivated intercourse throughout the entire peninsula from the Alps to the Sicilian Straits. Gracchus first distinctly developed the idea of colonizing or Romanizing the provinces of the Roman state through Italian emigration. He endeavored to carry out this project, despite conservative opposition that resisted the bold initiative. Although the opposition largely destroyed the beginnings of the endeavor and prevented its continuation, the colony of Narbo was preserved. This colony was significant in itself, as it extended the domain of the Latin tongue. More importantly, it served as a landmark for a great idea, laying the foundation for a mighty structure to come. Hellenism, however, despite the rapid and vigorous spread of the Latin language and nationality, acknowledges the Hellenic nationality as having an equally valid, if not earlier and superior, claim. It enters into close alliances with it or becomes intertwined with it in a shared development.

The Italian revolution, which otherwise leveled all the non-Latin nationalities in the peninsula, did not disturb the Greek cities of Tarentum, Rhegium, Neapolis, and Locri. In like manner, Massilia, though now surrounded by Roman territory, continued to be a Greek city and maintained its connection with Rome. While Greek literary men of note had previously resided temporarily in Rome as ambassadors, exiles, or otherwise, they now began to settle there. For example, Panaetius, already mentioned, lived in the house of Scipio, while Archias of Antioch settled in Rome around 652 and supported himself through his skill in improvisation and writing epic poems about Roman consuls. While intellectual and literary life thus connected the more refined elements of both nations, on the other hand, the arrival of troops from Asia Minor and Syria, along with mercantile immigration from the Greek and half-Greek east, brought the coarsest aspects of Hellenism - largely mixed with Oriental and barbaric influences - into contact with the Italian working class, giving it a distinctly Hellenistic hue.

Italy was inhabited by Greeks, Syrians, Phoenicians, Jews, and Egyptians, while the provinces were populated by Romans. The blending of cultures resulted in the exchange of national peculiarities, which eventually merged into a common identity. It seemed as if nothing was left but the general impression of utilitarianism. Cicero assures us that about 660 the general culture in Latin towns stood higher than in Rome. This is confirmed by the literature of this period, whose most pleasing, healthiest, and most characteristic products, such as national comedy and Lucilian satire, are with greater justice described as Latin rather than Roman. As Marcus Cicero, the father of the renowned orator, noted, in Roman society as well as among Syrian slaves, those who were less valuable became more so with greater understanding of Greek.

The religious revolution was largely a product of the preceding period, where it primarily prepared itself for acceptance in men's minds rather than taking on an outward form. This is evident in the fact that the Gracchan and Sullan ages were responsible for its substance, at least. Plato and Aristotle, along with other ancient philosophers such as Socrates, had little direct impact on Roman culture. However, their renowned names were often invoked, and their more accessible writings were widely read and translated. Epicurus, following Democritus' atomic theory, posited that the first principle was rigid matter. He derived the multiplicity of things from this matter through mechanical variations alone. In contrast, Zeno, influenced by Heraclitus of Ephesus, introduced a dynamic antagonism and movement into his primordial matter, allowing for fluctuation up and down.

Even in the writings of Cicero, it is declared that a citizen's duty is to resist Euhemerism, which is prejudicial to religious worship. If the Academic and Epicurean schools appear in his dialogues, the former must plead that, as a philosopher, he is a disciple of Carneades; as a citizen and pontifex, he is an orthodox confessor of Capitoline Jupiter. The Epicurean, however, ultimately has to surrender and convert. The plain intelligible character of Euhemerism undoubtedly exerted a certain power of attraction over the Romans. In particular, it produced only too deep an effect on the conventional history of Rome, which was characterized by its childish and senile conversion of fable into history. However, Euhemerism had no material influence on Roman religion, as the latter dealt exclusively in allegory rather than fable. This distinction made it impossible to write biographies of Zeus, even if one were to consider him as a first, second, or third incarnation. The Stoic philosophy was widely accepted by notable statesmen and scholars, including Stilo and Quintus Scaevola, who founded scientific philology and scientific jurisprudence.

The speculative element, initially impressed by Zeno's system, but with little energy, was further weakened when that system gained acceptance in Rome. Following a century of Greek schoolmasters promoting this philosophy among boys, it had driven the spirit out of it. As a result, the speculative element fell into complete obscurity in Rome, where only money-changers speculated. The ideal development of the God ruling within the soul of man and the divine world-law were barely discussed. It suited itself to the hollow pride of virtue, a sentiment the Romans of this period sought to compensate for amidst the humbling circumstances of their contact with the Greeks. In doing so, it formalized a dogmatic morality that, like any well-bred system of morals, combined rigid precision with complacent indulgence in its details. Its practical results can hardly be estimated as much more than that, as we have said, a few families of modest means ate simple fare to please the Stoa. One of the most prominent men of the Scipionic circle, the Greek historian Polybius, candidly declares that the strange and ponderous ceremonial of Roman religion was invented solely on account of the multitude, which, as reason had no power over it, required to be ruled by signs and wonders. People of intelligence, however, had certainly no need of religion. The Romans who were friends with Polybius likely agreed on this point, but they didn't take a stark contrast between science and religion.

Oriental Religions in Italy

In stark contrast to the ghost of religion we have just described, Oriental religions stood out as distinct foreign worship practices that flourished during this era. They were undoubtedly characterized by a strong vitality. The Roman Senate was forced to comply with the demands of a mad prophetess during the turmoil of 667. It is significant that the ossification of the Romano-Hellenic religion and the increased craving for stronger religious stimulants led to superstition no longer associating itself with national religion. Even Etruscan mysticism has been left behind, while the matured worships in the eastern regions have taken their rightful place.

A similar effect was produced by commercial intercourse, particularly after the wares of Berytus and Alexandria were conveyed directly to the Italian ports. Ostia and Puteoli became great markets, not only for Syrian unguents and Egyptian linen, but also for the faith of the East. It was even necessary to prohibit human sacrifices forty years later. We had already demonstrated how the fundamental concept of the Roman system, civil equality, was beginning to be eroded in this area as early as the sixth century.

He was indeed an old man before it was granted to him, with the Homeric poems in mind, to stand before the Zeus of Phidias; yet his heart remained young enough to carry home the full sunshine of Hellenic beauty and the unconquerable longing for the golden apples of the Hesperides in his soul. Poets and artists had found in the foreigner a more earnest and cordial devotee than any of the wise men of Greece at that time. It is true that this new mode of juvenile instruction, revolutionary and anti-national as it was, encountered partial resistance from the government. However, the edict of dismissal, which the authorities issued in 593 against rhetoricians and philosophers, remained largely ineffective due to the frequent change of Roman chief magistrates. After the death of old Cato, there were likely frequent complaints in accordance with his views, but no further action was taken. Cultivated Romans, stirred by the Homeric prelections of Crates, began reading their own literature's recitative works. This included Naevius' Punic War, Ennius' Annals, and Lucilius' Poems. Initially, they were shared with a select circle; later, on designated days, in public, and before large crowds. Occasionally, these works were also discussed critically, following the precedent of Homeric grammarians.

To the solid practical advocate, the procedure of the Greek rhetoricians, so totally estranged from life, could appear worse for the beginner than no preparation at all. For the man of thorough culture, matured by the experience of life, the Greek rhetoric seemed shallow and repulsive. In contrast, the man of serious conservative views observed a close affinity between a professionally developed rhetoric and the trade of the demagogue. There is certainly an improvement. Inadequate efforts are now less frequent in this epoch. Performances of this kind, which are complete and thoroughly pleasing, occur more often than before or afterwards. From a linguistic perspective, Cicero refers to this period as the golden age of pure, unadulterated Latin, specifically the era of Laelius and Scipio. We now face the national tragedy (-praetexta-), a creation attributed to Naevius, which is preserved solely through Pacuvius' works. Two poets alone acquired a considerable name among those who imitated Greek tragedies.

Marcus Pacuvius from Brundisium (c. 535 - c. 465 BCE) Aemulus Barucius, who in his earlier years earned his livelihood in Rome by painting, only composed tragedies when advanced in life, belongs as respects both his years and his style to the sixth century, although his poetical activity falls within the seventh. He followed the style of his countryman, uncle, and master Ennius. Lucius Accius, son of a freedman from Pisaurum, provided more readable and adroit imitations of Greek tragedy. He was the only notable tragic poet of the seventh century, except for Pacuvius.

The method of working up several Greek pieces into one Latin work was retained by Terence, as it was inevitable given the state of the case. However, he handled this process with significantly greater skill and care than Roman editors. The Plautine dialogue departed from its models with great frequency. Terence boasted about the verbal fidelity of his imitations to the originals, but this should not be taken to mean a literal translation as we understand it today. The elegance of language was a hallmark of the poet, and it was this unique charm that earned him the highest praise from the most discerning judges of art in later times, including Cicero, Caesar, and Quintilian.

This was the more significant, for after the early death of Terence, no man of conspicuous talent occupied this field again. It wasn't until the present period that the Atellan piece was handed over to actors properly known as such, and it was used, like the Greek satyric drama, as an afterpiece, especially after tragedies. This shift naturally led to the expansion of literary activity into that area. The city's diverse life was acknowledged, but the farmer's experiences, including his joys and sorrows, were not fully considered.

Although these plays, at least after they were written, adhered to the general laws of literature, and in their meter followed the Greek stage, they still retained a distinctly Latin and more popular flavor than even the national comedy. The introduction of masks around the time of Terence is likely linked to the improved scenic arrangements and the fact that the construction and upkeep of the stage and its equipment were funded by the public treasury in 580. The plays that Lucius Mummius produced after the capture of Corinth in 609 BCE marked a significant era in the history of theater. It was likely then that a theater, acoustically constructed in the Greek style and equipped with seating, was first built, and more attention was given to the exhibitions. Now also there is frequent mention of the bestowal of a prize of victory, implying a competition among several pieces. The audience takes a lively part for or against the leading actors, with cliques and claqueurs present.

In recitative poetry, the most striking aspect is the relative insignificance of the epic. By the sixth century, the epic had dominated literature intended for reading, with numerous representatives appearing in the following century. However, there was no poet who achieved even temporary success during this period. The humorous epistles written by one of the younger men in the Scipionic circle, Spurius Mummius, brother of the destroyer of Corinth, were still read with pleasure a century later. This was due to numerous poetical pleasantries not intended for publication that likely originated from the rich social and intellectual life of Rome's better circles during that time. The aspiration of the Scipionic circle for literary correctness, particularly in matters of language, is best represented by Lucilius.

Whole books of his poems are occupied with the settlement of Latin orthography and prosody, combating Praenestine, Sabine, and Etruscan provincialisms, as well as exposing current solecisms. However, the poet by no means forgets to ridicule the insipid systematic purism of Isocratean words and phrases, nor does he fail to reproach his friend Scipio in a jesting manner with the exclusive fineness of his language. But the poet inculcates a greater emphasis on moral purity in both public and private life than he advocates for pure Latin. 546-c Megalopolis in the Peloponnesus, son of the Achaean statesman Lycortas, took part apparently as early as 565 in the Roman expedition against the Celts of Asia Minor. He was afterwards employed on various occasions, especially during the third Macedonian war, in both military and diplomatic affairs. After the crisis caused by that war in Hellas, he was taken along with other Achaean hostages to Italy, where he lived in exile for seventeen years (587-604) and was introduced to the elite circles of the capital by the sons of Paullus.

If in his youth he had paid homage to the honorable but impractical local patriotism of the Achaeans, during his later years, with a clear understanding of inevitable necessity, he advocated in the community to which he belonged the policy of closest adherence to Rome. The legend, anecdote, and mass of worthless chronicle notices are thrown aside; the description of countries and peoples, as well as political and mercantile relations, all the facts of infinite importance that escape the annalist because they do not fit a particular year, are put into possession of their long-suspended rights. The whole has value for him, both in nature and in the state. The particular event or individual man, no matter how remarkable they may seem, are merely isolated components, insignificant parts of a complex system that we call the state.

To these should be added the digest of the official annals of the city in eighty books, prepared by Publius Mucius Scaevola (consul in 621), a man esteemed also as a jurist. As pontifex maximus, he published this work, thereby closing the city-chronicle. From then on, although not exactly discontinued, the pontifical records were no longer taken into account by private chroniclers, who instead focused their efforts with increasing diligence in literature. Chronicle certainly nowhere presents truth without fiction. It would be very foolish to quarrel with Naevius and Pictor because they have not acted otherwise than Hecataeus and Saxo Grammaticus. However, the later attempts to build houses out of such castles in the air put even the most tried patience to a severe test. No tradition presents a wider chasm than this system of smooth and downright invention, which fills it up with playful facility. These exhibited valuable materials and an earnest spirit of truth. Antipater also displayed a lively style of narrative, although it was strongly affected. Judging from all testimonies and fragments, none of these books came up to the "Origines" of Cato in terms of pithy form or originality. Unfortunately, Cato created little of a school in both history and politics.

During the revolutionary commotions, pamphlet-literature increased in extent and importance. Among the mass of ephemeral productions, some works stood out, like Demosthenes' Philippics and Courrier's fugitive pieces, which acquired a permanent place in literature due to their authors' significant positions or their own substantial impact. It had been mentioned earlier that at the beginning of this century, Latin epic poets discovered their -diaskeuastaeand revisers of their text;(33) it was also observed that not only did the Scipionic circle generally emphasize correctness above all else, but several notable poets, such as Accius and Lucilius, also focused on regulating orthography and grammar. During the same period, we find isolated attempts to develop archaeology from a historical perspective. The dissertations of annalists from this era, such as those by Hemina on "The Censors" and Tuditanus on "The Magistrates," are unlikely to have been more accurate than their chronicles.

There was nothing left to do but to write manuals and exercise books in the style of the Greek compendia of Hermagoras and others. The schoolmasters accordingly supplied these, partly out of necessity, partly out of vanity and a desire for profit. Although the method was entirely dependent on the Greeks, the Romans rejected all "useless matter" that the Greeks had collected, solely for the purpose of making the science appear more difficult to learn. The harshest criticism is directed at those who engage in pedantic analysis—the "loquacious science of being unable to express oneself." Its founder, out of a desire to avoid ambiguity, eventually stopped uttering his own name. The author emphasizes the importance of teachers guiding students to help themselves. He also stresses that while education is crucial, it is secondary to life itself. In support of this view, he cites examples of independent scholars who have spoken out on issues relevant to their time, echoing the sentiments of prominent Roman advocates.

It is worth noting that the opposition to Hellenism's extravagances sought to prevent the rise of native Latin rhetoric. However, once this rhetoric arose, it influenced its development in order to secure Roman eloquence, which had a greater dignity and usefulness compared to the contemporary Greek eloquence. Although this development of law proceeded independently of Hellenism, an acquaintance with the philosophico-practical scheme-making of the Greeks gave a general impulse to its more systematic treatment. The Greek influence is evident in the case of the last-mentioned treatise, as indicated by its title. The first picture to be publicly exhibited in Rome was the Bacchus by Aristides. Even during Sulla's lifetime, when all other opposition was silenced, the strict jurists resisted the regent. The Cornelian laws, which deprived various Italian communities of Roman citizenship, were deemed null and void in judicial decisions. Similarly, courts ruled that a burgess who had been captured as a prisoner of war and sold into slavery during the revolution could not have his franchise forfeited. There were also the Populares, known strictly as such, who were genuinely credulous and narrow-minded radicals. They risked their property and lives in support of the party's current slogans, only to discover with painful surprise after their victory that they had been fighting not for reality, but for a phrase. Their special aim was to re-establish the tribunician power, which Sulla had not abolished but had divested of its most essential prerogatives. This power exercised over the multitude a charm all the more mysterious because the institution had no obvious practical use and was in fact an empty phantom—the mere name of tribune of the people.

All those who had squandered their means and substance in either refined or vulgar excess; the aristocratic lords, whose sole mark of distinction was their debt; the Sullan troopers, transformed into landholders by the regent's fiat but not into husbandmen, who after spending their first inheritance on proscribed goods now longed to inherit a second—waited only for the banner that called them to fight against the existing order. From a like necessity, aspiring men of talent sought popularity by attaching themselves to the opposition. Those who were denied admission or faced limited opportunities for rapid promotion, and thus attempted to break through the laws of oligarchic exclusivity and seniority with popular favor, joined the opposition as well. This included individuals whose ambition aimed higher than mere collegiate intrigue, seeking a more significant role in shaping world destinies. The means of resistance organized by Sulla were considerable and lasting. Despite the majority of the nation being disinclined to the government he had installed, and even animated by hostile feelings towards it, the government could maintain itself for a long time in its stronghold against a disunited opposition that lacked a clear end or means, and was fragmented into numerous factions.

All these things were done in accordance with a certain rule, and so to speak, publicly. The system of Hetaeriae was better organized and managed than any branch of state administration. Although there was, as is usual among civilized swindlers, a tacit understanding that there should be no direct mention of the nefarious proceedings, this did not prevent anyone from openly discussing their involvement with the Hetaeriae. Publius Cethegus, a character whose allegiance was far from equivocal, had once been one of the most zealous supporters of Marius. However, after deserting, he won favor with Sulla and played a significant role in the political events of this period. As a skilled mediator and tale-bearer, Cethegus was able to navigate the complex divisions within the senate. His ability to uncover and share secrets about cabals made him an invaluable asset to those seeking power. On occasion, even the most critical appointments to command were influenced by his mistress Praecia's word alone. Lucius Philippus, a member of the Luculli family from an older generation, was one of the few notable individuals who survived the civil wars. The old and eloquent consul, Lucius Philippus (consul in 63 BC), had initially been popular but later became a leader of the capitalist party against the senate. He was also closely associated with the Marians. In sufficient time to join the victorious oligarchy, he managed to avoid being caught between the warring parties.

Metellus was not merely estimable in his personal character, but an able and experienced officer. It was not solely due to his close relationship as a kinsman and colleague with the regent that he was sent to Spain in 675, after resigning the consulship, but rather because of his recognized ability. There, the Lusitanians and Roman emigrants under Quintus Sertorius were stirring anew. He adhered to the cultural norms of his era; as a soldier, he made an effort to appreciate and show respect for the local scholars in Rhodes. But this did not prevent him from separating from his beloved wife at Sulla's command, for she belonged to an outlawed family. Nor did he hesitate to order that men who had stood by him and helped him in times of difficulty be executed before his eyes with the mere nod of his master: he was not cruel, though accused of being so; but what was worse, he was cold, unimpassioned, and devoid of emotion, whether good or evil.

Purchases of estates during the revolution laid the groundwork for his wealth. However, he refused to limit himself to any single source of income; instead, he expanded into various ventures. He invested heavily in building projects within the capital city, exercising prudence and foresight. In addition, he formed partnerships with his freedmen on numerous business ventures. As a banker, he operated both within and outside Rome, often personally or through his agents. He also lent money to his colleagues in the senate and occasionally undertook construction projects or bribed officials on their behalf. On occasion of the Sullan proscriptions, a forgery in the lists had been proved against him. For this reason, Sulla made no further use of him in state affairs. He did not refuse to accept an inheritance because the testamentary document containing his name was notorious for being forged. He also made no objection when his bailiffs forcibly or fraudulently evicted petty holders from lands adjacent to his own. He avoided open collisions, but with criminal justice, he lived a life of simplicity and authenticity.

Crassus rose to prominence over the course of a few years, transforming from an ordinary senatorial fortune into one of the greatest wealth accumulations in history. Just before his death, he had incurred enormous and extraordinary expenses, yet his net worth still stood at approximately 1.7 million pounds. It is evident that a person with such a disposition and circumstances would not pursue modest goals. In contrast to Pompeius, Crassus understood the objects and means of political speculation as well as a banker understands his clients' assets. He inspired the admiration of learned advocates despite his lack of training as an orator, thanks to the natural flow and striking self-assurance in his speech.

He was known as the new Hannibal by his Spanish soldiers, not just because he had lost an eye in battle, like the famous hero. After Sulla's generals compelled him to leave Spain, he had led a restless life of adventure along the Spanish and African coasts. Sometimes, he sailed in league with the Cilician pirates who frequented these seas, while at other times he was at war with them. He also interacted with the chieftains of the roving tribes of Libya. There were no more than twenty Lusitanian communities that placed themselves under his orders. He also gathered only a few hundred Romans, including 260 men who had deserted from the army of Pacciaecus and a small group of Africans who had been trained in the Roman style.

In Further Spain, Metellus penetrated into the Lusitanian territory. However, Sertorius succeeded during the siege of Longobriga, a town near the mouth of the Tagus River, in luring Aquinus' division into an ambush. This forced Metellus to lift the siege and evacuate the Lusitanian territory. There was no Roman army in the Hither province after the victories of Hirtuleius. The sons of those who had been declared guilty of treason by Sulla, and were subject to the harsh laws of restoration, as well as the most prominent men who supported Marian policies, were summoned to pledge their allegiance.

Not a few, such as the young Lucius Cinna, joined the movement. Others, however, followed the example of Gaius Caesar, who had returned home from Asia on receiving accounts of Sulla's death and Lepidus' plans. After becoming more accurately acquainted with the character of the leader and the movement, he prudently withdrew. A conspiracy was hatched among the Etruscan malcontents. All this took place under the eyes of the government. The consul Catulus, along with the more judicious Optimates, urged immediate decisive interference and suppression of the revolt in its early stages. However, the indolent majority could not make up their minds to begin the struggle. Instead, they tried to deceive themselves as long as possible by a system of compromises and concessions. The senate put the oracular machinery in motion to induce him to return, and committed to him the conduct of the impending consular elections. However, Lepidus evaded compliance, and as messengers passed to and fro, the official year drew to a close amidst proposals of accommodation. His force swelled to an army.

While Pompeius quickly completed his mission and closed in on the enemy's general in Mutina, Lepidus arrived at the capital to seize it for the revolution, following in Marius' footsteps who had previously captured it by storm. However, Catulus emerged victorious; and Lepidus was forced to retreat to Etruria, while another division, led by his son Scipio, occupied the fortress of Alba. After a prolonged siege, Alba fell due to famine, and its leader was also executed.

The governor of the island opposed him with a vigorous resistance. He himself died shortly after landing from consumption, which brought an end to the war in Sardinia. Neither of the consuls that year showed any desire to measure themselves against Sertorius. What Lucius Philippus said in a full meeting of the senate had to be admitted as true: among all the senators of note, not one was able or willing to command in a serious war. They may have overcome this, acting like oligarchs who fill a position only when no suitable candidate is available. If Pompeius had simply wanted the command without demanding it as the commander of an army. Pompeius received proconsular authority and the chief command in Hither Spain from the senate, not from the people, which constitutionally ought to have been consulted. Forty days later, he crossed the Alps in the summer of 67.

Pompeius' appearance in Spain was met with little enthusiasm from Sertorius, who had not been idle. The isolated Celtiberian towns there, still loyal to Rome, were attacked and reduced one by one; eventually, the strong Contrebia (south-east of Saragossa) fell in the midst of winter. Despite sending multiple messages to Pompeius, the hard-pressed towns' pleas went unanswered; he remained resolute in his slow and deliberate advance.

With the exception of maritime towns, which were defended by the Roman fleet, Pompeius established himself in the north-east corner of Spain after crossing the Pyrenees. He made his raw troops bivouac throughout the winter to harden them for hardships. The rest of Hither Spain had become dependent on Sertorius by the end of 677, either through treaty or force. From then on, the district along the upper and middle Ebro became the mainstay of his power. For the campaign of Sertorius, Hirtuleius led the corps against Metellus. Perpenna positioned a strong army along the lower course of the Ebro to prevent Pompeius from crossing the river if he marched southward with the intention of joining forces with Metellus and securing supplies for his troops along the coast. Pompeius was defeated by Pompeius, however, he forced the passage of the Ebro against Perpenna and took up a position on the river Pallantias, near Saguntum, whence, as we have already said, the Sertorians maintained their communications with Italy and the east.

Metellus fought with better fortune. Meanwhile, Metellus advanced, and Pompeius offered battle to the main army of the enemy beforehand, aiming to erase the stain of Lauro and gain expected laurels, if possible, alone. The armies clashed on the Sucro River (Xucar). After a sharp conflict, Pompeius was defeated on the right wing and severely wounded; he was carried from the field.

Afranius undoubtedly conquered the left side and took the camp of the Sertorians. However, during his plundering, he was unexpectedly attacked by Sertorius and forced to yield as well. Meanwhile, Metellus had arrived, having defeated the corps of Perpenna that opposed him, and captured his camp. It was now impossible to continue fighting against the two armies united. The successes of Metellus led to a junction of hostile forces, which caused sudden stagnation after victory. This diffused terror among the Sertorians. As was often the case with Spanish armies, the majority of Sertorian soldiers subsequently dispersed. But despondency soon passed; a white fawn, symbolizing the general's military plans in the eyes of the multitude, gained even more popularity. Shortly after, Sertorius arrived with a new army confronting the Romans in the level country south of Saguntum (Murviedro), which remained loyal to Rome. Meanwhile, Sertorian privateers hindered Roman supplies by sea, and scarcity began to affect the Roman camp. While they vainly invested this rocky stronghold, the insurgent communities' contingents assembled elsewhere. Sertorius escaped from the fortress and, before the end of the year, regained his position as general at the head of an army.

It was not possible to choose quarters in Valentia, a region crucial for communication with Italy and the east, due to its devastation by both friend and foe. Pompeius led his troops first into the Vascones' territory (Biscay) and then spent the winter in the Vaccaei's territory (near Valladolid). Metellus even campaigned in Gaul. Spain was devastated and impoverished. The Roman civilization, which had promised such greatness, suffered a severe blow. It was only natural that an insurrectionary war, fought with such bitterness, would cause widespread destruction and devastation to entire communities. It was not their fault or that of their generals that a genius like Sertorius was able to sustain this petty warfare for years, despite the numerical and military superiority of their opponents, who were well-suited to the type of warfare that favored insurrection and piracy.

Sertorius had formed connections with the Italian and Macedonian enemies of Rome. It is uncertain whether these alliances were formal or merely informal. He was indeed in regular contact with the Marians in Italy. With the pirates, he had previously formed an avowed league. He now concluded a formal treaty of alliance with the Pontic king, whom he had long maintained relations with through Roman emigrants staying at his court. The treaty included Sertorius ceding to the king the client-states of Asia Minor, but not the Roman province of Asia. In addition, Sertorius promised to send the king an officer qualified to lead his troops and a number of soldiers. The king, in turn, agreed to transmit to Sertorius forty ships and 3,000 talents (720,000 pounds). The wise politicians in the capital recalled a time when Italy was threatened by Philip from the east and Hannibal from the west. They believed that the new Hannibal, following in his predecessor's footsteps, would easily arrive in Italy with the forces of Spain before Pompeius could do so. This would allow him to summon the Etruscans and Samnites to arms against Rome, as the Phoenicians had done formerly.

Sertorius may not retreat or step aside; he was compelled to follow the path he had chosen, no matter how narrow or treacherous it became. Metellus occupied the Celtiberian towns of Segobriga, between Toledo and Cuenca, and Bilbilis, near Calatayud. Marcus Antonius, Sertorius' neighbor at the table, struck the first blow against him. When Sertorius turned round to rise, the assassin rushed towards him and pinned him down until the other guests at the table, all implicated in the conspiracy, attacked the struggling pair, stabbing the defenseless general while his arms were bound.

After Sertorius' death, Perpenna, as the highest-ranking Roman officer in the Spanish army, claimed the chief command. Men had murmured against Sertorius during his lifetime. Death reinstated the hero in his rights, and the soldiers' indignation was vehement upon learning that Perennius was one of the heirs on the publication of his testament. Soon after, the Plotian law, which was zealously supported by young Caesar in particular, opened up to a portion of them the opportunity of returning home. However, all those who had taken part in the murder of Sertorius, with one exception, died a violent death.

The good fortune of Sulla appeared to still be with his creation even after he had been laid in the grave, and it was protected better by the vigilant guardians who were appointed to safeguard it. This defeat was not due to the efforts of its opponents, but rather the result of internal divisions. It was a victory for the oligarchy nonetheless. In the preceding chapter, we have described the movements in Italy and Spain connected with the proceedings of democracy and their subjugation by the senatorial government.

The expedition to the Graeco-Illyrian peninsula was designed to subjugate or at least tame the barbarous tribes that roamed the entire interior from the Black Sea to the Adriatic. The Bessi, particularly those in the great Balkan, were notorious as robbers, even among a race of robbers. The expedition also aimed to destroy corsairs in their haunts, especially along the Dalmatian coast. In Dalmatia, the former praetor Gaius Cosconius held command. He marched through the country in all directions and captured the fortress of Salona after a two-year siege. As during the Norman era, pirate fleets attacked coastal towns, forcing them to pay hefty bribes or risking a swift takeover.

When Samothrace, Clazomenae, and Samos were pillaged by pirates in 670 BCE, under the eyes of Sulla, it is clear that no Roman forces were present to intervene. All the ancient temples along the coastlines of Greece and Asia Minor were pillaged one by one. A single temple, Samothrace, was said to have yielded an enormous treasure worth 240,000 pounds. The corsairs felt on a level with any legitimate state. Their pride, pomp, and humour were attested by many genuine pirate tales of mad merriment and chivalrous banditry. They professed to live at righteous war with all the world, claiming what they gained was not plunder but military spoil. Captured corsairs were sure to face the cross in every Roman seaport, and so too did they claim the right to execute any of their captives. It was by no means remarkable that the corsairs thrived there, for they had never been more successful elsewhere.

They possessed signal-places and stations along the coast. Further inland, in the most remote recesses of Lycia, Pamphylia, and Cilicia, they built rock-castles where they hid their wives, children, and treasures when they were at sea. In times of danger, these castles likely served as a refuge for them as well. The war, which lasted until 679, yielded significant results for Publius Servilius. The Romans gained control over a large number of pirates and pirate vessels as a result. Lycia, Pamphylia, and West Cilicia were severely affected, with the territories of destroyed towns being confiscated. As a consequence, the province of Cilicia was expanded by incorporating these regions into its territory. After the death of Sulla, the Armenians even advanced into Cappadocia proper, and carried off to Armenia the inhabitants of the capital city, Mazaca (later known as Caesarea), along with eleven other towns of Greek organization.

Mithradates, resolved to maintain the strictest defensive posture, retired without engaging in battle from Sinope to Amisus, and then from Amisus to Cabira (afterwards Neocaesarea, now Niksar) on the Lycus, a tributary of the Iris. Under ordinary circumstances, the general would have asked and obtained from his government the dispatch of a second army. The external wars, however, had produced a result still less gratifying. It is true that the Thraco-Macedonian war had yielded a result not directly unfavourable, although far from corresponding to the considerable expenditure of men and money. The government was unjustly blamed for all its curable and incurable problems; nonetheless, it was undeniable that the government played a significant role in the general culpability.

The nation had good reason to lay the blame for its failure primarily on the government of the restoration. A similar misrule had indeed always accompanied the re-establishment of the oligarchy, following the fall of the Gracchi as well as that of Marius and Saturninus. However, it had never shown such violence and at the same time such laxity before, nor had it emerged so corrupt and pernicious. The government, under pressure from the agitation of Lepidus immediately after Sulla's death, had already consented to a limited revival of grain largesse. It also took steps to satisfy the capital's proletariat regarding this vital issue.

When piracy chiefly caused the high price of grain to lead to an oppressive dearth in Rome as violent tumults erupted in the streets in 679, extraordinary purchases of Sicilian grain were made on account of the government's relief for a time. A corn-law brought in by the consuls in 681 regulated future purchases of Sicilian grain and provided the government with better means to prevent similar evils at the expense of the provincials. The dispute over the tribunician magistracy was opened as early as 678, immediately after the defeat of Lepidus, by the tribune of the people Lucius Sicinius. It is believed that he may have been a descendant of the man with the same name who had first filled this office more than four hundred years earlier. However, it ultimately failed before the resistance offered to it by the active consul Gaius Curio. Demosthenes' words about his Athenians were aptly applied to the Romans of this era. The people were fervent about taking action as long as they stood around the platform and listened to proposals for reforms; however, once they returned home, no one gave further thought to what he had presented in the market-place.

The party of conservatives, friendly to reform, which lost its most notable head by the early death of Cotta occurring soon after about 681, dwindled away more and more – crushed between the extremes that were becoming daily more marked. The breach already apparent had been widened irreparably during the Spanish campaigns of a general from 677 to 683.

Pompeius, although he had held several offices in an extraordinary manner with great official authority, had not yet served as a magistrate, nor even held the quaestorship, and was still not a member of the senate. Only one who had passed through the usual sequence of lesser magistracies could become consul, only one who had been invested with supreme power could triumph. Although the senate's feebleness even in animosity made it conceivable that they would yield, and concede to the victorious general, in return for his service against the democratic chiefs, the triumph, consulate, and land assignments were not enough. The most favourable lot the oligarchy could hold was an honourable annihilation among the long series of peaceful senatorial Imperators, which the general of thirty-six had been able to secure. The democrats and Pompeius, however, were not the only parties to the league.

Although he shared similarities with Sulla, his politics were more akin to Pompey's, being predominantly personal rather than those of the ruling elite. Like Pompey, he was currently in Italy, leading a large and victorious army that had recently quelled the slave uprising. The senate granted the necessary dispensations for the consulship and triumph. It may readily be conceived that these men put in motion all the means which their important and grave office placed at their command, for the purpose of paying homage to the new holders of power and annoying the Senate. The coalition, held together solely by a shared goal of setting aside restoration work, dissolved in practice if not formally, once that objective was achieved. The question of where the majority of power initially shifted remained unresolved, as it too approached a swift and violent resolution. The former had indeed promised to disband his soldiers after his triumph. This approach was also adopted by the army of Crassus. The man had already demonstrated his contradictory nature: on one hand, he was a loyal Republican; on the other, he was the master of Rome. Pompeius refused to dismiss the army because he distrusted Crassus. Pompeius, after discharging his soldiers, resigned his consulship on December 31, 684. He then retired from public life and declared his intention to live a life of quiet leisure as a private citizen. Sulla merely strengthened the senatorial government, not created it. After the bulwarks erected by Sulla had fallen, the government remained primarily with the senate. Although the constitution that governed it—the restored Gracchan constitution—was largely influenced by a spirit hostile to the oligarchy.

Between the oligarchy and democracy, a new factor emerged: the capitalist party. In the recent crisis, this party had formed an alliance with the latter, but the oligarchs now sought to win its support, hoping to gain a counterbalance to the democracy. The putting an end to the government of the senate could not but be desirable. However, if it happened in this manner, it would be far less a victory for their party than a personal victory for their powerful ally. The overthrow of the senatorial rule led to a new power for Pompeius. On this occasion, however, Pompeius himself took action or at least permitted others to act on his behalf.

In 687, two projects of law were introduced. One of these decreed the discharge, long since demanded by the democracy, of the soldiers in the Asiatic army who had served their term. It also recalled Lucius Lucullus, commander-in-chief of the army, and appointed one of the consuls for the current year, either Gaius Piso or Manius Glabrio, to replace him. The second project revived and extended a plan proposed by the senate seven years earlier to clear the seas of pirates. The Senate had to choose this man from among the consulars as a formality; for selection was left to them simply because there was no real alternative, and in the presence of the excited multitude, the Senate could entrust the command of the seas and coasts to none other than Pompeius alone. While the office of general was formerly restricted to a term of one year, tied to a specific province, and subject to strictly measured military and financial resources, the new extraordinary office from its inception enjoyed a duration of three years, which did not preclude further extension. It had control over the majority of provinces, including Italy, which was previously exempt from military jurisdiction. The state's soldiers, ships, and treasures were placed at its disposal almost without restriction.

The fact that Pompeius acted more resolutely on this occasion than during his consulate is quite capable of explanation. It was only natural that he would indicate a position independent of the Senate as the first condition for taking command, and that the burgesses would readily agree to it. When Tribune Gabinius appeared in the senate-house after introducing his proposals, the city's fathers were nearly about to strangle him with their own hands, driven by their fervor, but not realizing that this impulsive approach would ultimately prove highly disadvantageous for them.

The exasperation of the multitude remained undiminished, finding fresh sustenance in the high prices of grain and the numerous rumors that circulated, many of which were absurd. Some claimed Lucullus had invested the money meant for war efforts at interest in Rome, or even attempted to use it to persuade Quinctius to withdraw from supporting the people's cause. Others speculated that the senate was preparing for a "second Romulus" – as they called Pompeius – and that his fate would mirror that of the first. The price of grain fell immediately after the passage of the Gabinian laws to ordinary rates, an indication of the hopes attached to the grand expedition and its glorious leader. The clearing of the seas was completed in three months.

The confusion on the Asiatic continent had grown even more severe. Glabrio, who was destined to take up the chief command against Mithradates and Tigranes in place of Lucullus, remained stationary in western Asia Minor. He instigated the soldiers with various proclamations against Lucullus, but did not assume the supreme command. As a result, Lucullus was forced to retain it. This occurrence clearly showed how disorganized the machinery of the Roman constitution was. The power of legislation was placed in the hands of any demagogue, regardless of their significance. Similarly, the final determination rested with the incapable multitude. At the same time, this extension was applied to the most important questions of administration.

The moderate Optimates supported the Manilian proposal because, after the Gabinian law resistance, it was futile in any case. Nearly 400 ships and boats, including 90 war vessels properly so called, were either taken by Pompeius or surrendered to him. In all about 1,300 piratical vessels are said to have been destroyed. Additionally, the richly-filled arsenals and magazines of the buccaneers were burnt. About 10,000 pirates died, while over 20,000 were captured alive.
