In the summer of 687, three months after the start of the campaign, commerce returned to normal and instead of the former famine, abundance prevailed in Italy. Dissensions between Pompeius and Metellus over Crete created an unpleasant interval in the island of Crete, but it did not significantly disrupt the pleasing success of the Roman arms. The Cretan communities, however, who had witnessed their subdued countrymen being taken to task by Metellus with great severity, learned from this experience that Pompeius imposed gentle terms on townships that surrendered to him in southern Asia Minor. They preferred to yield to Pompeius' demands rather than risk further conflict.

Octavius protested in vain, as he had arrived without troops. He summoned Lucius Sisenna, the lieutenant of Pompey stationed in Achaia, but Metellus paid no attention to either Octavius or Sisenna. Instead, he besieged Eleutherna and captured Lappa by storm. During the capture, Octavius was taken prisoner and humiliated, while the Cretans who were taken with him were handed over to the executioner. Formal conflicts arose between Sisenna's troops, led by Octavius after that leader's death, and those of Metellus. Even when ordered to return to Achaia, Octavius continued the war in concert with Cretan Aristion and Hierapytna, where both made a stand, until it was finally subdued by Metellus after intense resistance. The Roman state was plagued by indescribable disorganization. The incidents that followed led to only a bitter exchange of letters between the two generals, who would eventually sit together again, this time in harmony, in the senate a couple of years later.

Pompeius took supreme command against Mithradates. During these events, Pompeius remained in Cilicia, preparing for the next year, as it seemed, a campaign against the Cretans or rather against Metellus. In reality, he was waiting for the signal that would call him to intervene in the utterly confused affairs of Asia Minor's mainland. The portion of the Lucullan army that remained after suffering losses and the departure of the Fimbrian legions remained inactive on the upper Halys, in the country of the Trocmi, bordering on the Pontic territory. The kings of the east did not launch a serious offensive against the Romans. This was either because it was not part of their plan or because the arrival of Pompey in Cilicia led Mithridates and Tigranes to abandon further advances.

They set out to protect the coasts with guards of cavalry and small squadrons. Though expeditions against Cyprus in 696 and Egypt in 699 show that piracy was not thoroughly mastered, it still did not raise its head again after the expedition of Pompeius amidst all the vicissitudes and political crises of Rome. It could never dislodge the Romans from the sea as it had done under the government of a mouldering oligarchy. A son of the great king Tigranes bore the same name and rebelled against his father. He did so either because he was unwilling to wait for the death of the old man or because his father's suspicion had already cost several of his brothers their lives, leading him to discern his only chance of safety in open insurrection. It wasn't until the troops from Cilicia arrived and made it possible to resume the offensive with superior force that Pompeius again advanced. He then invested the king's camp with a chain of posts stretching almost 18 miles in length, effectively blockading him formally. Meanwhile, Roman detachments scoured the countryside far and wide.

The distress in the Pontic camp was great; even the draught animals had to be killed. After forty-five days, the king ordered that his sick and wounded soldiers, whom he could not save and was unwilling to leave in enemy hands, be put to death by his own troops. He then departed during the night with utmost secrecy towards the east. When the Roman general realized Mithradates intended not to settle the conflict within his own territory, but to lure the enemy deeper into the eastern provinces, he decided not to allow it. During the rest at noon, the Roman army set out without the enemy observing their movement. They made a circuit and occupied the heights that lay in front of them, which commanded a defile to be passed by the enemy on the southern bank of the River Lycus (Jeschil-Irmak), not far from the modern city of Enderes, where Nicopolis was later built.

The next morning, the Pontic troops dispersed in their customary fashion. Assuming the enemy remained in their usual position behind them, they continued their march and set up camp in the same valley that the Romans had previously occupied, surrounded by its enclosing hills. During the conflicts between Mithradates and Pompeius, the king of the Parthians, yielding to the urgency of the Romans and above all that of the exiled Armenian prince, invaded the kingdom of Tigranes by force of arms, compelling him to withdraw into the inaccessible mountains. The invading army began the siege of the capital Artaxata. However, when the siege became prolonged, King Phraates departed with the majority of his troops. As a result, Tigranes overpowered the remaining Parthian corps and the Armenian emigrants led by his son, re-establishing his dominion throughout the kingdom. Notably, the king was reluctant to engage in battle against the newly victorious Romans, especially considering his distrust of Mithradates, whose treachery had been confirmed through intelligence reports.

The son of the great king was met by him. He hoped to receive the Armenian diadem from the Romans after his father's fall. To achieve this, he had worked tirelessly to prevent the treaty between his father and the Romans from being concluded. After having given up at the bidding of the lictors, as was required by Roman camp regulations, he surrendered. He threw himself at the feet of the proconsul in a barbarian manner. In token of unconditional surrender, he placed the diadem and tiara in his hands. The mode of fighting was largely the same for both nations. They primarily employed arrows and light javelins, which they often fired from concealed positions in the woods behind tree trunks or hurled down from tree tops onto their enemies. The Albanians also had a significant number of horsemen, partly armored in a style similar to the Medo-Armenian manner, wearing heavy cuirasses and greaves.

Celer, on whom the main attack was directed, made a brave stand. After delivering himself from the division sent to attack him, Pompeius pursued the defeated barbarians as far as the Kur. The Iberian archers, however, stood firm against the Roman legions' advance. When Artoces saw that the Pelorus was also occupied by the Romans, he eventually surrendered to the victor's terms and sent his children as hostages. For several days, the Roman army marched through the hot, waterless flat country without encountering the enemy. It was only on the left bank of the Abas river that the Albanians, led by Coses, brother of King Oroizes, formed a force against the Romans. The Albanians, including those who had arrived from the Transcaucasian steppes, numbered around 60,000 infantry and 12,000 cavalry.

The army of the barbarians was driven into the woods. Pompeius ordered them to invest and set the woods on fire. He remained a threat to the Romans as long as he lived, driven by hope and determination, even in his old age. Like an aged refugee who had once led a large army to conquer Hellas and Macedonia from the Romans. His last preparations against Rome were focused on a different strategy.

Revolt against Mithradates. In fact, while Pompeius, without troubling himself further with the threats of the impotent giant, was occupied with organizing the territory he had gained, the destinies of the aged king drew on to their fulfillment without Roman aid in the remote north. As in earlier years, he had compelled his distinguished general Archelaus to seek protection in the Roman camp. During Lucullus' campaigns, his most trusted officers, Diodes and Phoenix, defected to the enemy, even as notable Roman emigrants like them did so. Now, with his star fading and the old, infirm sultan accessible only to his eunuchs, desertion followed more rapidly than ever before. While the insurrection spread among the Bosporan towns, including Chersonesus, which was not far from Sebastopol, Theodosia, and other cities, joined the Phanagorites. The king permitted his suspicion and cruelty to run unchecked.

The death of King Mithradates occurred after the country and army had deserted him. The capital city of Panticapaeum eventually surrendered to the insurgents, who took the old king captive within his palace. Seeing therefore that he had now to die, the sultan resolved at least to die as he had lived. His wives, concubines, and daughters, including the youthful brides of the kings of Egypt and Cyprus, would all suffer the bitterness of death before he took it. When the draught did not take effect quickly enough, he presented his neck for the fatal stroke to a Celtic mercenary named Betuitus. Mithradates Eupator died in 691. He was sixty-eight years old when he died and had reigned for fifty-seven years, having fought against the Romans for the first time twenty-six years before his death. The multitude felt it instinctively: just as Scipio had triumphed over Hannibal more thoroughly than over Carthage, so too was the conquest of the eastern tribes and the great king himself all but forgotten with Mithradates' death. At Pompeius' solemn entry, nothing captivated the crowd's attention like the images, which depicted King Mithradates as a fugitive leading his horse by the reins, only to sink down in death between the lifeless bodies of his daughters.

Pompeius, after his warfare with the peoples of the Caucasus in 689, returned to the kingdom of Pontus. There, he reduced the last castles still offering resistance. He razed these castles to check the evils of brigandage and rendered their wells unusable by rolling rocks into them. The most notable emirs were Abgarus, chief of the Arab tribe of the Mardani, who had settled about Edessa and Carrhae in upper Mesopotamia. To the west of the Euphrates lay Sampsiceramus, emir of the Arabs of Hemesa (Homs), between Damascus and Antioch, master of the strong fortress Arethusa. Azizus led another horde roaming in the same region. Alchaudonius, prince of the Rhambaeans, had already established communication with Lucullus. The leaders of the Pharisees, who, after the manner of priests, felt that their faction's victory was not too dearly won at the cost of their country's independence and integrity, sought aid from King Aretas of the Nabataeans against Aristobulus. In return, they promised to restore all the territories conquered by Jannaeus from Aristobulus. Aretas advanced with an army of 50,000 men into Judea. He was reinforced by followers of the Pharisees and kept King Aristobulus besieged in his capital.

There was no want of a legitimate king in Syria; there were even two or three of them. A prince Antiochus from the House of Seleucus had been appointed by Lucullus as ruler of the northernmost province in Syria, Commagene. Antiochus Asiaticus, whose claim to the Syrian throne was recognized by both the senate and Lucullus, was received in Antioch after the Armenians' retreat and acknowledged as king. Antiochus soon lost his life at the hands of Emir Sampsiceramus, who had appointed him as a puppet ruler in Antioch. Thereafter, there is no further mention of these mock kings or their claims to power.

Establishing a new Roman government in Syria required advancing with a military force to introduce tolerable order amidst the chaos. To achieve this, it was necessary to terrify or subdue all those who had disrupted the peace, which had arisen during years of anarchy, using the Roman legions. Pompeius had already turned his attention to the affairs of Syria during the campaigns in the kingdom of Pontus and the Caucasus. He then directed detached commissioners and corps to intervene where necessary. Soon after, another adjutant of Pompey, Marcus Scaurus, arrived in Judea, to quell the ongoing feuds that had been brewing anew.

Lucius Afranius also played a significant role during Pompeius' expedition to the Caucasus. He led the Roman troops in Armenia and later marched from Corduene (in northern Kurdistan) to upper Mesopotamia. After successfully navigating the perilous desert terrain with the assistance of the Hellenes settled in Carrhae, he brought the Arabs in Osrhoene under control. The mediators sent by Pompeius, Gabinius, and Scaurus, who were allegedly bribed with substantial sums, sided with Aristobulus in the dispute between brothers Hyrcanus and Aristobulus. They also persuaded King Aretas to lift the siege of Jerusalem and return home, but he suffered a defeat at the hands of Aristobulus instead. The capital was captured; the steep temple-rock was defended by a fanatical band for three months with an obstinacy ready to brave death. At last, the besiegers gained entry while the besieged rested on the Sabbath. The defenders possessed themselves of the sanctuary and handed over their authors - those who had not fallen under the sword of the Romans - to the axes of the lictors.

The most suspicious circumstance, however, was that the Romans seemed not at all inclined to respect the boundary of the Euphrates fixed by treaty. Shortly after, his daughter accompanied her son and husband to the triumph of the Roman general. He then expanded his already considerable territory by acquiring the country of the Celtic Trocmi, from which he had dispossessed their tetrarch.

Elevation of Urban Life in Asia

The Romans recognized that with the task of representing Hellenism and protecting Alexander's domain in the east, they had a primary duty to elevate the urban system. Cities are the pillars of civilization everywhere, but the contrast between Oriental and Occidental societies was most sharply embodied in the difference between the Oriental, military-despotic, feudal hierarchy and the Helleno-Italic urban commonwealth, which promoted trade and commerce. Lucullus and Pompeius, although differing in other respects, shared a common goal in reducing complexities in the east. Despite their differences, Pompeius was inclined to scrutinize and modify his predecessor's arrangements in detail. Nevertheless, both men were united in their commitment to promoting urban life as far as possible in Asia Minor and Syria. After the subjugation of the pirates he had, instead of following the example of his predecessors, he chose not to crucify his prisoners. Instead, he settled them partly in the desolated cities of Plain Cilicia, such as Mallus, Adana, and Epiphaneia. He also settled others in Soli, which from then on was known as Pompeius' city (Pompeiupolis). Some were placed at Dyme in Achaia, while others went to Tarentum.

We have already observed how poorly provided with towns the Pontic empire was. Most districts of Cappadocia even a century after this had no towns, but merely mountain fortresses as a refuge for the agricultural population during war. The whole east of Asia Minor, apart from the sparse Greek colonies on its coasts, must have been in a similar plight at this time. Pompeius established 39 new towns in these provinces, with some attaining great prosperity. Most of the towns established were formed not by bringing colonists from a distance, but by the suppression of villages and the collection of their inhabitants within the new ring-wall. In Nicopolis, Pompeius settled the invalids and veterans of his army, who preferred to establish a home for themselves there immediately, rather than later in Italy.

In Paphlagonia, a city named Pompeiupolis was located at the site where Mithradates' army defeated the Bithynians in 66 BCE. In Cappadocia, a region that had suffered greatly due to the war, the royal residence of Mazaca was rebuilt by Pompeius. The city was later renamed Caesarea and eventually became Kaisarieh. Seven other townships were also re-established in the area, receiving urban institutions. The inhabitants of the capital ridiculed the mighty conqueror of the globe, who was nicknamed by the great powers he had conquered. They now saluted him as "conqueror of Salem," then as "emir" (-Arabarches-), and finally as the Roman Sampsiceramus. As General Lucullus displayed no common talents and a self-confidence bordering on rashness, while Pompey displayed military judgment and a rare self-restraint; for hardly has any general with such forces and a position so wholly free ever acted as cautiously as Pompey in the east.

Although for centuries afterwards many an Asiatic village lying in ruins recalled the times of the great war, Sinope might well begin a new era with the date of its re-establishment by Lucullus. Almost all the more considerable inland towns of the Pontic kingdom might gratefully honour Pompeius as their founder. The organization of Roman Asia by Lucullus and Pompeius may have its undeniable defects, but overall it can be described as judicious and praiseworthy. Despite the serious evils that still adhered to it, it was welcome to the sorely tormented Asiatics because it brought them both inward and outward peace, a state they had long felt deeply and painfully. The East after Pompeius' departure saw a substantial increase in activity eastward, until the idea, merely suggested by Pompeius with his characteristic timidity, of joining the regions east of the Euphrates to the Roman Empire was revived energetically but ultimately unsuccessful. The new triumvirate of Roman regents soon took up this idea, but it proved futile. Shortly thereafter, the civil war drew all eastern provinces and the rest into its deadly grasp.

Alexander, son of the deposed King Aristobulus, and Aristobulus himself, who after some time escaped from captivity, led three different revolts during the governorship of Aulus Gabinius (697-700), to each of which the government of High Priest Hyrcanus, installed by Rome, succumbed impotently. The able governor successfully rescued the few remaining Romans who had fled to Mount Gerizim, finding temporary refuge there. The insurgents, however, kept them under siege, but the governor ultimately overpowered the revolt after several intense battles. The vast treasure of nearly 7,000 talents (1.7 million pounds), which the equally covetous and miserly king could not bring himself to use for bribes necessary to save his crown, fell along with the latter to the Romans, and filled their treasury after a desirable fashion.

Ptolemaeus was recognized but expelled by his subjects. In contrast, his brother, who ruled Egypt, successfully purchased recognition from the people through a decree issued by the new Roman masters in 695. The purchase price was reportedly 6,000 talents (1,460,000 pounds). It was only when the triumvirate was confirmed anew at the conference of Luca that this affair was also arranged. After Ptolemaeus had agreed to a further payment of 10,000 talents, the governor of Syria, Aulus Gabinius, now obtained orders from those in power to take immediate action for bringing back the king. The citizens of Alexandria had placed the crown on the head of Berenice, the eldest daughter of the ejected king, and given her a husband in the person of Archelaus, one of the spiritual princes of Roman Asia. He was the high-priest of Comana, who possessed ambition enough to hazard his secure position in hopes of mounting the throne of the Lagids.

The capital had fallen, and with it, all resistance came to an end. The payment of the reward agreed upon with the regents broke down due to the absolute impossibility of exacting enormous sums from an exhausted land. Despite taking the last penny from poor people, care was taken to keep the country quiet by maintaining a garrison of Roman infantry and Celtic, German cavalry in the capital, which took the place of native praetorians and emulated them with some success. As a conservative of note, he possessed integrity and courage, and was always ready to take action. This led him to become the recognized champion of the Optimates, despite not having the same level of talent or intellect as others.

The Don Quixote of the aristocracy proved himself through his character and actions that, even during this period when an aristocracy still existed, the aristocratic policy had become nothing more than a myth. The praetors were now expressly required to administer justice in accordance with the rules they had established, following the traditional Roman method, upon taking office. On the other hand, the same leaders were by no means disposed to advocate the political equality of the freedmen. The tribune of the people, Gaius Manilius, who in a thinly attended assembly, had secured the renewal (December 31). The law regarding the suffrage of freedmen, as stated in the Sulpician law, was immediately rejected by the leading men of the democracy. With their consent, the senate cancelled the law on the following day.

But three years later, when the emblems of victory, erected by Marius in the Capitol and ordered destroyed by Sulla, glittered anew in gold and marble at their original site, a crowd of veterans from the African and Cimbrian wars gathered around the statue of their beloved general, overcome with emotion. The jubilant masses watched as the senate hesitated to seize the trophies that had been restored by the same bold hand, defying the laws. The worthlessness of democratic successes is a matter of debate. However, despite the numerous controversies and disagreements that arose during this period, it is essential to recognize their relatively minor political significance. In the struggle against the aristocracy, the democrats remained victors; but they had not conquered alone. The fiery trial still awaited them—the reckoning not with their former foe, but with their powerful ally, to whom in the struggle against the aristocracy they were substantially indebted for victory. They had now entrusted an unprecedented military and political power to his hands because they dared not refuse it to him.

The parties in Rome sat and waited. The democrats, on the contrary, waited with painful anxiety and sought, during the interval still allowed them by the absence of Pompeius, to lay a countermeasure against the impending disaster. While in public, Democrats described the absent general as the head and pride of their party. They seemed to direct all their arrows against the aristocracy. However, secretly, preparations were made against Pompeius. These attempts by the democracy to escape a looming military dictatorship have historically greater significance than the noisy agitation, largely used as a mask, against the nobility. The design of the democrats was to possess themselves of the reins of government after the example of Marius and Cinna. They intended to entrust one of their leaders with either the conquest of Egypt or the governorship of Spain, or some similar ordinary or extraordinary office. This would allow them to find a counterbalance in him and his military force to Pompeius and his army.

Catiline, Piso, and their followers readily agreed to any plan that offered the possibility of proscriptions and cancellation of debt books. Catiline had a particular animosity towards the aristocracy, as they had opposed his candidacy for consulship. The number of individuals who joined it was said to have exceeded 400. It included associates from all districts and urban communities in Italy. Additionally, numerous recruits would inevitably join the insurrection, drawn from the ranks of dissolute youth, which featured a seasonal program aimed at eliminating debt. January 1 The senate house was to be attacked by armed men. The new consuls and those otherwise designated for death would be executed. Sulla and Paetus were to be proclaimed consuls after their judicial sentence was cancelled, excluding them from office.

Crassus was then to be invested with the dictatorship, and Caesar with the mastership of the horse. It is likely that this arrangement was intended to create an imposing military force, as Pompeius was stationed far away in the Caucasus. Catiline gave the signal too early, for the bandits who were about to arrive had not yet gathered. Crassus and Caesar likely planned to seize control of the military dictatorship during Pompeius' absence. Egypt was chosen as the foundation for this democratic military power. The 689 insurrection was actually a means to achieve these goals, with Catilina and Piso serving as Crassus and Caesar's instruments.

Democratic strategists clearly showed that the Mithridatic war could only be regarded as terminated by the capture of the king. To achieve this, it was necessary to undertake a pursuit around the Black Sea, with keeping Syria at bay being the top priority. Pompeius, however, remained unconcerned about such talk and set out in the spring of 69 from Armenia, marching towards Syria. The aristocracy was in a state of great confusion, mainly because they were unable to even field any counter-candidates. The nobility contented themselves with making a feeble attempt to check electioneering intrigues by issuing a new law respecting the purchase of votes. However, this effort was thwarted by the veto of a tribune of the people. As a result, they turned over their votes to a candidate who, although not acceptable to them, was at least inoffensive.

He had connections in both the capital and rural towns that gave him a chance alongside the candidates proposed by the democracy. The nobility, though reluctant, and the Pompeians ultimately voted for him, securing his election by a significant margin. It is reported that Piso was killed in Spain, allegedly at the behest of his rival Pompeius. With Consul Antonius alone nothing could be done; Cicero broke the loose bond that attached him to the conspiracy, even before they entered on their offices. He renounced his legal privilege of having the consular provinces determined by lot and handed over the lucrative governorship of Macedonia to his deeply embarrassed colleague. The essential preliminary conditions of this project had therefore miscarried.

The development of Oriental affairs grew daily more perilous for democracy. The ground for this, however, was not to be gained by dispossession; on the contrary, all existing private rights were guaranteed, and even the illegal occupations of the most recent times were converted into full property. It required little insight to recognize that in this decennial college, its purpose was to establish a power modeled after that of Pompey, but with less emphasis on military might and more on democratic principles.

Outbreak of the Insurrection in Etruria led to repressive measures by the government. However, before this, the civil war had already begun. This rising, however, remained isolated. At other places of rendezvous, the conspiracy went no further than the collection of arms and the institution of secret conferences, where resolute leaders were present. This was fortunate for the government, as the impending civil war had been openly announced for some time. However, its own irresolution and the inefficiency of the administrative machinery had prevented it from making any military preparations.

It was only now that the general levy was called out, and superior officers were ordered to each region of Italy, where they might suppress the insurrection in their respective districts. Meanwhile, gladiatorial slaves were removed from the capital, and patrols were dispatched due to concerns about incendiarism. The more considerable of the conspirators—Publius Lentulus Sura, consul in 683, who was later expelled from the senate and now sought re-entry, along with his two former colleagues, Publius Autronius and Lucius Cassius—were incapable men. Lentulus was an ordinary aristocrat, known for grandiose language but lacking in conception and indecisive in action. Autronius stood out only for his powerful voice. As for Cassius, it was unclear how a man of considerable size and limited intellect had managed to join the conspiracy. Catiline nevertheless resolved to remain in Rome for a time despite the urgency of his army's desire for him and the danger he faced by staying at the seat of government after the revolt.

The projects of Catiline failed. The main reason was that the government's agents had infiltrated the conspiracy group and provided them with accurate information about the plot. As little as steps were taken against the leaders of the conspiracy who had remained behind in the capital, despite everyone pointing the finger at them. The insurrection in the capital was far from being abandoned by the conspirators; on the contrary, the plan of it had been settled by Catilina himself before his departure from Rome. A tribune was to give the signal by calling an assembly of the people. The following night, Cethegus was to dispatch the consul Cicero. Gabinius and Statilius were to set the city on fire at twelve places simultaneously. A communication was to be established as soon as possible with the army of Catilina, which should have advanced in the meantime.

If  Cicero ordered the arrest of the most dangerous leaders of the plot with utmost secrecy on the following morning. He targeted Lentulus, Cethegus, Gabinius, and Statilius for execution. However, not all conspirators were caught; some managed to escape by fleeing. Immediately after the arrest, letters seized from the prisoners were laid before the senate. The seals and handwriting acknowledged by the captives could not be denied. Further confirmatory facts emerged, including deposits of arms in the houses of the conspirators and threatening expressions they had employed. These facts fully established the actual subsistence of the conspiracy, and important documents were immediately published as news-sheets on Cicero's suggestion.

How much of these rumours was true? We cannot tell. However, there was cause for apprehension because, according to the constitution, neither troops nor even a respectable police force were present at the government's command in the capital. In reality, it was left vulnerable to every gang of bandits. Although the consuls and the great majority of the Senate had already declared in favor of execution, most of them, led by Cicero, now appeared once again inclined to remain within the bounds of the law. Suppression of the Etruscan Insurrection

After the conspiracy had been thus stifled in the capital, even before it came to an outbreak, there remained the task of putting an end to the insurrection in Etruria.

The army, numbering around 2,000 men, had grown to approximately 10,000 by the time Catilina arrived. This was due to the large number of recruits who joined, and as a result, two full legions were formed. However, only about one-fourth of the men in these legions were adequately armed. Petreius led his guard in a decisive charge, breaking through the enemy's center. He then overthrew the center and launched a counterattack on both wings simultaneously. Immediately after Lentulus' arrest and that of his comrades, a messenger sent by the conspirators in the capital to Catiline was intercepted by government agents. After being assured of immunity, he was persuaded to make a detailed confession at a full senate meeting.

The public was not so nice. Young men who had taken up arms to ward off incendiaries were exasperated against Caesar more than anyone else. On December 5, when he left the senate, they pointed their swords at his breast. Even now, he narrowly escaped with his life on the same spot where a fatal blow fell seventeen years later. He did not return to the senate for a considerable time afterwards. While the course of events themselves testifies that the threads of the Catilinarian plot reached far higher than Lentulus and Catilina, it is also worth noting that at a much later period, when Caesar had risen to power, he formed a close alliance with Publius Sittius, the sole surviving Catilinarian leader of the Mauretanian free bands. Pompeius occupied a position similar to Sulla's in the east. Crassus and Caesar aimed to establish a power in Italy comparable to that held by Marius and Cinna, with the goal of utilizing it more effectively than they had in the past.

The more reputable leaders of democracy kept themselves in the background, leaving the execution of dirty work to their less reputable associates. They hoped to reap the benefits of these actions later. The coalition of two distinguished men from the opposing party was a scandal for democrats who believed in pure democracy. The multitude of the capital, though indifferent to street-riots, grew uneasy when their homes were threatened with destruction.

Gnaeus Pompeius had been partly warned, partly exasperated by all these cabals. After all that had occurred, and after the democracy had virtually torn asunder the ties connecting it with Pompeius, it could no longer make a request with propriety that he should not destroy with the sword the democratic power he had raised, which had also raised him. As he was being showered with praise and tributes, a new scheme was constantly brewing against him; and just as quickly, each one would pop on its own. The attempt to kindle civil war had miserably failed. If the anarchist section had shown any energy, the pure democracy would have been better equipped to lead them, save them, or even share in their fate.

King Mithridates had died. Asia Minor and Syria were under regulation. The return of Pompey to Italy could happen at any moment. CHAPTER VI: The Retirement of Pompeius and the Coalition of the Pretenders

Pompeius, after completing his duties, returned home. This was not his first return; he had previously left to deal with matters in the east. Upon his second departure, he found the diadem at his feet once again. Twenty years previously, Sulla, after concluding a temporary peace with Mithradates, had successfully carried out a restoration that ran counter to the natural development of things. He did this with his five legions, despite the opposition from the whole liberal party, which had been arming itself in mass for years, encompassing even moderate aristocrats and the liberal mercantile class down to anarchists.

He had returned, having fulfilled all of his duties both at sea and on land. While personal and political reasons co-operated, Pompeius and the leaders of the democracy remained in their previous connection despite all that had taken place. Nothing was done on the opposite side to fill up the chasm that separated him since his desertion to the camp of the democracy from his Sullan partisans. Nepos naturally found himself under no inducement to spare the aristocracy. Instead, he attached himself more readily to the democrats. When these, pliant as ever, submitted to what was inevitable and chose freely to concede the office of general in Italy as well as the consulate rather than have it forced upon them by arms.

When Pompeius was offered the chance to replace Catulus' name with his own on the city's proudest monument, a gesture that delighted him and posed no threat to democracy, he was presented with abundant but empty honour. Meanwhile, the aristocracy, unable to accept its best man falling, found itself in an uncomfortable collision with Pompeius. The burgesses of Rome considered Nepos' proposals regarding Pompeius. Encouraged by this victory of their bands over those of their antagonist, the Senate suspended both the tribune Nepos and the praetor Caesar, who had vigorously supported him in bringing in the law. Their proposed deposition, which had been put forward in the Senate, was prevented by Cato, more likely because it was unconstitutional than because it was unjust.

Nepos himself had immediately left the city and set sail for Asia to report to Pompeius on the outcome of his mission. After the illegal condemnation of Catiline's followers, following the unprecedented acts of violence against the plebeian tribune Metellus, Pompeius might immediately wage war as a defender of Roman public freedom's two fundamental pillars: the right to appeal and the inviolability of the people's tribunate. He would do so against the aristocracy, championing the party of order against Catiline's faction. But near as seemed the opportunity of placing the white chaplet around his brow, he hesitated. Much as his own soul longed for it, when the question of action presented itself, his heart and hand faltered again.

His prolonged stay in Asia during the winter of 691-692 had proximately the injurious consequence that the aristocracy, which accelerated the campaign against Catilina as it best could, had meanwhile got rid of his bands, and thus set aside the most feasible pretext for keeping together the Asiatic legions in Italy. He probably said to himself, moreover, that even if he dismissed his army, he did not let it entirely out of his hand. He could still raise a force ready for battle sooner than any other party-chief. The democracy was waiting in a submissive attitude for his signal, and he could deal with the refractory senate even without soldiers. Such further considerations as suggested themselves had enough truth to make them appear plausible to one who wished to deceive himself. While in the capital, preparations were underway for the arrival of the new monarch. News arrived that Pompeius, having just landed at Brundisium, had split his legions and was traveling to the capital with a small entourage.

The promise of a grant of land to the soldiers of the Asiatic army was indeed ratified by the senate in general. However, it was also extended to the Cretan legions of Metellus. Furthermore, what was worse was that it was not executed, as the public chest was empty and the senate was unwilling to intervene with the domains for this purpose. Democratic leaders, despite not openly opposing him, had no reason to make his interests their own, and thus remained aloof.

He required several years of uninterrupted work in Italy to establish a solid military position. The nobility would undoubtedly exhaust all possible means to hinder his plans during this period of preparation. Caesar was assured of the consulship for the following year and a governorship in due course. Pompeius was promised the ratification of his arrangements made in the east, and an assignment of lands for the soldiers of the Asiatic army. Caesar also promised to procure for the equites by means of the burgesses what the senate had refused. Crassus, who was inevitable, was allowed at least to join the league, although without obtaining definite promises for an accession he could not refuse. The democracy had become a mere political faction, with its allies being triumphant generals leading their armies. In contrast, the leader of the democracy was now an Imperator, having been crowned for his victories, and was filled with grandiose military plans, while his former allies were retired generals without any army to command. The democracy then conquered questions of principle, and in return conceded the highest offices of state to its two confederates. Now it had become more practical and grasped supreme civil and military power for itself, while concessions were made to its allies only in subordinate points – not even Pompeius's demand for a second consulship was attended to.

The character of democracy itself was the only thing that remained unchanged. Neither the simple civil power, as Gaius Gracchus possessed it, nor the arming of the democratic party, such as Cinna, was able to maintain a permanent superiority in the Roman commonwealth. The military machine, fighting not for a party but for a general, soon proved superior to all political parties. He himself came forward eleven years later as a condottiere.

If we still bow in reverence after so many centuries, it is not because he desired and gained a crown (to do which is as little of a great thing as the crown itself) but because his mighty ideal—of a free commonwealth under one ruler—never forsook him, and preserved him even when he was a monarch from sinking into vulgar royalty. It could not be rationally denied that, following the establishment of provinces in Pontus and Syria, state finances should dispense with monies from Campanian leases. Withholding one of Italy's finest districts, particularly suited for small holdings, from private enterprise was unwarrantable. Furthermore, it was unjust and ridiculous to deny municipal rights to the township of Capua after extending the franchise to all of Italy. Caesar could truthfully claim that the senate had dismissed most rational and essential proposals presented to them with respect, primarily due to their origin from a democratic consul.

The nobility, however, left no stone unturned in their efforts to thwart Caesar's proposals. The consul Bibulus shut himself up in his house for the remainder of the year. At the same time, he publicly announced his intention to observe the celestial signs on all days suitable for public gatherings throughout the year. His colleagues once again admired the great man who, as Ennius had said of the old Fabius, "saved the state by wise delay." They followed his example. Most of them, including Cato, no longer attended senate meetings but instead helped their consul to fret over the fact that history continued unabated despite political machinations.

Caesar, however, had publicly declared that he would introduce no proposal in his own favor. The tribune of the people, Publius Vatinius, therefore undertook to submit the proposal to the burgesses, who naturally gave their unconditional assent. The public initially believed that it was destined for Pompeius and Crassus themselves; however, those in power preferred to elect two subordinate but trustworthy men of their party—Aulus Gabinius, the best among Pompeius' adjutants, and Lucius Piso, who was less important but Caesar's father-in-law. Julia, inheriting her father's charm, lived in happy domestic relations with her husband, who was nearly twice her age. The burgesses, weary from so many troubles and crises, saw in this marriage the promise of a peaceful and prosperous future.

On this occasion, however, they had obtained sufficient evidence of the total disorganization of the aristocracy and the boundless alarm of the genteel lords. Even a man like Lucius Lucullus had thrown himself in person at Caesar's feet and publicly declared that he was compelled by reason of his great age to withdraw from public life. Cato and Cicero were ultimately removed. Therefore, they were content with a few isolated victims.

The democratic party, which played a leading role in the coalition, could not remain unpunished for its role in the judicial murder on December 5, 1913. It was not the advisers of the consul, but the consul himself, who bore responsibility for it. The gentler course was to hold the consul accountable alone and exclude the senatorial college from the matter altogether. This approach was taken in the grounds of the proposal directed against Cicero, which led to a senate decree ordering execution. Cato was thus removed by an honorable mission. Cicero was visited with the gentlest possible punishment, and he was not named in the proposal.

The irresistible force of circumstances had paved the way for the establishment of the future Roman dominion in both the west and east through the senate's reluctant efforts. The subsequent Roman emigration to the provinces, which was a public calamity but also brought about a higher culture in western regions, followed instinctively. It was Gaius Gracchus, the creator of the Roman democracy, who grasped this opportunity with statesmanlike clarity and began to implement his vision with decisive action. As long as Spain had obeyed the Romans, its western shore remained substantially independent of them, even after Decimus Brutus's expedition against the Callaeci. The Romans had not set foot on the northern coast; instead, they were limited to predatory raids from those quarters, which exposed the subject provinces to continual harm and did significant damage to Spain's civilization and Romanization efforts. He crossed the Sierra de Estrella mountain range, which bounded the Tagus to the north. After conquering its inhabitants and relocating some of them to the plain, he reduced the country on both sides of the Douro River and arrived at the northwest point of the Iberian Peninsula. There, with the aid of a fleet brought up from Gades, he occupied Brigantium (Corunna).

By this means, the peoples adjoining the Atlantic Ocean, the Lusitanians and Callaecians, were forced to acknowledge Roman supremacy. The conqueror was at the same time careful to render the position of the subjects generally more tolerable by reducing the tribute paid to Rome and regulating the financial affairs of the communities. Although the same talents and leading ideas that would later be evident on a greater stage were present in this military and administrative debut of the great general and statesman, his impact in the Iberian peninsula was fleeting and had little lasting effect. This was particularly true, as the region's unique physical and national characteristics made it difficult for any single action to have a profound influence there unless sustained over time. The region that played a crucial role in the Romanic development of Western Europe lay between the Pyrenees and the Rhine, stretching along the Mediterranean and Atlantic coasts. Since the Augustan age, this land has been commonly referred to as Gallia, although technically it was not exclusively inhabited by the Celts. The Celtic territory was more limited in some areas and wider in others, never forming a unified nation or political entity prior to the reign of Augustus.

While the Celtic and Ligurian populations in these regions were losing their nationality, they languished under political and economic oppression. The intolerable nature of this oppression is evident in their hopeless insurrections. Meanwhile, the decline of the native population coincided with the naturalization of a higher culture, similar to what we find in Italy at this time. There were also walled towns that surprised the Romans with their suitability and the elegant interweaving of timber and stone in their construction. In contrast, the towns of the Allobroges built only with wood. The Helvetii had twelve towns, while the Suessiones had an equal number. In contrast, in the more northern districts, such as among the Nervii, there were likely also towns, but during times of war, the population sought shelter in the morasses and forests rather than behind their walls. Beyond the Thames, a primitive wooden barricade took the place of traditional towns, serving as the only safe haven for men and herds during conflict.

Here, therefore, we meet for the first time with navigation in the open ocean. We find that here the sailing vessel took full place of the oared boat—an improvement it is true, which the declining activity of the old world did not know how to turn to account, and whose immeasurable results our own epoch of renewed culture is gradually reaping. The regular maritime intercourse between the British and Gallic coasts facilitated a close political connection between the inhabitants on both sides of the Channel, which in turn enabled the flourishing of transmarine commerce and fisheries. The manufacture of linen and woolen goods did not significantly surpass the ordinary level in most branches. It was only through the Romans' influence that linen and woolen production flourished in central and northern Gaul.

The art of poetry was highly valued by the Celts, and it was deeply intertwined with their religious and political institutions. We can see this in the flourishing religious poetry, as well as the poetry of the court and the mendicants. Natural science and philosophy also found attention among the Celts, despite being influenced by the local theology. Hellenic humanism was received readily whenever it approached them in any form. The communities could no longer maintain public peace, and the law of the strong arm prevailed. The dependent found protection only from his master, whom duty and interest compelled to redress the injury inflicted on his client. The state had lost its power to protect those who were free, and so they surrendered themselves in large numbers to a powerful man as clients.

Efforts towards national unity were underway while the individual cantons were in decline. At the same time, a powerful sense of unity was stirring in the nation, seeking various ways to take shape and hold. The entire Celtic nobility, in contrast to the individual canton-unions, disrupted the existing order and sparked the idea of a unified nation. The lack of political centralization hindered the development of the Celtic nations. Despite the growing sense of mutual understanding among the tribes, the nation remained unable to establish a stable political foundation like that of Italy, where the Roman burgesses and the Hellenes and Germans had achieved stability under the Macedonian and Frankish kings.

The Celtic priesthood and nobility, although both represented and combined the nation in a certain sense, were yet unable to unite it due to their respective class interests. On one hand, they lacked the unity required for such a task. On the other hand, they possessed sufficient power to prevent any king or canton from achieving this goal. A powerful canton induced a weaker canton to become subordinate, on such a footing that the leading canton acted for both the other and itself in its external relations and stipulated for it in state treaties. The dependent canton bound itself to provide military service and, in some cases, pay tribute. In this way, a series of separate leagues emerged. However, there was no single leading canton that united all of Gaul, nor any tie, however loose, that bound the nation together as a whole. The Belgic League, The Maritime Cantons, The Leagues of Central Gaul. It has been mentioned earlier that the Romans encountered a Britanno-Belgic league in the north, led by the Suessiones, and in central and southern Gaul, the Arverni confederation, with which the Haedui, although having fewer clients, engaged in rivalry. In Caesar's time, the Belgae inhabited north-eastern Gaul, situated between the Seine and Rhine rivers. They maintained an association that no longer extended to Britain. Alongside them, in modern-day Normandy and Brittany, emerged the Aremorican league or maritime cantons. In central Gaul, two parties vied for dominance: one led by the Haedui, while the other was headed by the Sequani. After the Arvernians, weakened by their wars with Rome, retreated from the scene.

The rivalries for hegemony created divisions within every league, which time failed to heal but exacerbated. For despite the triumph of one competitor, their opponent retained a foothold in political existence, and this vulnerability remained open to them, even after submitting to clientage, as they sought to renew the struggle. This practice is evident in the stories of Celtic customs, despite their anecdotal nature. The Celts would engage in tilting as a sport, and occasionally, it would escalate into fighting for one's life or death at a banquet. What's more striking is that they would sell themselves to be killed for a fixed price or a set quantity of wine, and willingly accept the fatal blow on their shield before the entire crowd. The Roman general passed a censure even more severe than this judgment on the Celtic infantry, for after having encountered them in his first campaign, he never again used them in conjunction with Roman infantry.

The most reliable indicator of cultural growth is the shared sense of community within a nation. In contrast, the Celtic wars fought on the land that is now Lombardy reveal little about this aspect, whereas the struggle against Julius Caesar highlights its significance. The Transalpine Celts' civilization in Caesar's time presents several aspects that are estimable and interesting. Although we have limited knowledge of it, their culture shares some similarities with modern societies rather than ancient Hellenic-Roman cultures. Notably, they employed sailing vessels, a system of knighthood, an ecclesiastical constitution, and made efforts to build the state on tribal and national foundations, rather than solely on city-based structures. Although we encounter the Celtic nation at the peak of its development, it becomes clear that their lower level of moral endowments or, equivalently, their lower cultural capacity, are more pronounced.

The Celts, who followed northern fashion, were fond of fiery drinks. Like the Scythians, they drank generous wine unmixed and to excess, which surprised and disgusted the temperate southerners. However, traders had no objections to dealing with such customers. Roman burgesses acquired landed property beyond the Roman frontier and turned it into a profitable venture, following Italian fashion. For instance, Roman estates in the Segusiavi region near Lyons are documented as early as around 673. It was a consequence of this. In free Gaul itself, it had been mentioned earlier. g Among the Arverni, the Roman language was not unknown even before the conquest. Although this knowledge was presumably still limited to a few, even the men of rank in the allied canton of the Haedui had to be communicated with through interpreters.

Celts and Germans were more violent than the pressure of the Romans from the Mediterranean. The Germanic tribes coming from the Baltic and North Sea brought a fresh stock from the great cradle of peoples in the east, which made room for itself alongside its elder brethren with youthful vigor, although also with youthful rudeness. It is significant in this respect that their western neighbours at this time hardly knew how to name any one of the peoples of the interior of Germany by its cantonal name. These were only known to them under the general appellations of the Suebi, meaning the roving people or nomads, and the Marcomani, meaning the land-guard. Among these German tribes were the Aduatuci, who from a fragment of the Cimbrian horde had grown into a considerable canton. The Tungri, comprising several other tribes, later came to be known in the Maas region around Liege. Even the Treveri, located near Treves, and the Nervii, inhabiting Hainault, were among the largest and most powerful peoples in this area, as recognized by respected authorities.

Although the movements that arose from there had not directly affected them up to the present time, they and their most important interests were still closely involved in the issue of those movements. While the Greeks faced threats to their nationality only from one side, the Celts were hard pressed by two national foes simultaneously. It was natural for them to seek protection from one source against the other. If one Celtic party allied itself with the Romans, their opponents would naturally form an alliance with the Germans. It was his intention that the numerous bands he had brought with him, and those that followed at his call from home, would eventually settle in Gaul. The estimated number of Germans who crossed the Rhine was around 120,000. This massive immigration of the German nation, which poured through the once-open gates like a stream, should form a solid foundation for him to build his dominion over Gaul.

Even the Sequani, as whose hired commander-in-chief he had crossed the Rhine, were obliged to cede to him a third of their territory - presumably upper Alsace, later inhabited by the Triboci - where Ariovistus permanently settled with his followers. Furthermore, a second third was subsequently demanded of them for the Harudes who arrived afterwards. From the sources of the Rhine to the Atlantic Ocean, the German tribes were in motion. The entire line of the Rhine was threatened by them. It was a moment like this when the Alamanni and Franks launched an attack on the falling empire of the Caesars. Even now, it seemed that they were about to be carried out against the Celts, just as they would be five hundred years later against the Romans. They preferred to pass over, above the point where the Rhone breaks through, to the left Allobrogian bank, with the aim of regaining the right bank further down the stream where the Rhone enters the plain, and then march on towards the level west of Gaul; there, they selected the fertile canton of the Santones (Saintonge, the valley of the Charente) on the Atlantic Ocean as their new home.

But three legions were stationed far off at Aquileia; although he called out in haste the militia of the Transalpine province, it seemed scarcely possible with such a small force to hinder the innumerable Celtic host from crossing the Rhone, between its exit from Lake Geneva and the point where it broke through the mountains, over a distance of more than fourteen miles. Caesar followed the majority of the expedition across the river to the right bank. He accomplished the passage in 24 hours, a feat that the cumbersome Helvetii host could not achieve in 20 days. The general, while declaring that in case of need he would march with the tenth legion alone against the enemy, knew not merely how to influence these by such an appeal to honour, but also how to bind the other regiments to their eagles through warlike emulation and inspire the troops with a spark of his own energy.

Nothing remained for the Roman general but to imitate the movement of the Germans and recover his communications by having two legions march past the enemy and take up a position beyond their camp, while four legions remained behind. The Rhine River's Impact

The consequences of this single campaign were immense; they were felt for many centuries afterwards. The Romans governed Gaul from its southern coast.

The Bellovaci, equal in power to the Suessiones, were increasingly dissatisfied that the supreme command of the confederate army had not fallen to them. This discontent was further exacerbated by news that the Haedui, as allies of the Romans, were making preparations to enter their territory. As Caesar advanced into the western cantons of the Belgae, one after another surrendered without much resistance. The powerful Suessiones, who controlled Soissons, and their rivals, the Bellovaci, who ruled over Beauvais, as well as the Ambiani, who governed Amiens, soon followed suit. When these arrived on the Sambre, not far from Bavay, the legions pitched their camp on the crest of the left bank. Meanwhile, the cavalry and light infantry explored the opposite heights. Suddenly, they were attacked by the entire mass of enemy forces and forced down the hill into the river.

They had already partially extricated themselves and restored the connection between the two legions on this wing. Help arrived, partly from the crest of the bank where the Roman rearguard with baggage had arrived, partly from the other bank of the river, where Labienus had meanwhile infiltrated the enemy's camp and taken possession of it. Now perceiving the danger threatening the right wing, he dispatched the victorious tenth legion to aid his general. The subjugation of the Belgae followed an annihilating defeat by the Romans. The Nervii, Atrebates, and Viromandui were forced to acknowledge Roman dominance. A nighttime attack on the Roman camp outside the town, which they launched after the surrender, failed; and the treachery was repaid with severe punishment.

The clients of the Aduatuci, comprising the Eburones between the Maas and Rhine, and other small adjoining tribes, were declared independent by the Romans. The Aduatuci who had been taken prisoner were sold into slavery in large numbers to benefit the Roman treasury. The country was much intersected and poorly furnished with grain. The towns were situated on cliffs and tongues of land, making them inaccessible from the mainland except via shallow waters that were difficult to cross. This presented a significant challenge for the army attacking by land, as it made provision of supplies and conducting sieges equally difficult. However, the Celts could easily furnish the towns with everything they needed, thanks to their vessels, which allowed them to evacuate the towns in the event of the worst-case scenario. Caesar, motivated by a desire to impress the Celtic nation, took action after numerous instances of clemency shown towards defeated enemies. He chose an example of extreme severity against those who had resisted, rather than punishing the breach of treaty and arresting Roman officers. Instead, Caesar ordered the execution of the entire common council and sold every last person from the Venetian canton into slavery.

Sabinus, opposed to the levy of the coast-states, assembled on the Channel, employed the same tactics that Caesar had used the previous year to conquer the Belgic general levy on the Aisne. He stood on the defensive until impatience and hunger weakened the enemy's ranks, and then deceived them about the temper and strength of his troops, as well as their own impatience, luring them into an ill-advised assault on the Roman camp, which they were subsequently defeated in. As a result, the militia dispersed, and the country, stretching from the Seine to the coast, submitted to Roman rule. The Romans attempted to build a road through the forest using an axe, felling trees on either side to create a barricade against enemy attacks. However, even Caesar, known for his bravery, decided it was wise after several days of grueling marching, especially as winter approached, to order a retreat. Although only a small portion of the Morini had surrendered, and the powerful Menapii had not been conquered, he deemed it prudent to withdraw. In the following year, 699, while Caesar himself was stationed in Britain, the majority of the army was redeployed against these tribes. However, this expedition ultimately proved unsuccessful.

While central Gaul had submitted to it without resistance, the Belgic and maritime cantons were compelled to acknowledge Roman rule in 697, and this was followed by a similar acknowledgment the following year. Establishment of Communications with Italy by the Valais

While Caesar formed the Roman domain in the west by force of arms into a compact whole, he did not neglect to establish communication with the newly-conquered country. This country was destined to fill the gap between Italy and Spain, both in terms of communication with its Italian homeland and with its Spanish provinces. The Roman officer who excelled in leadership successfully navigated the challenges, winning several decisive battles. He then persuaded the people living along the Garonne River and in its vicinity up to the Pyrenees Mountains to surrender to their new rulers.

The German tribes of the Usipetes and Tencteri had previously attempted to cross the Rhine into the territory of the Menapii, as mentioned earlier. However, after a series of feigned retreats, they successfully evaded their opponents by crossing the river in vessels belonging to the Menapii. It is reported that this massive host, which included women and children, numbered approximately 430,000 persons. They still lay in the region of Nimeguen and Cleves. It was said that following the invitations of the Celtic patriot party, they intended to advance into Gaul's interior. The rumour was confirmed by the fact that bands of their horsemen already roamed as far as the borders of the Treveri. As negotiations continued, a suspicion arose in the Roman general's mind: the Germans aimed solely to buy time until their mounted units, dispatched earlier, would return.

The behaviour of Caesar towards these German immigrants was met with severe and just censure in the senate. However little it could be excused, the German encroachments were emphatically checked by the terror it occasioned. Caesar led his legions across the Rhine. In a similar manner, the powerful Suebian canton, which oppressed the Ubii—presumably the same one that subsequently appeared under the name of the Chatti—caused the districts immediately adjoining the Ubian territory to be evacuated and the non-combatant portion of the people to be placed in safety. All men capable of arms were directed to assemble at the centre of the canton.

The Roman general had neither occasion nor desire to accept this challenge. His object, partly to reconnoitre, partly to produce an impressive effect if possible upon the Germans or at least on his countrymen at home, was substantially attained. After remaining eighteen days on the right bank of the Rhine, he again arrived in Gaul and broke down the Rhine bridge behind him. The forces of the Britons assembled on the shore this time as well, but they retreated before the mighty armada without engaging in battle. Caesar immediately set out on his march into the interior and after some successful conflicts crossed the river Stour. However, he was forced to halt significantly against his will because the fleet in the open waters had been severely damaged by the storms of the Channel once again. He soon realized that nothing could be done with the Celtic infantry against the Roman army, and that the mass of the general levy was only a hindrance to defense. It was difficult to feed and control such a large force, so he dismissed it and retained only the war-chariots. He collected 4,000 of these chariots, which allowed warriors accustomed to fighting from chariots to also engage on foot. This enabled them to fight in two ways, similar to the early Roman burgess cavalry.

The danger grew with each forward step, and the attack, launched by the princes of Kent at the behest of Cassivellaunus against the Roman naval camp, though it was repelled, served as a timely reminder to reconsider their course. Cassivellaunus was wise enough not to drive the dangerous enemy to extremes. He promised, as Caesar desired, to abstain from disturbing the Trinobantes and pay tribute. No mention was made of delivering up arms or leaving behind a Roman garrison. Even those promises, if they concerned the future, were neither given nor received in earnest. Caesar returned to the naval camp and then proceeded to Gaul.

The immediate object of rousing the islanders out of their haughty security and inducing them in their own interest to no longer allow their island to be a rendezvous for continental emigrants seems certainly to have been attained; at least no complaints are afterwards heard regarding the provision of such protection. But when Haeduan Dumnorix, who was also present in the army bound for Britain, nominally as a cavalry officer but in reality as a hostage, refused to embark and rode home instead, Caesar had no choice but to have him pursued as a deserter. He was accordingly overtaken by the division sent after him and, when he stood on his defence, was cut down. Although the council of war heard many voices, including that of Lucius Aurunculeius Cotta, who gave significant weight to the idea, the commander ultimately decided to accept Ambiorix's proposal.

After the commander's fall, the Eburones attacked the Romans from all sides simultaneously, breaking their exhausted and despairing lines. Most of them, including Cotta, who was already wounded, fell in this attack; a small group, which had regained the abandoned camp, took their own lives by flinging themselves on their swords during the night that followed. Quintus Cicero, who commanded there, had with his weak corps a difficult position. The besiegers, learning from the foe, constructed ramparts and trenches, including moveable towers after the Roman fashion. They showered fireballs and burning spears over the straw-covered huts of the camp. Caesar immediately set out upon receiving the startling news, despite being outnumbered by only two weak legions, totaling approximately 7,000 soldiers, along with 400 skilled horsemen. The mere announcement of Caesar's impending arrival was enough to persuade the insurgents to lift the siege.

The insurrection continued despite a temporary suspension of arms. As winter drew to a close, Caesar set out with his reinforced army against the insurgents. Meanwhile, Labienus prepared for the same fate for the Treveri. Following Caesar's expeditions against the Menapii and Labienus' campaign against the Treveri, the entire Roman army reunited in the territory of the latter.

The Eburones, helpless and passive, sat in their huts, watching as neighboring cantons one by one surrendered to the Romans. The Roman cavalry, advancing from the Treverian territory, then entered their land through the Ardennes. The cavalry had almost seized King Ambiorix in his house. With great difficulty, his attendants sacrificed themselves to save him, and he escaped into a nearby thicket. There were more hunters than game.

The government of this community, which had formerly been under the kingship of its rulers, was the first in southern Gaul. After the fall of its principality, occasioned by the unfortunate wars against Rome, it remained one of the wealthiest, most civilized, and most powerful in all Gaul. Despite this, it had hitherto adhered to the principles of Rome. The attacks of the patriots were therefore directed against the common council and the existing constitution itself; and the more so, since the change of constitution that among the Arverni had substituted the common council for the prince had occurred after the Roman victories and was likely influenced by them. While the insurgents were labouring to induce the cantons that still hesitated, especially the Haedui, to join them, they also sought to gain possession of Narbo. One of their leaders, the daring Lucterius, had already appeared on the Tarn within the limits of the old province. The Roman commander-in-chief then suddenly presented himself in the depth of winter, unexpected alike by friend and foe, on this side of the Alps.

They might as well try to shake the Alps by throwing stones at them as to rouse the legions with the Celtic bands, whether they are gathered in large numbers or scattered across individual cantons. Caesar, following the destruction of Cenabum, the capital of the Carnutes, which had sparked the uprising, marched his troops across the Loire River into the territory of the Bituriges. The task of supplying an army of nearly 60,000 men with provisions in a country devastated far and wide and ravaged by far superior bodies of cavalry became increasingly difficult each day.

For Vercingetorix, seizing the opportunity of Caesar's departure, launched a swift attack on them, which nearly resulted in their defeat and the capture of the entire Roman camp. Caesar resolved not to let matters come to this pass. However painful and even dangerous it was to retire from Gergovia without having accomplished his object, he decided rather to set out immediately and march into the Haedui canton to prevent their formal desertion at any cost. Caesar defeated at Alesia. Before entering the city, however, on this retreat, which was far from agreeable to his quick and confident temperament, he made one final attempt to break through the enemy lines.

The Roman general observed his chance to strike while the bulk of the garrison of Gergovia was busy fortifying its flank, expecting an assault. He seized the opportunity to attack a more vulnerable position that had been left unguarded. The powerful canton of the Bellovaci rose up with a view to attacking in the rear the corps of Labienus, while it confronted the levy of the surrounding cantons of central Gaul at Lutetia. Caesar united with Labienus. It was a grave and decisive moment, for after the retreat from Gergovia and the loss of Noviodunum, a council of war was held in Caesar's headquarters to determine the next course of action.

Vercingetorix was executed five years after his capture. He was led in triumph through the streets of Rome's Italian capital, and while his conqueror offered solemn thanks to the gods on the summit of the Capitol, Vercingetorix was beheaded at its foot for high treason against the Roman nation. As the sun breaks through the clouds after a day of gloom at its setting, so too can destiny bring hope to nations in their decline with one final great leader. It is impossible to part from the noble king of the Arverni without a feeling of historical and human sympathy. However, this sentiment is somewhat diminished by the fact that his status as a great leader was largely due to his position as a knight.

The loss of Vercingetorix was irreparable. The desire to rest everywhere prevailed naturally. Of the seven years of his governorship, two had elapsed, and the last was called into question by his political opponents in the capital. He could only reckon with some degree of certainty on two more summers, and, while his interest as well as his honour required that he should hand over the newly-acquired regions to his successor in a condition of tolerable peace and tranquillity, there was scanty time to bring about such a state of things.

In the two most eminent cantons of central Gaul, the Haedui and Arverni, a strong party was well disposed to Rome. Following the fall of Alesia, the cantons regained their former relations with Rome, and even their captives, numbering 20,000, were released without ransom.
