He initially made a concession without being prompted, then withdrew it, and finally attempted to justify his reversal with deceitful language. The attempt to shorten Caesar's governorship was indirect. The regulations issued at the same time aimed to achieve the same object directly.

As, however, according to earlier practice, the proconsul or propraetor had the right to enter his provincial magistracy immediately after the termination of his consulship or praetorship, the successor of Caesar was to be nominated. He could not be chosen from the urban magistrates of 704, but rather from those of 705, and therefore could not enter before January 1st. 706 Caesar had the right to command for the remaining ten months of the year 705, not under the Pompeio-Licinian law, but due to an ancient rule that granted a commander's authority until the end of their term or the arrival of their successor. The pitiful dissimulation and procrastination of Pompeius are after a remarkable manner mixed up, in these arrangements, with the wily formalism and the constitutional erudition of the Republican Party.

Caesar's recall was a topic of debate. As a result, the gradual development of constitutional procedures became apparent. They hinted, however, without making a binding declaration on the point, that they might grant Caesar admission to the elections for 706, provided he did not require his personal announcement, and would continue his governorship up to the 13th November. 705 During Caesar's party manoeuvres with his antagonists before war, he successfully eliminated the Gallic insurrection and restored peace throughout the entire region.

Pompeius was recalled by the senate just as much as Caesar, and while Caesar was willing to comply with the order, Pompeius refused obedience outright. The Catonian faction had vowed to drive the issue to a breaking point and persuade the senate to follow suit. Now, they found themselves stranded, their efforts thwarted by the inaction of the majority. After the champions of the constitution and senatorial government had already declared the constitutional rights of the burgesses and tribunes of the people to be meaningless formalities, they now found themselves driven by necessity to treat the senate's constitutional decision in a similar manner. Since the legitimate government would not let itself be saved with its own consent, it was forced to save the senate against its will.

The consul, in concert with the two consuls elected for 705, who also belonged to the Catonian party, proceeded to Pompeius. These three men, by virtue of their own power, requested the general to put himself at the head of the two legions stationed at Capua and to call the Italian militia to arms as he saw fit. He had, while maintaining his own legal footing, compelled Pompeius to declare war, not as a representation of the legitimate authority, but as the leader of an openly revolutionary minority within the senate that dominated the majority. This result was not of slight importance, despite the instinct of the masses being able to discern that the war concerned matters beyond formal law.

Caesar had immediately gone to Ravenna, vividly representing the state of affairs to his master. It hardly needed such a representation to convince Caesar that further delay would now be injurious. He sent an ultimatum to Rome, which, if useful for nothing else, made his opponents' extreme submissiveness further compromise them in public opinion, and possibly even prompted them to be less diligent in preparing against him. In this ultimatum, Caesar dropped all counter-demands he had made on Pompeius and offered to resign as governor of Transalpine Gaul and dismiss eight of the ten legions under his command. He agreed to leave either the governorship of Cisalpine Gaul and Illyria with one legion or that of Cisalpine Gaul alone with two legions, but not beyond his consulship. Can it be determined with certainty whether Caesar was genuinely committed to these concessions and believed he could successfully carry out his strategy against Pompeius even after making such significant concessions, or did he think the opposing side had already overstepped by accepting these proposals, which would only serve as evidence that Caesar had lost sight of his own cause?

The consul Lentulus stated that even the senate's decree had lost significance. If it continued to show deference, he would take matters into his own hands and, along with his influential allies, proceed with further action. When Caesar was informed by the tribunes who had fled to his camp, entreating protection, he called together the soldiers of the thirteenth legion, which had meanwhile arrived from its cantonments near Tergeste, and unfolded before them the state of affairs in the capital. When he returned after nine years' absence, he stepped onto the soil of his native land, and in doing so, also embarked on the path of revolution. The die had been cast.

Caesar stood alone, surrounded by military-political adjutants who had risen from the army. As soldiers, they were trained not to question the reason or purpose of anything, but to obey unconditionally. Caesar's treatment of his officers as adjutants without independence rendered his case apparent for the first time. This highlighted the gravity of the evil, which was that he admitted no men fitted to undertake a separate command in his camp. At the same time, Caesar stood urgently in need of such men, given the diffusion of the impending struggle throughout all the provinces of the wide empire. It still numbered around nine legions of infantry. At its peak, it was estimated that no more than 50,000 men were part of the force, although only a third of them had actually seen combat.

The eight-year war, marked by various challenges, had provided Caesar with an opportunity to organize his army in the way that only he could. The soldiers, determined to prove that the war was just as much their concern as that of their general, agreed among themselves that they would receive credit for pay promised by Caesar at the outbreak of the civil war. This credit would be doubled and would remain in effect until the end of the conflict. In addition, the soldiers would support their poorer comrades using funds from the general's means. Furthermore, each subaltern officer was required to equip and pay a trooper out of his own pocket. Caesar's power was based on his control over a relatively small area in upper Italy. Despite having the essential elements of power—unlimited political and military authority, as well as a loyal army at his disposal—he was able to exert significant influence over this specific region.

The conduct of a division of recruits from Opitergium (Oderzo in the delegation of Treviso) is a testament to the bravery that prevailed there. Shortly after the outbreak of war in the Illyrian waters, they found themselves surrounded by enemy war-vessels on a rickety raft. Despite being bombarded throughout the day until sunset, they refused to surrender. Those who managed to escape the hail of missiles took their own lives during the following night. As they had already granted to Caesar the means of more than doubling his original army, he soon received a large number of recruits following the outbreak of the civil war, which led to immediate and extensive levies. The Coalition While Caesar thus entered the struggle only as commandant of Gaul, without other essential resources than efficient adjutants, a faithful army, and a devoted province, Pompeius began it as de facto supreme head of the Roman commonwealth, and in full possession of all the resources that stood at the disposal of the legitimate government of the great Roman Empire.

While his position was politically and militarily significant, it was also less clear-cut and decisive. The unity of leadership, which arose naturally from Caesar's position, was inconsistent with the nature of a coalition. Pompeius, too much of a soldier to deceive himself about its indispensability, attempted to force it on the coalition but ultimately failed. Despite his efforts, he was nominated by the senate as sole and absolute generalissimo by land and sea. However, the senate's influence on politics remained significant, while its occasional interference with military affairs proved detrimental. The coalition of powers led by Juba of Numidia, despite being comprised of naturally hostile entities, had a significant impact on Caesar's adversaries. However, it is essential to note that the coalition itself was not necessarily a powerful force, but rather a collection of disparate entities that contributed to its overall strength.

In the other provinces, as well, the governorships and command positions were recently filled by trustworthy individuals, largely due to the influence of Pompey and the minority of the Senate. If no text is provided, I will return nothing. No correction needed. In Italy, only two legions recently raised by Caesar were present at the outset. These legions, whose effective strength was less than 7,000 men, were highly unreliable due to several factors. They had been levied in Cisalpine Gaul and consisted of old comrades-in-arms who were deeply resentful towards Caesar for the unbecoming intrigue that forced them to change camps. This led to their longing for their general, who had generously provided them with presents beforehand as a gesture of goodwill upon their departure.

Immediately after the decisive decree of the Senate on 7 January. In the depths of winter, prominent members of the aristocracy embarked on a journey to various districts, with the aim of hastening the recruitment of soldiers and preparing for war. The lack of cavalry was deeply felt, as they had grown accustomed to rely solely on the provinces and especially on the Celtic contingents. To make a start, three hundred gladiators belonging to Caesar were taken from the fencing schools of Capua and mounted. However, this step met with widespread disapproval, prompting Pompeius to disband the troop and replace it with 300 horsemen from the mounted slave-herdmen of Apulia. The troops under Caesar's command, excluding those still in the process of formation, were stationed. One half was positioned along the Saône and Loire rivers, while the other half was located in Belgica. Meanwhile, Pompeius' Italian reserves had begun arriving from all directions to meet them; even before the first Transalpine divisions under Caesar could arrive in Italy, a superior army would inevitably be ready to receive it there. Now perhaps, accustomed as he was to operate slowly and surely with superior masses, he might be disconcerted by a wholly improvised attack. The suddenness of the war and the toil of a winter campaign could not but disorganize the Pompeian corps, consisting of old soldiers of Caesar or ill-trained recruits, even in the course of formation.

All was confusion; consequently, a great council of war was held in Teanum Sidicinum on 23 January. Pompeius, Labienus, and both consuls were present. The answer was that if Caesar were to immediately return to his province, they would bind themselves to procure the disarming of Italy and the departure of Pompeius by a decree of the senate to be passed in due form in the capital. Perhaps this reply was intended not as a bare artifice to deceive, but as an acceptance of the proposal of compromise; it was, however, in reality the opposite. In the northernmost Picenean town of Auximum, Publius Attius Varus collected a considerable band of recruits. However, at the request of the municipality, Varus evacuated the town before Caesar arrived. A small group of Caesar's soldiers who overtook the troop not far from Auximum then dispersed it after a brief conflict – the first battle of this war.

Gaius Lucilius Hirrus soon evacuated Camerinum with 3000 men, while Publius Lentulus Spinther led 5000 Asculanians. However, Pompeius failed to appear, and this was justified; for although he could potentially use his two untrustworthy legions as a reserve force for the Picenian general levy, he couldn't challenge Caesar alone with them. Instead, Caesar arrived after a few days (February 14).

As long as Domitius awaited Pompeius' arrival, he ensured the town's defense; once Pompeius' letters finally disabused him, he decided not to persist in a futile endeavor – by which he would have provided the greatest service to his faction – nor even surrender. Instead, while informing the common soldiers that relief was near, he planned to make his own escape, along with his officers of quality, during the next night. The larger half went first on March 4. With the smaller division of about 10,000 men, Pompeius waited at Brundisium for the return of the fleet. However desirable the possession of Brundisium might be for an eventual attempt to reoccupy Italy, they did not presume to hold it permanently against Caesar. Caesar arrived in Brundisium before the siege commenced.

The common soldiers, presumably indifferent to politics, were incorporated into his own army. In contrast, the officers were not only spared but also freely released without distinction of person or any promises being extracted from them. All property claimed by the officers was then relinquished, with no investigation into the validity of their claims. Lucius Domitius was treated as such. Even Labienus had his remaining funds and belongings sent to the enemy's camp after he departed. He wished to obtain from the Senate's approval of what had been done and full powers for the continuation of the war. When Caesar arrived in the capital at the end of March, the tribunes from his faction summoned the senate on 1 April.

Caesar desired to be named dictator in order to regulate his position. However, his wish was not fulfilled because such a magistrate could only be constitutionally appointed by one of the consuls. Caesar attempted to buy the consul Lentulus, but due to the consul's disordered finances, there was little hope for success. Caesar's aggressive actions had foiled his original plan to launch a simultaneous campaign in both Gauls through offensive operations from Italy and Spain. As a result, Pompeius decided to head to Spain instead. While Caesar still had adherents in the Further Province during his governorship, the more important province of Eboracum was attached to him by ties of veneration and gratitude. Twenty years earlier, he had commanded the province during the Sertorian War, and after the war's end, he reorganized it.

Pompeius could evidently do no better than proceed to Spain with the remaining forces of his army, and then lead his entire army to meet Caesar. Why, as master of the sea and Sicily, he did not subsequently revert to his original plan cannot be determined. Perhaps the aristocracy, after their short-sighted and distrustful nature, showed no desire to entrust themselves to the Spanish troops and population. It is enough to say that Pompeius remained in the east, while Caesar had the option of directing his first attack either against the army being organized in Greece under Pompeius' command or against the one ready for battle under his lieutenants in Spain. Of the Spanish troops, two legions were left behind under Varro's command in the Further province. In contrast, five best legions, reinforced by 40,000 Spanish infantry (partly Celtiberian infantry of the line and partly Lusitanian light troops) and 5,000 Spanish cavalry, were led by Afranius and Petreius to close the Pyrenees against the enemy, as ordered by Pompeius and transmitted by Vibullius.

Caesar occupied the Pyrenees position at Ilerda. Meanwhile, Caesar himself arrived in Gaul, where the commencement of the siege of Massilia still detained him in person. He immediately despatched the greater part of his troops assembled on the Rhone—six legions and the cavalry—to Narbo (Narbonne), then to Rhode (Rosas), with the aim of anticipating the enemy at the Pyrenees. For an army that had to endure a siege, it was an excellent position. However, the defense of Spain, after the occupation of the Pyrenees line had been neglected, could only be undertaken in earnest behind the Ebro River. Since no secure communication was established between Ilerda and the Ebro, and no bridge existed over the latter river, the retreat from a temporary to a true defensive position was not sufficiently secured. The Caesarians established themselves above Ilerda, where the river Sicoris meets the Cinga (Cinca), which joins it below Ilerda. However, the attack did not begin until Caesar arrived at the camp on 23 June. Under the walls of the town, both sides maintained a struggle with equal exasperation and valour, alternating frequently between success and failure. However, the Caesarians failed to achieve their objective: establishing themselves between the Pompeian camp and the town, thereby gaining control of the stone bridge. As a result, they remained dependent on two hastily constructed bridges over the Sicoris River for communication with Gaul. The river itself was too wide to be bridged at Ilerda; instead, the Caesarians built their bridges about eighteen miles upstream.

In the camp, actual famine prevailed. The cost of wheat was 50 denarii per modius (1 pound 16 shillings), and dangerous diseases broke out. In contrast, on the left bank, there were accumulated provisions and varied supplies, as well as troops of all sorts. These included reinforcements from Gaul, comprising cavalry and archers, officers and soldiers on furlough, foraging parties returning, totaling around 6,000 men. The Pompeians attacked this mass with superior force, driving them to the mountains with great loss. Meanwhile, the Caesarians on the right bank were forced to remain passive spectators of the unequal conflict. He ordered boats made of lightweight wood frames and woven osier, lined with leather, following the design used by the Britons in the Channel and later adopted by the Saxons. The vessels were then assembled in the camp and transported on wagons to the site where the bridges once stood. His soldiers therefore worked day and night to lower the depth of the river by constructing canals that drew off the water, allowing the infantry to wade through it.

The preparations of the Pompeians to cross the Ebro were completed before those of the Caesarians to invest Ilerda. The Pompeians, having finished building a bridge of boats, began their march towards the Ebro along the left bank of the Sicoris River. In contrast, the Caesarian canals seemed insufficient to create a ford for infantry; therefore, he ordered only his cavalry to cross the stream and, by clinging to the rear of the enemy, at least to delay and harass them. Caesar's legions observed the enemy's columns retreating since midnight in the gray morning. With the seasoned veterans' instinct, they recognized the strategic value of this retreat, which would force them to pursue their adversaries into distant and impractical regions infested with hostile troops. The general, at his own request, led the infantry across the river as well, despite the water reaching up to the men's shoulders. They crossed without incident. The Roman infantry under Caesar outmaneuvered them and set up camp across from them in the evening and throughout the night, as they had initially planned a nighttime march that was abandoned due to fear of cavalry attacks.

On the very day after he had succeeded in cutting off the Pompeians from the Ebro, the soldiers of both armies began to fraternize and negotiate a surrender. The terms asked by the Pompeians, particularly regarding the sparing of their officers, had already been conceded by Caesar. However, Petreius, accompanied by his escort consisting of slaves and Spaniards, interrupted the negotiations. He then ordered the Caesarians, whom he could easily harm, to be put to death. Their cavalry became increasingly fearful, forcing the infantry to take them into the center. The legions were deployed as the rear guard. Procuring water and forage grew more challenging, and they were already forced to slaughter their pack animals, as they could no longer sustain them. Caesar granted officers and soldiers their life and liberty, as well as the possession of property they still retained. He also restored what had been taken from them, ensuring its full value was made good by himself. Furthermore, while he had forcibly enrolled recruits captured in Italy into his army, Caesar honoured the old legionaries of Pompeius by promising that no one would be compelled to join his army against their will.

The soldiers who were native Spaniards made up approximately one-third of the army. They were immediately disbanded. The Italian soldiers, meanwhile, were discharged along the borders of Transalpine and Cisalpine Gaul. When Lucius Nasidius' small squadron from the east, via Sicily and Sardinia, arrived at Massilia's port, the Massiliots again prepared their naval forces and set sail with Nasidius' ships to confront Brutus. Expeditions of Caesar to the Corn-Provinces. While in the western provinces, the war after various critical vicissitudes was finally decided in favour of Caesar. Spain and Massilia were subdued, and the chief army of the enemy was captured to the last man. The decision of arms had also taken place on the second arena of warfare, where Caesar had assumed the offensive immediately after the conquest of Italy. Sardinia was occupied, and Sicily followed suit. It is already known that the Pompeians intended to reduce Italy to starvation.

Sicily was occupied without a fight. Cato, with no proper army and no military experience, left the island after warning the Sicilians not to waste their efforts with futile resistance. With the aid of his superior fleet, Curio landed easily between Hadrumetum and Utica. The first legion of the enemy was stationed there, along with their warships, while the second legion, led by Varus, was positioned in front of Utica. Animated by the fiery appeal of their youthful leader, Curio's cavalry charged and routed the enemy horsemen. In the presence of both armies, they also cut down the light infantry that had accompanied the horsemen. Emboldened by this success and inspired by Curio's personal example, his legions advanced through the difficult ravine separating the two lines to attack. However, the Pompeians did not wait; instead, they disgracefully fled back to their camp and evacuated it in the night following.

Curio immediately began preparations to besiege Utica. When news arrived that King Juba was advancing with all his forces to relieve the city, Curio decided, just as Scipio had done when Syphax arrived, to lift the siege and return to Scipio's original camp until reinforcements from Sicily could arrive. King Juba had been induced by the attacks of neighboring princes to turn back with his main force. He was sending only a moderate corps, led by Saburra, to aid the besieged. After the nocturnal march and the hot conflict, there were not more than 200 Roman cavalrymen left, along with the infantry, who were extremely exhausted from fatigue and fighting. They were surrounded in the wide plain they had allowed themselves to be lured into by the continually increasing hosts of the enemy.

Caesar had good reason to entrust the most important independent command to the young man, despite his lack of military experience and notorious reputation for a dissolute lifestyle. There was a spark of Caesar's own spirit in the youthful leader. He bore a striking resemblance to Caesar, for just as he had drained every last drop of pleasure from the cup, so too did he eschew the path of statesmanship solely because he was an officer. Instead, it was his political actions that ultimately led him to wield the sword. His eloquence was not characterized by polished, rounded phrases, but rather by a depth of feeling that resonated with those who heard him speak. He fought with swift, decisive action, often relying on minimal resources to achieve his objectives. Despite this, he possessed a lighthearted and carefree nature, marked by a frankness that was both pleasant and unbridled, as if he lived life fully in the present moment. Pompeius' plan of campaign for 705 is unclear due to the limited information available. However, it is known that Pompeius' general plan for the campaign was significantly affected by the war in 705. After the loss of Italy, Pompeius assigned a crucial role to the military corps in the west. The extent to which these events impacted his overall strategy remains uncertain and can only be speculated upon.

It is much more likely that he still followed his original plan to attack Caesar from both sides in Gaul, whether it was Transalpine or Cisalpine, even after losing Italy. He then contemplated a simultaneous attack on Spain and Macedonia. Provisions came to an end, and the troops became troublesome and mutinous. A few divisions succeeded in reaching the mainland on rafts, but the corps, still fifteen strong, laid down their arms and were conveyed in the vessels of Libo to Macedonia, where they would be incorporated with the Pompeian army. Octavius was left to complete the subjugation of the Illyrian coast, now denuded of troops. Caesar's double aggressive movement against Spain and against Sicily and Africa was successful. In the former case, it was complete, while in the latter, it was at least partially successful. Pompeius' plan to starve Italy was thwarted mainly by the loss of Sicily, which also frustrated his overall campaign strategy. The destruction of the Spanish army completely undermined his plans.

Organizations in Macedonia. The ultras, on the other hand, displayed a stark contrast to the utter prostration of weakness. If the counterrevolution in favor of the friends of the constitution, for which all elements were in existence, had not broken out in Italy during Caesar's absence, the reason, according to the assurance of discerning opponents of Caesar, lay chiefly in the general dread of the unbridled fury of the republican ultras after restoration.

While Pompeius' action was hindered by the need to consider the authority of a collegiate board that personally disapproved of him, this obstacle was greatly exacerbated when the senate of emigrants settled almost in his very headquarters. The animosity of the emigrants now found expression in these senatorial meetings. The Legions of Pompeius. While Pompeius treated the political aspect of his position with characteristic perversity, making it even more undesirable, he showed commendable zeal in organizing his military forces. He commanded an army of approximately 7,000 cavalry and eleven legions, with only about five being seasoned warriors. He also had control over a fleet of around 500 ships.

The temperament of the soldiers, for whose provisioning and pay Pompeius showed adequate care, and to whom in the event of victory the most abundant rewards were promised, was throughout good. In several divisions, even the most efficient ones, it was excellent. However, a great part of the army consisted of newly-raised troops, the formation and training of which, despite zealous prosecution, necessarily required time. On the other hand, the land army, with its headquarters still at Berrhoea on the Haliacmon, had not yet arrived; the main body of it was moving slowly along the great highway from Thessalonica towards the western coast, destined for the future headquarters at Dyrrhachium. Two legions, led by Metellus Scipio, remained in winter quarters at Pergamus in Asia and were expected to arrive in Europe only with the spring. He had long ago ordered the collection of vessels of war and transports in Brundisium. After the Spanish army's capitulation and the fall of Massalia, he directed the majority of the selected troops stationed there to proceed to that destination.

The regions of Illyria were indeed rugged and impoverished. However, it was not long before other armies traversed these lands, rendering the initial obstacle less formidable for a skilled conqueror like Julius Caesar. He realized that during the tumultuous march through Illyria, Pompey might lead his entire force across the Adriatic, allowing their positions to shift simultaneously – with Caesar in Macedonia and Pompey in Italy. Such a swift change was unlikely, given his slow-moving adversary. Caesar likely chose the maritime route based on the assumption that his fleet would soon be ready to command respect. Upon returning from Spain, however, he discovered the actual situation in the Adriatic, which may have made it too late to alter the original plan.

Caesar lands in Epirus; his first successes were short-lived. Caesar set sail with six legions, greatly thinned by toil and sickness, and 600 horsemen from Brundisium along the coast of Epirus. Caesar scaled the Acroceraunian mountains. Although he expanded his operations in Epirus as far as his limited resources would allow, the sustenance of his army proved difficult and precarious. The enemy, in control of the magazines at Dyrrhachium and masters of the sea, enjoyed an abundance of supplies. With an army presumably little above 20,000 strong, he could not offer battle to Pompeius at least twice as numerous. Instead, he deemed himself fortunate that Pompeius went methodically to work and, instead of immediately forcing a battle, took up his winter quarters between Dyrrhachium and Apollonia on the right bank of the Apsus River, facing Caesar on the left. This allowed him to annihilate the enemy with an irresistibly superior force once the legions from Pergamum arrived in the spring.

His sole hope still depended on the transport fleet. It was hardly likely that they would either steal or fight their way through the blockade. After the initial act of foolishness, however, this second attempt was made necessary by circumstances. How desperate Caesar's situation appeared to himself is shown by his resolution: when the fleet still had not arrived, he decided to sail alone in a fisherman's boat across the Adriatic to Brundisium to fetch it. In reality, this was only feasible because no skilled mariner was available to undertake the perilous voyage. Pompeius, after vainly attempting to prevent the junction of the two enemy armies and force Antonius' corps to fight alone, took up a new position at Asparagium on the river Genusus (Skumbi), which flows parallel to the Apsus between the latter and the town of Dyrrhachium.

Caesar's veterans invested the Pompeian camp with a chain of posts sixteen miles long. They then added an additional inner line, similar to that at Alesia, to protect themselves from attacks by sea from Dyrrhachium and potential flanking maneuvers facilitated by their naval presence. The lack of provisions for the animals was deeply felt. The fleet's inability to provide adequate fodder for the beasts of burden and horses resulted in a significant number of deaths. Even when the horses were transported to Dyrrhachium by the fleet, they still lacked sufficient food. While he caused the inner line of Caesar's entrenchments to be attacked by legions from the camp, and the outer line by light troops placed in vessels that had landed beyond the enemy's entrenchments, a third division landed in the space between the two lines and attacked their already occupied defenders in the rear. Caesar seized the opportunity with great eagerness, and it soon presented itself. He then attacked a Pompeian legion that had become isolated, with the majority of his infantry.

Caesar was more completely driven from the sea than ever, since Pompeius' elder son Gnaeus had launched a bold attack on the port of Oricum. He had partly burnt and partly carried off Caesar's few warships that were moored there. Shortly after, Gnaeus also set fire to the transport fleet left behind in Lissus. As a result, all possibility of bringing fresh reinforcements to Caesar by sea from Brundisium was lost. Pompeius might leave Caesar himself and his best troops in Greece, and cross over personally. He had been making preparations for this with the main army bound for Italy, where the atmosphere was decidedly anti-monarchical. After dispatching the best troops and their trusted commander to the Greek army, Caesar's forces would be of little consequence. He then retreated to Apollonia after that unfortunate engagement.

The march from Dyrrhachium to Apollonia along a difficult road, crossed by several rivers, was no easy task for a defeated army pursued by the enemy. However, the dexterous leadership of their general and the indomitable marching energy of the soldiers compelled Pompeius to suspend the pursuit after four days. Despite its appeal, he chose not to leave the Scipio corps, particularly since he aimed to capture Calvinus' forces during this march. Calvinus lay on the Egnatian road at Heraclea Lyncestis, between Pompeius and Scipio. After Caesar retreated to Apollonia, Calvinus found himself farther from Pompeius than from the larger army of Pompeius. Unaware of events in Dyrrhachium and his precarious situation, Calvinus remained ignorant of the shift in momentum following Caesar's successes at Dyrrhachium, which had won over much of the country to Pompeius' side, and Caesar's messengers were being detained everywhere. Caesar had arrived unopposed at Apollonia.

After the disaster at Dyrrhachium, he decided to shift the conflict inland in order to escape the enemy's naval power, which had been the main reason for his earlier failures. They were willing to be resolved at any cost to fight with Caesar and get to him as soon as possible, using whatever means were most convenient. The army of Pompeius, numbering eleven legions or approximately 47,000 men and 7,000 horse, was more than double that of Caesar in infantry, and seven times as numerous in cavalry. Fatigue and conflicts had so decimated Caesar's troops that his eight legions did not number more than 22,000 men under arms, consequently not nearly half their normal amount. The victorious army of Pompeius was well-provided with a large cavalry and abundant stores. In contrast, the troops of Caesar struggled to survive due to lack of provisions, relying on the hope that better supplies would soon be available from the nearby corn harvest. Pompeius intended to hold his infantry back on defense, while using his cavalry to disperse the small group of lightly armed horsemen who, following German tactics, combined with light infantry, faced him. He then planned to attack Caesar's right flank from behind. Labienus likewise dispersed the enemy's cavalry after a brave but short resistance, and then deployed his force to the left in order to turn the infantry.

The day was lost for Pompeius as his flight ended in failure. Many able soldiers had fallen, but the army remained substantially intact. In contrast, Pompeius's situation was far less perilous than Caesar's after the defeat at Dyrrhachium. While Caesar had come to understand that fortune often withdraws herself at certain moments, even from those it favors, he knew that perseverance was necessary to win her back. In contrast, Pompey saw fortune as a constant and unyielding force, and his own fate seemed sealed when she withdrew from him. Unlike Caesar's more robust nature, which only deepened in despair but ultimately yielded greater strength, Pompey's weaker spirit succumbed to an abyss of hopelessness. His army discouraged and leaderless, Scipio sought protection behind the camp walls. However, Caesar allowed it no respite; the Roman and Thracian guard's obstinate resistance was soon overcome, compelling the mass to withdraw in disorder to the heights of Crannon and Scotussa, where the camp was pitched.

The enemy's army was not only defeated but annihilated. After the battle, 15,000 enemy soldiers lay dead or wounded on the field. The Caesarians suffered relatively few casualties, missing only 200 men. The remaining enemy forces, numbering nearly 20,000, laid down their arms the morning after. However, isolated troops, including the officers of most note, sought refuge in the mountains. The little town of Megara was one of the few exceptions to this general submission. It allowed itself to be besieged and stormed by the Caesarians. Juba, king of Numidia, had long expected that his kingdom would be annexed by Caesar after Curio's victory. With greater certainty than ever, he anticipated this outcome. As a result, he was forced to accept the defeated party. The Aristocracy after the Battle of Pharsalus

Like the client communities that submitted to the victor of Pharsalus, those who had joined the constitutional party - all who had done so with genuine commitment or merely followed suit like Marcus Cicero and his contemporaries - approached the new monarch to make amends. Their peace was readily granted by the monarch's indulgent attitude, which showed no contempt for their petitions.

Into whatever abyss of degeneracy the aristocratic rule had now sunk, it had once been a great political system; the sacred fire, by which Italy was conquered and Hannibal was vanquished, still glowed dimly in the Roman nobility as long as that nobility existed. This rendered a cordial understanding between the men of the old regime and the new monarch impossible. A significant number of constitutionalists publicly acknowledged the monarchy and accepted pardons from Caesar, often retreating from public life as much as possible. However, this was rarely done without secretly reserving hope for a potential shift in circumstances. In most cases, genuine aristocrats were driven by passionate desire rather than calm consideration. This, in turn, was likely accompanied by self-deception about the possibility of future success and a fear of the consequences that would inevitably follow the victor's wrath.

Convinced that after Ilerda and Pharsalus, monarchy was inevitable, and morally firm enough to acknowledge this bitter truth, he hesitated for a moment whether the constitutional party should continue a war, which would necessitate sacrifices for a lost cause on the part of many who did not know why they made them. Whether he could not bring himself to trust Caesar, or if his usual indecisiveness had led him to lose sight of the disaster at Pharsalus, Pompeius became resolute in continuing the fight against Caesar and sought out a new battlefield. The military effects of the battle were devastating. Despite Caesar's efforts to appease his opponents through prudence and moderation, the conflict continued unabated.

The leading men had almost all taken part in the fight at Pharsalus. Although they escaped with the exception of Lucius Domitius Ahenobarbus, who was killed in the flight, they were yet scattered in all directions, making it impossible to coordinate a common plan for the continuation of the campaign. Cato, who immediately upon hearing the news of his defeat, evacuated Dyrrhachium, still held Corcyra. Rutilius Lupus, however, controlled the Peloponnese during this period for the constitutional faction. On the Italian and Sicilian coasts, the Pompeian squadrons dispatched vessels after the victories of Dyrrhachium (36) had achieved notable successes against the ports of Brundisium, Messana, and Vibo. Notably, at Messana, they had burnt a significant portion of the fleet that was being prepared for Caesar's arrival; however, the ships that were active in these regions, mostly from Asia Minor and Syria, were recalled by their communities following the Pharsalian battle. This led to the expedition coming to an end.

In Egypt, a considerable Roman army remained, formed from the troops left behind by Gabinius and later recruited from Italian vagrants and Syrian bandits. The court of Alexandria soon confirmed this with the return of Egyptian vessels, making it clear that they had no intention of supporting the defeated party or placing their troops at its disposal. While the entire eastern region fell to the coalition following the Battle of Pharsalus, it is possible that the war could continue in an honorable manner, perhaps in Spain and certainly in Africa. Claiming the support of the king of Numidia, who had long been a subject of the Roman community, against revolutionary city-states would be a painful blow for Romans, but not necessarily treasonous. Even after the recall of squadrons from the east, they still possessed a considerable fleet of their own. Caesar, however, was virtually without vessels of war. Their connection with the Dalmatae, who had risen in opposition to Caesar, controlled the most important seas and seaports, presenting advantageous prospects for a naval war on a small scale.

Caesar pursued Pompeius to Egypt. As the remnants of the defeated party were helplessly driven about by fate, even those who had determined to continue the struggle knew not how or where to do so. Caesar, as ever quick to resolve and act, laid everything aside to pursue Pompeius—the one opponent he respected as an officer, and whose capture would have probably paralyzed a significant portion of his opponents. He might have joined his partisans at Corcyra or Africa; but repugnance toward his aristocratic allies and the thought of the reception he would face there after Pharsalus, particularly after his disgraceful flight, led him to take a different course. Instead, he opted for the protection of the Parthian king rather than that of Cato. While he was employed in collecting money and slaves from Roman revenue-farmers and merchants in Cyprus, he received news that Antioch had declared for Caesar.

The dagger of the rash assassin prevented an answer to the question of how Caesar would have dealt with the captive Pompeius. However, while humane sympathy still found a place in the great soul of Caesar alongside ambition, it enjoined that he should spare his former friend. His interest, on the other hand, required that he annihilate Pompeius in a manner other than by executioner's hand. Caesar regulated Egypt. Meanwhile, in Egypt, Caesar had nothing further to do. The Romans and Egyptians expected him to immediately set sail and apply himself to the subjugation of Africa and the huge task of organization that awaited him after the victory. Although the aid they had given to Pompeius justified the imposition of a war contribution, the exhausted land was spared from this. The arrears on the sum stipulated for in 695 and since then were only partially paid; however, a final payment of 10 million denarii (400,000 pounds) was required.

He accordingly ordered reinforcements to be sent from Asia immediately, and in the meantime, he maintained a show of utmost self-possession. There was never greater joy in his camp than during their stay at Alexandria. While the beautiful and intelligent Cleopatra was not sparing of her charm, neither was she with her judge, Caesar. He also valued most of his victories that were won by beautiful women. Under the leadership of Achillas, and later proved by secret orders from the king and his guardian, the Roman army stationed in Egypt suddenly appeared in Alexandria. As soon as the citizens saw that it was coming to attack Caesar, they joined forces with the soldiers.

The insurrection at the head of which Princess Arsinoe and her confidant Eunuch Ganymedes had placed themselves was gaining momentum. Meanwhile, it had free course in all Egypt and a significant portion of the capital. Caesar controlled both the island and the eastern harbour. The western harbour, along with the mole, belonged to the citizens. Following the destruction of the Alexandrian fleet, Caesar's ships were able to enter and exit freely. The Alexandrians, after vainly attempting to introduce fire-ships from the west into the eastern harbour, equipped a small squadron with the remaining arsenal and blocked the way of Caesar's vessels. These were towing in a fleet of transports accompanied by a legion that had arrived from Asia Minor. However, the excellent Rhodian mariners under Caesar successfully overcame the enemy.

Not long afterwards, however, the citizens captured the lighthouse island, and from that point, they totally closed the narrow and rocky mouth of the east harbour for larger ships. This forced Caesar's fleet to take its station in the open roads before the east harbour. As a result, his communication with the sea hung by a very thin thread. While a violent conflict erupted here around the entrenchers, a division of Egyptian troops landed unexpectedly on the lower part of the mole adjoining the island, attacking the Roman soldiers and sailors who were crowded together on the mole at the transverse wall. They drove the entire mass into the sea in wild confusion. From Pelusium, where Mithradates had the fortune to occupy on the day of his arrival, he took the great road towards Memphis with the view of avoiding the intersected ground of the Delta and crossing the Nile before its division. During this movement, his troops received manifold support from Jewish peasants who were settled in significant numbers in this part of Egypt.

Caesar had his camp attacked from both the front and the side as he marched along the Nile. He then ordered a third group of soldiers to secretly climb up to the high ground behind the camp. Immediately after the battle, Caesar advanced at the head of his cavalry from the land side straight into the part of the capital occupied by the Egyptians. Cleopatra and her younger brother Ptolemaeus gained control of Egypt under Roman supremacy. The princess Arsinoe was sent to Italy, preventing her from serving as a pretext for future Egyptian uprisings. Like their ancestors, the Egyptians were deeply devoted to their dynasty, but showed little interest in individual rulers. Cyprus once again became part of the Roman province of Cilicia.

Calvinus had been obliged to despatch to Egypt two out of the three legions left behind with him. He formed these legions out of Pharsalian prisoners of war. To fill the gap, he hastily gathered one legion from Romans domiciled in Pontus and two more legions under Deiotarus, who exercised them according to Roman methods. Calvinus then advanced into Lesser Armenia. Caesar's war by land and sea in Illyria ended with his defeat of Gabinius. He then secured a naval victory at Tauris. Meanwhile, in Illyria, serious incidents took place while Caesar was in Egypt. The Dalmatian coast had long been a source of discontent for the Roman Empire, with its inhabitants in open revolt against Caesar since the conflicts surrounding Dyrrhachium. Meanwhile, the interior region had also been plagued by the presence of dispersed Pompeian refugees since the time of the Thessalian war.

Quintus Cornificius, however, maintained order with the legions that followed him from Italy, keeping both the native inhabitants and refugees in control. At the same time, he successfully addressed the challenging task of provisioning the troops in these remote regions. The unfavourable weather and difficulties in supplying troops forced Gabinius to begin his withdrawal. As he retreated, he was ambushed and decisively defeated by the Dalmatians. With a greatly diminished force, he struggled to reach Salona, where he eventually succumbed to illness or injury and died. King Juba had borne rule at Pharsalus; he had vanquished Curio. His flying horsemen and numerous archers formed the main strength of the army. The Pompeian governor Varus played a subordinate part by his side. He was forced to deliver the soldiers of Curio, who had surrendered to him, over to the king. As a result, Varus had to watch them being executed or taken into Numidia's interior. After the Battle of Pharsalus, a significant change occurred.

Further Metellus Scipio demanded the supreme command for himself, as Pompey had recognised him in the Thessalian campaign as being on an equal footing, largely due to their familial relationship rather than military achievements. He did so, not because he felt himself unequal to such a task, or because his vanity lay in declining rather than accepting. He was also not motivated by love or respect for Scipio, with whom he was personally at odds. Scipio's notorious inefficiency had earned him importance solely due to his position as father-in-law to Pompeius. However, it was simply a matter of obstinate legal formalism that led him to choose letting the republic go to ruin through due course of law over saving it in an irregular manner. In the present state of the Roman forces in Africa, Juba could not avoid lowering his claims to some extent. He still carried the point that the pay of his troops should be charged on the Roman treasury and that the cession of the province of Africa would be assured to him in the event of victory.

Had he proceeded to Africa immediately after Pompeius' death, he would have found a weak, disorganized, and frightened army, with utter anarchy among the leaders. In contrast, there was now in Africa, largely due to Cato's energy, an army equal in number to that defeated at Pharsalus, under noted leaders, and under effective supervision. Gnaeus Pompeius, who had lost time on the way, made a vain attempt to establish himself in Mauretania and arrived too late. Gaius Trebonius, whom Caesar sent to Spain after his return from the east (autumn of 49 BCE), met with absolute obedience everywhere. The only man who held influence over them had been absent and almost unheard-of for a year; while the officers placed over them were far more afraid of the soldiers than the soldiers feared them. The conquerors of the world overlooked every outrage against those who provided them with quarters, and every breach of discipline.

Caesar, after being sufficiently persuaded, granted permission. However, the leaders of the rebellion had already received their share of spoils. Caesar's situation was, however, quite unpleasant. The simplest consideration suggested this plan of campaign; even Cato, although far from a strategist, counselled its adoption. He offered to lead a corps to Italy and rally the Republicans there, an idea that might have met with success amidst the chaos in that region. As each successive force arrived on the battlefield, Scipio and Juba lost the will to engage in a pitched battle. Caesar, however, lacked the means to compel them to fight due to their exceptional superiority in light cavalry. Caesar was compelled by the enemy's horsemen to hold as much ground as possible on high ground or to cover his flanks with fortified lines. However, he gradually accustomed his soldiers to the foreign method of warfare during this prolonged and seemingly endless conflict. The Battle of Thapsus took place when Caesar, having received his final reinforcements, shifted his forces laterally towards the city. Scipio had, as we have said, strongly garrisoned this town. This action committed a blunder by presenting an easy target to his opponent. He soon added another error: refusing to give up the battle at Thapsus, which Caesar had wanted and Scipio had previously refused, on grounds that would decide the outcome in favor of the infantry line.

His legions, accustomed to war, had already formed a correct estimate of the enemy from the lack of precision in their mode of array and ill-closed ranks. This compelled them to sound the trumpet for an attack before even the general gave the signal, and they advanced along the whole line, led by Caesar himself. When he saw his men advancing without waiting for his orders, Caesar galloped forward to lead them against the enemy. Cato's death in Utica marked the end of the struggle in Africa following the Battle of Thapsus. This event was comparable to one that occurred a year and a half earlier in the east, after the defeat at Pharsalus.

The more courageous view found several supporters. It was proposed that the state manumit slaves capable of arms, but Cato rejected this as an illegal encroachment on private property. Instead, he suggested a patriotic appeal to the slave-owners. The end of the monarchy. After four years of struggle between Pompeius and the republicans, the monarchy of Caesar was finally defeated, resulting in a complete victory for the new ruler. The monarchy was not established for the first time on the battlefields of Pharsalus and Thapsus. It may have originated earlier, with Pompey and Caesar forming a partnership to overthrow the existing aristocratic constitution. The End of the Republic

The constitutional struggle had come to an end. This was proclaimed by Marcus Cato as he took his own life in Utica. For many years, he was the foremost man in the struggle of the legitimate republic against its oppressors. He had lost all hope of victory by then.

Cato has played a greater part in history than many men who were far superior to him in intellect. It only adds to the depth and tragedy of his demise that he was, in fact, a fool. In reality, it is precisely because Don Quixote is a fool that he becomes a tragic figure. This republican opposition derived from Cato's whole attitude: stately, transcendental in its rhetoric, pretentiously rigid, hopeless, and faithful to death. It began even immediately after his death to revere him as a saint—the man who in his lifetime was not infrequently its laughing stock and scandal.

A man of genius was also a gentleman and a monarch, yet he still possessed a heart. With the most excellent men of his time, of both high and humble rank, he maintained noble relations of mutual fidelity. As he himself never abandoned any of his followers, Pompeius had adopted a pusillanimous and unfeeling manner. However, he adhered to his friends - not just for personal gain - through both good times and bad, without wavering. Several of these, such as Aulus Hirtius and Gaius Matius, gave noble testimonies of their loyalty even after his death.

Literature had a profound impact on him, but it was Alexander's thoughts of Homer's Achilles that kept him awake at night. Meanwhile, Julius Caesar spent his sleepless hours pondering the nuances of Latin noun and verb inflections. While wine was meant to accompany Alexander the Great, the temperate Roman, after the revels of his youth had ended, he avoided it entirely. Despite enjoying the company of women, the monarch kept them at arm's length, allowing no significant impact on his decisions. His infamous relationship with Queen Cleopatra was actually a strategic move to address a vulnerability in his political standing. Caesar was a realist and a man of sense. Whatever he undertook, he achieved with the cool sobriety that characterized his genius. Caesar's individual accomplishments are not worth discussing because his actions were always part of a larger context. Caesar's accomplishments were largely overshadowed by his role as a skilled politician. As a renowned orator, author, and military leader, he excelled in each area due to his exceptional leadership skills. It was natural that he should remain even more a statesman than a general. Like Cromwell, who transformed himself from an opposition leader into a military chief and democratic king, Cromwell seems little like the dissolute Roman prince, yet in his development and the results of his endeavors, he is perhaps most akin to Caesar. The general's losses are offset by the statesman's gains.

Although a master of the art of war, he nonetheless took statesmanlike measures to prevent civil unrest. When such conflict arose, he sought to minimize the bloodshed associated with his laurels. The founder of a military monarchy was unlike anyone else in history. He possessed an extraordinary energy that prevented any hierarchical system of marshals or the establishment of a government by praetorians from coming into being. Caesar entirely avoided the blunder into which so many men otherwise on an equality with him have fallen, of carrying military tone of command into politics; however much occasion his disagreeable relations with the senate gave for it, he never resorted to outrages such as that of the 18th Brumaire.

Alexander on the Hypanis, Napoleon at Moscow, were forced to retreat due to circumstances. They resented fate for granting them only limited victories; Caesar voluntarily withdrew from the Thames and Rhine, and considered implementing well-thought-out border regulations even in the Danube and Euphrates regions. Caesar was a man who placed himself at the forefront of his time, and possessed the essential characteristic of the Roman people - practical ability as a citizen. His Hellenism, however, was merely an extension of the Italian nationality that had been deeply intertwined for centuries. The Roman hero stood beside his youthful Greek predecessor, not just as an equal, but as a superior. However, the world had grown old, and its former youthful luster had faded.

For this reason, the delicate poetic taste of the nations has not troubled itself with the unpoetic Roman, and on the other hand has bestowed upon Philip's son all the golden luster of poetry, with all the vibrant colors of legend. But with equal reason, the political life of nations has repeatedly reverted to the lines drawn by Caesar over thousands of years. The fact that the peoples who belong to the world still designate their highest monarchs by his name today conveys a warning that is both significant and deeply troubling. All the older quarrels of the republican past were regarded as done away for ever.

While Caesar issued orders that the statues of Sulla, which had been toppled by the mob in the capital upon hearing of the Battle of Pharsalus, should be re-erected, he also recognized the fact that history alone would sit in judgment on that great man. At the same time, he cancelled the last remaining effects of Sulla's exceptional laws and recalled from exile those who had been banished during the Cinnan and Sertorian troubles. He then restored to the children of those outlawed by Sulla their forfeited privilege of eligibility to office. The former should have been, if possible, even less satisfied than the latter with Caesar's conduct after the victory and with his summons to abandon the old standing ground of party, which was to be expected. The Roman popular party had been driven forward in gradual progression from reform to revolution, from revolution to anarchy, and ultimately to a war against property. They celebrated among themselves the memory of the reign of terror and now adorned the tomb of Catiline with flowers and garlands, just as they had formerly done for the Gracchi. Having placed themselves under Caesar's banner, they expected him to fulfill what Catiline had not achieved.

The rabble of this description, high and low, out of sheer frustration at the failure of their political economy, began to flirt with the Pompeians, and later even during Caesar's prolonged absence of nearly two years from Italy. Caesar attributed little importance to these attempts to revive Catiline's projects. He tolerated Dolabella in Italy and later even welcomed him back into favor. To political prosecutions, Caesar assigned the narrowest possible limits after his victory.

The rule that guided him thereafter was this: anyone who served as an officer in the enemy's army or sat in the opposition senate after Ilerda's capitulation, if he survived the struggle, would lose his property and political rights, and be banished from Italy for life. If he did not survive, his property would at least go to the state. However, anyone who had previously received a pardon from Caesar and was found again in the enemy's ranks forfeited their life. These rules were however materially modified during their execution. The praise of Cato became the fashionable theme of oppositional pamphleteers, who were all the more grateful for it. Even literature itself was no longer free.

No course remained but to overawe the authors; as a result, men well known and dangerous in a literary sense, such as Publius Nigidius Figulus and Aulus Caecina, had more difficulty obtaining permission to return to Italy than other exiles. In contrast, oppositional writers who tolerated in Italy were subjected to practical censorship, which was all the more annoying because the measure of punishment they feared was arbitrary. The underground machinations of the overthrown parties against the new monarchy will be more fittingly set forth in another connection. Caesar's attitude towards the parties was one of disdain. Despite his tendency to show a reckless disregard for matters concerning his personal well-being, he could not ignore the grave threat posed by the growing discontent among his subjects, which not only endangered his own life but also that of his legacy. The most prominent chiefs of the defeated parties were indeed removed. However, full pardon was not withheld from men of the second and third rank, particularly those of the younger generation. They were not allowed to passively oppose the new administration but were instead encouraged to take an active part through more or less gentle persuasion, ultimately accepting honours and offices from it.
