As with Henry IV and William of Orange, so with Julius Caesar his greatest difficulties began only after his victory. Note: I made some minor changes to preserve the original meaning while improving readability and flow. Every revolutionary conqueror learns by experience that if he does not remain like Cinna and Sulla a mere party-chief, but instead adopts the approach of Caesar, Henry the Fourth, and William of Orange, where the welfare of the commonwealth takes precedence over his own party's one-sided programme, then for a moment all parties - both his own and those he has vanquished - will unite against the new leader. The more great and pure his idea of his new vocation is, the stronger this unity will be. It is proper to claim, once for all, that historians everywhere tacitly presume and protest against the custom of using historical praise and censure as phrases of general application, dissociated from the given circumstances. This practice is common to simplicity and perfidy. In this case, construing the judgment on Caesar's rule into a judgment on what is called Caesarism is inappropriate.

Every constitution, no matter how defective, that allows for the free self-determination of the majority of its citizens infinitely surpasses even the most brilliant and humane forms of absolutism. This is because such a system can evolve and adapt, allowing it to truly live, whereas an absolutist regime is static and therefore stagnant. From Caesar's time, as the sequel will show, the Roman system had only an external coherence. It received only a mechanical extension, whereas internally, it became utterly withered and dead. Caesar's work was necessary and salutary, not because it was or could be inherently blessed, but because— with the national organization of antiquity, based on slavery and alien to republican-constitutional representation, and in the presence of the legitimate urban constitution that had ripened into oligarchic absolutism over five hundred years—it became logically necessary and the least of evils for absolute military monarchy.

History will not allow the true Caesar to receive his rightful honor, as her judgment may be misled by the presence of bad rulers and may provide an opportunity for deceit and dishonesty. This dictatorship, both in its first ephemeral and second enduring tenure, was not that of the old constitution, but rather the supreme exceptional office arranged by Sulla; an office whose functions were fixed not by constitutional ordinances regarding the supreme single magistracy, but by a special decree of the people. As a result, the holder received an official prerogative de jure unlimited, which superseded the republican partition of powers. For other magistracies in the proper sense, there was no room alongside this dictatorship. Caesar did not take up the censorship as such, (11) but he undoubtedly exercised censorial rights, particularly the important right to nominate senators, after a comprehensive manner.

It is not easy to determine what definitive formal shape Caesar had in view. This is partly due to the fact that during this period of transition, ephemeral and permanent buildings are not clearly distinguished from one another. Additionally, the devotion of his clients, who already anticipated their master's nod, placed him under a multitude of decrees and laws conferring honors. The dictatorship emerged as a dominant force, often taking center stage due to Julius Caesar's desire to utilize its significance within the constitutional framework. He sought to employ this extraordinary measure to address exceptional challenges. The new name of Imperator is indeed more suitable for formal expressions of the monarchy. Its introduction lacks a clear reason.

During various periods and from different perspectives—within the decemviral power, Sullan regency, and Caesar's dictatorship—the concept of an exceptional authority had been revisited. This practical recurrence was driven by a logical necessity: whenever an extraordinary power seemed necessary, it gave rise to an unlimited imperium—a form of regal power that was essentially unbounded. Mankind has had infinite difficulty in reaching new creations, and therefore cherishes the once-developed forms as sacred heirlooms. The patrician body continued to exist, albeit without its essential privileges as an order, maintaining a close aristocratic character. However, it could no longer receive new members, leading to a decline over the centuries. By Caesar's time, there were only about fifteen or sixteen remaining patrician families.

There was therefore no need for special restrictive measures against the comitia. Many years of experience had shown that every government, whether an oligarchy or a monarchy, easily maintained good relations with this formal sovereign. However,  The supreme, or sole, magistrate's commands are unconditionally valid as long as they remain in office. However, it is unclear whether legislation solely resides with the king and the burgesses in concert. Nevertheless, a royal edict can be considered equivalent to law at least until its author steps down from office. The Senate as the state council of the monarch. While the democratic king thus conceded to the community of the people at least a formal share in the sovereignty, it was not his intention to divide his authority with the governing body that had previously existed, the college of senators.

In the case of ordinary additions, he secured a permanent influence through the circumstance that electoral colleges were bound by law to give their votes to the first twenty candidates for quaestorship who had been provided with letters of recommendation from the monarch. The crown was also at liberty to confer honorary rights attaching to the quaestorship or any office superior to it, including a seat in the senate, even on individuals not qualified. Personal Government by Caesar

While the rule of the nobility was set aside and its existence undermined, the senate in its new form became a mere tool of the monarch. At the same time, autocracy was strictly carried out in the administration and government of the state, with the entire executive concentrated in the hands of the monarch. The Imperator naturally decided on every matter of importance. Caesar was able to establish personal government to an extent that we puny men can hardly conceive. This achievement cannot be attributed solely to the unprecedented speed and decisiveness of his work, but rather has its roots in a broader cause.

Where it was inevitable, as especially when during his frequent absence from Rome he had need of a higher organ there, the person destined for this purpose was significantly enough not the legal deputy of the monarch or the prefect of the city. Instead, it was Lucius Cornelius Balbus, Caesar's banker and a Phoenician merchant from Gades, who held the position without officially recognized jurisdiction. In administration, Caesar was careful to resume control of the state's treasury—appropriated by the senate after the fall of royal power—and entrusted its management to only those servants who were entirely devoted to him. The country along the Nile, geographically isolated and politically centralized, was better suited than any other district to break off permanently under an able leader from the central power. This was evident in the repeated attempts made by hard-pressed Italian party chiefs to establish themselves there during the recent crisis, which had proven futile.

It was probably this consideration that induced Caesar not to declare the land formally a province. Instead, he left the harmless Lagids there. This decision likely stemmed from the fact that the legions stationed in Egypt were not entrusted to a man belonging to the senate or the former government. In contrast, command positions like those of tax-receivers were considered menial offices. In general, however, Caesar considered it a weighty matter that the soldiers of Rome should not be commanded in the same way as those of Oriental kings. The spiritual hierarchy on the other hand differed from Caesar's approach to state-economy. Although he issued a detailed law respecting this portion of the state, no material alteration was made. Instead, Caesar connected the regent with the supreme pontificate and possibly also with membership in the higher priestly colleges. Additionally, three new stalls were created in each of the three highest colleges, and three more in the fourth college of the banquet-masters. The faint lustre that the worship of Jupiter was still able to impart shone around the newly established throne; and the old national faith became in its final stages the instrument of a Caesarian papacy, which, however, was from the outset both hollow and feeble.

In fact, we find him sitting in judgment publicly in the Forum of the capital, a practice reminiscent of ancient kings. He is presiding over an inquiry into the alleged high treason of client princes, who are accused of the same crime as those Roman burgesses on trial. This highlights the peculiar privilege that Roman burgesses enjoyed: the right to a public judicial procedure. The supreme jurisdiction of the kings was revived, but despite Caesar's impartial and diligent efforts, it had limited practical application. While formerly there had been a zealous competition for the posts of staff officers, now every man of equestrian rank who chose to serve was guaranteed a military tribuneship. In fact, several of these posts were filled by men of humbler rank. Any man of quality at all who still served sought to complete his term of service in Sicily or some other province where he could avoid facing the enemy.

The general obligation to bear arms still existed legally. However, when combined with conscription, the levy was implemented in an irregular manner. Many individuals who were required to serve were overlooked, while those who had already been levied remained under military service for thirty years or more. The analogy of the relation between the praetor and the consul, or the consul and the dictator, served as a basis. The governor, in his own right, retained supreme military authority in his province. However, the Imperator was entitled to take it away from him at any moment and assume it for himself or his delegates. The governor's authority was limited to the province, whereas the Imperator's authority, similar to that of the regal and earlier consular authorities, extended over the entire empire. Caesar's military plans were focused on defending the frontier. However, despite the urgent need for a strong military monarchy, and Caesar's clear intention to hold supreme command, he was not keen on establishing his authority through the army alone.

He undoubtedly recognized the need for a standing army in his state, primarily due to its geographical location necessitating a thorough regulation of the frontiers and establishment of permanent frontier garrisons. He had worked at the tranquillization of Spain during earlier periods and partly during the recent civil war. He established strong positions for the defense of the frontier in Africa, along the great desert, and in the northwest of the empire, along the line of the Rhine. Although the institution of a special bodyguard for the general had already existed for some time, it was largely absent from Caesar's system; his praetorian cohort primarily consisted of orderly officers or non-military attendants, and was not considered a select corps. As a result, it never became an object of jealousy among the troops of the line. While Caesar, even as a general, practically dropped the bodyguard, he still tolerated a smaller guard around his person. Although constantly beset by lurking assassins and aware of it, he rejected the senate's proposal to establish a select guard. As soon as things grew quiet, he dismissed the Spanish escort he had initially used in the capital; contenting himself with the retinue of lictors sanctioned by traditional usage for Roman supreme magistrates.

The impracticality of his ideal was evident, yet Caesar still clung to the notion of establishing a Periclean government in Rome, one that would be based on the confidence of the nation rather than the sword. This idea had been a cornerstone of his party and his youth, but as he navigated the harsh realities of power, he was forced to abandon it. Even now, however, Caesar retained a deep commitment to not establishing a military monarchy, driven by an energy that history rarely sees. It was owing to the work of Caesar that the Roman military state did not become a police-state until after several centuries had passed. The Roman Imperators, although they differed little from their great founder in terms of sovereignty, still employed soldiers primarily against public enemies rather than citizens. They held both the nation and army in high esteem, and thus considered it inappropriate to appoint the latter as constable over the former. Under the former head, the distribution of corn to the multitude of the capital claimed almost exorbitant sums. Through an extension given by Cato in 691 (40), the yearly expenditure for that purpose rose to 30 million sesterces (300,000 pounds). After its abolition in 696, it consumed even a fifth of the state's revenues.

The Roman Empire's military expenditures were substantial. The construction of fleets was a significant expense, with an example being the large sum spent five years after the great raid in 687, when 34 million sestertii (approximately £340,000) were allocated simultaneously. But considerable as these demands made on the Roman exchequer were, it would still have been able to meet them, had not its administration once been so exemplary been affected by the universal laxity and dishonesty of this age; the payments of the treasury were often suspended merely because of the neglect to call up its outstanding claims. Financial reforms under Caesar's leasing of direct taxes abolished. However, as soon as the threads of Roman state finance were concentrated no longer in the senate, but in Caesar's cabinet, a new era of stricter order and more efficient connection was ushered in, pervading all aspects of the machinery. The two institutions that originated with Gaius Gracchus had a profound impact on the Roman financial system. They included the leasing of direct taxes and the distribution of grain, which were partially abolished and partially reformed.

Caesar did not wish to follow his predecessor's example, where the nobility was kept in check by the banker-aristocracy and the capital's populace. Instead, he aimed to set them aside and free the commonwealth from all parasites, regardless of their social rank. As a result, Caesar approached these two critical issues differently than Gaius Gracchus, who had taken on Sulla, an oligarch. The distribution of corn in the capital had long been seen as a privilege reserved for the ruling community. As a result, it was expected that the community would be fed by its subjects. The number of those whose proportion of tribute was lowered increased; communities in Further Spain, for instance, had already received a reduction of their tribute by Caesar's suggestion to the senate. Now, the most oppressed province of Asia not only had its direct taxes levied more easily, but also had a third of its tribute wholly remitted. It is true that various towns, such as Little Leptis in Africa and Sulci in Sardinia, and several Spanish communities, saw an increase in their tribute due to penalties imposed on them for their behavior during the last war.

The power of the nobility rested in great measure on their colossal wealth. It was this wealth that made their power effective, and it was this very wealth that needed to be broken in order for the nobility's power to be effectively challenged. The confiscations' odium was somewhat mitigated by Caesar's decision to allocate their proceeds solely for the state's benefit. Unlike Sulla, who often overlooked fraudulent acts by his favorites, Caesar demanded payment in full, even from his most loyal supporters. g Marcus Antonius The former pay of 1 1/3 sesterces (3 1/4 pence) per day had been fixed in ancient times, when money held a different value from that of Caesar's Rome. It could only have been retained until the common day-labourer in the capital earned an average of 3 sesterces (7 1/2 pence) daily by the labour of his hands, because soldiers entered the army not for pay, but mainly for the illicit benefits of military service. The first condition for a serious reform in the military system and to eliminate those irregular gains made by soldiers, which were a burden mostly on provincials, was an increase suitable to the times in regular pay. Fixing it at 2 1/2 sesterces (6 1/2 pence) can be regarded as an equitable step. However, this decision imposed a significant burden on the treasury, which was both necessary and ultimately beneficial in its consequences.

The wars themselves consumed enormous sums. The amount of money required to fulfill the promises Caesar had made during the civil war was likely similar. It was a bad example that every common soldier received 20,000 sesterces for participating in the civil war, while burgesses in the capital received 300 sesterces for not participating, as an addition to their allowance. However, Caesar, who had once pledged his word under pressure of circumstances, was too proud to renege on it. Caesar answered numerous demands for honourable liberality and invested large sums to build more, particularly, as the republic's financial struggles had left many structures neglected. The cost of his buildings, partly constructed during the Gallic campaigns and partly afterwards in Rome, was estimated at 160 million sesterces (1.6 million pounds).

The police supervision, so urgently required, of associations by the rabble was initially neglected and later declared punishable as an infringement on the people's freedom. If we were to imagine a London with the slave population of New Orleans, the police force of Constantinople, and the non-industrial character of modern Rome, stirred by politics similar to those in Paris in 1848, we would gain an approximate understanding of republican glory and its departure, which Cicero and his contemporaries lamented in their letters. However offensive was the mixed population of parasites, especially the Helleno-Oriental, in Rome, he never opposed its expansion. It is significant that at his popular festivals for the capital, he caused dramas to be performed not merely in Latin and Greek, but also in other languages, such as Phoenician, Hebrew, Syrian, and Spanish.

This emigration, however, must have been more than a mere temporary arrangement. Caesar, convinced like every other man of sense, believed that the only true remedy for the misery of the proletariat lay in a well-regulated system of colonization. Given his position within the empire, he was uniquely positioned to realize this vision on an almost unlimited scale. It is likely that Caesar intended to permanently continue this process, thereby creating a constant means of alleviating an evil that continually reproduced itself. With the exception of ancient guilds and associations, religious unions of the Jews, and other specially excepted categories, permission to form a permanent society with fixed assembly times and standing deposits was granted by the senate, subject to a concession that typically required the monarch's consent. Projects were already formed for a new senate-house, a new magnificent bazaar, a theatre to rival Pompeius', a public Latin and Greek library after the model of the recently destroyed one at Alexandria—the first of its kind in Rome—and finally, a temple of Mars that would surpass all existing ones in riches and glory.

By this gigantic plan, on one hand, the most dangerous enemy of the capital, malaria in the neighborhood, would be eradicated. On the other hand, the extremely limited facilities for building in the capital would be immediately enlarged by substituting the Campus Vaticanus, now located on the left bank of the Tiber, for the Campus Martius. This would allow the latter spacious field to be used for public and private buildings. At the same time, the capital would gain a safe seaport, a facility sorely needed. The city of Rome, however, gained greatly from the new order of things, becoming more commodious and magnificent. Nevertheless, its political supremacy was lost irreparably through this very change. Rome, despite its inability to develop a living communal character as a capital, was arguably less essential than other municipalities of the imperial period.

The Roman Republic was a den of robbers, yet it was also the state. The Rome of the monarchy, though it began to adorn itself with the glories of three continents and shine in gold and marble, remained nothing but a royal residence connected to a poorhouse – essentially a necessary evil. Italian agriculture faced significant challenges during this period. In the capital city, efforts focused on eliminating palpable evils through police ordinances on a large scale. However, addressing the deeper disorganization of Italy's economy proved to be an even more daunting task. Despite the most earnest attempts to eradicate small holdings, farm-husbandry had largely ceased to be the dominant form of economy in Italy during this period, except for the valleys of the Apennine and Abruzzo regions. The pastoral husbandry, as previously explained, had been expanding particularly in the south and southeast of Italy. However, this practice was ultimately a step backward in every respect. Nevertheless, it did contribute to the overall advancement of agriculture. Significant progress was made in improving livestock breeds. g Breeding stock was valued at 60,000 sesterces, 100,000 sesterces, and 400,000 sesterces.

Lucius Domitius Ahenobarbus, a wealthy individual, pledged to provide twenty thousand soldiers with four iugera of land each from his own property. Pompeius' estate was valued at seventy million sesterces, equivalent to seven hundred thousand pounds. Aesopus, an actor, had an estate worth twenty million sesterces, or two hundred thousand pounds. Marcus Crassus, the wealthiest among them, initially possessed seven million sesterces, equivalent to seventy thousand pounds, before his fortune grew to one hundred seventy million sesterces, or seventeen hundred thousand pounds. If the common man was saved from starvation only by support from the resources of the state, then it was a necessary consequence of this mendicant misery that he became accustomed to the beggar's laziness and its accompanying good cheer. The house of Marcus Lepidus, consul in 676 BC, was considered the finest in Rome at the time of Sulla's death. However, by the next generation, it had fallen to 100th place among Roman palaces.

Even at popular festivals, three sorts of foreign wine were distributed: Sicilian, Lesbian, and Chian. A generation earlier, it had been sufficient to serve Greek wine once at great banquets. In the cellar of orator Hortensius, a stock of 10,000 jars was found, each containing 33 quarts of foreign wine. The epicureans of that time were no less zealous in their search for new ingredients than naturalists are today. The practice of having guests induce vomiting after a meal was no longer unusual. Caesar's wealth was surpassed only by his liabilities. After deducting his assets, Caesar owed approximately 25 million sesterces, equivalent to £250,000. Marcus Antonius, at the age of 24, owed 6 million sesterces, which rose to 40 million after 14 years. Curio and Milo each owed 60 million and 70 million sesterces, respectively.

True friendship. While at the core of national life, a sense of camaraderie can be deceivingly superficial. Beneath the surface, however, lies a complex web of relationships that can be detrimental if not managed properly. The more genuine connections we make, the more fragile they become, making it essential to maintain a delicate balance between our personal and public lives. All the world's visitors were accommodated; so that in houses of quality, it was necessary to admit persons presenting themselves every morning for the levee in a predetermined order fixed by the master or occasionally by the attendant. Only the more notable ones were given audience one by one, while the rest were admitted summarily, either in groups or en masse at the end—a distinction attributed to Gaius Gracchus, who paved the way for the new monarchy. A scandal unparalleled by Publius Clodius, such as that produced at the women's festival in the house of the Pontifex Maximus in 693, was far worse than the incidents that fifty years earlier had led to a series of capital sentences, passed almost without investigation and wholly without punishment.

While this notion of marriage had long been viewed as a burden, people would only undertake it for the public good. However, even in ancient Cato and those who shared his views, we find the maxim that Polybius had identified a century earlier: that a citizen's duty is to preserve great wealth and, consequently, not have too many children. As the Roman community expanded into an empire encompassing many nations, the governing aristocracy lost sight of Italy as their exclusive homeland. The number of men levied or enlisted for service dwindled significantly, with a considerable portion succumbing to the many wars, particularly the bloody civil war. Another portion became entirely disconnected from their native country due to the prolonged period of service, which sometimes spanned multiple generations. As a result of these events, Italy gained two main groups of people: the slaves and freedmen on one hand, and craftsmen and traders from Asia Minor, Syria, and Egypt on the other. These individuals primarily settled in the capital city and even more so in the seaport towns of Ostia, Puteoli, and Brundisium. In the largest and most important part of Italy, however, even such a substitution of impure elements for pure had little effect. The population was visibly declining.

Tusculum, although still one of the most esteemed communities of Latium, consisted almost solely of genteel families who lived in the capital but retained their native Tuscan franchise. It was far inferior to other communities in terms of the number of burgesses entitled to vote, even those in small interior towns in Italy. Caesar was not one of those wise men who refused to build a sea wall, because no barrier could withstand a sudden surge of the tide. It was Caesar who first gave an insolvent the right--on which our modern bankruptcy regulations are based--of formally ceding his estate to his creditors. This allowed him to save at least some of his personal freedom, albeit with diminished honorary and political rights. By doing so, he could begin a new financial existence, in which he would only be liable for claims from the earlier period and not protected during liquidation, provided he could pay them without falling into renewed financial ruin.

The great democrat had the honor of emancipating personal freedom in principle from capital. He also sought to curb the excessive power of capital by introducing laws against usury. Transgressions of this enactment were treated as criminal offenses and sent before a special jury. It is highly likely that he did not limit himself to the injunction that applied only to Italy, which set a maximum amount on loans, but also established specific interest rate limits for the provinces.

The fact that these Lucullan enactments later appeared in all their forms as imperial law and had thus become the basis of both Roman and modern legislation regarding interest, may also be traced back to an ordinance by Caesar. While hitherto nobody in Italy had been sure of his life or property, whether movable or immoveable, Roman condottieri for instance, during intervals when their gangs were not involved in managing the capital's politics, turned to robbery in the forests of Etruria or acquired fresh country estates from their paymasters, this sort of club-law was now at an end; and particularly, the agricultural population of all classes must have felt the beneficial effects of the change. The regulation that Caesar established, requiring new owners to wait twenty years before alienating the lands they received, was a moderate approach between granting full rights of alienation, which would have quickly returned a significant portion of distributed land to the great capitalists, and the permanent restrictions on land dealing imposed by Tiberius Gracchus and Sulla.

Elevation of the Municipal System

While the government energetically applied itself to remove the diseased and strengthen the sound elements of Italian national life, the newly-regulated municipal system, which had recently developed out of the crisis of the Social War, was intended to communicate to the new absolute monarchy the communal life compatible with it. Its purpose was to impart a quickened action to the sluggish circulation of the noblest elements of public life once more. The leading principles in the two municipal ordinances issued in 705 for Cisalpine Gaul and in 709 for Italy are apparent. The latter, which remained the fundamental law for all succeeding times, is first, the strict purification of urban corporations from immoral elements, without any political police presence. Second, there is a restriction on centralization and a significant freedom of movement within communities. This includes reserved rights to elect magistrates and limited civil and criminal jurisdiction. Their material well-being, almost the only thing for which the provincials still cared, was far less disturbed by those occurrences, which although numerous in proportion to the many tyrants yet affected merely isolated individuals, than by the financial exactions that pressed heavily on all, which had never previously been prosecuted with such energy.

While the taxation in its original character had been an indemnification for the burden of military defense undertaken by Rome, and the community paying tribute had thus a right to remain exempt from ordinary service, garrison-service was now— as is attested — not applicable. g In the case of Sardinia, for the most part, the provincials were subjected to this imposition, and even in ordinary armies, they were tasked with bearing the entire weight of cavalry service, alongside other duties. During Gaius Verres' three-year administration of Sicily, the number of farmers in Leontini decreased from 84 to 32, in Motuca from 187 to 86, in Herbita from 252 to 120, and in Agyrium from 250 to 80. This resulted in a significant decline in agricultural activity in four of Sicily's most fertile districts, with 59% of landholders opting to leave their fields fallow rather than cultivate them under Verres' government. These landholders were neither small farmers nor did they have a small number; rather, they were respectable planters and in large part Roman burgesses.

The evil of piracy has been previously described in another context. The panaceas of the prohibitive system, which the Roman governor would often impose when there was a shortage of money or a dearth, did not provide a lasting solution. By prohibiting the export of gold or grain from the province, they hoped to alleviate the situation, but it ultimately proved ineffective. The conditions of provinces generally, where such grievances afflicted communities and individuals for generations, with an inevitable, steady, and yearly-increasing oppression, would prove fatal to even the best-regulated public or private economy. The most extreme misery would inevitably spread across all nations from the Tagus to the Euphrates. All communities are ruined, according to a treatise published as early as 684. The same truth is also attested for Spain and Narbonese Gaul, two provinces that, compared to others, were still in relatively good economic condition. Caesar and the Provinces

The Optimates ridiculed the new master who personally inspected his "farms" in succession; however, the condition of each province required the utmost earnestness and wisdom of one such exceptional individual, who could redeem the name of king from being perceived by nations as merely a symbol of human fallibility.

We are not expressly informed that Caesar instituted similar general liquidations of debt in the provinces after the civil war. However, from what has been remarked and from what was done in Italy,(89) it can hardly be doubted that Caesar directed his efforts towards this object or that it formed part of his plan. While thus the Imperator, as far as lay within human power, relieved the provincials from the oppressions of the magistrates and capitalists of Rome, it might be expected that the government he imparted fresh vigour to would also scare off the wild border peoples and disperse the freebooters by land and sea, like the rising sun chases away the mist. Caesar brought a new era of tolerance to the sorely-tortured subjects, ushering in the first intelligent and humane government in centuries. This marked a significant shift from previous policies, which were often driven by fear rather than strength.

The Greeks were already everywhere—and, most decidedly, just where the national feeling was purest and strongest, on the frontiers threatened by barbaric denationalization. g In Massilia, on the north coast of the Black Sea, and along the Euphrates and Tigris rivers, he described the protector and avenger of Hellenism in Rome. In fact, Pompeius' foundation of towns in the far east resumed the beneficent work of Alexander after an interruption of centuries. It was already numerous in Rome before Caesar's time, and the Jews maintained their unity as fellow-countrymen even then. This is evident from an author of this period who noted that it was dangerous for a governor to offend the Jews in his province, lest he face ridicule upon his return to the capital. Even at this time, the predominant business of the Jews was trade. The Jewish trader followed the conquering Roman merchant everywhere, just as he would later accompany the Genoese and Venetian merchants. Capital flowed into the Jewish community alongside the Roman merchants.

This aspect of Judaism, although not the most pleasing feature in the overall picture of a mixed nation, was nevertheless an historical element that developed naturally over time. It was something a statesman could neither ignore nor combat, and Caesar, like his predecessor Alexander, recognized its importance with correct discernment, fostering it as far as possible. Alexander laid the foundation of Alexandrian Judaism, much like King David planned the temple of Jerusalem. However, Caesar also promoted Jewish interests in both Alexandria and Rome through special favors and privileges. Notably, he safeguarded their unique worship from both Roman and Greek local priests. In Caesar's organizations, the Latin nationality maintained its preeminence. This is evident from the fact that he issued all his laws and decrees in Latin, even though those intended for Greek-speaking territories were also issued in Greek.

This was necessary because an entire equalization of Greek and Latin elements in the state would have led to that catastrophe brought about by Byzantinism several centuries later. The Greek element was superior to the Roman in all intellectual aspects and in terms of its predominance. Within Italy, there were numerous Hellenes and half-Hellenes who migrated, either compulsorily or voluntarily, whose influence could not be underestimated. He was not entirely without reason when his countrymen treated him with divine honors after his death. In fact, he began the valet de chambre government of the imperial period, which in some respects was akin to a Greek dominance over the Romans. Massilia and Alexandria were not destroyed or denationalized despite political crises that may have suggested the destruction of strong Hellenistic pillars in the West and Egypt.

This province had already largely adopted Latin culture during the 40 years following its acquisition of Latin rights. The Province of Narbo, while Cisalpine Gaul was substantially merged with Italy, the place it had occupied was taken by the Transalpine province, which Caesar's conquests had converted from a frontier into an inland province. Its proximity and climate made it suitable for becoming an Italian land in due course of time. Similar rights were conferred on some Spanish communities in 709, as well as Latin rights on even more.

The stately provincial towns, which the insane fury of Juba and the desperate remnant of the constitutional party had converted into ruins, did not revive so rapidly as they had been reduced to ashes. Many a ruinous site recalled long afterwards this fatal period. However, the two new Julian colonies, Carthage and Cirta, emerged as the centres of African-Roman civilization. The formal contrast between Italy and the provinces had always depended on other distinctions. Even now, it is essential to maintain this distinction, as Italy was the sphere of civil jurisdiction and governed by consuls and praetors, while the provinces were districts under martial law, subject to proconsuls and propraetors. However, the procedure for both civil and martial law had become practically coincident over time, and the different titles of magistrates signified little once the Imperator held supreme authority. While the destruction of the two greatest markets of commerce in the Roman dominions marked the turning-point at which the protectorate of the Roman community degenerated into political tyranny over and financial exaction from the subject lands, the prompt and brilliant restoration of Carthage and Corinth marked the foundation of a new great commonwealth that was to train up all the regions on the Mediterranean to national and political equality, union in a genuine state.

The new united empire was furnished with a national character, which necessarily lacked individuality and was rather an inanimate product of art than a fresh growth of nature. However, it further required unity in those institutions that expressed the general life of nations - in constitution and administration, religion, jurisprudence, money, measures, and weights. Local diversities of varying character were compatible with essential union. The bestowal of Latin and then Roman rights on communities ready for full admission to the United States gradually led to uniform communal arrangements. Lastly, it had already become subsidiary law throughout the Roman Empire. While local statutes remained in force for non-commercial relations and local transactions within the same legal district, dealings relating to property between subjects of different districts were regulated after the model of the urban edict - although not applicable in law to these cases - both in Italy and its provinces.

The plan was not executed. As a result, the troublesome state of transition in Roman jurisprudence persisted for six centuries until a necessary reform was finally achieved by one of Caesar's successors, Emperor Justinian. The use of silver or copper for small denomination coins continued among several Western communities. In southern Gaul, Latin communities struck three-quarter denarii, while cantons in northern Gaul produced half-denarii. Copper small coins were also minted by various communes in the west, even after Caesar's time. However, this small money was standardized to follow the Roman standard, and its acceptance was likely mandatory only for local transactions. Neither change came into effect on January 1, 709. The Julian calendar, named after its creator Julius Caesar, had fallen out of use centuries earlier.

Caesar may have claimed that his goal had been achieved, but it is likely that the phrase he often uttered meant something else: that he had lived long enough. But precisely because the building was endless, the master continued to add stone to stone, with the same dexterity and elasticity, working tirelessly day after day, never faltering or delaying, as if he had only today and no tomorrow. He worked and created like no other mortal before or after him. As a master craftsman and innovator, his legacy endures to this day, nearly 2,000 years later, as the first and unparalleled Emperor Caesar. The Romano-Hellenic state religion and Stoic state philosophy were inseparably combined with it. For every government - oligarchy, democracy, or monarchy - they were not merely a convenient instrument, but indispensable. This was because it was impossible to construct a state wholly without religious elements, just as it was impossible to discover a new state religion that could replace the old one.

It was primarily due to antipathy towards boastful and tiresome Roman Pharisees, coupled with an increasing inclination to seek refuge from practical life in indolent apathy or empty irony, that occasioned during this epoch the extension of Epicurus' system to a larger circle and the naturalization of Diogenes' Cynic philosophy in Rome. A philosophy that did not alter traditional terms in pursuit of wisdom, but instead accepted existing concepts, and recognized only sensory perceptions as true, was always superior to the empty terminological games and hollow conceptions of Stoic wisdom. In contrast, the Cynic philosophy stood out among its contemporaries for its simplicity: it rejected all systems and systematizers, embracing a lack of structure in favor of skepticism. The ablest men of the older generation waged war against the Stoa, but the younger generation, exemplified by Catullus, showed no connection to it whatsoever. Instead, they harshly criticized it by deliberately ignoring it.

The worship of Mithra was a fresh addition, which is said to have first reached the Occident through the medium of pirates who met on the Mediterranean, coming from both the east and the west. The oldest known seat of this cult in the West is stated to be Mount Olympus in Lycia. Consequently, by the seventh century, the sciences of war, jurisprudence, and agriculture had become specialized fields of study. The increasing eagerness for Greek lore imparted a refined character to instruction.

To explain Homer or Euripides was, after all, no art. Teachers and scholars found their accounts better in handling the Alexandrian poems, which, besides, were more congenial to the Roman world of that day than genuine Greek national poetry. The Alexandrian poems, if not quite as venerable as the Iliad, possessed an age sufficiently respectable to be considered classics among schoolmasters. The Alexandrian poems had a lasting impact on Italian scholastic instruction, particularly as trial themes. They promoted knowledge, but at the cost of refinement and tact. The circumstance that gave rise to the term "urbanitas" and the idea of a polished national culture it expressed occurred during this period. This proves that urbanitas was not widespread, but rather in decline, and that people were keenly aware of its absence in the language and habits of Latinized barbarians or barbarized Latins.

The Roman Senate initially resisted and later merely tolerated Greek culture. In contrast, the newly formed Italo-Hellenic Empire, driven by its core value of humanitas, adopted policies to foster it in the Greek manner. The Vulgarism of Asia Minor

During this period, the development of the language was marked by a distinction between the classical Latin used in cultivated society and the vernacular language spoken in everyday life. They demanded full recognition for the language of life, regardless of its origin in Attica, Caria, or Phrygia. They spoke and wrote not to please learned elites, but for the benefit of the general public. There could be little objection to the principle; however, the result was far from ideal. The public of Asia Minor at that time had largely lost its appreciation for modesty and purity in production, and instead craved flashy and extravagant displays.

Ciceronianism was a literary and philosophical movement in Italy, characterized by Marcus Tullius Cicero (106-43 BCE), who initially followed the Hortensian style but later adopted a more refined approach after being influenced by Rhodian masters. He became devoted to strict linguistic purity and the periodic arrangement and modulation of his discourse. The models of language that he followed were particularly evident in the upper echelons of Roman society, which had largely escaped the influence of vulgarity. The earlier Latin and Greek literature, however, were of little significance in comparison to the influence of the latter on the rhythm of his oratory. The purifying of the language was not a response to the language of books versus conversation, but rather a reaction against the jargon of superficial and pretentious culture.

The scholastic and artificial classicism of the Ciceronian period stood as a form of repentance to innocence, much like the French classicists under Napoleon emulated the model French of Molière and Boileau. In contrast, the former classicism arose from the full vitality of life, while the latter was caught at the precise moment when a dying race was on the brink of extinction. In orthography, various changes were made to bring written language more fully into correspondence with spoken language. As a result, the "-u" in the middle of words like "maximus" was replaced by "-i", following Caesar's precedent. Of the two letters that had become superfluous, "-k" and "-q", the removal of the first was implemented, while the second was at least considered for removal. The Greek language was now universally current, and a Greek treatise found a quite different public from a Latin one. Romans of rank, such as Lucius Lucullus, Marcus Cicero, and Titus Atticus, like the kings of Armenia and Mauretania, occasionally published Greek prose and even Greek verses.

The Greek origin of authors was not a significant issue for the native Romans. Instead, it became a trivial matter that brought little attention. The literary and political factions in Italy shared a common goal: to uphold their Italian identity, with varying degrees of influence from Hellenistic culture. Cicero, for instance, was one of the chief representatives of the modern tendency in prose. Despite this, he still revered the older national poetry with nearly the same antiquarian respect as he did the aristocratic constitution and the augural discipline. He once said, "Patriotism requires that we should rather read a notoriously bad translation of Sophocles than the original." While this modern literary tendency shared similarities with the democratic monarchy, it had a number of secret adherents among its orthodox admirers of Ennius. However, there were also bolder judges who viewed the native literature with equal disregard for the senatorial politics. It is essentially similar to that literature of scholars, which developed among a cosmopolitan circle of erudite philologues, keeping aloof from living Romanic nationalities and their idioms. This artificial aftergrowth emerged during the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries. In contrast to classical Greek, the period of the Diadochi saw less marked differences between Latin and Italian languages, akin to the distinction between Manutius's Latin and Macchiavelli's Italian.

The season of comparative brilliance was shortly before and after the first Punic War. During this period, Roman poets such as Naevius, Ennius, Pacuvius, and others, including Varro and Lucretius, drew inspiration from Homer, Euripides, Menander, and other masters of living Greek literature. The Greek literature that originated after Alexander found its first Roman imitators among the contemporaries of Cicero and Caesar. The slight initial attempts from the Marian age can scarcely be taken into account, as they are not significant enough to be considered. Instead, the Roman Alexandrinism spread with singular rapidity. The Alexandrian poetry had a well-established place in teaching Italian youth. This reaction influenced Latin literature even more, as it was inherently reliant on Hellenistic schooling throughout its history. The new empire rested on the fact that the mighty streams of Greek and Latin nationality, after having flowed in parallel channels for many centuries, now at length coalesced. As a result, Italian literature was no longer solely reliant on Greek foundations but had to establish itself on an equal footing with contemporary Greek literature, or more specifically, Alexandrianism.

The Mediterranean monarchy of Caesar was undoubtedly a grand and necessary creation; however, it was brought into existence by an arbitrary superior will, and as such, it lacked the fresh popular life and overflowing national vigour that are characteristic of younger, more limited, and more natural commonwealths. The Italian state of the sixth century still managed to exhibit these qualities. While the Romano-Hellenic Alexandrinism of the time of Caesar and Augustus must be considered inferior to the older, imperfect national literature, it is on the other hand decidedly superior to the Alexandrinism of the time of the Diadochi as Caesar's enduring structure compared to the ephemeral creation of Alexander. We shall have to demonstrate that Augustan literature, compared with the kindred literature of the Diadochi period, was less associated with philologists and more imperial in nature. As a result, it had a far more lasting and widespread impact on upper-class society than Greek Alexandrianism ever did.

The mime originated from dances that accompanied the flute, a practice that had become customary. These performances took place on various occasions. g For the entertainment of the guests during dinner, but more especially when the theatre was empty and the actors were taking their breaks. This new mime appeared on the stage around 1672 and quickly absorbed the national harlequinade, sharing many essential similarities with it. It was used as a standard interlude and often served as an afterpiece alongside other dramatic performances. The plot was indeed still more indifferent, loose, and absurd than in the harlequinade. If it had been sufficiently chequered, the public would have asked why they laughed and not remonstrated with the poet, who instead of untangling the knot, cut it to pieces. Dramatic representations gained their regular place in public life not only in capital cities but also in country towns. The former, too, now acquired a permanent theatre, thanks to Pompeius, and the Campanian custom of stretching canvas over the theatre for protection during performances, which had always taken place in open air, is now also found in Rome.

In recitative poetry, metrical annals after Ennius' model seem not to have been lacking. However, they may have been subject to criticism by the gracious vow of his mistress, as Catullus sings - that the worst of the bad heroic poems should be presented as a sacrifice to holy Venus, if she would only bring back her lover from his vile political poetry to her arms.
